topics.title: Radiation Mechanism from an Antenna
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Radiation Mechanism from an Antenna

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Radiation Mechanism from an Antenna** is the physical process by which a time-varying current (or equivalently, accelerating charges) on a conducting structure launches electromagnetic (EM) energy into the surrounding free space. According to Maxwell's equations, any time-varying current distribution produces a **time-varying electric field**, which in turn produces a **time-varying magnetic field**, and this mutual regeneration propagates outward as an **electromagnetic wave** at the speed of light ($c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *An antenna is a transition device between a guided wave (transmission line) and a free-space wave, and radiation occurs whenever the current distribution on the conductor is non-uniform or time-varying. The fundamental source of radiation is the **accelerating charge**.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding one end of a long rope, and the other end is tied to a wall. If you shake the rope **slowly and gently** (low frequency), a small ripple travels down the rope and dies out. But if you shake it **abruptly** (a sudden jerk), you create a large wave that travels all the way to the wall and beyond. The "sudden jerk" corresponds to **acceleration of the rope's particles**.

Similarly:
- **Steady current (DC)** → no acceleration → no radiation.
- **Slowly varying current (low frequency)** → weak radiation.
- **Rapidly varying current (high frequency, like RF)** → strong radiation.

In an antenna, the **transmitter** forces electrons in the conductor to oscillate back and forth at radio frequencies. Because the electrons are constantly changing direction, they are **continuously accelerating** (positive acceleration one half-cycle, negative acceleration the next). It is this back-and-forth acceleration that "shakes off" the electromagnetic field into space — exactly like shaking the rope shakes off a wave.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight:**
> Stationary charge ⇒ Electrostatic field only.
> Uniformly moving charge (constant velocity) ⇒ Magnetostatic field only.
> **Accelerating charge** ⇒ Time-varying E and H fields ⇒ **Radiation**.
> Antennas are, fundamentally, devices engineered to create accelerating electrons on a macroscopic scale.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants will appear repeatedly throughout this topic:

- Speed of light in free space: $\mathbf{c = 3 \times 10^8}$ **m/s**
- Intrinsic impedance of free space: $\mathbf{\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377}$ **Ω**
- Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m**
- Permeability of free space: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m**
- Free-space wave number: $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ **(rad/m)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chain of Radiation — Step-by-Step Logic

The radiation process is governed by **Maxwell's curl equations** (Faraday's and Ampère-Maxwell laws):

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega\mu\vec{H}$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = j\omega\varepsilon\vec{E} + \vec{J}$$

- **Step 1 — Time-Varying Current Exists:** A high-frequency source (transmitter) drives a time-varying current $i(t) = I_0 \cos(\omega t)$ along the antenna conductor.
- **Step 2 — Current ⇒ Charges Are Accelerating:** Conduction electrons are forced to oscillate at angular frequency $\omega$. Since velocity is changing, $\dfrac{d\vec{v}}{dt} \neq 0$ → charges are accelerating.
- **Step 3 — Acceleration ⇒ Time-Varying Fields:** The accelerating charges produce a **time-varying electric field** $\vec{E}(t)$ surrounding the wire (different from the electrostatic Coulomb field of a static charge).
- **Step 4 — Time-Varying E ⇒ Time-Varying H (Faraday's Law):** A changing $\vec{E}$ curls around to produce a changing magnetic field $\vec{H}$.
- **Step 5 — Time-Varying H ⇒ Time-Varying E (Ampère-Maxwell Law):** A changing $\vec{H}$ regenerates $\vec{E}$, and the cycle continues.
- **Step 6 — Self-Sustaining EM Wave Propagates:** The mutually supporting $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ fields detach from the source and travel outward as a transverse electromagnetic (TEM) wave at velocity $c$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is the speed exactly $c$?**
> Combining the two curl equations in source-free regions yields the wave equation: $\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu\varepsilon \dfrac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2}$, whose solution propagates with velocity $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0}} = c$.

### 2.2 The Role of Antenna Geometry

The **shape** and **size** of the antenna determine the current distribution $I(z)$ along its length. A standing wave of current forms on the antenna (open at both ends ⇒ voltage maximum, current node; analogous to a half-wave resonator). Only the **non-uniform parts** of the current distribution contribute to radiation — uniform currents (like in an infinite transmission line) do not radiate.

| Parameter | Effect on Radiation |
|---|---|
| Antenna length $L$ | Determines resonant frequency; max radiation when $L = \lambda/2$ |
| Current distribution $I(z)$ | Sinusoidal on thin wire antennas; controls far-field pattern |
| Wire radius $a$ | Affects bandwidth and end effects (negligible for $a \ll \lambda$) |
| Frequency $\omega$ | Higher $\omega$ → more rapid charge acceleration → stronger radiation |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wireless Communication:** Every mobile phone, Wi-Fi router, and satellite uses this exact mechanism to send signals.
- **Radar Systems:** Pulsed RF accelerates charges in the antenna; the radiated pulse reflects off targets.
- **Radio Astronomy:** Sensitive receivers detect radiation from accelerating charges in distant celestial objects.
- **Medical:** MRI and RF ablation devices rely on controlled EM radiation.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they account for ~70% of numerical questions on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wave number | $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \dfrac{\omega}{c}$ | rad/m |
| 2 | Intrinsic impedance of free space | $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0}} = 120\pi \approx 377\,\Omega$ | Ω |
| 3 | Wave propagation velocity | $v = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}$ | m/s |
| 4 | Radiated E-field (Hertzian dipole) | $E_\theta = j\,\dfrac{\eta_0\,I_0\,dl\,\sin\theta}{2\lambda r}\,e^{-jkr}$ | V/m |
| 5 | Radiated H-field (Hertzian dipole) | $H_\phi = \dfrac{E_\theta}{\eta_0}$ | A/m |
| 6 | Time-average Poynting vector | $\vec{W}_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{Re}\{\vec{E}\times\vec{H}^*\}$ | W/m² |
| 7 | Radiated power (Hertzian) | $P_{rad} = I_0^2 \cdot R_{rad} \big/ 2$ | W |
| 8 | Radiation resistance (Hertzian) | $R_{rad} = 80\pi^2\left(\dfrac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | Ω |
| 9 | Effective aperture (relation) | $A_e = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{4\pi}\,D$ | m² |
| 10 | Directivity of Hertzian dipole | $D_0 = 1.5 \,\, (1.76\,\text{dBi})$ | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — From Maxwell's Equations to the Wave Equation

We start with the **source-free** Maxwell's curl equations in phasor form (assuming $e^{j\omega t}$ time convention):

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega\mu\vec{H}$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = j\omega\varepsilon\vec{E}$$

Take the curl of the first equation:

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = \nabla \times (-j\omega\mu\vec{H})$$

Using the vector identity $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) - \nabla^2 \vec{E}$:

$$\nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) - \nabla^2\vec{E} = -j\omega\mu(\nabla \times \vec{H})$$

In a source-free region, $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$, so the first term vanishes:

$$-\nabla^2\vec{E} = -j\omega\mu(j\omega\varepsilon\vec{E}) = \omega^2\mu\varepsilon\vec{E}$$

Rearranging:

$$\nabla^2\vec{E} + \omega^2\mu\varepsilon\vec{E} = 0$$

Recognising that $\omega^2\mu\varepsilon = k^2$:

$$\boxed{\nabla^2\vec{E} + k^2\vec{E} = 0}$$

This is the **Helmholtz wave equation**. Its spherical-coordinate solution (for a $\theta$-polarized wave radiating from a current element at the origin) takes the form of an outward-traveling spherical wave:

$$\vec{E}(r,\theta) = \vec{E_0}(\theta)\,\dfrac{e^{-jkr}}{r}$$

The factor $\dfrac{e^{-jkr}}{r}$ confirms that energy **decays as $\dfrac{1}{r}$** in amplitude (and as $\dfrac{1}{r^2}$ in power density $\vert \vec{S} \vert$), which is the hallmark of radiated (as opposed to reactive) fields.

### 3.2 Derivation — Field of an Infinitesimal Current Element (Hertzian Dipole)

Consider an infinitesimally short current element of length $dl \ll \lambda$ carrying a uniform phasor current $I_0$, located at the origin and oriented along the $z$-axis.

**Step A — Vector Potential:** The magnetic vector potential at a far-field point $(r,\theta)$ is

$$\vec{A} = \hat{z}\,\dfrac{\mu_0 I_0\,dl}{4\pi r}\,e^{-jkr}$$

**Step B — Magnetic Field:** Using $\vec{H} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_0}\nabla \times \vec{A}$, in spherical coordinates only the $\phi$-component survives at far range:

$$H_\phi = j\,\dfrac{k\,I_0\,dl\,\sin\theta}{4\pi r}\,e^{-jkr}$$

**Step C — Electric Field:** Using $\vec{E} = \dfrac{1}{j\omega\varepsilon_0}\nabla \times \vec{H}$ and simplifying for $kr \gg 1$:

$$E_\theta = j\,\dfrac{\eta_0\,k\,I_0\,dl\,\sin\theta}{4\pi r}\,e^{-jkr}$$

Substituting $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$:

$$\boxed{E_\theta = j\,\dfrac{\eta_0\,I_0\,dl\,\sin\theta}{2\lambda r}\,e^{-jkr}}$$

$$\boxed{H_\phi = j\,\dfrac{I_0\,dl\,\sin\theta}{2\lambda r}\,e^{-jkr}}$$

**Step D — Time-Average Power Density:** The time-average Poynting vector is

$$\vec{W}_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{Re}\{\vec{E}\times\vec{H}^*\}$$

Since $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ are in phase in the far field:

$$W_{avg} = \dfrac{\vert E_\theta \vert^2}{2\eta_0} = \dfrac{\eta_0}{2}\left(\dfrac{I_0\,dl\,\sin\theta}{2\lambda r}\right)^2$$

$$\boxed{W_{avg} = \dfrac{\eta_0\,I_0^2\,dl^2\,\sin^2\theta}{8\lambda^2\,r^2}}$$

**Step E — Total Radiated Power:** Integrate over a sphere of radius $r$:

$$P_{rad} = \oint W_{avg}\,dS = \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi} W_{avg}\,r^2\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi$$

$$P_{rad} = \dfrac{\eta_0\,I_0^2\,dl^2}{8\lambda^2}\,(2\pi)\int_0^{\pi}\sin^3\theta\,d\theta$$

The angular integral evaluates to $\dfrac{4}{3}$:

$$P_{rad} = \dfrac{\eta_0\,I_0^2\,dl^2\,(2\pi)(4/3)}{8\lambda^2} = \dfrac{\eta_0\,\pi\,I_0^2\,dl^2}{3\lambda^2}$$

Substituting $\eta_0 = 120\pi$:

$$P_{rad} = \dfrac{120\pi \cdot \pi \cdot I_0^2\,dl^2}{3\lambda^2} = 40\pi^2\,I_0^2\left(\dfrac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2$$

**Step F — Radiation Resistance:** Equating to $P_{rad} = \dfrac{1}{2}I_0^2 R_{rad}$:

$$\boxed{R_{rad} = 80\pi^2\left(\dfrac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2 \approx 789\left(\dfrac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2 \;\; \Omega}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Field Region | Distance Criterion | Dominant Terms | $1/r$ Dependence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reactive near-field** | $r \ll 0.62\sqrt{D^3/\lambda}$ | Stored energy $\propto 1/r^3$ | Strong |
| **Radiating near-field** | $0.62\sqrt{D^3/\lambda} < r < 2D^2/\lambda$ | Angular distribution forms | Moderate |
| **Far-field (Fraunhofer)** | $r \gg 2D^2/\lambda$ | $\vec{E} \propto 1/r$, $\vec{H} \propto 1/r$, transverse | Pure radiation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Radiation Mechanism — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following diagram maps the *cause-and-effect chain* of antenna radiation, since a literal physical drawing of accelerating charges and propagating fields cannot be rendered in Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transmitter RF Source] --> B[Time-Varying Current i(t) on Conductor]
    B --> C[Accelerating Electrons dV/dt is non-zero]
    C --> D[Time-Varying Electric Field E]
    D --> E[Faraday: Changing E produces Changing H]
    E --> F[Ampere-Maxwell: Changing H regenerates E]
    F --> G{Self-Sustaining EM Wave}
    G --> H[Wave Propagates at c equals 1 over sqrt mu epsilon]
    G --> I[Energy Decays as 1 over r squared]
    H --> J[Far-Field Region r greater than 2D squared over lambda]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Receiver Antenna Recaptures Energy]
    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Field Structure Around an Infinitesimal Dipole

The diagram below visualizes the **E-field (solid) and H-field (dashed) lines** around a vertical Hertzian dipole. Note that the fields close to the antenna are reactive (out of phase), while fields at large distance are purely radiative (in phase, transverse to the radial direction).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Near Zone r much less than lambda - Reactive"]
        D1[Hertzian Dipole along z-axis]
        E1[E-field lines: Closed loops anchored on dipole]
        H1[H-field: Concentric circles in phi direction]
        D1 --- E1
        D1 --- H1
    end
    subgraph S2["Far Zone r greater than 2D squared over lambda - Radiative"]
        W1[Spherical wavefront e to the power of minus jkr]
        E2[E_theta: Tangential to wavefront]
        H2[H_phi: Perpendicular to E_theta]
        W1 --- E2
        W1 --- H2
        E2 --- R1[Vector k in radial direction - propagation]
    end
    S1 --> S2
    style S1 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333
    style S2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Stages of Wave Detachment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[t equals 0 - No acceleration, only static field] --> T1[t equals T by 4 - Field begins to oscillate]
    T1 --> T2[t equals T by 2 - Previous field loop detaches]
    T2 --> T3[t equals 3T by 4 - New loop forming on source]
    T3 --> T4[t equals T - Steady outward propagation established]
    style T0 fill:#FFB6C1
    style T4 fill:#98FB98
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Hint:** Picture ripples expanding on a pond when you tap the water surface once. Each tap corresponds to one half-cycle of oscillation. A continuously oscillating antenna is like tapping the water many times per second, producing a continuous train of concentric expanding ripples (waves).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Why does a stationary charge not radiate, while an accelerating charge does?*

**Model Answer:**
A stationary charge produces only a static Coulomb (electrostatic) electric field, which remains attached to the charge and does not propagate. A charge moving with **constant velocity** (no acceleration) similarly produces only a static magnetic field in its own reference frame, with no energy detachment. However, when a charge **accelerates** ($d\vec{v}/dt \neq 0$), it produces a **time-varying electric field**, which by Faraday's law generates a **time-varying magnetic field**, and by Ampère-Maxwell law this regenerates the electric field. The mutual regeneration causes the fields to **detach** from the source and propagate outward as an electromagnetic wave, carrying energy away. This continuous loss of field energy from the source is what we call **radiation**. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the role of displacement current in the radiation mechanism of an antenna.*

**Model Answer:**
Between the open ends of an antenna (e.g., a dipole), there is no conduction current, yet the magnetic field loops must remain continuous. Maxwell introduced the **displacement current** term $\vec{J_d} = \dfrac{\partial\vec{D}}{\partial t} = \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{\partial\vec{E}}{\partial t}$ in Ampère's law to account for this. In the antenna radiation mechanism, the time-varying electric field across the gap acts as a **displacement current source**, allowing the magnetic field to be sustained across the open circuit. This continuous circulation of $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ via the displacement current is the mathematical reason an open-ended antenna (with no closed conduction path) can still radiate. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Writing $E_\theta = j\dfrac{\eta_0 I_0 dl \sin\theta}{2\lambda r}$ without the $e^{-jkr}$ phase term. KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** for missing retardation.
> - **Common mistake #2:** Computing radiation resistance in dB or forgetting to multiply by 2 (peak vs RMS) — recheck $P_{rad} = \tfrac{1}{2}I_0^2 R_{rad}$.
> - **Common mistake #3:** Confusing $D$ (directivity, dimensionless) with $A_e$ (effective aperture, m²). The relation $A_e = \dfrac{\lambda^2 D}{4\pi}$ is frequently tested.
> - **Common mistake #4:** Using $\eta_0 = 377$ in some places and $120\pi$ in others — be consistent.

---

#### Question A — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the far-zone electric field component $E_\theta$ radiated by an infinitesimal current element (Hertzian dipole) of length $dl$ carrying uniform current $I_0$ at free-space distance $r$. State clearly all assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A short antenna of length $dl = 0.05\lambda$ carries a uniform RMS current of $I_{rms} = 2$ A at a frequency of 100 MHz. Compute:
  (i) Radiation resistance $R_{rad}$
  (ii) Total radiated power $P_{rad}$
  (iii) Maximum directivity of the Hertzian dipole in dBi **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $E_\theta$:**

Given: Hertzian dipole of length $dl \ll \lambda$ carrying uniform current $I_0$ along $z$-axis.

**Assumptions:**
1. $dl \ll \lambda$ (so current is uniform along the element).
2. Far-field region: $r \gg 2\lambda$ (so $\tfrac{1}{r}$ terms dominate over $\tfrac{1}{r^2}, \tfrac{1}{r^3}$).
3. Free-space propagation: $\sigma = 0$, $\mu = \mu_0$, $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0$.
4. Harmonic time variation $e^{j\omega t}$.

**Step 1 — Vector potential:**

$$A_z = \frac{\mu_0\,I_0\,dl}{4\pi r}\,e^{-jkr}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $H_\phi$:**

$$H_\phi = \frac{1}{\mu_0 r}\left[\frac{\partial(r A_\theta)}{\partial r} - \frac{\partial A_r}{\partial \theta}\right]\bigg|_{far}$$

After far-field simplification:

$$H_\phi = j\,\frac{k\,I_0\,dl\,\sin\theta}{4\pi r}\,e^{-jkr}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $E_\theta$ from $H_\phi$:**

$$E_\theta = \eta_0 H_\phi = j\,\frac{\eta_0\,k\,I_0\,dl\,\sin\theta}{4\pi r}\,e^{-jkr}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $k = \tfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$:**

$$\boxed{E_\theta = j\,\frac{\eta_0\,I_0\,dl\,\sin\theta}{2\lambda r}\,e^{-jkr}}$$

**(7 marks distributed: 1 for assumptions, 2 for vector potential, 2 for $H_\phi$, 2 for $E_\theta$)**

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

Given: $dl = 0.05\lambda$, $I_{rms} = 2$ A, $f = 100$ MHz.

**(i) Radiation resistance:**

$$R_{rad} = 80\pi^2\left(\frac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2 = 80\pi^2(0.05)^2$$

$$R_{rad} = 80 \times 9.8696 \times 0.0025 = 1.974 \;\; \Omega \;\; \approx 1.97 \;\; \Omega$$

**(ii) Total radiated power:**

Since $I_{rms} = \tfrac{I_0}{\sqrt{2}}$, we have $I_0 = 2\sqrt{2}$ A:

$$P_{rad} = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R_{rad} = (2)^2 \times 1.974 = 7.896 \;\; W \;\; \approx 7.9 \;\; W$$

**(iii) Maximum directivity:**

For a Hertzian dipole, the maximum directivity (occurs at $\theta = 90°$):

$$D_0 = 1.5 \;\; \text{(linear)} = 10\log_{10}(1.5) = 1.76 \;\; \text{dBi}$$

**Final Answers:** $R_{rad} \approx 1.97\,\Omega$, $P_{rad} \approx 7.9\,$W, $D_0 = 1.76\,$dBi.

---

#### Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the radiation mechanism of an antenna using the concept of **accelerating charges** and **displacement current**. Show how Maxwell's equations lead to a self-sustaining electromagnetic wave. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to an antenna's field regions, distinguish clearly between the **reactive near-field**, **radiating near-field**, and **far-field (Fraunhofer) region**. For an antenna of largest dimension $D = 1.5$ m operating at $f = 300$ MHz, determine the boundaries of these regions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Radiation Mechanism — Accelerating Charges & Displacement Current:**

**Step 1:** A transmitter drives a sinusoidal current $i(t) = I_0 \cos(\omega t)$ through the antenna conductor. The conduction electrons therefore oscillate at angular frequency $\omega$ — their velocity is continuously changing, which means they are **accelerating** ($a = dv/dt \neq 0$).

**Step 2:** An accelerating charge, unlike a stationary charge, cannot maintain a static Coulomb field. The field lines get "kinked" and an outward-propagating transverse component is generated. This produces a **time-varying electric field** $\vec{E}(r,t)$ in the space around the antenna.

**Step 3:** In the **gap** between the two arms of a dipole (or any open-circuited antenna), there is no conduction current. Yet a magnetic field must encircle the antenna. Maxwell's modification of Ampère's law adds the **displacement current** density $\vec{J_d} = \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$. This term sustains the magnetic field across the gap, ensuring continuity of $\vec{H}$.

**Step 4:** Combining Faraday's law and Ampère-Maxwell law:

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\mu_0\frac{\partial \vec{H}}{\partial t}, \quad \nabla \times \vec{H} = \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$$

These two equations together imply that a time-varying $\vec{E}$ creates $\vec{H}$, and a time-varying $\vec{H}$ creates $\vec{E}$. This **mutual regeneration** makes the wave **self-sustaining** once launched.

**Step 5:** Eliminating $\vec{H}$ yields $\nabla^2\vec{E} = \mu_0\varepsilon_0\dfrac{\partial^2\vec{E}}{\partial t^2}$, whose solution is a wave propagating at $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0} = 3\times 10^8$ m/s. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Field Region Boundaries:**

**Definitions:**
- **Reactive near-field:** $r < 0.62\sqrt{D^3/\lambda}$ — stored energy dominates; $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ are largely out of phase.
- **Radiating near-field (Fresnel):** $0.62\sqrt{D^3/\lambda} < r < 2D^2/\lambda$ — radiation pattern begins to form but is not yet fully developed.
- **Far-field (Fraunhofer):** $r > 2D^2/\lambda$ — angular pattern is fully formed; $\vec{E} \perp \vec{H} \perp \vec{k}$.

**Given:** $D = 1.5$ m, $f = 300$ MHz.

**Step 1 — Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3\times 10^8}{300\times 10^6} = 1 \;\; \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Reactive/Fresnel boundary:**

$$r_1 = 0.62\sqrt{\frac{D^3}{\lambda}} = 0.62\sqrt{\frac{(1.5)^3}{1}} = 0.62\sqrt{3.375} = 0.62 \times 1.837 = 1.139 \;\; \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Fresnel/Fraunhofer boundary:**

$$r_2 = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda} = \frac{2(1.5)^2}{1} = 4.5 \;\; \text{m}$$

**Boundaries:**
- Reactive near-field: $r < 1.14$ m
- Radiating near-field: $1.14\,\text{m} < r < 4.5\,\text{m}$
- Far-field: $r > 4.5$ m

**(7 marks: 2 for definitions, 5 for numerical boundaries)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Exam Day:**

- **The ONLY source of EM radiation is the accelerating charge.** Stationary charges and uniformly moving charges do NOT radiate.
- **Three pillars of the mechanism:** (1) Time-varying current → (2) Time-varying $\vec{E}$ & $\vec{H}$ → (3) Self-propagating wave at $c$.
- **Displacement current** $\varepsilon_0 \dfrac{\partial\vec{E}}{\partial t}$ allows magnetic fields to exist even in regions without conduction current (e.g., dipole gap).
- **Hertzian dipole** (infinitesimal current element, $dl \ll \lambda$) is the **building block** of all antenna theory. Master its $E_\theta$ and $H_\phi$ expressions.
- **Far-field conditions:** $r \gg 2D^2/\lambda$ AND $r \gg D$ AND $r \gg \lambda$.
- **Radiation resistance of Hertzian dipole:** $R_{rad} = 80\pi^2(dl/\lambda)^2 \approx 789(dl/\lambda)^2\,\Omega$.
- **Directivity of Hertzian dipole:** $D_0 = 1.5$ (linear) or **1.76 dBi** — independent of $dl$ or frequency (only depends on $\sin^2\theta$ pattern).
- **Intrinsic impedance of free space:** $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377\,\Omega$ — never forget this constant.
- **Power density decays as $1/r^2$** (because $\vec{E} \propto 1/r$ and $\vec{S} \propto \vec{E}\times\vec{H} \propto 1/r^2$).
- **Angular pattern:** Hertzian dipole radiates maximum in the direction **perpendicular** to the antenna ($\theta = 90°$) and zero along its axis ($\theta = 0°, 180°$). Pattern $\propto \sin^2\theta$.
- **Always remember the $e^{-jkr}$ retardation factor** — it accounts for the finite time the wave takes to travel distance $r$.
- **Boundary condition:** $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0}$ — frequently tested as a derivation step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Current Distribution on a Thin Wire Antenna
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Current Distribution on a Thin Wire Antenna

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚡ SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In antenna engineering, the **current distribution** on a thin wire antenna refers to the **spatial variation of the time-harmonic radio-frequency (RF) current** flowing along the geometric length of a slender, straight, cylindrical conducting wire that is used as a radiator or receiver of electromagnetic waves. For a wire of radius $a$ such that $a \ll \lambda$ (where $\lambda$ is the operating wavelength) and length $L$ typically of the order of $\lambda$, the axial current $I(z)$ is a function of position $z$ measured along the wire axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A *thin wire antenna* is one whose diameter is electrically small ($2a \le \lambda/100$) and whose length is comparable to the wavelength. Under these conditions, the transverse current components are negligible and only the axial current distribution $I(z)$ needs to be considered for radiation analysis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Singing Ruler" Picture

Imagine a thin metal ruler clamped at both ends on a table. When you pluck it, it vibrates, and a **standing wave pattern** forms along its length. The ends (where it is clamped) cannot move — they are **nodes of displacement**. The middle has maximum vibration — an **antinode**.

A thin wire antenna behaves almost identically in the *electrical* domain:

| Mechanical Ruler | Electrical Wire Antenna |
|---|---|
| Displacement of material | Current of electrons |
| Clamped ends (zero motion) | Open ends (zero current) |
| Standing wave of vibration | Standing wave of RF current |
| Fundamental mode | Half-wave resonance |

> [!NOTE]
> The analogy is so direct that the *mathematical form* of both is governed by the same second-order ordinary differential equation — the **Helmholtz / wave equation**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following physical constants and standard metrics recur in this topic and are highlighted for quick reference:

- **Speed of light in free space:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
- **Free-space intrinsic impedance:** $\eta_0 \approx 377 \; \Omega$
- **Wave number:** $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \omega\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}$ rad/m
- **Electrical length:** $\dfrac{L}{\lambda}$ (dimensionless)
- **Slenderness ratio:** $\dfrac{L}{2a}$ (must be $\gg 1$ for the thin-wire assumption)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Physics — Why Does the Current Vary?

A wire antenna is an *open-ended* transmission line. At the **physical ends** of the wire, charge cannot leave the conductor (no path), so by continuity of current:

$$I(\text{end}) = 0 \quad \text{(Neumann boundary condition)}$$

The antenna is therefore a **resonator with open electrical boundaries**, supporting a standing wave of current just as a pipe organ supports a standing wave of air pressure. The dominant mode for a wire of total length $L = \lambda/2$ has **one half-cycle of sinusoidal current** with a maximum at the centre (feed point) and zeros at the two ends.

## 2.2 Assumptions Underlying the Sinusoidal Approximation

The classical result $I(z) \propto \sin[k(L/2 - \vert z \vert)]$ depends on four idealisations:

1. **Perfect electric conductor (PEC):** internal field = 0, surface tangential $E$ = 0.
2. **Thin wire:** $a \ll \lambda$ and $a \ll L$ — radial current components are neglected.
3. **Sinusoidal steady state:** time dependence is $e^{j\omega t}$ (assumed and suppressed).
4. **Hallen / Pocklington equation solution** with assumed sinusoidal form, exact only at the dipole centre.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The sinusoidal distribution is **exact at the centre of a centre-fed dipole** and a very good engineering approximation elsewhere. The exact solution (King–Middleton second-order, Mei–Van Bladel) differs by only a few percent and is rarely required at the B.Tech level.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| $\#$ | Formula (use $\vert$ for modulus) | Meaning / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ | Wave number (phase constant in free space) | rad/m |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength in free space | m |
| 3 | $I(z) = I_0 \sin\!\big[k(L/2 - \vert z\vert)\big]$ | Current on centre-fed dipole, $-L/2 \le z \le L/2$ | A |
| 4 | $I(z) = I_0 \cos(kz)$ | Current on half-wave dipole ($L = \lambda/2$) | A |
| 5 | $I(z) = I_0 \sin\!\big[k(L - \vert z\vert)\big]$ | Current on full-wave dipole ($L = \lambda$) | A |
| 6 | $I(z) = I_0 \sin\!\big[k(L - z)\big]$ | Current on base-fed monopole over PEC ground, $0 \le z \le L$ | A |
| 7 | $I(\pm L/2) = 0$ | Boundary condition at open ends | A |
| 8 | $I_{\max} = I_0$ at feed | Maximum occurs at the driving point (centre) | A |
| 9 | $\langle S \rangle = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{Re}(E H^{\ast})$ | Time-average Poynting vector (basis of radiated power) | W/m² |
| 10 | $R_{\mathrm{rad}} = \dfrac{2 P_{\mathrm{rad}}}{\vert I_0\vert^2}$ | Radiation resistance referred to current maximum | $\Omega$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the current distribution is **not academic** — it is the cornerstone of every practical antenna calculation:

- **Radiation pattern** is obtained by integrating $I(z) e^{jkR}/R$ over the wire length (vector potential $A_z$).
- **Input impedance** depends directly on the current at the feed point $I(0)$.
- **Half-power beamwidth** and **directivity** of long-wire antennas (e.g., $L = 1.5\lambda$, $3\lambda$ rhombics) are dominated by the multi-lobed sinusoidal current.
- **Antenna tuning & matching networks** are designed knowing the *current node and antinode positions* — the feed is placed at a current maximum for low feed-point impedance.
- **Yagi–Uda array design** uses induced current distributions on *parasitic elements* derived from the same sinusoidal theory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Current on a Centre-Fed Dipole of Total Length $L$

### Step 1: Start from the 1-D Wave Equation in Phasor Form

The axial current $I(z)$ on a thin straight wire obeys the **one-dimensional Helmholtz equation** (derived from Maxwell's equations by integrating the Poynting vector over the wire cross-section and using the thin-wire approximation):

$$
\frac{d^{2}I(z)}{dz^{2}} + k^{2} I(z) = 0
$$

This is a linear, second-order, homogeneous ODE with constant coefficients.

### Step 2: Write the General Solution

The general solution to the above ODE is a linear combination of sines and cosines:

$$
I(z) = A \sin(kz) + B \cos(kz)
$$

where $A$ and $B$ are complex constants to be determined by the boundary conditions.

### Step 3: Apply the Symmetry Condition (Centre Feed)

For a **centre-fed** dipole, the feed point is at $z = 0$ and the geometry is symmetric about the feed. The current distribution must therefore be an *even* function of $z$:

$$
I(-z) = I(+z)
$$

Substituting into the general solution:

$$
A \sin(-kz) + B \cos(-kz) = A \sin(kz) + B \cos(kz)
$$

Since $\sin(-kz) = -\sin(kz)$ and $\cos(-kz) = \cos(kz)$, the equality forces the sine term to vanish:

$$
A = 0
$$

Hence the symmetry condition reduces the solution to:

$$
I(z) = B \cos(kz)
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Open-End Boundary Condition

At the two free ends of the wire ($z = +L/2$ and $z = -L/2$), the current must vanish because the charge has nowhere to flow:

$$
I(\pm L/2) = 0
$$

Substituting $z = L/2$:

$$
B \cos(kL/2) = 0
$$

For a non-trivial solution ($B \neq 0$), we require:

$$
\cos(kL/2) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad kL/2 = \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2}, \frac{5\pi}{2}, \ldots
$$

The **lowest resonant mode** (fundamental) corresponds to $kL/2 = \pi/2$, i.e., $L = \lambda/2$.

### Step 5: Re-parameterise Using a Shifted Origin

Instead of a $\cos$ about $z=0$, we re-express the answer in a form symmetric about the centre *and* vanishing at the ends by shifting the argument:

$$
I(z) = I_0 \sin\!\big[k(L/2 - \vert z \vert)\big], \quad -L/2 \le z \le L/2
$$

Verify: at $z = L/2$, the argument is $k(L/2 - L/2) = 0$, so $I = 0$ ✓. At $z = 0$, the argument is $kL/2$, so $I(0) = I_0 \sin(kL/2)$ which equals $I_0$ for $L = \lambda/2$.

### Step 6: Specialise to the Half-Wave Dipole

Setting $L = \lambda/2 \;\Rightarrow\; kL/2 = \pi/2$:

$$
I(z) = I_0 \cos(kz) = I_0 \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi z}{\lambda}\right), \quad -\lambda/4 \le z \le \lambda/4
$$

The current is **maximum $I_0$ at the centre** ($z=0$) and **zero at both ends**, exactly matching the mechanical ruler analogy.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Current on a Base-Fed Monopole of Length $L$ (Image Theory)

A monopole of length $L$ erected over a perfectly conducting (PEC) ground plane is equivalent (via image theory) to a dipole of total length $2L$. Since the monopole occupies $0 \le z \le L$ and the image occupies $-L \le z \le 0$, the current in the physical region is:

$$
I(z) = I_0 \sin\!\big[k(L - z)\big], \quad 0 \le z \le L
$$

**Verification of boundary conditions:**

- At the **base** ($z = 0$): $I(0) = I_0 \sin(kL)$ — the input current, *not* zero because the base is the feed.
- At the **top** ($z = L$): $I(L) = I_0 \sin(0) = 0$ ✓ — the open end has zero current.

For a quarter-wave monopole, $L = \lambda/4 \;\Rightarrow\; kL = \pi/2$:

$$
I(z) = I_0 \cos(kz), \quad 0 \le z \le \lambda/4
$$

The current is **maximum $I_0$ at the base** and **zero at the top**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Input Current $I_0$ in Terms of Feed Voltage $V_0$

For a half-wave dipole fed at the centre, the input impedance (at current maximum) is approximately $R_{\mathrm{in}} \approx 73\;\Omega$ (purely resistive). By Ohm's law at the feed terminals:

$$
I_0 = \frac{V_0}{R_{\mathrm{in}}} = \frac{V_0}{73}\;\text{A}
$$

The complete phasor current is therefore:

$$
I(z) = \frac{V_0}{R_{\mathrm{in}}}\,\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi z}{\lambda}\right), \quad -\lambda/4 \le z \le \lambda/4
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Why Sinusoidal? The Pocklington / Hallén Perspective

The exact current satisfies **Pocklington's integral equation**:

$$
\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I(z') \,\frac{e^{-jkR}}{4\pi R}\,dz' = \frac{j}{\eta_0}\!\left(C_1 \cos(kz) + C_2 \sin(kz)\right)
$$

where $R = \sqrt{(z - z')^2 + a^2}$ is the distance from source point $z'$ to field point $z$ on the wire surface, and $C_1, C_2$ are constants set by the excitation.

- At the **driving point** (delta-gap feed), the **kernel is highly peaked** and the integral is dominated by the *local* current, which is the **current at $z=0$**.
- The Hallén solution assumes the **trial function $I(z) = I_0 \sin[k(L/2 - \vert z\vert)]$** and determines $C_1$ by enforcing the integral equation.
- The error is small except near the feed (capacitive end-effect) and the wire ends (small but non-zero current exists physically because of finite radius).

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU B.Tech level, the **sinusoidal assumption** is accepted as a working model. Marks are awarded for the form, the boundary conditions, and its consequences for radiation — not for solving the full Pocklington equation.

---

## 3.5 Summary Matrix — Current Form for Canonical Antennas

| Antenna Type | Length $L$ | Current $I(z)$ | Max Current Location | Zero-Current Locations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Centre-fed dipole (full-wave) | $L = \lambda$ | $I_0 \sin\!\big[k(L/2 - \vert z\vert)\big]$ | Centre, $z = 0$ | $z = \pm L/2$ |
| Centre-fed half-wave dipole | $L = \lambda/2$ | $I_0 \cos(kz)$ | Centre, $z = 0$ | $z = \pm L/2$ |
| Centre-fed 1.5 $\lambda$ dipole | $L = 1.5\lambda$ | $I_0 \sin\!\big[k(L/2 - \vert z\vert)\big]$ | Two maxima at $z = \pm \lambda/4$ | $z = 0, \pm L/2$ |
| Base-fed quarter-wave monopole | $L = \lambda/4$ | $I_0 \cos(kz)$ | Base, $z = 0$ | $z = L$ (top) |
| Base-fed half-wave monopole | $L = \lambda/2$ | $I_0 \sin\!\big[k(L - z)\big]$ | Base, $z = 0$ | $z = L$ (top) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🖼️ SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Current Distribution Along a Centre-Fed Half-Wave Dipole

The following Mermaid block renders a **block-level functional architecture** describing how the sinusoidal current profile is conceptually generated, boundary-applied, and used downstream for radiation calculation. (Mermaid cannot faithfully draw a 2-D physical current curve, so a functional flow is the correct adaptation per protocol.)

```mermaid

    A[Maxwell Equations] --> B[Thin Wire Approximation a lt lambda]
    B --> C[1D Helmholtz Equation d2I dz2 + k2 I = 0]
    C --> D[General Solution A sinkz + B coskz]
    D --> E[Symmetry at Feed I -z = I z]
    E --> F[Solution I z = B coskz]
    F --> G[Boundary Condition I L/2 = 0]
    G --> H[Resonance kL = pi for L = lambda over 2]
    H --> I[Final Form I z = I0 cos 2 pi z over lambda]
    I --> J[Radiation Pattern via Vector Potential A mu]
    I --> K[Input Impedance Zin = 73 ohm]
    I --> L[Feed Current I0 from V0 Zin]
```


```mermaid
    M4[L = lambda 4 quarter wave monopole] --> N4[Quarter sinusoid I max at base I zero at top]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The ASCII art below (rendered as a code block) sketches the actual physical shape of $I(z)$ for a 1.5 $\lambda$ dipole. This is the *only* place a monospaced visual is used, because Mermaid cannot draw continuous curves reliably.

```

   |    |            |             |             |            |
   |   ___           |             |             |           ___

   (ends zero)       (zero)      (zero)        (zero)        (ends zero)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🎯 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**State and justify the boundary conditions on the axial current at the open ends of a thin wire antenna.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

At the open (free) ends of a thin wire antenna, the axial current must vanish:

$$
I(\pm L/2) = 0
$$

**Justification (2 marks):** Since charge cannot leave the conductor at a free end, the conductive current must equal the displacement current at the tip. In the thin-wire limit, the tip is vanishingly small, so the only consistent solution is **zero net axial current** at $z = \pm L/2$. This Neumann-type boundary condition forces the standing-wave pattern to have a node at each end, leading to the **sinusoidal** distribution $I(z) = I_0 \sin\!\big[k(L/2 - \vert z\vert)\big]$.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Write the expression for the current distribution on a centre-fed half-wave dipole. Indicate where the maximum occurs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a centre-fed half-wave dipole of length $L = \lambda/2$:

$$
I(z) = I_0 \cos(kz) = I_0 \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi z}{\lambda}\right), \quad -\lambda/4 \le z \le \lambda/4
$$

**Maximum:** $I_{\max} = I_0$ at the **feed point** $z = 0$. The current tapers to zero at both open ends $z = \pm \lambda/4$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module-level ESE questions carry **14 marks** split into two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with internal choice between two complete alternative questions (OR).

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]

**(a)** Starting from the one-dimensional wave equation, derive the current distribution on a centre-fed dipole of total length $L$. State clearly all boundary conditions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A half-wave dipole operating at $f = 300$ MHz is fed by a source of $V_0 = 73$ V (rms). Compute: **(i)** the wavelength, **(ii)** the current at the centre, **(iii)** the current at $z = \lambda/8$, **(iv)** the input impedance used. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Wave equation (1 mark):**
$$
\frac{d^{2}I(z)}{dz^{2}} + k^{2} I(z) = 0
$$

2. **General solution (1 mark):**
$$
I(z) = A \sin(kz) + B \cos(kz)
$$

3. **Symmetry condition $I(-z) = I(z)$ forces $A = 0$ (1 mark):**
$$
I(z) = B \cos(kz)
$$

4. **Boundary condition at open ends $I(\pm L/2) = 0$ (1 mark):**
$$
B \cos(kL/2) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; kL/2 = (2n+1)\pi/2
$$

5. **Fundamental mode $n = 0 \Rightarrow L = \lambda/2$ (1 mark).** Re-parameterise:
$$
I(z) = I_0 \sin\!\big[k(L/2 - \vert z\vert)\big], \quad -L/2 \le z \le L/2
$$

6. **For half-wave dipole, $kL/2 = \pi/2 \Rightarrow I(z) = I_0 \cos(kz)$ (1 mark).**

7. **Conclusion — physical interpretation (1 mark):** current is maximum at the feed, zero at ends, sinusoidal in between.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Wavelength (2 marks):**
$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{300 \times 10^{6}} = 1\;\text{m}
$$

**(ii) Current at centre $z = 0$ (2 marks):** With $R_{\mathrm{in}} = 73\;\Omega$ for a half-wave dipole:
$$
I_0 = \frac{V_0}{R_{\mathrm{in}}} = \frac{73}{73} = 1\;\text{A (rms)}
$$

**(iii) Current at $z = \lambda/8 = 0.125$ m (2 marks):**
$$
I(\lambda/8) = I_0 \cos(k \cdot \lambda/8) = 1 \cdot \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{8}\right) = \cos(\pi/4) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707\;\text{A}
$$

**(iv) Input impedance (1 mark):** $Z_{\mathrm{in}} \approx 73 + j\,0\;\Omega$ (resistive, resonant).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how the current distributes on a half-wave dipole. Compare it with the current distribution on a full-wave dipole. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A base-fed quarter-wave monopole over a perfect ground plane carries a feed current of $I_0 = 2$ A at $f = 100$ MHz. Compute: **(i)** wavelength, **(ii)** current at $z = \lambda/8$, **(iii)** current at the top of the monopole, **(iv)** the radiation resistance if the corresponding dipole is $73\;\Omega$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

- **Half-wave dipole (3.5 marks):** $L = \lambda/2$, $I(z) = I_0 \cos(kz)$, single lobe, **max at centre**, **zero at both ends**. (Sketch: a single cosine lobe.)

- **Full-wave dipole (3.5 marks):** $L = \lambda$, $I(z) = I_0 \sin\!\big[k(L/2 - \vert z\vert)\big]$. After expansion:
$$
I(z) = I_0 \sin(kL/2)\cos(kz) - I_0 \cos(kL/2)\sin(kz)
$$
For $L = \lambda$, $kL/2 = \pi$, so $\cos(kL/2) = -1$ and $\sin(kL/2) = 0$. Thus $I(z) = I_0 \sin(kz) = I_0 \sin(2\pi z/\lambda)$. This has **two equal maxima of magnitude $I_0$ at $z = \pm \lambda/4$** and a **zero at the feed $z = 0$**, with zeros at the ends. (Sketch: a full sine wave with a node at the feed.)

**Key contrast:** The half-wave dipole has its current maximum at the feed, while the full-wave dipole has a current **null** at the feed — leading to a very high input impedance ($\sim 200\;\Omega$ approximately) and a *figure-8 in-phase* radiation pattern with two main lobes.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Wavelength (2 marks):**
$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 3\;\text{m}
$$

**(ii) Current at $z = \lambda/8$ (2 marks):** For a quarter-wave monopole $I(z) = I_0 \cos(kz)$:
$$
I(\lambda/8) = 2 \cdot \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{8}\right) = 2 \cos(\pi/4) = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414\;\text{A}
$$

**(iii) Current at the top $z = \lambda/4$ (1 mark):**
$$
I(\lambda/4) = 2 \cos(\pi/2) = 0\;\text{A}
$$

**(iv) Radiation resistance of monopole (2 marks):** By image theory, a $\lambda/4$ monopole radiates only into the upper half-space, so its radiation resistance is **half** that of the corresponding $\lambda/2$ dipole:
$$
R_{\mathrm{rad,\,mono}} = \frac{R_{\mathrm{rad,\,dipole}}}{2} = \frac{73}{2} = 36.5\;\Omega
$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute value** $\vert z\vert$ — students often write $\sin(kz)$ instead of $\sin\!\big[k(L/2 - \vert z\vert)\big]$, which fails the symmetry test and loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $L$ and $L/2$** — a centre-fed dipole has *total length* $L$ but *half-length* $L/2$. Writing $I(z) = I_0 \sin\!\big[k(L - z)\big]$ is wrong for a centre-fed geometry.
> 3. **Wrong boundary conditions** — for a *base-fed monopole*, $I(0) \neq 0$ (it's the feed); only $I(L) = 0$. Many students write $I(0) = 0$ by reflex, costing 2 marks.
> 4. **Using peak vs. rms** — KTU typically uses rms unless stated. Mixing the two in numerical problems is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Skipping the symmetry argument** — the $A = 0$ step is *the* key logical step; omitting the justification loses the conceptual 2 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers — every numerical answer in KTU valuation must carry units (A, m, Hz, $\Omega$).

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Thin-wire assumption:** radius $a \ll \lambda$ and $a \ll L$; only axial current matters.
- **Governing ODE:** $\dfrac{d^2 I}{dz^2} + k^2 I = 0$, with general solution $A\sin(kz) + B\cos(kz)$.
- **Boundary condition:** $I(\pm L/2) = 0$ at the open ends (Neumann).
- **Centre-fed half-wave dipole:** $L = \lambda/2$, $I(z) = I_0 \cos(kz)$, max at feed, zero at both ends.
- **Base-fed quarter-wave monopole:** $L = \lambda/4$, $I(z) = I_0 \cos(kz)$ for $0 \le z \le L$, max at base, zero at top.
- **Full-wave dipole:** $L = \lambda$, $I(z) = I_0 \sin(kz)$, **zero at feed**, two maxima at $z = \pm \lambda/4$, zero at ends.
- **Image theory:** monopole radiation = dipole radiation / 2 in the upper half-space only.
- **Input impedance at current max:** $R_{\mathrm{in}} \approx 73\;\Omega$ for half-wave dipole; $36.5\;\Omega$ for quarter-wave monopole.
- **Feed current relation:** $I_0 = V_0 / R_{\mathrm{in}}$.
- **Why sinusoidal?** The current is a standing wave on an open-ended resonator, just like acoustic pressure in an open pipe.
- **Practical deviation:** Exact solutions (King–Middleton, Hallén, Pocklington) show a small shift of the maximum current node off-centre, capacitive end-effect at the tips, and slight amplitude asymmetry — all negligible at B.Tech level.
- **KTU high-yield keywords:** *thin-wire approximation, sinusoidal current distribution, open-end boundary condition, standing wave on a wire, image theory, half-wave dipole, quarter-wave monopole, current maximum at feed, current node at open end.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Friis Transmission Equation and Radar Range Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Friis Transmission Equation and Radar Range Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Friis Transmission Equation (Free-Space Link Budget)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> The Friis Transmission Equation, formulated by Harald T. Friis in 1946, is the foundational relation in radio-link engineering that quantifies the **power received** by one antenna from another, given a known transmitted power, transmit/receive antenna gains, carrier wavelength, and the separation distance between the antennas operating in free-space (line-of-sight, far-field region with no multipath, absorption, or ground reflection).

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$
\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t \, G_r \left( \frac{\lambda}{4 \pi R} \right)^{\!2}
$$

where:
- $P_t$ = Transmitted power (Watts)
- $P_r$ = Received power (Watts)
- $G_t$ = Gain of the transmitting antenna (linear, dimensionless)
- $G_r$ = Gain of the receiving antenna (linear, dimensionless)
- $\lambda$ = Operating wavelength (meters)
- $R$ = Distance separating the two antennas (meters)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this equation is a "Power Budget" tool**
> The Friis equation is essentially a power-balance sheet between a transmitter and a receiver. It tells the link engineer: *“Of all the power I push into my transmit antenna, after spreading loss and antenna directivity, how much actually arrives at the receive antenna terminals?”* It is the bedrock of **wireless communication system design**, satellite link planning, and radar cross-section estimation.

### Conceptual Analogy: "The Whistle in an Open Field"

Imagine two friends standing in a vast, foggy, open field with no echoes. Friend A blows an air-horn (the **transmit antenna**); Friend B holds a paper cone to their ear (the **receive antenna**). The Friis equation answers these intuitive questions:

1. **How loud is the horn?** → $P_t$ (transmit power)
2. **How focused is the sound beam?** → $G_t$ (a horn is more directional than an omnidirectional whistle)
3. **How much air is between them?** → $R$ (more distance = quieter sound — *inverse-square law*)
4. **What pitch is the whistle?** → $\lambda$ (higher pitch / shorter wavelength spreads more in fog)
5. **How good is the listener's ear-cone?** → $G_r$ (a big parabolic dish collects more)
6. **How much sound actually enters the cone?** → $P_r$ (the answer we need!)

The expression $\left( \frac{\lambda}{4 \pi R} \right)^{2}$ is the **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** factor, often denoted as $L_{fs}$ when inverted, capturing the natural geometric spreading of an electromagnetic wave in vacuum.

---

## 1.2 The Radar Range Equation (Monostatic Radar)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> The Radar Range Equation is the radar-system equivalent of the Friis equation. It computes the **echo power returned to a radar receiver** from a distant target, accounting for the transmit power, antenna gains, target's Radar Cross-Section (RCS), wavelength, and the *fourth-power* dependence on range caused by the round-trip nature of the echo.

The most general form (monostatic, common antenna for Tx/Rx) is:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 \, R^4}
$$

or in compact form using effective aperture $A_e$:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G \, A_e \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^2 \, R^4}
$$

where $\sigma$ = Radar Cross-Section of the target (m²).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Contrast with Friis Equation**
> The Radar equation has an **$R^4$** (fourth-power) denominator instead of $R^2$. This is because the signal travels **TWICE** — once from radar to target (Friis-style $1/R^2$ spreading) and again as the scattered echo back to the radar (another $1/R^2$). The factor $(4\pi)^3$ instead of $(4\pi)^2$ arises for the same two-way reason.

### Conceptual Analogy: "Shining a Flashlight at a Mirror in the Fog"

Hold a flashlight in fog. The light that reaches a distant mirror is reduced by **distance²** (Friis). The light *reflected back to you* suffers another **distance²** reduction on the return journey. So total light that returns to your eye falls off as **distance⁴**. The Radar equation is the EM-wave analogue of this flashlight-and-mirror experiment, except the "mirror" has a *size and material property* characterized by its **Radar Cross-Section (RCS, $\sigma$)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Constants Used in this Module**
> - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
> - Impedance of free space: $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377 \; \Omega$
> - Wavelength-frequency relation: $\lambda = c / f$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation Logic Behind the Friis Transmission Equation (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

The Friis equation is not a magic formula — it is the *concatenation* of three physical laws:

1. **Power Density at Distance R (Spreading Loss):** A radiator of gain $G_t$ produces a far-field power density:
   $$S = \frac{P_t G_t}{4 \pi R^2}$$
   *Why $4\pi R^2$?* Because the radiated power spreads uniformly over the surface of an imaginary sphere of radius $R$, whose area is $4\pi R^2$. The gain $G_t$ boosts the density in the direction of the receiver.

2. **Effective Aperture of the Receive Antenna:** A receiving antenna of gain $G_r$ intercepts power through its **effective aperture**:
   $$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4 \pi}$$
   *Why?* This is a fundamental antenna theorem (IEEE definition) — a larger effective area collects more energy. Higher-gain antennas have larger apertures, and shorter wavelengths give smaller apertures for the same gain.

3. **Multiplication of Density and Aperture:** The received power is the intercepted flux:
   $$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t}{4 \pi R^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4 \pi} = P_t G_t G_r \left( \frac{\lambda}{4 \pi R} \right)^2$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why does shorter wavelength give MORE received power?**
> This is counter-intuitive — short waves spread more (higher FSPL in dB). But a *fixed-gain* receiving antenna is physically *smaller* for short wavelengths, and conversely, for the same physical size, short-wavelength antennas have *higher gain*. So if you redesign your antenna for the new frequency, the gain increase can outweigh the spreading loss. This is why mm-wave 5G systems can deliver strong links at short range with small dishes.

---

## 2.2 Derivation Logic Behind the Radar Range Equation (Monostatic)

For a monostatic radar, the same antenna transmits and receives (with a duplexer). The logic chain is:

1. **Power density at the target:** $S_t = \dfrac{P_t G}{4 \pi R^2}$
2. **Power intercepted by the target:** $P_{\text{inc}} = S_t \cdot \sigma = \dfrac{P_t G \sigma}{4 \pi R^2}$
3. **Scattered power density back at the radar:** $S_r = \dfrac{P_{\text{inc}}}{4 \pi R^2} = \dfrac{P_t G \sigma}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4}$
4. **Echo power collected by radar antenna:** $P_r = S_r \cdot A_e = \dfrac{P_t G A_e \sigma}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4} = \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 R^4}$ (since $A_e = G\lambda^2/4\pi$ and $G_t = G_r = G$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The $R^4$ Law — why doubling range requires 16× power**
> To double the maximum detection range, the required transmit power increases by a factor of $2^4 = 16$ (or **12 dB**). This brutal scaling is the central engineering challenge of long-range radar design (e.g., over-the-horizon radar, deep-space tracking).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Power density from isotropic radiator | $S = \dfrac{P_t}{4 \pi R^2}$ | W/m² |
| 2 | Power density from directive radiator | $S = \dfrac{P_t G_t}{4 \pi R^2}$ | W/m² |
| 3 | Effective aperture of antenna | $A_e = \dfrac{G \lambda^2}{4 \pi}$ | m² |
| 4 | **Friis Transmission Equation** | $\dfrac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \left( \dfrac{\lambda}{4 \pi R} \right)^{\!2}$ | dimensionless ratio |
| 5 | Friis Equation (in dB form) | $P_r(dBm) = P_t + G_t + G_r + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4 \pi R}\right)$ | All dB/dBi/dBm |
| 6 | Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) | $L_{fs} = \left(\dfrac{4 \pi R}{\lambda}\right)^{\!2}$ | dimensionless; > 1 |
| 7 | FSPL in dB | $L_{fs}(dB) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(R_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | dB |
| 8 | **Radar Range Equation (monostatic)** | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 R^4}$ | W |
| 9 | Radar equation (bistatic, $G_t \neq G_r$) | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 R_1^2 R_2^2}$ | W |
| 10 | Maximum radar range | $R_{\max} = \left( \dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 S_{\min}} \right)^{\!1/4}$ | m |
| 11 | Relation: wavelength–frequency | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | m |
| 12 | Antenna gain (dBi) conversion | $G(dBi) = 10 \log_{10}(G_{\text{linear}})$ | dBi |
| 13 | Antenna beamwidth (approx) | $\theta \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \pi}{G}}$ | radians |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Engineering Pitfall (KTU Examiner's Note):** When $\sigma$ is given in **dBsm** (decibels relative to 1 m²), students often forget to convert back to linear m² before plugging into the radar equation. Always convert $\sigma_{dBsm} \to \sigma_{lin} = 10^{\sigma_{dBsm}/10}$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Friis / Radar Equation |
|-------|----------------------------------------|
| **Satellite communication (SATCOM)** | Downlink/uplink budget planning for GEO, LEO constellations (Starlink, OneWeb) |
| **Mobile networks (5G/6G)** | Cell-tower link budget, mm-wave small-cell coverage |
| **Radar systems** | Air-traffic control, weather (Doppler radar), military surveillance, automotive radar (77 GHz) |
| **RFID & NFC** | Tag-read range estimation |
| **Radio astronomy** | Sensitivity (system equivalent flux density) calculations for telescopes like the VLA, GMRT |
| **Wi-Fi / Bluetooth** | Indoor link margin and range prediction |
| **Deep-space probes** | Voyager, New Horizons — link budget from outer planets |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Friis Transmission Equation

**Starting Premise:** Two antennas, Tx and Rx, separated by a distance $R \geq 2D^2/\lambda$ (Fraunhofer / far-field region), operate in free space with no obstructions.

**Step 1 — Define the transmit power density at the receive antenna's location.**

If the transmit antenna were a lossless isotropic radiator, the power would spread uniformly over a sphere of radius $R$. The surface area of that sphere is $4\pi R^2$. Hence, the isotropic power density is:

$$
S_{\text{iso}} = \frac{P_t}{4 \pi R^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Account for directivity (gain) of the transmit antenna.**

The transmit antenna concentrates power in the receiver's direction by a factor equal to its gain $G_t$ (relative to an isotropic). Therefore, the actual power density at the receiver is:

$$
S = G_t \cdot S_{\text{iso}} = \frac{P_t G_t}{4 \pi R^2}
$$

This step makes physical sense: a high-gain transmit antenna (e.g., a parabolic dish) focuses energy like a flashlight beam.

**Step 3 — Define the effective aperture of the receive antenna.**

An antenna exposed to a plane wave of power density $S$ delivers to its load a power $P_r = S \cdot A_e$, where $A_e$ is the **effective aperture**. The relationship between effective aperture and gain, for any lossless antenna, is given by:

$$
A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4 \pi}
$$

*Derivation of the aperture-gain relation (advanced but KTU-relevant):*
Consider an isotropic antenna ($G = 1$). Its effective aperture is $A_{e,\text{iso}} = \lambda^2 / (4\pi)$. A directive antenna with gain $G$ intercepts $G$ times more power in its main beam, so $A_e = G \cdot \lambda^2/(4\pi)$.

**Step 4 — Multiply density by effective aperture to get received power.**

$$
P_r = S \cdot A_e
$$

Substituting the expressions from Step 2 and Step 3:

$$
P_r = \left( \frac{P_t G_t}{4 \pi R^2} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4 \pi} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify algebraically.**

Combine the two $4\pi$ factors into $(4\pi)^2 = 16\pi^2$, and re-group the $R^2$ with them:

$$
P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \cdot \frac{\lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 R^2}
$$

Factor the squared term:

$$
P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left( \frac{\lambda}{4\pi R} \right)^{\!2}
$$

**Step 6 — Express in decibel form for practical link-budget work.**

Taking $10 \log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$
P_r (dBm) = P_t (dBm) + G_t (dBi) + G_r (dBi) + 20 \log_{10}(\lambda) - 20 \log_{10}(4\pi R)
$$

Or equivalently, with the standard FSPL formula:

$$
P_r (dBm) = P_t (dBm) + G_t (dBi) + G_r (dBi) - L_{fs}(dB)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** If $R$ doubles, $L_{fs}$ increases by $20 \log_{10}(2) \approx 6.02$ dB. So received power drops by 6 dB per range-doubling — this is the well-known **"6 dB per octave"** rule in free-space radio links.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Radar Range Equation (Monostatic)

**Starting Premise:** A monostatic radar (single antenna for Tx and Rx) illuminates a target of radar cross-section $\sigma$ at range $R$.

**Step 1 — Power density incident on the target.**

The transmit antenna of gain $G$ (here $G_t = G_r = G$) produces a power density at the target:

$$
S_1 = \frac{P_t G}{4 \pi R^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Power intercepted by the target.**

The target intercepts an amount of power equal to its radar cross-section $\sigma$ (in m²):

$$
P_{\text{inc}} = S_1 \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{4 \pi R^2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **What is $\sigma$ physically?** RCS $\sigma$ is *not* the geometric area of the target. It is a *fictional* equivalent area that, if it scattered isotropically, would return the same power density to the radar as the actual target. A small stealth fighter can have a small $\sigma$ (~0.01 m²) while a large bomber can have huge $\sigma$ (~100 m²), or vice-versa with shaping.

**Step 3 — Scattered power density back at the radar.**

The intercepted power is re-radiated (scattered) isotropically by the target. The back-scatter power density at the radar location is reduced by another $4\pi R^2$:

$$
S_2 = \frac{P_{\text{inc}}}{4 \pi R^2} = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}
$$

This is the **first appearance of $R^4$** — the $R^2$ from forward path multiplied by the $R^2$ from return path.

**Step 4 — Echo power captured by radar antenna.**

The radar antenna's effective aperture $A_e$ collects:

$$
P_r = S_2 \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G \sigma \, A_e}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $A_e = G \lambda^2 / (4\pi)$ (monostatic assumption $G_t = G_r = G$).**

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4} \cdot \frac{G \lambda^2}{4 \pi}
$$

Combine the three $4\pi$ factors:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}
$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to find maximum range.**

Set the received power equal to the **minimum detectable signal** $S_{\min}$ (the receiver sensitivity threshold):

$$
S_{\min} = \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R_{\max}^4}
$$

Solving for $R_{\max}$:

$$
R_{\max}^4 = \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 S_{\min}}
$$

$$
R_{\max} = \left[ \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 S_{\min}} \right]^{\!1/4}
$$

This is the most-cited form of the radar equation in KTU exam questions.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick — Doubling the range:** From the $R_{\max}^4$ relation, doubling $R_{\max}$ requires $(2)^4 = 16\times$ more $P_t$, or $(2)^4 = 16\times$ more $\sigma$ (a 16× larger or more reflective target), or 16× lower receiver noise floor. Alternatively, you could double $G$ to get $\sqrt{2}$ × $G$ which… wait, gain enters as $G^2$ in the equation. So $\sqrt{16} = 4\times$ more $G$ will also double the range. The most leveraged knob is the **antenna gain**, because it's squared.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 5.8 GHz radar uses a 30 dBi dish antenna, transmits 1 kW peak power, and must detect a target with $\sigma = 1$ m². The receiver sensitivity is $-90$ dBm. Find the maximum detection range.

**Solution (linear-domain first, then conversion to dB):**

Step A: Compute the wavelength.

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{5.8 \times 10^9} = 0.0517 \text{ m}
$$

Step B: Convert gain to linear.

$$
G_{\text{lin}} = 10^{30/10} = 1000
$$

Step C: Convert sensitivity to linear Watts.

$$
S_{\min} = -90 \text{ dBm} = 10^{-90/10} \text{ mW} = 10^{-9} \text{ mW} = 10^{-12} \text{ W}
$$

Step D: Substitute into $R_{\max}$ formula.

$$
R_{\max} = \left[ \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 S_{\min}} \right]^{1/4}
$$

Numerator (inside bracket): $P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma = 1000 \times 1000^2 \times (0.0517)^2 \times 1$

Compute stepwise:
- $G^2 = 10^6$
- $\lambda^2 = 0.002674$ m²
- Numerator = $10^3 \times 10^6 \times 0.002674 = 2.674 \times 10^6$

Denominator: $(4\pi)^3 \times S_{\min} = (12.566)^3 \times 10^{-12} = 1984 \times 10^{-12} = 1.984 \times 10^{-9}$

Bracket value: $2.674 \times 10^6 \; / \; 1.984 \times 10^{-9} = 1.348 \times 10^{15}$

Step E: Take the fourth root.

$$
R_{\max} = (1.348 \times 10^{15})^{0.25}
$$

Take log: $\log_{10}(R_{\max}) = 0.25 \times 15.13 = 3.78$

$$
R_{\max} = 10^{3.78} \approx 6020 \text{ m} \approx 6.02 \text{ km}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $R_{\max} \approx 6.02$ km. This is a typical KTU Part B (14-mark) numerical — examiners often grade partial credit for correctly handling unit conversions (dB → linear), the FSPL substitution, and the fourth root.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Friis Link Budget

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TxSource["Tx Power Source Pt"] --> TxAmp["Tx Amplifier & Filter"]
    TxAmp --> TxAnt["Tx Antenna Gain Gt"]
    TxAnt -->|"Radiated Wave Lambda"| Channel["Free Space Channel 4 pi R squared"]
    Channel -->|"Path Loss FSPL"| RxAnt["Rx Antenna Gain Gr"]
    RxAnt --> RxLNA["Rx LNA and Downconverter"]
    RxLNA --> RxDetect["Rx Detector Pr"]
    RxDetect --> BudgetCalc["Link Budget Equation Pr = Pt Gt Gr Lambda by 4 pi R squared"]
    BudgetCalc --> Output["Link Margin dB Output"]

    style TxSource fill:#fff4cc
    style TxAnt fill:#ffe1a8
    style Channel fill:#cce5ff
    style RxAnt fill:#ffe1a8
    style BudgetCalc fill:#d4f4dd
    style Output fill:#ffd6d6
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follow the signal flow from the Tx source on the left, through the antenna, across the free-space channel (where the $1/(4\pi R^2)$ spreading loss happens), to the receive antenna and into the receiver chain. The final node consolidates the Friis equation.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Radar Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PulseGen["Pulse Generator Pt"] --> Duplexer{"Duplexer Switch"}
    Duplexer -->|"Transmit path"| TxAnt["Antenna Gain G"]
    TxAnt -->|"Beam to target"| Target["Target with RCS sigma"]
    Target -->|"Scattered echo 1 by R squared forward, 1 by R squared back"| Duplexer
    Duplexer -->|"Receive path"| RxChain["Receiver with effective aperture Ae"]
    RxChain --> Detector["Threshold Detector Smin"]
    Detector --> RangeEq["Radar Range Equation Pr = Pt G squared Lambda squared sigma over 4 pi cubed R to the 4"]
    RangeEq --> Rmax["Maximum Range Rmax Output"]

    style PulseGen fill:#fff4cc
    style Duplexer fill:#e8d4f4
    style Target fill:#ffd6d6
    style Detector fill:#cce5ff
    style RangeEq fill:#d4f4dd
    style Rmax fill:#ffe1a8
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Duplexer Block:** The duplexer is a critical monostatic-radar component that routes the high-power transmit pulse to the antenna and then switches to the sensitive receiver path to listen for the echo, all while protecting the receiver from burnout. In the diagram, the diamond-shaped "Duplexer" node represents this T/R switch.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Flowchart — Friis vs Radar Equation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Friis Equation: One-way communication link"] --> B["Power ratio falls as 1 by R squared"]
    C["Radar Equation: Two-way echo link"] --> D["Power falls as 1 by R to the 4"]
    B --> E["Fspl in dB = 20 log 4 pi R by lambda"]
    D --> F["Fspl in dB round trip = 40 log 4 pi R by lambda"]
    E --> G["Used in: SATCOM, Wi-Fi, 5G"]
    F --> H["Used in: ATC radar, weather radar, military"]
    G --> I["Receiver threshold: Sensitivity Smin"]
    H --> J["Receiver threshold: Smin plus SNR plus noise figure"]

    style A fill:#cce5ff
    style C fill:#ffd6d6
    style G fill:#d4f4dd
    style H fill:#d4f4dd
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Friis transmission equation and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer:**
The Friis transmission equation relates received power $P_r$ to transmitted power $P_t$ in a free-space communication link:

$$
\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t \, G_r \left( \frac{\lambda}{4 \pi R} \right)^{\!2}
$$

Significance of each term:
- $G_t$, $G_r$ — Directional gain of transmit/receive antennas; quantify how much the antenna focuses energy in the desired direction (linear scale).
- $\lambda$ — Operating wavelength; appears in numerator because a fixed-gain antenna's effective aperture scales as $\lambda^2$.
- $R$ — Separation between Tx and Rx; inverse-square dependence is the **free-space spreading loss**.
- $(4\pi)^{-2}$ — Geometric factor from spherical wavefront spreading in free space.

> [!NOTE]
> The equation is valid in the far-field (Fraunhofer) region, where $R \geq 2D^2/\lambda$, with $D$ being the largest dimension of either antenna.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why does the radar range equation have an $R^4$ dependence, while the Friis transmission equation has only an $R^2$ dependence?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Friis equation** models a *one-way* communication link. The signal travels directly from the transmit antenna to the receive antenna, so the geometric spreading loss scales as $1/(4\pi R^2)$, giving an $R^2$ dependence in the denominator.

The **radar equation** models a *two-way* monostatic process. The transmit signal first propagates to the target (loss $\propto 1/R^2$), gets scattered by the target, and the echo must propagate back to the radar (another loss $\propto 1/R^2$). The two losses multiply, giving $1/R^4$ in the denominator, and the geometric factor becomes $(4\pi)^3$ instead of $(4\pi)^2$.

> [!TIP]
> A useful way to remember: *Radar = Friis forward × Friis backward.* This is why long-range radars require very high antenna gain and high transmit power.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** Each Part B question is 14 marks, split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks, with internal choice within a question pair. Solutions should be diagram-supported and show full derivation steps.

### **Question B — Option (A)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the Friis transmission equation for a free-space communication link, clearly stating the assumptions used.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4 GHz microwave link uses two identical parabolic dish antennas of 30 dBi gain each. The transmitter delivers 10 W of power, and the receiver requires a minimum of –70 dBm to operate correctly. Find the maximum range over which the link will function.  **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

1. **Assumptions:**
   - Antennas are in the far-field / Fraunhofer region of each other.
   - Free-space propagation: no reflection, refraction, absorption, or multipath.
   - Antennas are polarization-matched and impedance-matched.
   - $G_t$, $G_r$ are linear (real, positive) gains.

2. **Power density at distance R from a transmit antenna of gain $G_t$:**
   $$S = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi R^2}$$
   (Power $P_t G_t$ spreads over spherical surface of area $4\pi R^2$.)

3. **Effective aperture of receive antenna:**
   $$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

4. **Received power = Density × Effective Aperture:**
   $$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi R^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$
   $$\boxed{P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left( \frac{\lambda}{4\pi R} \right)^2}$$

**(b) Numerical:**

Given: $f = 4$ GHz, $G_t = G_r = 30$ dBi = 1000 (linear), $P_t = 10$ W, $P_{r,\min} = -70$ dBm.

Wavelength: $\lambda = c/f = 3\times 10^8 / 4\times 10^9 = 0.075$ m

Sensitivity in Watts: $P_{r,\min} = 10^{-70/10}$ mW $= 10^{-7}$ mW $= 10^{-10}$ W

Rearranging the Friis equation for range:

$$
R = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \sqrt{\frac{P_t G_t G_r}{P_{r,\min}}}
$$

Compute the term inside the square root:

$$
\frac{P_t G_t G_r}{P_{r,\min}} = \frac{10 \times 1000 \times 1000}{10^{-10}} = \frac{10^7}{10^{-10}} = 10^{17}
$$

Square root: $\sqrt{10^{17}} = 10^{8.5} = 3.162 \times 10^8$

Compute wavelength factor: $\lambda/(4\pi) = 0.075 / 12.566 = 5.97 \times 10^{-3}$ m

Therefore:

$$
R_{\max} = 5.97 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.162 \times 10^{8} = 1.888 \times 10^{6} \text{ m} \approx \boxed{1888 \text{ km}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a line-of-sight range exceeding Earth's curvature, so the practical maximum would be limited by terrain/obstacles.

---

### **Question B — Option (B)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the monostatic radar range equation and obtain an expression for the maximum detectable range.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An X-band (10 GHz) air-surveillance radar has a peak transmit power of 1 MW, antenna gain of 40 dBi, target RCS of 2 m², and a minimum detectable signal of –100 dBm. Calculate the maximum range at which the radar can detect this target.  **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

A monostatic radar uses the *same* antenna for transmission and reception (gains are equal: $G_t = G_r = G$).

1. **Power density at the target** (one-way path):
   $$S_1 = \frac{P_t G}{4\pi R^2}$$

2. **Power intercepted and scattered by the target** (with RCS $\sigma$):
   $$P_{\text{inc}} = S_1 \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{4\pi R^2}$$

3. **Power density of echo at radar** (one-way return path):
   $$S_2 = \frac{P_{\text{inc}}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}$$

4. **Echo power captured by radar antenna** ($A_e = G\lambda^2/4\pi$):
   $$P_r = S_2 \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$$

5. **Maximum range** by setting $P_r = S_{\min}$ (receiver threshold):
   $$R_{\max} = \left[ \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 S_{\min}} \right]^{1/4}$$

**(b) Numerical:**

Given: $f = 10$ GHz, $P_t = 10^6$ W, $G = 40$ dBi = 10,000 (linear), $\sigma = 2$ m², $S_{\min} = -100$ dBm.

Wavelength: $\lambda = 3\times 10^8 / 10^{10} = 0.03$ m

Sensitivity: $S_{\min} = 10^{-100/10}$ mW $= 10^{-10}$ mW $= 10^{-13}$ W

Compute the term inside the fourth root:

$$
\frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 S_{\min}} = \frac{10^6 \times (10^4)^2 \times (0.03)^2 \times 2}{(12.566)^3 \times 10^{-13}}
$$

Numerator: $10^6 \times 10^8 \times 9 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 = 1.8 \times 10^{12}$

Denominator: $1984.4 \times 10^{-13} = 1.984 \times 10^{-10}$

Ratio: $1.8 \times 10^{12} / 1.984 \times 10^{-10} = 9.07 \times 10^{21}$

Fourth root: $(9.07 \times 10^{21})^{0.25}$

Take $\log_{10}$: $0.25 \times (0.958 + 21) = 0.25 \times 21.958 = 5.49$

$$
R_{\max} = 10^{5.49} \approx 3.10 \times 10^5 \text{ m} \approx \boxed{310 \text{ km}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> A realistic X-band radar at 1 MW with a 40 dBi antenna can indeed detect large targets (~2 m² RCS) at several hundred km — comparable to legacy air-surveillance systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert dB to linear:** $G$ (dBi) and $P_{r,\min}$ (dBm) MUST be converted to linear values before substitution. (–1 mark per occurrence.)
> 2. **Confusing $R^2$ and $R^4$:** Some students write Friis' $1/R^2$ in the radar problem. (–3 marks.)
> 3. **Wrong RCS units:** $\sigma$ must be in m² (linear), not dBsm. (–2 marks.)
> 4. **Forgetting to take the fourth root** in $R_{\max}$ for radar problems — students sometimes just take a square root by mistake. (–4 marks.)
> 5. **Wrong free-space constant:** $4\pi \approx 12.566$ is acceptable; some use $4\pi \approx 12.57$ — both fine, but do NOT confuse with $4\pi R^2$ formula.
> 6. **Unit of $P_{r,\min}$:** dBm → mW → W. Skipping the mW → W conversion is a frequent error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Friis Transmission Equation** (one-way link): $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda/4\pi R)^2$. Dependence on $R^2$.
- **Radar Range Equation** (two-way echo): $P_r = P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma / (4\pi)^3 R^4$. Dependence on $R^4$.
- **Free-Space Path Loss** in dB: $L_{fs}(dB) = 20\log_{10}(4\pi R/\lambda) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(R_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$.
- **Effective aperture** relation: $A_e = G \lambda^2 / 4\pi$ (must memorize).
- **Far-field condition:** $R \geq 2D^2 / \lambda$ — both equations require this.
- **Doubling the radar range** requires **16× more transmit power**, or **16× lower receiver noise**, or **4× antenna gain** (because $G^2$ is in the equation).
- **Wavelength–frequency** conversion: $\lambda = c/f$ with $c = 3\times 10^8$ m/s.
- **dB to linear** conversions are critical: $X_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(X_{lin})$ for power/gain, so $X_{lin} = 10^{X_{dB}/10}$.
- **RCS ($\sigma$)** is in m²; **dBsm** values must be converted to linear.
- **Radar equation derives from Friis applied twice** — once forward, once backward.
- **Practical limits** on Friis: ignores multipath, atmospheric absorption, rain fade, polarization mismatch, and impedance mismatch (each adds a loss term in real systems).
- **Cross-check the equation form for monostatic vs bistatic** — bistatic uses two different ranges $R_1$, $R_2$ and possibly $G_t \neq G_r$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Infinitesimal Dipole and Small Dipole
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Infinitesitesimal Dipole and Small Dipole

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Infinitesimal Dipole — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

An **Infinitesimal Dipole** (also called a **Hertzian Dipole** or **Elementary Electric Dipole**) is a hypothetical, idealized straight wire antenna whose physical length $l$ is infinitesimally small compared to the operating wavelength $\lambda$ of the excitation signal. The limiting mathematical condition is:

$$l \ll \lambda \quad \text{or in the strict limit,} \quad l \longrightarrow 0$$

Because the wire length is vanishingly small relative to $\lambda$, the **current distribution along its length is assumed to be uniform** (constant in both magnitude and phase). The standard convention adopted in KTU 2024 scheme is to orient the dipole along the **z-axis**, centered at the origin, with the current $I_0$ flowing uniformly through it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PEECT868 Module 1:** The infinitesimal dipole is the *fundamental building block* used to construct (by integration) the fields of *all* practical linear antennas — including the finite-length dipole, small loop, and antenna arrays. Mastering its field derivations is mandatory for Module 1 and forms the basis of Module 2 (Antenna Arrays).

### 1.2 The Small Dipole — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Small Dipole** is a physically realisable antenna whose total length is small but **not negligibly zero** compared to the wavelength. The standard engineering threshold used across the Balanis textbook (the KTU reference text) is:
