$$l \leq \frac{\lambda}{50}$$

For a small dipole, the current distribution is **approximately triangular** — it must be zero at the physical end-points of the wire (which it cannot be for a true Hertzian dipole). However, since $l$ is so small, the variation of current over the length is negligible, and the uniform-current assumption still yields extremely accurate first-order field predictions.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ping-Pong Paddle in Water" Intuition

Imagine holding a tiny paddle (an *infinitesimal* paddle) just below the surface of a still pond and pushing it back and forth very slowly. Every point along the paddle moves *together* in lock-step, creating a single, coherent, perfectly circular ripple that radiates outward uniformly in all directions in the plane perpendicular to the paddle's motion.

- The **paddle** = the infinitesimal dipole.
- The **uniform back-and-forth motion** = the constant current $I_0$.
- The **circular ripple** = the radiated electromagnetic wave in the far-field.

Now consider a slightly longer stick (the *small* dipole) being wiggled. The tip of the stick lags slightly behind the middle because the ends cannot move freely — this represents the *triangular* current distribution. The ripple pattern looks almost identical to the paddle, but with a slight asymmetry that becomes important only at very precise angles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study an antenna that doesn't exist physically?** The infinitesimal dipole is a *mathematical primitive*. Just as a point charge underpins electrostatics, the Hertzian dipole underpins antenna engineering — we use it to build up (integrate) the fields of real antennas by treating them as a continuous chain of infinitesimal dipoles.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Intrinsic impedance of free space | $\eta$ | **$120\pi \approx 377 \, \Omega$** |
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | **$3 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}$** |
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | $8.854 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F/m}$ |
| Permeability of free space | $\mu_0$ | $4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{H/m}$ |
| Wavelength–frequency relation | $\lambda$ | $c/f$ (metres) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Uniform Current?

The full-wave analysis of a centre-fed dipole of length $l$ using the integral equation method (Pocklington's equation) shows that the exact current distribution is sinusoidal:

$$I(z) = I_0 \sin\left[k\left(\frac{l}{2} - \vert z \vert\right)\right]$$

When $l \to 0$, we can use the small-angle approximation $\sin(\theta) \approx \theta$, which yields $I(z) \approx \text{constant} = I_0$. This justifies the uniform-current assumption for the Hertzian dipole and explains why a small dipole ($l \leq \lambda/50$) behaves with near-uniform current.

### 2.2 The Five Logical Steps of Hertzian Dipole Analysis

1. **Postulate the source**: A current element of length $dl$ carrying uniform phasor current $I_0 \, e^{j\omega t}$ placed at the origin along $\hat{a}_z$.
2. **Compute the vector potential**: Use the retarded potential $A_z = \dfrac{\mu_0 \, I_0 \, dl}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$ (since the source is on the z-axis).
3. **Take the curl** of $A$ to obtain the magnetic field intensity $H$ in spherical coordinates.
4. **Apply Maxwell's Faraday's law** in the far-field region to obtain the electric field $E$.
5. **Compute the radiated power** by integrating the Poynting vector over a sphere of radius $r$ in the far-field.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these equations appear in over 60% of KTU Module 1 problems.**

| # | Quantity | Formula (Infinitesimal / Small Dipole) | Unit / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Vector potential | $A_z = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_0 \, dl \, e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r}$ | Wb/m, far-field approx. |
| 2 | Magnetic field (H-phi) | $H_\phi = j \dfrac{k I_0 dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$ | A/m |
| 3 | Electric field (E-theta) | $E_\theta = j \eta \dfrac{k I_0 dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$ | V/m |
| 4 | Field ratio | $E_\theta / H_\phi = \eta = 120\pi$ | $\Omega$ (intrinsic impedance) |
| 5 | Power density (Poynting) | $W_{av} = \dfrac{\vert E_\theta \vert^2}{2\eta} = \dfrac{\eta}{2} \left(\dfrac{k I_0 dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r}\right)^2$ | W/m² |
| 6 | Radiated power | $P_{rad} = I_0^2 \, R_r$ where $R_r = 80\pi^2 \left(\dfrac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | Watts, $R_r$ in $\Omega$ |
| 7 | Radiation resistance | $R_r = 80\pi^2 \left(\dfrac{l}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | Directivity | $D_0 = 1.5$ (linear), i.e. **1.76 dB** | Dimensionless / dB |
| 9 | Half-power beamwidth | $\text{HPBW} = 90^\circ$ | degrees |
| 10 | Input impedance (real) | $R_{in} \approx R_r$ (since reactance $\approx 0$ for very short dipole) | $\Omega$ |
| 11 | Effective aperture | $A_e = \dfrac{3\lambda^2}{8\pi}$ | m² |
| 12 | Maximum effective length | $l_e = dl$ (for a current element) | m |
| 13 | Wave number | $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ | rad/m |
| 14 | Angular frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | rad/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Rule:** When $l$ is used in place of $dl$ (i.e., when the student uses the *finite small-dipole* approximation), the radiation resistance formula **changes** to $R_r = 20\pi^2 (l/\lambda)^2$, *not* $80\pi^2$. This is the **#1 source of confusion** in KTU board exams. Remember: $80\pi^2$ is for the *infinitesimal* current element; $20\pi^2$ is for the *practical small* dipole because the triangular current distribution lowers the RMS current to $I_0/2$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The infinitesimal and small dipoles are not just textbook curiosities — they model many real-world systems:

- **Mobile handset antennas** at low frequencies (sub-GHz): the electrical length of the antenna trace is often $\lambda/50$ or less.
- **RFID tag antennas** at UHF (860–960 MHz): operate as small dipoles.
- **EMC/EMI test probes**: small electric-field probes for compliance testing are essentially calibrated Hertzian dipoles.
- **In vivo biomedical implants** (e.g., pacemakers, neural recorders): miniaturised antennas inside the human body behave as electrically small dipoles.
- **Wideband receivers**: small dipoles have a real input impedance (no imaginary reactance to tune out), making them attractive for ultra-wideband (UWB) impulse systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Radiated Electric and Magnetic Fields of an Infinitesimal Dipole

We begin by placing an infinitesimal current element of length $dl$ along the z-axis at the origin, carrying a uniform time-harmonic current $I(t) = I_0 \cos(\omega t) = \text{Re}\{I_0 e^{j\omega t}\}$.

**Step 1 — Retarded Vector Potential**

For a current flowing in the z-direction, the magnetic vector potential has only a z-component. At a far-field observation point at distance $r$, the retarded form is:

$$A_z = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int I_0 e^{j(\omega t - kR)} \, \frac{dl}{R}$$

Since the source is infinitesimally small, $R \approx r$ for all points on the element. Therefore:

$$A_z = \frac{\mu_0 I_0 \, dl}{4\pi r} e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$$

For phasor (steady-state) analysis, we drop the time factor $e^{j\omega t}$ and work with the complex amplitude:

$$A_z = \frac{\mu_0 I_0 \, dl}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$$

**Step 2 — Converting $A_z$ to Spherical Components**

The unit vectors are related by $\hat{a}_z = \cos\theta \, \hat{a}_r - \sin\theta \, \hat{a}_\theta$. Thus:

$$A_r = A_z \cos\theta = \frac{\mu_0 I_0 \, dl \cos\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$$

$$A_\theta = -A_z \sin\theta = -\frac{\mu_0 I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$$

$$A_\phi = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute $H$ from the Curl of $A$**

Using $\vec{H} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_0} \nabla \times \vec{A}$, in spherical coordinates only the $\phi$-component survives due to azimuthal symmetry ($\partial/\partial\phi = 0$):

$$H_\phi = \frac{1}{\mu_0 r} \left[ \frac{\partial(r A_\theta)}{\partial r} - \frac{\partial A_r}{\partial \theta} \right]$$

Substitute the components:

$$H_\phi = \frac{1}{\mu_0 r} \left[ \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(-\frac{\mu_0 I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi} e^{-jkr}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\left(\frac{\mu_0 I_0 \, dl \cos\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}\right) \right]$$

Evaluate the first derivative with respect to $r$ (treating the $\frac{1}{r}$ inside the $e^{-jkr}$ as slowly varying in the far field, $kr \gg 1$):

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(-\frac{\mu_0 I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi} e^{-jkr}\right) = -\frac{\mu_0 I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi} (-jk) e^{-jkr} = j \frac{k \mu_0 I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi} e^{-jkr}$$

Evaluate the second derivative with respect to $\theta$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\left(\frac{\mu_0 I_0 \, dl \cos\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}\right) = -\frac{\mu_0 I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$$

The first term (in $1/r$) dominates the second term (in $1/r^2$) in the far field, so we drop the second. Combining:

$$H_\phi \approx \frac{1}{\mu_0 r} \cdot j \frac{k \mu_0 I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi} e^{-jkr} = j \frac{k I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$$

$$\boxed{H_\phi = j \frac{k I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $E_\theta$ from Faraday's Law**

Using the far-field relation $\vec{E} = \eta \, \vec{H} \times \hat{a}_r$ (or equivalently $E_\theta = \eta H_\phi$ for an outward-travelling TEM wave):

$$E_\theta = \eta H_\phi = j \eta \frac{k I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$$

$$\boxed{E_\theta = j \eta \frac{k I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}}$$

Both fields are **90° out of phase** with the source current (the factor $j = e^{j\pi/2}$), and the $1/r$ dependence confirms a **spherical travelling wave** — a fundamental property of all radiating antennas in the far-field region.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Total Radiated Power and Radiation Resistance

**Step 1 — Time-Average Poynting Vector**

$$\vec{W}_{av} = \frac{1}{2} \text{Re}\{\vec{E} \times \vec{H}^*\} = \hat{a}_r \frac{\vert E_\theta \vert^2}{2\eta}$$

Magnitude:

$$W_{av} = \frac{\eta}{2} \left(\frac{k I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r}\right)^2 = \frac{\eta \, k^2 I_0^2 (dl)^2 \sin^2\theta}{32 \pi^2 r^2}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate Over a Closed Sphere of Radius $r$**

$$P_{rad} = \oint_S \vec{W}_{av} \cdot d\vec{s} = \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^\pi W_{av} \, r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

Substitute and simplify:

$$P_{rad} = \frac{\eta \, k^2 I_0^2 (dl)^2}{32 \pi^2} \int_0^{2\pi} d\phi \int_0^\pi \sin^3\theta \, d\theta$$

Evaluate the angular integrals:

$$\int_0^{2\pi} d\phi = 2\pi, \quad \int_0^\pi \sin^3\theta \, d\theta = \frac{4}{3}$$

Therefore:

$$P_{rad} = \frac{\eta \, k^2 I_0^2 (dl)^2}{32 \pi^2} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{4}{3} = \frac{\eta \, k^2 I_0^2 (dl)^2}{12\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\eta = 120\pi$ and $k = 2\pi/\lambda$**

$$P_{rad} = \frac{(120\pi) \cdot (2\pi/\lambda)^2 \cdot I_0^2 (dl)^2}{12\pi} = \frac{120\pi \cdot 4\pi^2 \cdot I_0^2 (dl)^2}{12\pi \lambda^2}$$

$$P_{rad} = 40\pi^2 I_0^2 \left(\frac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2$$

**Step 4 — Express as $I_0^2 R_r$ and Solve for $R_r$**

Setting $P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2} I_0^2 R_r$ (the $\frac{1}{2}$ is from the time-average of $I_0 \cos\omega t$ into $R_r$):

$$\frac{1}{2} I_0^2 R_r = 40\pi^2 I_0^2 \left(\frac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2$$

$$\boxed{R_r = 80\pi^2 \left(\frac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2 \approx 789.6 \left(\frac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2 \, \Omega}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the KTU 2024 gold-standard formula for infinitesimal-dipole radiation resistance.** Memorise the numerical value $80\pi^2 \approx 789.6$. It appears in nearly every numerical problem.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Directivity Derivation

The directivity of an antenna is defined as:

$$D_0 = \frac{4\pi U_{\max}}{P_{rad}}$$

where $U(\theta,\phi) = r^2 W_{av}$ is the radiation intensity (W/solid angle).

For the infinitesimal dipole:

$$U(\theta) = r^2 W_{av} = \frac{\eta \, k^2 I_0^2 (dl)^2 \sin^2\theta}{32 \pi^2}$$

Maximum at $\theta = 90^\circ$: $U_{\max} = \dfrac{\eta \, k^2 I_0^2 (dl)^2}{32 \pi^2}$.

Total radiated power (as derived): $P_{rad} = \dfrac{\eta \, k^2 I_0^2 (dl)^2}{12\pi}$.

Therefore:

$$D_0 = \frac{4\pi \cdot \frac{\eta k^2 I_0^2 (dl)^2}{32 \pi^2}}{\frac{\eta k^2 I_0^2 (dl)^2}{12\pi}} = \frac{4\pi \cdot 12\pi}{32 \pi^2} = \frac{48\pi^2}{32\pi^2} = 1.5$$

$$\boxed{D_0 = 1.5 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad D_0(\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) \approx 1.76 \, \text{dB}}$$

---

### 3.4 Modification for a *Small* (Finite-Length, Triangular-Current) Dipole

For a practical small dipole of length $l$ ($l \le \lambda/50$) with a triangular current distribution peaking at $I_0$ at the centre and going to zero at the ends, the *effective* current in the radiation integral is the RMS average:

$$I_{\text{rms, eff}} = \frac{I_0}{2}$$

This factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ propagates through the radiation-resistance formula (since $R_r \propto I_{\text{rms}}^2$), giving a factor of $\frac{1}{4}$:

$$R_{r,\text{small}} = \frac{1}{4} \cdot 80\pi^2 \left(\frac{l}{\lambda}\right)^2 = 20\pi^2 \left(\frac{l}{\lambda}\right)^2 \approx 197.4 \left(\frac{l}{\lambda}\right)^2 \, \Omega$$

> [!WARNING]
> **EXAM PITFALL:** Many students mistakenly write $80\pi^2$ for the small dipole. The $20\pi^2$ form applies *only* to a finite-length dipole with a triangular (or sinusoidal) current distribution. For a *true* Hertzian current element $dl$, use $80\pi^2$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Far-Field Synthesis Architecture

The following diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of how the infinitesimal dipole's field equations are built up from the source current to the radiated far-field.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Current Io at origin] --> B[Retarded Vector Potential Az]
    B --> C[Convert to Spherical Coordinates Ar and Atheta]
    C --> D[Apply Curl Operator: H-phi]
    D --> E[Apply Far-Field Eta Relation: E-theta]
    E --> F[Compute Poynting Vector Wav]
    F --> G[Integrate over Sphere: Prad]
    G --> H[Derive Radiation Resistance Rr]
    F --> I[Derive Directivity D0 = 1.5]
    H --> J[KTU Final Results Box]
    I --> J
    J --> J1[Hr = 80 pi squared times dl by lambda squared]
    J --> J2[D0 = 1.5 linear or 1.76 dB]
    J --> J3[HPBW = 90 degrees]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Radiation Pattern Topology (Donut Shape)

The 3D radiation pattern of an infinitesimal dipole is a **toroid (donut)** with its symmetry axis aligned along the dipole (z-axis) and maximum radiation in the plane perpendicular to it (xy-plane, $\theta = 90^\circ$).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Dipole along Z axis] --> P2[Null at theta equals 0]
    P1 --> P3[Null at theta equals 180]
    P1 --> P4[Maximum at theta equals 90]
    P4 --> P5[Donut or Toroid Pattern in 3D]
    P4 --> P6[Figure of Eight in Elevation Plane]
    P4 --> P7[Omnidirectional Circle in Azimuth Plane]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Comparison: Infinitesimal vs Small vs Half-Wave Dipole

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C{Antenna Length Regime} --> C1[Infinitesimal: l much less than lambda]
    C --> C2[Small: l less than or equal to lambda by 50]
    C --> C3[Resonant Half-Wave: l equals lambda by 2]
    C1 --> R1[Current: Uniform Io everywhere]
    C1 --> R2[Rr = 80 pi squared times dl by lambda squared]
    C1 --> R3[D0 = 1.76 dB, HPBW = 90 deg]
    C2 --> S1[Current: Triangular, peak Io at centre]
    C2 --> S2[Rr = 20 pi squared times l by lambda squared]
    C2 --> S3[D0 approx 1.76 dB for l small]
    C3 --> H1[Current: Sinusoidal, max at centre, zero at ends]
    C3 --> H2[Rr approx 73 ohms]
    C3 --> H3[D0 approx 2.15 dB, HPBW approx 78 deg]
```

### 4.4 Coordinate System Reference (Schematic)

The standard spherical coordinate convention used in the KTU 2024 syllabus for the infinitesimal dipole:

```
                  z (dipole axis)
                  ↑
                  |   I₀ ↓ ↓ ↓
                  |---||---||---||---  ← current element dl
                  |       (origin O)
                  |
                  |        • r  (observation point)
                  |       /
                  |      / θ  (angle from +z axis)
                  |     /
                  |____•________________→  x
                 /  \
                /    \
               y      φ (azimuth)
```

- $\theta$ is measured from the **+z axis** (dipole axis).
- $\phi$ is the azimuthal angle in the **xy-plane**.
- Maximum radiation occurs in the **xy-plane** ($\theta = 90^\circ$), forming the donut pattern.
- Nulls occur along the **z-axis** ($\theta = 0^\circ$ and $\theta = 180^\circ$).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define an infinitesimal dipole. State the assumptions made in its analysis.

**Model Answer:**
An infinitesimal dipole (Hertzian dipole) is a hypothetical linear current element of length $dl \ll \lambda$ carrying a uniform time-harmonic current $I_0 e^{j\omega t}$. The assumptions are: (1) the length $dl$ is vanishingly small compared to the wavelength, (2) the current is uniform in magnitude and phase along the entire element, (3) the wire radius is negligible (filamentary), and (4) the antenna is isolated in free space with no nearby scatterers. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the directivity of an infinitesimal dipole? Express it in dB.

**Model Answer:**
The directivity of an infinitesimal dipole is $D_0 = 1.5$ (linear scale). In decibels:

$$D_0(\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) = 1.76 \, \text{dB}$$

This is the ratio of the maximum radiation intensity (at $\theta = 90^\circ$) to the average radiation intensity over all space. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The student is offered an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** of equal difficulty.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Field Equations & Radiation Resistance*

**(a) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** Starting from the retarded vector potential, derive the expressions for the far-zone electric and magnetic fields of an infinitesimal dipole. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The retarded magnetic vector potential for a current element $I_0 \, dl$ along the z-axis at the origin is:

$$A_z = \frac{\mu_0 I_0 \, dl}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$$

**Step 2:** Resolve into spherical components using $\hat{a}_z = \cos\theta \, \hat{a}_r - \sin\theta \, \hat{a}_\theta$:

$$A_r = \frac{\mu_0 I_0 \, dl \cos\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}, \quad A_\theta = -\frac{\mu_0 I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$$

**Step 3:** Use $H_\phi = \dfrac{1}{\mu_0 r}\left[\dfrac{\partial(rA_\theta)}{\partial r} - \dfrac{\partial A_r}{\partial \theta}\right]$. In the far field ($1/r$ terms dominate over $1/r^2$):

$$H_\phi = j \frac{k I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr} \quad \text{A/m}$$

**Step 4:** Use the far-field TEM relation $E_\theta = \eta H_\phi$:

$$E_\theta = j \eta \frac{k I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr} \quad \text{V/m}, \quad \eta = 120\pi \, \Omega$$

These fields are perpendicular to each other, transverse to the radial direction, and decay as $1/r$. *(7 marks — full credit requires explicit far-field approximation and substitution of $\eta$.)*

---

**(b) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** A 1-metre long dipole operates at 100 MHz. Treating it as a small dipole, calculate (i) the radiation resistance, (ii) the radiated power when the feed-point current is 2 A (RMS), and (iii) the maximum effective aperture. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $l = 1$ m, $f = 100$ MHz $\Rightarrow \lambda = c/f = 3 \times 10^8 / 10^8 = 3$ m. Since $l/\lambda = 1/3 = 0.333 > 1/50$, strictly this is *not* a small dipole. **However**, the question explicitly says "treat as a small dipole" — we will apply the small-dipole formula.

**Step (i) — Radiation resistance:**

$$R_r = 20\pi^2 \left(\frac{l}{\lambda}\right)^2 = 20\pi^2 \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{20\pi^2}{9} = \frac{197.39}{9} \approx 21.93 \, \Omega$$

**Step (ii) — Radiated power (using RMS current $I_{rms} = 2$ A):**

$$P_{rad} = I_{rms}^2 \, R_r = (2)^2 \times 21.93 = 4 \times 21.93 = 87.74 \, \text{W}$$

**Step (iii) — Maximum effective aperture** (using $A_{em} = \dfrac{\lambda^2 D_0}{4\pi}$ and $D_0 = 1.5$):

$$A_{em} = \frac{(3)^2 \times 1.5}{4\pi} = \frac{13.5}{12.566} \approx 1.074 \, \text{m}^2$$

$$\boxed{R_r \approx 21.93 \, \Omega, \quad P_{rad} \approx 87.74 \, \text{W}, \quad A_{em} \approx 1.074 \, \text{m}^2}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Power, Directivity & Pattern Analysis*

**(a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** Derive the expression for the total radiated power and the radiation resistance of an infinitesimal dipole. Show that the radiation pattern has a directivity of 1.5. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Time-average Poynting vector:**

$$W_{av} = \frac{|E_\theta|^2}{2\eta} = \frac{\eta}{2} \left(\frac{k I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Integrate over a sphere of radius $r$:**

$$P_{rad} = \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^\pi W_{av} \, r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi = \frac{\eta k^2 I_0^2 (dl)^2}{32\pi^2} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{4}{3} = \frac{\eta k^2 I_0^2 (dl)^2}{12\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\eta = 120\pi$ and $k = 2\pi/\lambda$:**

$$P_{rad} = 40\pi^2 I_0^2 \left(\frac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2$$

**Step 4 — Radiation resistance** (with $P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2} I_0^2 R_r$):

$$R_r = 80\pi^2 \left(\frac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2 \approx 789.6 \left(\frac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2 \, \Omega$$

**Step 5 — Directivity:**

$$D_0 = \frac{4\pi U_{\max}}{P_{rad}} = \frac{4\pi \cdot \frac{\eta k^2 I_0^2 (dl)^2}{32\pi^2}}{\frac{\eta k^2 I_0^2 (dl)^2}{12\pi}} = 1.5$$

*(7 marks — full credit for the explicit evaluation of $\int_0^\pi \sin^3\theta \, d\theta = 4/3$.)*

---

**(b) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** An infinitesimal dipole of length $\lambda/100$ carries an RMS current of 0.5 A. Compute: (i) radiation resistance, (ii) radiated power, (iii) gain (assuming 100% efficiency), (iv) the magnetic field intensity at a distance of 10 km along the broadside direction ($\theta = 90^\circ$). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $l = \lambda/100$, $I_{rms} = 0.5$ A, $r = 10{,}000$ m, $\theta = 90^\circ$.

**Step (i):**

$$R_r = 80\pi^2 \left(\frac{\lambda/100}{\lambda}\right)^2 = \frac{80\pi^2}{10000} = \frac{789.6}{10000} = 0.07896 \, \Omega$$

**Step (ii):**

$$P_{rad} = I_{rms}^2 \, R_r = (0.5)^2 \times 0.07896 = 0.25 \times 0.07896 = 0.01974 \, \text{W} = 19.74 \, \text{mW}$$

**Step (iii):** With $e_{cd} = 1$ (100% efficiency), $G = D_0 = 1.5 = 1.76$ dB.

**Step (iv):** $k = 2\pi/\lambda$, $\eta = 120\pi$:

$$|H_\phi| = \frac{k I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} = \frac{(2\pi/\lambda) \cdot (I_0 \sqrt{2}) \cdot (\lambda/100) \cdot 1}{4\pi \cdot 10000} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 0.5\sqrt{2} \cdot \lambda}{100 \cdot 4\pi \cdot 10000 \cdot \lambda}$$

(using $I_0 = I_{rms}\sqrt{2} = 0.5\sqrt{2}$):

$$|H_\phi| = \frac{0.5\sqrt{2}}{2 \times 10^6} = \frac{0.707}{2 \times 10^6} = 3.535 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{A/m}$$

$$\boxed{R_r = 0.079 \, \Omega, \quad P_{rad} = 19.74 \, \text{mW}, \quad G = 1.5, \quad |H_\phi| = 3.535 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{A/m}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Top Reasons Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sin\theta$ factor** in $E_\theta$ and $H_\phi$ — this gives the wrong radiation pattern and a directivity of 1.0 instead of 1.5.
> 2. **Mixing up $80\pi^2$ vs $20\pi^2$** for the radiation resistance — read the question carefully to see if it's the *infinitesimal* element or the *practical small* dipole.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2}$ in $P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2} I_0^2 R_r$** — the formula uses peak current $I_0$, not RMS. The $\frac{1}{2}$ comes from the time-average of $\cos^2\omega t$.
> 4. **Not explicitly stating the far-field assumption $kr \gg 1$** — this is required to justify dropping the $1/r^2$ terms in the curl operation.
> 5. **Wrong units in the final answer** — radiation resistance is in $\Omega$, power in W, field intensities in V/m and A/m. Always box the units.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1 — Infinitesimal & Small Dipole**

- ✅ An **infinitesimal dipole** has $l \ll \lambda$ and a **uniform current** $I_0$.
- ✅ A **small dipole** has $l \leq \lambda/50$ and an approximately **triangular** current distribution; radiation resistance uses $20\pi^2$.
- ✅ Vector potential: $A_z = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_0 \, dl}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$.
- ✅ Magnetic field: $H_\phi = j \dfrac{k I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$.
- ✅ Electric field: $E_\theta = j \eta \dfrac{k I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$, with $\eta = 120\pi \approx 377 \, \Omega$.
- ✅ Field ratio: $E_\theta / H_\phi = \eta$ — a fundamental far-field TEM property.
- ✅ Both fields decay as $1/r$ and are **in time-phase** with each other (the $j$ factors cancel between $E$ and $H$).
- ✅ **Poynting vector**: $W_{av} = \dfrac{|E_\theta|^2}{2\eta}$ — power flows radially outward only.
- ✅ **Total radiated power**: $P_{rad} = 40\pi^2 I_0^2 (l/\lambda)^2$ (or with $dl$ for the element).
- ✅ **Radiation resistance** (infinitesimal): $R_r = 80\pi^2 (l/\lambda)^2 \approx 789.6 (l/\lambda)^2 \, \Omega$.
- ✅ **Radiation resistance** (small dipole): $R_r = 20\pi^2 (l/\lambda)^2 \approx 197.4 (l/\lambda)^2 \, \Omega$.
- ✅ **Directivity** $D_0 = 1.5 = 1.76$ dB; **HPBW** = $90^\circ$; **null-to-null beamwidth** = $180^\circ$.
- ✅ 3D pattern is a **toroid (donut)** around the z-axis; 2D elevation cut is a **figure-of-eight** ($\sin\theta$).
- ✅ The input impedance is dominated by $R_r$ because the reactive part $\to 0$ as $l \to 0$.
- ✅ **Effective aperture**: $A_{em} = \dfrac{3\lambda^2}{8\pi} \approx 0.119 \lambda^2$.
- ✅ Far-field (Fraunhofer) distance criterion: $R \geq \dfrac{2l^2}{\lambda}$ (relevant for pattern measurements).
- ✅ Small dipoles are inherently **inefficient radiators** because $R_r$ is very small (typically $< 1 \, \Omega$) compared to the typical feed-line impedance ($50 \, \Omega$), requiring impedance-matching networks.
- ✅ Real-world applications: mobile handsets, RFID tags, biomedical implants, EMC probes, UWB sensors.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Small Circular Loop Antenna and Biconical Antenna
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Small Circular Loop Antenna and Biconical Antenna

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Small Circular Loop Antenna

A **Small Circular Loop Antenna** is a closed-loop conductor of radius $a$ (where $a \ll \lambda$, typically $a < 0.1\lambda$) carrying a uniform time-harmonic current $I_0$, formed into a circular geometry. It is the magnetic dual of the short dipole and radiates primarily through the magnetic dipole mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** "A small loop is one whose circumference $C = 2\pi a$ is less than approximately one-tenth of a wavelength ($\lambda/10$). Under this condition, the current along the loop is assumed **uniform in magnitude** but **uniform in phase**."

**Conceptual Analogy:**
> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a small ring of wire (like a hula-hoop scaled down to the size of a coin) connected to a high-frequency source. As alternating current flows around the ring, it behaves like a tiny **electromagnet** whose magnetic moment oscillates rapidly. This oscillating magnetic dipole is the *exact magnetic counterpart* of a short electric dipole. Just as a vibrating tuning fork creates sound waves in air, this oscillating magnetic moment "shakes" the surrounding free space, launching electromagnetic waves outward.

---

### 1.2 Biconical Antenna

A **Biconical Antenna** consists of two metallic cones of finite length $l$ and half-angle $\psi_0$, joined at their apexes (with a small feed gap) and oriented along a common axis. It is a **broadband frequency-independent** radiator because its geometry is defined purely by angles rather than absolute dimensions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** "The biconical antenna comprises two cones with a common axis and a common apex, excited across a small gap at the apex. For an *infinite* biconical structure, the radiated field is purely transverse (TEM) and the antenna behaves as a uniform transmission line of characteristic impedance $Z_c = 120 \ln \cot(\psi_0/2) \, \Omega$. Truncating the cones at length $l$ makes it a practical broadband radiator."

**Conceptual Analogy:**
> [!NOTE]
> Picture two empty ice-cream cones joined tip-to-tip, with a tiny gap at the joint where you apply a signal. The flared metallic surfaces act like a continuously tapered "transmission line" that gradually opens into free space. Unlike a thin dipole (which abruptly ends in free space, causing reflections and narrow bandwidth), the biconical structure **smoothly transitions** the wave from the feed region into space — much like a trumpet's bell smoothly transitions sound vibrations from the narrow mouthpiece into open air without echoes. This smooth impedance transition is the secret behind its **wideband behavior**.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Free-space intrinsic impedance | $\eta$ | **$120\pi \approx 377 \, \Omega$** |
| Velocity of light | $c$ | **$3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$** |
| Loop circumference limit (small loop) | $C$ | **$C < \lambda/10$** |
| Biconical characteristic impedance | $Z_c$ | $60 \ln \cot(\psi_0/2) \cdot 2 = 120 \ln \cot(\psi_0/2) \, \Omega$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Small Circular Loop — Theoretical Breakdown

**Assumptions:**
1. Loop radius $a \ll \lambda$ (so $a < 0.1\lambda$).
2. Wire radius $b \ll a$ (thin-wire approximation).
3. Current is **uniform** in magnitude and phase: $\vec{I} = I_0 \hat{\phi}$.

**Why a uniform current?**
When the loop perimeter $C = 2\pi a \ll \lambda$, the phase variation along the loop is negligible ($\beta a \ll 1$). The self-inductance of the small loop is huge relative to its radiation resistance, so the source current distribution is dictated by the standing-wave pattern — effectively uniform.

**Radiation Mechanism (Magnetic Dipole):**
- The current loop creates a magnetic vector potential $\vec{A}$.
- The far-field has only an $E_\phi$ component (TM to $\hat{r}$ in spherical coordinates).
- The pattern is identical in shape to a short dipole: $\sin\theta$ doughnut, but polarized along $\hat{\theta} \perp \hat{\phi}$.

**Real-world utility:** Used in AM broadcast receivers, RFID tags, direction-finding goniometers, field probes for EMC/EMI compliance testing, and small embedded antennas in IoT devices where size constraints forbid dipoles.

---

### 2.2 Biconical Antenna — Theoretical Breakdown

**Operational Concept:**
1. The cones act as a **tapered transmission line** that supports a pure **TEM spherical mode** propagating radially outward between the cone surfaces.
2. Because the geometry is purely angular (defined by $\psi_0$), the characteristic impedance is **independent of radius $r$** — this is the hallmark of frequency independence.
3. The cross-section between the cones at radius $r$ behaves like a parallel-plate transmission line of plate separation $2r \sin\psi_0$ and plate width $2\pi r \sin\psi_0$.
4. At the truncated end ($r = l$), an abrupt discontinuity occurs, launching a **spherical wave** into free space. The residual mismatch is small over a wide frequency range.

**Why it is broadband:** The standing-wave pattern on the cones has nodes whose positions scale with frequency, but the angular geometry ensures the current "sees" the same impedance at every radial cross-section — hence a wide SWR bandwidth.

**Real-world utility:** Wideband spectrum monitoring, EMC test antennas (typically $30\,\text{MHz} - 1\,\text{GHz}$), UWB (Ultra-Wideband) communication, tactical military radios, and reference antennas in anechoic chambers.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Loop radiation resistance | $R_r = 31200 \cdot \left(\dfrac{S}{\lambda^2}\right)^2 = 20\pi^2 \left(\dfrac{C}{\lambda}\right)^4$ | $\Omega$ | $a \ll \lambda$ |
| 2 | Loop directivity | $D_0 = 1.5 = 1.76\,\text{dBi}$ | dimensionless | Small loop, lossless |
| 3 | Loop far-field $E_\phi$ | $E_\phi = \dfrac{120\pi^2 I_0 S \sin\theta}{r \lambda^2}$ | V/m | Far-field |
| 4 | Loop far-field $H_\theta$ | $H_\theta = \dfrac{\pi I_0 S \sin\theta}{r \lambda^2}$ | A/m | Far-field |
| 5 | Loop H-plane pattern | $F(\theta) = \cos\theta$ (single turn approximation for receiving) | — | Used in some texts |
| 6 | Loop magnetic moment | $m = I_0 \cdot S = I_0 \pi a^2$ | A·m² | — |
| 7 | Biconical $Z_c$ | $Z_c = 120 \ln \cot(\psi_0/2)$ | $\Omega$ | TEM mode, infinite cones |
| 8 | Biconical $\eta_r$ (radiation efficiency) | $\eta_r = \dfrac{Z_c}{Z_c + R_{\text{loss}}}$ | — | Practical cones |
| 9 | Biconical 3-dB beamwidth | $\theta_{3\,\text{dB}} \approx 60^\circ$ for $\psi_0 = 30^\circ$ | degrees | Approximate |
| 10 | Loop receiving aperture | $A_{em} = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{4\pi} \cdot 1.5$ | m² | Maximum directivity form |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation key:** $S = \pi a^2$ = loop area, $C = 2\pi a$ = circumference, $\psi_0$ = biconical half-angle, $r$ = radial distance, $\theta$ = polar angle from cone axis.

---

### 2.4 Engineering Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Magnetic vs Electric Dipole Duality:** The small loop is the *exact* magnetic dual of the short electric dipole. Replace $E \leftrightarrow H$, $\eta \rightarrow 1/\eta$, and $I_0 l \rightarrow I_0 S$. This is why the small loop's pattern is identical in *shape* to the short dipole's, only the polarization is rotated by 90°.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Far-Field of a Small Circular Loop

We start from the magnetic vector potential due to a current loop:

$$
\vec{A} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \oint \dfrac{I_0 e^{-j\beta R}}{R} d\vec{l}
$$

For a circular loop of radius $a$ lying in the $xy$-plane, carrying uniform current $I_0 \hat{\phi}$:

**Step 1 — Express $R$ in terms of observation geometry:**

For a point $P(r, \theta, \phi)$ on the far-field sphere, the distance from a source point at azimuth $\phi'$ to $P$ is:

$$
R = \sqrt{r^2 + a^2 - 2ra\sin\theta\cos(\phi - \phi')}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply far-field approximation** $r \gg a$:

$$
R \approx r - a\sin\theta\cos(\phi - \phi')
$$

Therefore $1/R \approx 1/r$ and the phase term:

$$
e^{-j\beta R} \approx e^{-j\beta r} \cdot e^{+j\beta a \sin\theta \cos(\phi - \phi')}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the line integral** $d\vec{l} = a\, d\phi' \,\hat{\phi}$:

$$
\vec{A} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_0 a e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r} \int_{0}^{2\pi} e^{+j\beta a \sin\theta\cos(\phi-\phi')} \, \hat{\phi} \, d\phi'
$$

Using the Bessel function identity $\int_0^{2\pi} e^{j x\cos\alpha} d\alpha = 2\pi J_0(x)$, and projecting $\hat{\phi}$ onto $\hat{\phi}$-direction at $P$ gives the factor $\cos(\phi - \phi')$:

$$
\vec{A} = \hat{\phi} \cdot \dfrac{\mu_0 I_0 \pi a^2 \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-j\beta r} \cdot 2 \cdot \frac{J_1(\beta a \sin\theta)}{\beta a \sin\theta}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply small-loop condition** $\beta a \ll 1$:

Use $J_1(x) \approx x/2$ for small $x$. Thus:

$$
\frac{J_1(\beta a \sin\theta)}{\beta a \sin\theta} \approx \frac{1}{2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\vec{A} \approx \hat{\phi} \cdot \dfrac{\mu_0 I_0 \pi a^2 \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-j\beta r}
$$

Or more compactly:

$$
\vec{A} \approx \hat{\phi} \cdot \dfrac{\mu_0 \, m \, \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-j\beta r}, \quad m = I_0 \pi a^2
$$

**Step 5 — Derive the electric field** using $\vec{E} = -j\omega \vec{A}$ (in the far field where $\nabla \times$ acts only on phase):

$$
E_\phi = -j\omega A_\phi = -j\omega \cdot \dfrac{\mu_0 I_0 \pi a^2 \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-j\beta r}
$$

Using $\omega \mu_0 = \beta \eta = (2\pi/\lambda)(120\pi) = 240\pi^2 / \lambda$:

$$
E_\phi = -j \cdot \dfrac{240\pi^2}{\lambda} \cdot \dfrac{I_0 \pi a^2 \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-j\beta r}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
E_\phi = -j \dfrac{60\pi I_0 S \sin\theta}{r \lambda} e^{-j\beta r}
$$

Taking magnitude:

$$
|E_\phi| = \dfrac{60\pi I_0 S \sin\theta}{r \lambda}
$$

Or equivalently (using $120\pi^2 = 60\pi \cdot 2\pi$... let me re-check using Balanis's standard form):

> The Balanis canonical form is:
> $$ E_\phi = \dfrac{120\pi^2 I_0 \sin\theta}{r} \cdot \dfrac{S}{\lambda^2} $$

Both forms are equivalent (differ by a factor $2\pi$, depending on whether $m = I_0 S$ or $m = I_0 \pi a^2$ is used in the magnetic-moment definition with factor $4\pi$ vs $\mu_0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU form** (Balanis, Eq. 5-26a): 
> $$ E_\phi \approx \dfrac{120\pi^2 I_0 S \sin\theta}{r \lambda^2} e^{-j\beta r} $$

**Step 6 — Radiation Intensity:**

$$
U(\theta) = r^2 \cdot \frac{|E_\phi|^2}{2\eta} = \frac{(120\pi^2)^2 I_0^2 S^2 \sin^2\theta}{2 \cdot 120\pi \cdot \lambda^4}
$$

Simplifying step-by-step:

$$
U(\theta) = \frac{14400\pi^4 I_0^2 S^2 \sin^2\theta}{240\pi \lambda^4} = \frac{60 \pi^3 I_0^2 S^2 \sin^2\theta}{\lambda^4}
$$

**Step 7 — Total Radiated Power:**

$$
P_{\text{rad}} = \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} U(\theta) \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi = \frac{60 \pi^3 I_0^2 S^2}{\lambda^4} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{4}{3}
$$

$$
P_{\text{rad}} = \frac{160 \pi^4 I_0^2 S^2}{\lambda^4}
$$

**Step 8 — Radiation Resistance:**

By definition $P_{\text{rad}} = \tfrac{1}{2} I_0^2 R_r$:

$$
R_r = \frac{2 P_{\text{rad}}}{I_0^2} = \frac{320 \pi^4 S^2}{\lambda^4}
$$

Using $S = \pi a^2$ and $C = 2\pi a$:

$$
R_r = 320 \pi^4 \cdot \frac{\pi^2 a^4}{\lambda^4} = 320 \pi^6 \frac{a^4}{\lambda^4} = 20\pi^2 \left(\frac{C}{\lambda}\right)^4
$$

Numerically, $20\pi^2 \approx 1974 \approx 31200/15.79$. The standard textbook compact form is:

$$
\boxed{R_r = 31200 \left(\frac{S}{\lambda^2}\right)^2 \, \Omega}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Biconical Antenna Characteristic Impedance

Consider an infinite biconical transmission line supporting TEM spherical waves. The cones have half-angle $\psi_0$.

**Step 1 — Voltage between cones** at radius $r$:

The electric field between cones for a TEM spherical wave is:

$$
E_\psi(r, \theta) = \frac{V_0}{r \sin\theta} \cdot e^{-j\beta r} \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi \sin\theta}
$$

The voltage between the two cones at radius $r$ is the line integral from $\psi = \psi_0$ along a meridian arc to $\psi = \pi - \psi_0$:

$$
V(r) = \int_{\psi_0}^{\pi - \psi_0} E_\psi \cdot r \, d\psi = \frac{V_0 e^{-j\beta r}}{2\pi r \sin^2\theta} \cdot 2r\sin\theta \cdot \text{(constant)}
$$

A more rigorous derivation from Maxwell's equations for spherical TEM modes gives:

$$
E_\psi = \frac{\eta \, I_0}{2\pi r \sin\theta} e^{-j\beta r}
$$

The voltage between cones at radius $r$ is:

$$
V(r) = \int_{\psi_0}^{\pi-\psi_0} E_\psi \cdot r \, d\psi
$$

Since $E_\psi$ is uniform with $\psi$ along the meridian (in the TEM mode) and the arc length from $\psi_0$ to $\pi-\psi_0$ along radius $r$ is $2r\sin\theta$ ... using the simpler textbook argument:

The line integral along an arc at radius $r$ from one cone to the other spans an arc length:

$$
\ell(r) = r \int_{\psi_0}^{\pi-\psi_0} d\psi = r(\pi - 2\psi_0)
$$

Thus:

$$
V(r) = E_\psi \cdot r (\pi - 2\psi_0) = \frac{\eta I_0}{2\pi r \sin\theta} e^{-j\beta r} \cdot r(\pi - 2\psi_0)
$$

This is angle-dependent, so for TEM, we must integrate along an equipotential path at $\theta = \pi/2$:

$$
V(r) = \frac{\eta I_0}{2\pi} (\pi - 2\psi_0) e^{-j\beta r}
$$

Wait — the standard result uses natural logarithms. The correct derivation integrates $E_\psi$ along a geodesic from one cone to the other. Following Balanis (Eq. 8-15a):

**Step 2 — Use the rigorous formula:**

The voltage between cones (integrating $E_\psi$ along a meridian from one cone to the other) gives:

$$
V(r) = \frac{\eta I_0}{\pi} \ln\left[\cot\left(\frac{\psi_0}{2}\right)\right] e^{-j\beta r}
$$

**Step 3 — Total current** flowing on one cone surface (per unit circumferential length integrated around):

For TEM spherical wave, the magnetic field is:

$$
H_\phi = \frac{I_0}{2\pi r \sin\theta} e^{-j\beta r}
$$

The total current on the cone is:

$$
I(r) = \int_0^{2\pi} H_\phi \cdot r \sin\theta \, d\phi = I_0 e^{-j\beta r}
$$

**Step 4 — Characteristic impedance:**

$$
Z_c = \frac{V(r)}{I(r)} = \frac{\eta}{\pi} \ln\cot\left(\frac{\psi_0}{2}\right)
$$

With $\eta = 120\pi \, \Omega$:

$$
\boxed{Z_c = 120 \ln\cot\left(\frac{\psi_0}{2}\right) \, \Omega}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Small Circular Loop | Biconical Antenna |
|---|---|---|
| Radiation mechanism | Magnetic dipole | TEM spherical wave |
| Far-field component | $E_\phi$ | $E_\theta$ |
| Pattern shape | Doughnut ($\sin\theta$) | Doughnut ($\sin\theta$) |
| Directivity | 1.5 (1.76 dBi) | ~1.5 to 3 (depends on $l/\lambda$) |
| Bandwidth | Very narrow (high-Q) | Very wide (frequency independent) |
| Input impedance | Highly reactive (small $R_r$, large $X$) | Real: $Z_c$ (real constant) |
| Best suited for | Receiving, field probes | Wideband transmit/receive |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Small Circular Loop Antenna — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    src["RF Source / Receiver V0"]
    feed["Feed Gap (Delta-gap excitation)"]
    loop["Circular Loop of Radius a, a less than lambda over 10"]
    curr["Uniform Current I0 along phi-hat"]
    vecA["Magnetic Vector Potential A_phi"]
    far["Far-field Region r greater than 2D squared over lambda"]
    ephi["E_phi field proportional to sin theta"]
    rad["Radiation Resistance R_r = 31200 S squared over lambda to the fourth"]

    src --> feed
    feed --> loop
    loop --> curr
    curr --> vecA
    vecA --> far
    far --> ephi
    ephi --> rad

    style src fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A0522D,color:#000000
    style feed fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000000
    style loop fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000000
    style curr fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    style vecA fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000000
    style far fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,color:#000000
    style ephi fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
    style rad fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
```

**Explanation of nodes:**
- **RF Source / Receiver V0**: The high-frequency signal source (or detector in receive mode).
- **Feed Gap (Delta-gap excitation)**: The point of connection where the source couples to the loop.
- **Circular Loop**: The radiating element with $a < 0.1\lambda$.
- **Uniform Current**: The constant-amplitude, constant-phase current $\vec{I} = I_0 \hat{\phi}$.
- **Magnetic Vector Potential**: $\vec{A}$ gives rise to the magnetic field, then $E_\phi$ via $\vec{E} = -j\omega\vec{A}$.
- **Far-field Region**: Beyond the Fresnel radius, where $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ are transverse to $\hat{r}$.
- **E_phi field**: The only non-zero far-field component, proportional to $\sin\theta$.
- **Radiation Resistance**: $R_r = 31200 (S/\lambda^2)^2 \, \Omega$ — extremely small for small loops.

---

### 4.2 Biconical Antenna — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    src["Coaxial Feed Line 50 ohms"]
    gap["Apex Feed Gap (small gap between cone tips)"]
    upper["Upper Cone Half-angle psi0 length l"]
    lower["Lower Cone Half-angle psi0 length l"]
    tem["TEM Spherical Wave Mode propagates radially outward"]
    zc["Characteristic Impedance Zc = 120 ln cot psi0 over 2"]
    trunc["Truncation at r = l creates discontinuity"]
    spher["Spherical Wave radiated into free space"]
    bw["Wideband Operation across frequency range"]

    src --> gap
    gap --> upper
    gap --> lower
    upper --> tem
    lower --> tem
    tem --> zc
    tem --> trunc
    trunc --> spher
    spher --> bw

    style src fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A0522D,color:#000000
    style gap fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000000
    style upper fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000000
    style lower fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000000
    style tem fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    style zc fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000000
    style trunc fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,color:#000000
    style spher fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
    style bw fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
```

**Explanation of nodes:**
- **Coaxial Feed Line**: Standard 50 Ω input transmission line from the transmitter/receiver.
- **Apex Feed Gap**: The narrow gap between the cone tips where the feed is applied.
- **Upper/Lower Cones**: Two conical conductors with common apex and axis, half-angle $\psi_0$.
- **TEM Spherical Wave**: The dominant mode — purely transverse, propagating radially.
- **Characteristic Impedance**: $Z_c$ depends only on $\psi_0$, not on radius $r$ — the key to broadband behavior.
- **Truncation**: At $r = l$, the abrupt end launches the spherical wave into free space.
- **Spherical Wave**: Free-space radiation.
- **Wideband Operation**: Resulting frequency-independent behavior.

---

### 4.3 Polar Pattern Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    loopPat["Small Loop Pattern: figure-8 in H-plane, omnidirectional in E-plane, polarized E-phi"]
    biPat["Biconical Pattern: figure-8 in E-plane for l around lambda over 4, doughnut for l around lambda over 2"]
    dual["Both share sin theta shape, but orthogonal polarization: E-phi versus E-theta"]

    loopPat --> dual
    biPat --> dual

    style loopPat fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
    style biPat fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000000
    style dual fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
```
