## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Define a small loop antenna. State the condition under which a loop antenna is classified as "electrically small".**

**Model Answer:**

A small loop antenna is a closed conducting loop, usually circular, carrying a uniform time-harmonic current. It is electrically small when its maximum dimension (the circumference $C = 2\pi a$) is less than approximately $\lambda/10$ (i.e., $C < 0.1\lambda$ or equivalently $a < 0.016\lambda$).

Under this condition, the current is uniform in magnitude and phase because the phase variation $\beta a$ around the loop is negligible.

**Marking:** Definition (2 marks) + Condition (1 mark).

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is a biconical antenna? Why is it called a frequency-independent antenna?**

**Model Answer:**

A biconical antenna consists of two metallic cones of finite length $l$ with a common axis and common apex, excited across a small gap at the apex.

It is called frequency-independent because its geometry is defined purely by angles (the half-angle $\psi_0$) rather than by absolute linear dimensions. As a result, the characteristic impedance $Z_c = 120 \ln\cot(\psi_0/2)$ is independent of frequency, and the radiation pattern repeats (scales) with wavelength — providing wideband performance.

**Marking:** Structure (2 marks) + Reason (1 mark).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice between Q1A/Q1B is permitted.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **radiation resistance** of a small circular loop antenna of radius $a$ carrying uniform current $I_0$. State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small circular loop of radius $a = 0.05\,\text{m}$ is operating at $f = 100\,\text{MHz}$. Compute:
- (i) The radiation resistance $R_r$
- (ii) The directivity in dBi
- (iii) The effective aperture $A_e$ **(7 marks)**

##### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Loop radius $a \ll \lambda$ (electrically small, $C < \lambda/10$).
2. Wire is thin ($b \ll a$).
3. Current is uniform in magnitude and phase: $I = I_0 \hat{\phi}$.
4. Far-field region observation.

**Derivation Summary:**

The far-field electric field is:

$$
E_\phi = \frac{120\pi^2 I_0 S \sin\theta}{r \lambda^2} e^{-j\beta r}, \quad S = \pi a^2
$$

Radiation intensity:

$$
U(\theta) = \frac{r^2 |E_\phi|^2}{2\eta} = \frac{60\pi^3 I_0^2 S^2 \sin^2\theta}{\lambda^4}
$$

Total radiated power:

$$
P_{\text{rad}} = \int_0^{2\pi}\!\!\int_0^{\pi} U \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi = \frac{160\pi^4 I_0^2 S^2}{\lambda^4}
$$

Radiation resistance (using $P_{\text{rad}} = \tfrac{1}{2}I_0^2 R_r$):

$$
R_r = \frac{320 \pi^4 S^2}{\lambda^4} = 31200 \left(\frac{S}{\lambda^2}\right)^2 \, \Omega
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

Given: $a = 0.05$ m, $f = 100$ MHz.

**Wavelength:**
$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{100 \times 10^6} = 3 \, \text{m}
$$

**Check small-loop condition:** $C = 2\pi a = 2\pi(0.05) = 0.314$ m, while $\lambda/10 = 0.3$ m. Since $C \approx \lambda/10$, the small-loop formula is at the edge of validity (we proceed with the formula for the model solution).

**Loop area:**
$$
S = \pi a^2 = \pi (0.05)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2
$$

**(i) Radiation resistance:**
$$
R_r = 31200 \left(\frac{S}{\lambda^2}\right)^2 = 31200 \left(\frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}{9}\right)^2
$$

$$
\frac{S}{\lambda^2} = 8.727 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
R_r = 31200 \times (8.727 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 31200 \times 7.616 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_r \approx 2.376 \times 10^{-2} \, \Omega = 23.76 \, \text{m}\Omega}
$$

**(ii) Directivity:**
$$
D_0 = 1.5 \Rightarrow D_0\,(\text{dBi}) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) = 1.76 \, \text{dBi}
$$

**(iii) Effective Aperture:**
$$
A_e = \frac{\lambda^2 D_0}{4\pi} = \frac{9 \times 1.5}{4\pi} = \frac{13.5}{12.566} \approx 1.074 \, \text{m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{A_e \approx 1.074 \, \text{m}^2}
$$

**Marking Split for Q-A (b):**
(i) 3 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 2 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **geometry and operating principle** of a biconical antenna. Derive the expression for its characteristic impedance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A biconical antenna has half-angle $\psi_0 = 30^\circ$ and length $l = 0.5$ m, operating at $f = 300$ MHz. Compute:
- (i) Characteristic impedance $Z_c$
- (ii) Input impedance if matched (approximate)
- (iii) Comment on broadband behavior. **(7 marks)**

##### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Geometry, Principle, and Derivation:**

**Geometry:** Two right-circular metallic cones with half-angle $\psi_0$, joined apex-to-apex along the $z$-axis, fed by a coaxial line at the apex through a small gap.

**Operating Principle:**
- The cones support a **TEM spherical wave mode** propagating radially outward.
- The cross-section at any radius $r$ behaves as a tapered parallel-plate transmission line.
- Impedance is determined only by the half-angle $\psi_0$, hence broadband.
- Truncation at length $l$ launches the wave into free space.

**Derivation of $Z_c$:**

For TEM spherical mode, the magnetic field is:

$$
H_\phi = \frac{I_0}{2\pi r \sin\theta} e^{-j\beta r}
$$

Integrating $H_\phi$ around the cone (at $\theta$):

$$
I(r) = \int_0^{2\pi} H_\phi \cdot r\sin\theta \, d\phi = I_0 e^{-j\beta r}
$$

The electric field:

$$
E_\theta = \eta H_\phi = \frac{\eta I_0}{2\pi r \sin\theta} e^{-j\beta r}
$$

The voltage between cones (integrating $E_\theta \cdot r \, d\theta$):

$$
V(r) = \int_{\psi_0}^{\pi-\psi_0} E_\theta \cdot r \, d\theta = \frac{\eta I_0}{\pi} \ln\cot\left(\frac{\psi_0}{2}\right) e^{-j\beta r}
$$

Characteristic impedance:

$$
Z_c = \frac{V(r)}{I(r)} = \frac{\eta}{\pi} \ln\cot\left(\frac{\psi_0}{2}\right) = 120 \ln\cot\left(\frac{\psi_0}{2}\right) \, \Omega
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

Given: $\psi_0 = 30^\circ$, $l = 0.5$ m, $f = 300$ MHz.

**(i) Characteristic impedance:**

$$
Z_c = 120 \ln\cot\left(\frac{30^\circ}{2}\right) = 120 \ln\cot(15^\circ)
$$

Computing $\cot(15^\circ) = 1/\tan(15^\circ) = 1/0.2679 = 3.732$:

$$
Z_c = 120 \ln(3.732) = 120 \times 1.317 = 158.04 \, \Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{Z_c \approx 158 \, \Omega}
$$

**(ii) Input impedance:** For an infinite biconical line, $Z_{\text{in}} = Z_c \approx 158 \, \Omega$. For finite length $l$, the input impedance is approximately:

$$
Z_{\text{in}} \approx Z_c \left(\frac{Z_L + j Z_c \tan\beta l}{Z_c + j Z_L \tan\beta l}\right)
$$

where $Z_L$ is the load (free space, $\approx 377\,\Omega$ mismatch). Since $l/\lambda = 0.5/1 = 0.5$, $\beta l = \pi$, $\tan\beta l = 0$, so:

$$
\boxed{Z_{\text{in}} \approx Z_c \approx 158 \, \Omega \text{ (radiation resistance, approximately real)}}
$$

**(iii) Comment on Broadband Behavior:**

Because $Z_c$ depends only on $\psi_0$ and not on $r$ or $\lambda$, the antenna exhibits nearly constant real input impedance over a frequency range where the cones are electrically long ($l > \lambda/4$) but where the truncation does not produce severe standing waves. Typical bandwidth is **3:1 to 10:1 (octave or multi-octave)**. For our case with $l = 0.5$ m, useful operation spans roughly from $f_{\text{low}} \approx c/(4l) = 150$ MHz up to several GHz (limited by cone-tip feed effects).

**Marking Split for Q-B (b):** (i) 3 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 2 marks.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Using loop formula outside its range**: $R_r = 31200 (S/\lambda^2)^2$ is valid ONLY when $C < \lambda/10$. For larger loops, current is no longer uniform and you must use Bessel-function based expressions. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks if the validity condition is not stated.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\sin^2\theta$ factor**: When computing total radiated power from $U(\theta)$, students often forget to multiply by the solid-angle element $\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi$, leading to an answer off by a factor of $4/3$.
> 3. **Confusing the biconical $Z_c$ formula**: The textbook form is $Z_c = 120 \ln\cot(\psi_0/2)$. Students often write $60 \ln\cot(\psi_0/2)$ (missing a factor of 2) or use $\tan$ instead of $\cot$.
> 4. **Directivity in dBi vs dBd**: Always convert linear directivity using $D_{\text{dBi}} = 10\log_{10}(D_0)$ — do NOT add 2.15 dB unless explicitly comparing to a half-wave dipole.
> 5. **Units of $S/\lambda^2$**: This ratio is *dimensionless*. Failing to show units in the intermediate step is a minor mark loss.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **small circular loop** has circumference $C < \lambda/10$ and supports a **uniform current**.
- [x] The small loop is the **magnetic dual** of the short electric dipole — same $\sin\theta$ pattern, $E_\phi$ polarization.
- [x] **Radiation resistance of small loop:** $R_r = 31200 (S/\lambda^2)^2 \, \Omega$ — extremely small (milliohms to ohms), making matching difficult.
- [x] **Directivity of small loop:** 1.5 (1.76 dBi), identical to short dipole.
- [x] **Far-field** has only $E_\phi$ component: $E_\phi = (120\pi^2 I_0 S \sin\theta)/(r\lambda^2)$.
- [x] **Biconical antenna** = broadband radiator; geometry defined by angle $\psi_0$, not by absolute size.
- [x] **Biconical $Z_c$:** $Z_c = 120 \ln\cot(\psi_0/2) \, \Omega$ — purely real, frequency-independent.
- [x] **Biconical supports TEM spherical mode** — the only mode that propagates radially between cones.
- [x] **Practical biconical bandwidth**: 3:1 to 10:1, used in EMC testing and UWB systems.
- [x] For $R_r$ derivation: integrate $U(\theta)\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi$ carefully — the factor $4/3$ from $\int_0^\pi \sin^3\theta\,d\theta = 4/3$ must appear.
- [x] For $Z_c$ derivation: integrate $E_\theta \cdot r\,d\theta$ along meridian, NOT along a radial line.
- [x] **Effective aperture** of any antenna: $A_e = \lambda^2 D_0/(4\pi)$ — apply to loop with $D_0 = 1.5$.
- [x] Always verify small-loop condition **before** applying $R_r$ formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Triangular Sheet and Bow-Tie Antenna
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Triangular Sheet and Bow-Tie Antenna # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Triangular Sheet Antenna — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > The **Triangular Sheet Antenna** is a planar broadband radiator formed by two identical isosceles (usually equilateral) triangular conducting sheets, fed at their common apex (vertex) by a two-wire transmission line. It is the **limiting flat-plane case (cone half-angle $\theta_h \to 90^\circ$)** of the finite **Biconical Antenna**, retaining the latter's wideband, frequency-independent behaviour only over a finite frequency range determined by the arm length $L$. Mathematically, if $W$ is the half-base width of the triangle and $L$ is the slant height, the half-angle $\alpha$ of the sheet with respect to the antenna axis is given by: $$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)$$ ## 1.2 Bow-Tie Antenna — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > The **Bow-Tie Antenna** (also called a *flared-notch / bi-triangular dipole*) is the practical, scaled, and often dielectric-loaded realisation of the **Triangular Sheet Antenna**. It is constructed from two triangular (or trapezoidal) metallic patches flared symmetrically in a single plane, fed at the narrow gap between their tips. It is widely used as a **primary feed for parabolic reflectors** and in **UWB (Ultra-Wideband)** impulse radars. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy (Think of a hand-fan):** > - **Thin-wire dipole** = a thin wooden stick. Rigid, narrow, and resonant only at one specific length. Bend it slightly and the musical note changes sharply. > - **Biconical antenna** = two hollow ice-cream cones joined at their tips. The opening "guides" the current smoothly out, giving a wide operating range. > - **Triangular sheet** = the same two cones, but you have *pressed them flat* until the cone walls become flat triangles. The wide base acts like a gentle ramp, allowing the current to spread out gradually. > - **Bow-tie** = cut those flat triangles out of a piece of cardboard and solder a small gap at the tip. It is the *practical, easy-to-fabricate* version of the triangular sheet, with optional dielectric (a small plastic "bow-tie sandwich") to make it even more broadband. > > **Why does this matter?** A thin dipole changes its impedance violently with frequency. The flared (bow-tie) geometry smooths the current distribution, **smearing the resonance** over a much wider bandwidth — exactly what broadband communication and UWB radar need. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted) - Free-space intrinsic impedance: $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx \mathbf{377\ \Omega}$ - Velocity of light: $c = \mathbf{3 \times 10^{8}\ m/s}$ - Wavenumber: $k = \omega\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0} = \mathbf{2\pi/\lambda}$ - Standard feed gap for bow-tie: $\mathbf{1\ to\ 3\ mm}$ (mechanical rigidity vs. electrical continuity trade-off) - Typical substrate for printed bow-tie: **FR-4 ($\varepsilon_r \approx 4.4$)** or **Rogers RO4003 ($\varepsilon_r = 3.38$)** --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 — Geometric Origin (Biconical Connection).** A biconical antenna has two cones of half-angle $\theta_h$ sharing a common apex. Its surface is rotationally symmetric, so we can use spherical modes $TM_m$. For a finite length $2L$ biconical antenna, the dominant $TM_0$ mode gives a transmission-line-like input impedance with a wideband behaviour. **Step 2 — The "Flattening" Process.** As the cone half-angle $\theta_h$ increases, the cone walls become less steep. At $\theta_h = 90^\circ$ exactly, the cone collapses onto the equatorial plane, leaving behind **two flat triangular conducting sheets** lying in the $xy$-plane. The feeding transmission line sits at the apex. **Step 3 — Why Triangular (and not Rectangular)?** A rectangular flat dipole behaves like a slot; its impedance oscillates wildly. The triangular taper gradually widens the cross-section. This is a form of **impedance taper** — the same idea that governs Vivaldi and log-periodic antennas — producing a smooth impedance transition from the feed line to free space. **Step 4 — Current Distribution Assumption.** Balanis (Antenna Theory, Chapter on Broadband Dipoles and Frequency-Independent Antennas) shows that the dominant-mode current on the sheet can be approximated as **uniform across the width** and **sinusoidal along the length**: $$I(r) \;\approx\; I_0\,\frac{\sin\!\big(k(L-r)\big)}{\sin(kL)} \quad \text{for } 0 \le r \le L$$ **Step 5 — Far-Field Synthesis.** The far-zone field is obtained by integrating the surface current $J_s$ (tangential to the triangle) over the slant height $r$ and across the width, which yields a closed-form expression dominated by an obliquity factor $F(\theta)$. **Step 6 — Why the Bow-Tie?** The bow-tie inherits all the wideband virtues of the triangular sheet but is easier to manufacture (printed on a PCB, photo-etched, or simply cut from sheet metal). It is also the **canonical UWB radiator** (3.1 – 10.6 GHz) because its impulse response is nearly free of late-time ringing. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All quantities in **SI units**. $\theta$ measured from the antenna axis ($+z$). $\phi$ is the azimuth angle. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sheet half-angle | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(W/L)$ | rad | $W$ = half-base, $L$ = slant length | | 2 | Biconical limit | $\theta_h \to 90^\circ$ ⇒ triangular sheet | deg | Geometry limiting case | | 3 | Current on arm | $I(r)=I_0\sin\!\big(k(L-r)\big)/\sin(kL)$ | A | $0\le r\le L$ | | 4 | Far-field $E_\theta$ | $E_\theta=\dfrac{j\eta\,I_0\,e^{-jkr}}{2\pi r}\dfrac{\cos(kL\cos\theta)-\cos(kL)}{\sin\theta}$ | V/m | From sheet integral | | 5 | Pattern factor | $F(\theta)=\dfrac{\cos(kL\cos\theta)-\cos(kL)}{\sin\theta}$ | – | Same as thin dipole envelope | | 6 | Directivity (max at $\theta=90^\circ$) | $D_0 \approx 1.76$ (linear) → $2.46$ dBi for $L=\lambda/2$ | dBi | Triangular = thin-dipole-like | | 7 | Input impedance (approx.) | $Z_{in}\approx 60\,\text{Cin}(kL)$ for short arm; $73+j42.5\ \Omega$ at half-wave | $\Omega$ | Sheet edge-yields mild capacitive shift | | 8 | VSWR bandwidth criterion | $\text{VSWR} \le 2$ over $f_{\max}/f_{\min}$ | – | Typical bow-tie: $\mathbf{3:1}$ to $\mathbf{10:1}$ | | 9 | First resonance | $L = \lambda/2 \Rightarrow f_r = c/(2L)$ | Hz | Arm-length = half-wavelength | | 10 | Far-field distance | $R_{ff}=2D^2/\lambda$ | m | $D$ = largest dimension | **Pitfall Avoidance:** In row 7, *Cin* is the cosine integral function, **NOT** the unit "centimetre" nor the capacitance per unit length symbol *C*'. Do not confuse them in your KTU scripts. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **UWB communication (FCC 3.1–10.6 GHz band):** Bow-tie printed monopoles are the de-facto radiator in IEEE 802.15.3a / 4a devices. - **Ground-penetrating radar (GPR):** The short impulse response of a bow-tie is critical for time-domain resolution of buried objects. - **Parabolic-reflector feeds:** The original use; the bow-tie's wide pattern illuminates the reflector edge efficiently. - **EMC test antennas (1–10 GHz):** Used as broadband reference radiators. - **RFID and IoT gateways:** Dielectric-loaded bow-ties provide gain in a compact footprint. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Current Distribution on the Triangular Sheet (Balanis Method) We start from the **Biconical Antenna** of half-angle $\theta_h$ and arm length $L$. The dominant mode is $TM_0$ (azimuthally symmetric). The fields inside the conical region ($r # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Bow-Tie Antenna ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A["FEED NETWORK"] TX["Transmitter / Pulse Generator
50 ohm source"] BAL["Balun or Differential Feed
Stops common-mode currents"] end subgraph B["RADIATING STRUCTURE - Bow-Tie"] GAP["Feed Gap
Width = 1 to 3 mm"] T1["Upper Triangular Patch
Slant length L, base 2W"] T2["Lower Triangular Patch
Mirror image of T1"] SUB["Dielectric Substrate
epsilon-r = 2.2 to 4.4"] end subgraph C["RADIATED FIELDS - Free Space"] EFIELD["E_theta dominant, E_phi suppressed
Linear polarisation in H-plane"] PAT["8-shaped E-plane pattern
Omnidirectional in H-plane"] end TX --> BAL --> GAP GAP --> T1 GAP --> T2 T1 --- SUB T2 --- SUB T1 --> EFIELD T2 --> EFIELD EFIELD --> PAT ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Current Distribution & Equivalent Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Feed Point at Apex] -->|r = 0| B[Current maximum I0] B -->|r increases| C[Current tapers as sin k L minus r] C -->|r = L| D[Open end, Current approximately 0] subgraph GEOM["Equivalent Geometry"] E[Biconical theta-h less than 90] -->|theta-h increases| F[theta-h = 90 deg] F -->|Flatten cones| G[Triangular Sheet] G -->|Practical cut-out| H[Bow-Tie Antenna] end ``` ## 4.3 ASCII Schematic of the Triangular Sheet (Coordinate View) ``` z (antenna axis) | | * (feed gap at origin) /|\ / | \ / | \ / | \ Slant length L / | \ / I(r) \ / | \ / | \ /_______|_________\ <-- base 2W (at z = 0 plane) | +----> x ``` **Reading the figure:** - Two isosceles triangles share the apex at the origin. - The slant length $L$ runs along the triangle edges. - The half-base $W$ lies along the $x$-axis. - The current $I(r)$ flows from the apex outward along the sheet, sinusoidal in $r$. ## 4.4 Radiation Pattern (Conceptual) The 3-D radiation pattern of a half-wave triangular sheet is the same **figure-8** as a thin half-wave dipole, rotated about the antenna axis (i.e., omni in $\phi$): - **E-plane** ($\phi=0$): two-lobe pattern, nulls along $+z$ and $-z$, maxima at $\theta=\pm 90^\circ$. - **H-plane** ($\theta=90^\circ$): perfect circle (isotropic in azimuth). - **Front-to-back ratio:** 0 dB (the pattern is symmetric, no preferred "front"). --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **State the relationship between the triangular sheet antenna and the biconical antenna. Why is the triangular sheet considered a broadband radiator?** **Model Answer (≈3 marks):** The triangular sheet antenna is obtained as the **limiting case of a finite biconical antenna** when the cone half-angle $\theta_h \to 90^\circ$. The two cones flatten into two triangular conducting sheets sharing a common apex feed. It is broadband because the **sheet half-angle $\alpha$** acts as an impedance taper, smoothing the input impedance variation with frequency. The wider the flare (larger $\alpha$), the gentler the impedance transition from the feed line ($50\ \Omega$) to free space ($377\ \Omega$), giving a VSWR $\le 2$ bandwidth of $2:1$ to $>10:1$ depending on geometry. *(Mark split: 1.5 for relationship, 1.5 for broadband reasoning.)* ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **List any three practical applications of the bow-tie antenna.** **Model Answer (≈3 marks):** 1. **Primary feed for parabolic reflector antennas** in satellite and radar systems (wide beamwidth illuminates the dish efficiently). 2. **UWB impulse radar** in ground-penetrating radar (GPR) and through-wall imaging — the bow-tie has a near-distortionless impulse response. 3. **Broadband reference antenna in EMC/EMI test chambers** (1–10 GHz) and **UWB wireless links** (FCC 3.1–10.6 GHz). *(1 mark each.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not write** the bow-tie input impedance as simply "73 Ω". The value 73 Ω is the **half-wave thin-dipole** value; the bow-tie has a *smoother* variation and a *lower* resonance $Q$. Examiners deduct **2 marks** if you copy the dipole number without comment. > 2. **Always specify the limiting process** ($\theta_h \to 90^\circ$) when linking triangular sheet to biconical; a bare statement "they are related" loses 1 mark. > 3. **Units and limits**: When deriving $E_\theta$, keep $kL\cos\theta$ inside the cosine; do not expand using small-angle approximation unless the question demands it. > 4. **Directivity is in dBi** when the question asks for "in decibels" — not dBd. Marks lost if you forget the 2.15 dB reference. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Starting from the biconical antenna, derive the far-zone electric field $E_\theta$ of an equilateral triangular sheet antenna of slant length $L$. Clearly show the limiting process. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a half-wave triangular sheet ($L=\lambda/2$), compute (i) the directivity in dBi, and (ii) the input impedance at first resonance. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Q-A (a) (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Geometry and Limiting Process (1 mark):** A biconical antenna of half-angle $\theta_h$ and arm length $L$ is reduced to a triangular sheet by letting $\theta_h \to 90^\circ$. The cones flatten into two equilateral triangles sharing an apex feed. **Step 2 — Current on the Sheet (1 mark):** Assume uniform current across the local width and sinusoidal taper along the slant: $$I(r)=I_0\,\dfrac{\sin\!\big(k(L-r)\big)}{\sin(kL)},\quad 0\le r\le L$$ **Step 3 — Vector Potential / Sheet Integration (2 marks):** Treat each sheet as a strip of variable width $w(r)=2W\,r/L$. The far-field $E_\theta$ for a strip radiator: $$E_\theta=j\,\dfrac{k\eta\,e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r}\sin\theta \int_0^L I(r')\,w(r')\,e^{jkr'\cos\theta}dr'$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral (2 marks):** Substituting $I(r')$ and $w(r')$, then integrating by parts yields: $$E_\theta = j\,\dfrac{\eta\,I_0\,e^{-jkr}}{2\pi r}\cdot\dfrac{\cos(kL\cos\theta)-\cos(kL)}{\sin\theta}$$ **Step 5 — Interpret the Result (1 mark):** The form **matches the thin-dipole pattern** $F(\theta)=\big[\cos(kL\cos\theta)-\cos(kL)\big]/\sin\theta$, confirming consistency; the difference is hidden in the smoother $I(r)$ profile, which is the *physical reason* for the wider bandwidth. --- #### **Model Solution to Q-A (b) (7 marks)** **(i) Directivity at $L=\lambda/2$ (3 marks):** The total radiated power (numerical evaluation, Balanis Table 8-2): $$P_{rad}=\dfrac{\eta\,I_0^2}{4\pi^2}\times 1.218\ \text{W}$$ Maximum radiation intensity at $\theta=90^\circ$: $$U_{\max}=\dfrac{\eta\,I_0^2}{4\pi^2}\times 1.108\ \text{W/sr}$$ Directivity: $$D_0=\dfrac{4\pi\,U_{\max}}{P_{rad}}=\dfrac{4\pi\times 1.108}{1.218}\approx 1.64$$ In decibels: $D_0(\text{dBi})=10\log_{10}(1.64)\approx \mathbf{2.15\ dBi}$. *(Using the more accurate full-sheet numerical integral gives $D_0\approx 1.76\ (2.46\ dBi)$ — accept either if justified.)* **(ii) Input Impedance at First Resonance (4 marks):** From the biconical-limit expression (Derivation 1 above): $$Z_{in}=j\,60\big[\ln(2L/a)-1\big]\tan(kL)$$ At $L=\lambda/2$, $kL=\pi$, so $\tan(kL)=0$ — the imaginary part vanishes. The resistive part is recovered from the average stored-energy method: $$R_{in}\approx 73\ \Omega$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{Z_{in}\big|_{L=\lambda/2}\;\approx\;73\ \Omega\;\text{(purely resistive)}}$$ For a practical **bow-tie** with sheet half-angle $\alpha=30^\circ$, the equivalent input resistance at first resonance drops to $\approx 50\text{–}60\ \Omega$, which is why the bow-tie is naturally **better matched to a $50\ \Omega$ feed line** than a thin dipole. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **(a)** With the aid of a neat diagram, explain the construction of a **bow-tie antenna**. Discuss its current distribution and how it differs from a thin-wire dipole. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A bow-tie antenna is to be designed for an UWB system covering $3.1$ to $10.6$ GHz. If the sheet half-angle is $\alpha=45^\circ$ and the dielectric substrate is FR-4 ($\varepsilon_r=4.4$), determine (i) the **physical arm length $L$** for the lower edge frequency, and (ii) the **expected fractional bandwidth** using the empirical formula $BW_\% = 100\,[1-\exp(-0.4/\sin\alpha)]$. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution to Q-B (a) (7 marks):** **Construction (2 marks):** A bow-tie antenna consists of two identical triangular (or trapezoidal) metallic patches flared symmetrically in a single plane, separated by a small feed gap (1–3 mm) at the apex. The patches are usually photo-etched on a dielectric substrate (FR-4, Rogers, etc.) or cut from sheet metal. A **balun** (or differential feed) is mandatory to suppress common-mode currents on the feed cable. **Current Distribution (3 marks):** Unlike a thin-wire dipole where current is concentrated on a 1-D line, the bow-tie carries a **2-D surface current** that *spreads* across the local sheet width. The current magnitude tapers as $\sin\!\big(k(L-r)\big)$ along $r$, but the **uniformity across the width** (for the dominant mode) makes the effective radiating aperture larger, lowering the resonant input impedance and broadening the impedance bandwidth. **Difference from Thin Dipole (2 marks):** | Feature | Thin Dipole | Bow-Tie | |---|---|---| | Geometry | 1-D line | 2-D tapered sheet | | Impedance variation | Strong, $40$–$2000\ \Omega$ | Smooth, $50$–$200\ \Omega$ | | Bandwidth (VSWR $\le 2$) | $\sim 10\%$ | $50$–$300\%$ | | Polarisation purity | Excellent | Slightly degraded (edge currents) | #### **Model Solution to Q-B (b) (7 marks):** **(i) Arm length $L$ at $f_{\min}=3.1$ GHz (4 marks):** Effective wavelength in the dielectric: $$\lambda_{eff}=\dfrac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}=\dfrac{c/f_{\min}}{\sqrt{4.4}}=\dfrac{3\times 10^8/3.1\times 10^9}{\sqrt{4.4}}\approx \dfrac{0.0968}{2.098}\approx 46.1\ \text{mm}$$ For a resonant bow-tie, the arm length is approximately: $$L\approx \dfrac{\lambda_{eff}}{4}=\dfrac{46.1}{4}\approx \mathbf{11.5\ mm}$$ (If the question uses the free-space lower bound: $L\approx \lambda_0/4=24.2$ mm. Accept either with proper justification.) **(ii) Fractional Bandwidth for $\alpha=45^\circ$ (3 marks):** $$BW_\% = 100\big[1-\exp(-0.4/\sin 45^\circ)\big]=100\big[1-\exp(-0.5657)\big]$$ $$\exp(-0.5657)\approx 0.568$$ $$BW_\%\approx 100\,(1-0.568)=\mathbf{43.2\%}$$ **Verdict:** A $\alpha=45^\circ$ bow-tie alone gives $\sim 43\%$ bandwidth, *insufficient* to cover $3.1$–$10.6$ GHz (which is $110\%$ bandwidth). The remedy is to (a) **increase $\alpha$ toward $60^\circ$** ($BW\approx 130\%$), or (b) **add resistive/dielectric loading**, or (c) use a **log-periodic** or **Vivaldi** structure instead. This is a classic KTU design-insight check. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):** - ☐ **Triangular sheet = biconical with $\theta_h = 90^\circ$** (always quote the limiting process). - ☐ **Far-field pattern:** $E_\theta = j\dfrac{\eta I_0 e^{-jkr}}{2\pi r}\cdot\dfrac{\cos(kL\cos\theta)-\cos(kL)}{\sin\theta}$ — *same form as a thin dipole*. - ☐ **Directivity at $L=\lambda/2$:** $\approx 1.64$ to $1.76$ (i.e., **2.15 – 2.46 dBi**). - ☐ **Input impedance at half-wave resonance:** $\approx \mathbf{73\ \Omega}$ (resistive, no reactive part). - ☐ **Bow-tie = practical realisation** of the triangular sheet; **flared for wider bandwidth**. - ☐ **Sheet half-angle $\alpha$ is the design knob** — larger $\alpha$ ⇒ wider bandwidth. - ☐ **Empirical bandwidth formula:** $BW_\%\approx 100\,[1-\exp(-0.4/\sin\alpha)]$. - ☐ **Effective wavelength on substrate:** $\lambda_{eff}=\lambda_0/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$. - ☐ **First resonance:** $L \approx \lambda/4$ (on substrate) or $\lambda/2$ (full dipole pair). - ☐ **Polarisation:** Linear; H-plane omni; E-plane figure-8. - ☐ **Applications:** UWB (3.1–10.6 GHz), GPR, parabolic-reflector feeds, EMC test antennas. - ☐ **Always use a balun** with a bow-tie to suppress feed-line common-mode currents. - ☐ **Examiner traps:** Confusing Cin (cosine integral) with capacitance; quoting $73\ \Omega$ without explaining why; missing the $90^\circ$ limit step; wrong unit for directivity (dBd vs dBi). - ☐ **Memorise the Balanis far-field expression** — it appears in nearly every KTU Part B question on broadband dipoles.

topics.title: Traveling Wave and Broadband Antennas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Traveling Wave and Broadband Antennas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Traveling Wave Antennas?

A **Traveling Wave Antenna (TWA)** is a class of radiating structure in which the current distribution along the conductor does **not** form a standing wave (as in resonant antennas) but instead propagates as a **progressive traveling wave** from the feed point toward the open (terminated) end of the antenna. Because there is no reflection at the far end, the antenna behaves as a **non-resonant** structure and exhibits a **wideband** radiation behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> *A traveling wave antenna is a long wire antenna terminated in its characteristic impedance so that only a traveling wave (and not a standing wave) exists on it. The radiation pattern is end-fire and broadband.*

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Whip vs. The Garden Hose"

Imagine two ways of disturbing water:
- A **standing wave antenna** is like snapping a guitar string — the energy bounces back and forth between the two fixed ends, creating discrete "hot spots" and "dead spots." This is **resonant** and **narrowband**.
- A **traveling wave antenna** is like water flowing continuously through a long garden hose that is open at the far end. The water (current) keeps moving in one direction and never returns, so there are no standing wave nodes. This makes it inherently **broadband** and produces an **end-fire** beam (radiation shoots out from the open end).

> [!NOTE]
> The **3 essential ingredients** that make an antenna a "traveling wave" type:
> 1. A long, often **tapered** or **terminated** conductor structure.
> 2. A **matched termination** (resistive load ≈ characteristic impedance Z₀) at the far end to absorb un-radiated energy.
> 3. An **end-fire** radiation pattern (maximum radiation along the axis of the wire).

## 1.2 What are Broadband Antennas?

A **Broadband Antenna** is one whose input impedance and radiation characteristics (pattern, gain, polarization) remain **substantially constant over a wide range of frequencies**. The bandwidth ratio ($BW_{ratio} = f_{max}/f_{min}$) often exceeds **2:1** and can be as high as **10:1 or more** for truly frequency-independent designs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:**
> *A broadband antenna is one whose input impedance, pattern, and polarization do not change appreciably over a wide frequency range. The underlying principle is that the antenna geometry, when specified by angles only, becomes frequency independent (Rumsey's principle).*

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Trumpet vs. The Violin"

- A **narrowband antenna** (like a half-wave dipole) is like a **violin** — it produces beautiful sound only when the string length matches the wavelength of the note. Change the frequency too much, and the music turns to noise.
- A **broadband antenna** is like a **trumpet** — the geometry is defined by angles (the flared bell), not by absolute lengths, so it can amplify a wide range of frequencies nearly equally well. This is **Rumsey's Principle**.

## 1.3 Classification Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Classification of Antennas Covered Under This Topic:**

| Category | Key Antennas | Key Trait |
|----------|-------------|-----------|
| **Traveling Wave Antennas** | Long-wire, V-Antenna, Rhombic, Beverage | End-fire, terminated |
| **Helical (Broadband)** | Axial-mode Helix | Circularly polarized |
| **Aperture-type Broadband** | Biconical, Discone | Dipole generalization |
| **Frequency-Independent** | Log-Periodic (LPDA), Spiral | Defined by angles only |
| **Yagi-Uda (Quasi-Broadband)** | Yagi-Uda Array | Multi-element parasitic |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Long-Wire Traveling Wave Antenna

A long-wire antenna is a straight wire of length **L** carrying a **traveling-wave current** $I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-j\beta z}$, terminated in a load $Z_L \approx Z_0$ to suppress reflection.

### 2.1.1 Radiation Pattern (Theoretical Steps)

**Step 1 — Current Distribution:**
Assume uniform traveling wave: $I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-j\beta z}$, where $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$.

**Step 2 — Far-Field Expression (Balanis form):**
The normalized E-field radiation pattern is:

$$
E_{\theta}(\theta) = \sin\theta \, \frac{\sin\left[\frac{\beta L}{2}(1 - \cos\theta)\right]}{1 - \cos\theta}
$$

**Step 3 — Main Lobe Tilt:** The principal maximum occurs at angle $\theta_0$ measured from the wire axis, where:

$$
\theta_0 \approx \cos^{-1}\left(1 - \frac{0.371\lambda}{L}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Why "Why?" Matters:** As **L** increases, the main lobe tilts closer to the wire axis ($\theta_0 \to 0°$), concentrating the radiation into a sharper, more end-fire beam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** Long-wire antennas have a **directivity that increases with length**. A wire of length $L = 5\lambda$ has directivity ≈ **9.5 dB**, and $L = 10\lambda$ reaches ≈ **12.4 dB**.

### 2.1.2 Directivity of Long-Wire Antenna

A commonly used **approximate formula** for a single long wire is:

$$
D \approx 10\log_{10}\left(4.28 \cdot \frac{L}{\lambda}\right) \;\; \text{dB}
$$

(valid in the range $L/\lambda \gg 1$).

## 2.2 V-Antenna

A **V-antenna** consists of two long wires arranged in a "V" shape, fed at the apex with equal-amplitude, opposite-phase signals. The two legs act as long-wire radiators whose **tilted main lobes** are aimed to **intersect in the boresight direction** (broadside to the V's bisector).

**The Optimum Apex Angle** $\phi_{opt}$ for the maximum directivity for legs of length $L$ is:

$$
\phi_{opt} = 2\theta_0 = 2\cos^{-1}\left(1 - \frac{0.371\lambda}{L}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** V-antennas are widely used in **HF (3–30 MHz) point-to-point communications** and shortwave broadcast reception because they offer **high gain** without the complexity of a full array.

## 2.3 Rhombic Antenna

A **Rhombic antenna** is a four-sided "diamond" of long wires, fed at one corner and terminated at the opposite corner with a matched resistive load. It is essentially **two V-antennas placed back-to-back**.

### 2.3.1 Design Equations

- **Tilt angle** of each leg (from broadside): $\Delta = 90° - \theta_0$ (the same as the long-wire tilt).
- **Height (H)** of the rhombus above ground: typically $H \approx \lambda$ for HF.
- **Length (L)** of each leg: chosen for desired gain.
- **Side tilt angle** $\phi$ (half of apex angle): $\phi = 90° - \theta_0$.

The **maximum directivity** of a rhombic is roughly:

$$
D_{rhombic} \approx 1.84 \cdot D_{long\,wire}
$$

## 2.4 Helical Antenna — The Star of Broadband Design

A **helical antenna** is a wire wound in the form of a helix, usually backed by a ground plane. It has two principal modes of radiation:

1. **Normal Mode** (when $C < \lambda$): broadside, linearly polarized, low gain.
2. **Axial Mode** (when $C \approx \lambda$): end-fire, circularly polarized, **HIGH GAIN** — this is the **useful broadband mode**.

### 2.4.1 Geometric Parameters

- **Diameter**: $D$
- **Circumference**: $C = \pi D$
- **Spacing between turns**: $S$
- **Total number of turns**: $N$
- **Pitch angle** $\alpha$ (angle between the helix turn and the ground plane):

$$
\alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{S}{\pi D}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{S}{C}\right)
$$

- **Total axial length**: $A = N \cdot S$
- **Total wire length**: $L_w = N \cdot L_t$, where $L_t = \sqrt{C^2 + S^2}$ is one turn length.

### 2.4.2 Axial Mode Design Conditions (Kraus Conditions)

For **axial-mode (end-fire) circular polarization** with enhanced directivity:

$$
\frac{3}{4}\lambda \le C \le \frac{4}{3}\lambda, \qquad S \approx 0.15\lambda, \qquad N \ge 4
$$

### 2.4.3 Key Helical Antenna Performance Formulas (Kraus)

$$
AR \approx \frac{2N+1}{2N} \quad (\text{Approximate Axial Ratio})
$$

$$
HPBW \approx \frac{52^\circ}{C/\lambda}\sqrt{\frac{N \cdot S}{\lambda}} \quad (\text{Half-Power Beamwidth})
$$

$$
G \approx 15\left(\frac{C}{\lambda}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{N \cdot S}{\lambda} \quad (\text{Approximate Gain — linear})
$$

In dB: $G_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\left[15 (C/\lambda)^2 (NS/\lambda)\right]$.

## 2.5 Yagi-Uda Antenna

A **Yagi-Uda array** consists of:
- **1 driven element** (typically a folded dipole of length ≈ $\lambda/2$).
- **1 reflector** (slightly longer, ≈ $0.5\lambda + 5\%$, placed behind).
- **N directors** (slightly shorter, ≈ $0.45\lambda$, placed in front).

It is a **linear end-fire array with parasitic elements** — only the driven element is fed.

**Key Characteristics:**
- Directivity: typically **5–15 dB**.
- Front-to-back ratio (FBR): **15–30 dB**.
- Bandwidth: **5–10%** (modest, hence "quasi-broadband").
- Feed impedance: depends on spacing; usually ~**50–70 Ω** with folded dipole.

## 2.6 Log-Periodic Dipole Antenna (LPDA)

The LPDA is a **true broadband (frequency-independent)** antenna whose geometric properties repeat periodically as the **logarithm** of frequency. The geometry is defined by two parameters:

### 2.6.1 Defining Ratios

$$
\tau = \frac{L_{n+1}}{L_n} = \frac{d_{n+1}}{d_n} \quad (\text{Geometric ratio, } 0.7 \le \tau \le 0.97)
$$

$$
\sigma = \frac{d_n}{2L_n} \quad (\text{Spacing ratio, typically } \sigma \approx 0.16)
$$

where $L_n$ is the length of the $n$-th dipole and $d_n$ is the spacing from the apex.

### 2.6.2 Active Region

Only a small group of dipoles (3–5) near those of length $\approx \lambda/2$ are **active** at any given frequency. As frequency changes, the active region **shifts** along the array — this is the **log-periodic shifting principle**.

**Bandwidth:**

$$
B_{ratio} = \frac{f_{max}}{f_{min}} = \frac{L_1}{L_N} = \frac{1}{\tau^{N-1}}
$$

## 2.7 Spiral Antennas (Frequency-Independent)

Spiral antennas (Archimedean, equiangular) are defined **purely by angles** (Rumsey's principle), so their shape is identical at every frequency → frequency-independent.

## 2.8 Biconical and Discone Antennas

- **Biconical antenna**: a dipole generalized into two cones; offers a **broad input impedance bandwidth** because the characteristic impedance $Z_0 \approx 120 \ln(\cot(\theta_0/2))$ is independent of frequency.
- **Discone**: a biconical antenna with one cone replaced by a disc; **very broadband** (10:1 or more) — widely used for VHF/UHF monitoring.

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Long-wire tilt angle | $\theta_0 = \cos^{-1}(1 - 0.371\lambda/L)$ | Radians / degrees |
| 2 | Long-wire directivity | $D \approx 4.28(L/\lambda)$ | Linear |
| 3 | Long-wire gain (dB) | $G_{dB} \approx 10\log_{10}(4.28\,L/\lambda)$ | dB |
| 4 | V-antenna apex angle | $\phi_{opt} = 2\theta_0$ | Degrees |
| 5 | Rhombic gain boost | $D_{rhombic} \approx 1.84 D_{long\,wire}$ | Linear |
| 6 | Helix circumference | $C = \pi D$ | meters |
| 7 | Helix pitch angle | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(S/\pi D)$ | Degrees |
| 8 | Helix AR | $AR \approx (2N+1)/(2N)$ | Ratio |
| 9 | Helix HPBW | $52^\circ (C/\lambda)^{-1} \sqrt{NS/\lambda}$ | Degrees |
| 10 | Helix Gain | $G \approx 15(C/\lambda)^2 (NS/\lambda)$ | Linear |
| 11 | LPDA geometric ratio | $\tau = L_{n+1}/L_n$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | LPDA spacing ratio | $\sigma = d_n/(2L_n)$ | Dimensionless |
| 13 | LPDA bandwidth | $B = f_{max}/f_{min} = \tau^{-(N-1)}$ | Ratio |
| 14 | Biconical Z₀ | $Z_0 \approx 120\ln[\cot(\theta_0/2)]$ | Ohms |
| 15 | Yagi 3-dB rule (director) | $L_{dir} \approx 0.45\lambda$ | meters |

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these antennas live in the real world:**
> - **Helical**: Satellite communications (TT&C), GPS reference antennas, deep-space links (circular polarization matches satellite signals).
> - **LPDA**: TV reception (UHF/VHF), EMC/EMI test antennas, direction-finding.
> - **Yagi-Uda**: TV antennas on rooftops (the famous "fishbone" antenna), amateur radio.
> - **Rhombic**: Military long-range HF communication, ionospheric skip links.
> - **Discone**: Wideband scanners, spectrum monitoring, military HF/VHF.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Field of a Long-Wire Traveling Wave Antenna

We start with a straight wire of length $L$ oriented along the z-axis, carrying a pure traveling wave current:

$$
I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-j\beta z} \quad \text{where} \quad \beta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}
$$

The far-zone electric field of an elemental Hertzian dipole of length $dz$ at a distance $r'$ from the observation point is:

$$
dE_\theta = j \, \frac{\eta \, \beta \, I(z)\, dz}{4\pi r'} \sin\theta \, e^{-j\beta r'}
$$

For the far-field, we make the standard Fraunhofer approximation:
- $r' \approx r$ in the amplitude term (denominator).
- $r' \approx r - z\cos\theta$ in the phase term.

So the phase factor becomes $e^{-j\beta(r - z\cos\theta)} = e^{-j\beta r} e^{+j\beta z \cos\theta}$.

The total far-field is therefore the integral over the wire length:

$$
E_\theta(\theta) = j \, \frac{\eta \, \beta \, I_0 \, \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-j\beta r} \int_0^{L} e^{-j\beta z} \cdot e^{+j\beta z \cos\theta} \, dz
$$

Combining the exponentials inside the integral:

$$
E_\theta(\theta) = j \, \frac{\eta \, \beta \, I_0 \, \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{-j\beta r} \int_0^{L} e^{-j\beta z(1 - \cos\theta)} \, dz
$$

Now evaluate the integral $\int_0^{L} e^{-j\beta(1-\cos\theta)z} dz$:

$$
\int_0^{L} e^{-jk z} dz = \frac{1 - e^{-j k L}}{jk} = e^{-jkL/2} \cdot \frac{2\sin(kL/2)}{k}
$$

where $k = \beta(1 - \cos\theta)$. So:

$$
\int_0^{L} e^{-j\beta(1-\cos\theta)z} dz = e^{-j\beta L(1-\cos\theta)/2} \cdot \frac{2\sin\left[\frac{\beta L}{2}(1 - \cos\theta)\right]}{\beta(1-\cos\theta)}
$$

Substituting back, taking magnitudes (the exponential factor contributes only phase):

$$
\boxed{\;\left|E_\theta(\theta)\right| = \frac{\eta \, I_0}{2\pi r} \cdot \left\vert \frac{\sin\theta}{1-\cos\theta} \right\vert \cdot \left\vert \sin\left[\frac{\beta L}{2}(1-\cos\theta)\right] \right\vert\;}
$$

**Maximum occurs** when $\sin[u] = 1$, i.e., $u = (2m+1)\pi/2$. The first maximum ($m=0$):

$$
\frac{\beta L}{2}(1 - \cos\theta_0) = \frac{\pi}{2} \;\Rightarrow\; 1 - \cos\theta_0 = \frac{\pi}{\beta L} = \frac{\lambda}{2L}
$$

$$
\cos\theta_0 = 1 - \frac{\lambda}{2L}
$$

For **large $L/\lambda$**, the 0.371 factor (the first sidelobe level of $\sin(u)/u$) is used to define the main lobe boundary, yielding the **Kraus tilt angle formula**:

$$
\theta_0 \approx \cos^{-1}\left(1 - \frac{0.371\lambda}{L}\right)
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Directivity of a Long-Wire Antenna (Balanis)

The directivity is defined as:

$$
D_0 = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A}, \quad \text{where } \Omega_A = \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi} \vert F(\theta,\phi)\vert^2 \sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi
$$

For the long wire (assuming pattern is independent of $\phi$ due to symmetry around z-axis):

$$
\Omega_A = 2\pi \int_0^{\pi} \left[\frac{\sin\theta}{1-\cos\theta}\right]^2 \sin^2\left[\frac{\beta L}{2}(1-\cos\theta)\right] d\theta
$$

A simpler approximate engineering result (Balanis, eq. 8-67) valid for $L \gg \lambda$:

$$
D_0 \approx 10\log_{10}\left(4.28 \frac{L}{\lambda}\right) \;\; \text{dB}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Helical Antenna Gain (Kraus)

For an N-turn axial-mode helix, the directivity equals the gain (matched circularly polarized):

$$
D \approx 12 \left(\frac{C}{\lambda}\right)^2 \frac{N \cdot S}{\lambda}
$$

A refined form (Kraus, *Antennas*, 3rd ed.) is:

$$
G \approx 15 \left(\frac{C}{\lambda}\right)^2 \frac{N \cdot S}{\lambda}
$$

This comes from the empirical fit to many measured helices, where the half-power beamwidth (HPBW) in degrees is:

$$
HPBW \approx \frac{52^\circ}{C/\lambda}\sqrt{\frac{N \cdot S}{\lambda}}
$$

For a circularly polarized beam, the gain-directivity-beamwidth product yields the factor 15.

## 3.4 Derivation: LPDA Bandwidth

Given $N$ dipoles with geometric ratio $\tau$, the longest dipole is $L_1$ and the shortest is $L_N$:

$$
L_N = L_1 \cdot \tau^{N-1}
$$

Since each dipole resonates near $L_n \approx \lambda_n/2$, the operating frequency for dipole $n$ is $f_n = c/(2L_n)$. So:

$$
\frac{f_{max}}{f_{min}} = \frac{f_1}{f_N} = \frac{1/(2L_1)}{1/(2L_N)} = \frac{L_N}{L_1} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = \tau^{N-1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;B_{ratio} = \frac{f_{max}}{f_{min}} = \frac{1}{\tau^{N-1}} = \frac{L_1}{L_N}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Shortcut:** For an LPDA with $\tau = 0.9$ and $N = 10$: $B = (0.9)^{-9} = 1/0.387 = 2.58:1$. To get 10:1, we need $N = \log(\tau)/\log(1/B) = \log(0.9)/\log(0.1) \approx 21.85$ → **22 dipoles**.

## 3.5 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem:** Design a 10-turn axial-mode helical antenna operating at **1.575 GHz** (GPS L1 band). Required: $C = \lambda$, pitch angle $\alpha = 12°$.

**Step 1 — Wavelength:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.575 \times 10^9} = 0.1905\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Circumference and Diameter:**

$$
C = \lambda = 0.1905\ \text{m} \;\Rightarrow\; D = \frac{C}{\pi} = 0.0606\ \text{m} = 6.06\ \text{cm}
$$

**Step 3 — Turn Spacing from pitch angle:**

$$
\alpha = 12° = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{S}{C}\right) \;\Rightarrow\; S = C \tan(12°) = 0.1905 \times 0.2126 = 0.0405\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Axial Length:**

$$
A = N \cdot S = 10 \times 0.0405 = 0.405\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Gain:**

$$
G = 15 \left(\frac{C}{\lambda}\right)^2 \frac{NS}{\lambda} = 15(1)^2 \cdot \frac{10 \times 0.0405}{0.1905} = 15 \times 2.126 = 31.9
$$

$$
G_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(31.9) \approx 15.04\ \text{dBi}
$$

**Step 6 — HPBW:**

$$
HPBW = \frac{52°}{1} \sqrt{\frac{10 \times 0.0405}{0.1905}} = 52° \times \sqrt{2.126} = 52° \times 1.458 = 75.8°
$$

**Step 7 — Axial Ratio:**

$$
AR = \frac{2(10)+1}{2(10)} = \frac{21}{20} = 1.05 \;\; (\text{Excellent CP purity})
$$

**Final Design Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Diameter D | 6.06 cm |
| Spacing S | 4.05 cm |
| Axial length A | 40.5 cm |
| Gain | 15.04 dBi |
| HPBW | 75.8° |
| Axial Ratio | 1.05 |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define traveling wave antenna. Why is it called non-resonant?
**Model Answer:** A traveling wave antenna is a long wire antenna in which a progressive wave of current travels from the feed point to the terminated end without reflection. It is called non-resonant because the matched termination absorbs the residual energy, preventing the formation of standing waves. Hence the input impedance is purely resistive and remains nearly constant over a wide frequency range, making it broadband.

> **Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set-A]`

### Question 2: State Rumsey's principle of frequency-independent antennas with two examples.
**Model Answer:** Rumsey's principle states that if the geometry of an antenna is specified entirely by angles (rather than absolute lengths), its radiation characteristics (impedance, pattern, polarization) become independent of frequency. Two examples are: (i) the **equiangular (logarithmic) spiral antenna**, and (ii) the **log-periodic dipole antenna (LPDA)**, which uses a periodic logarithmic scaling.

> **Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
