## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
**Define antenna bandwidth. List any three antenna parameters that are used to specify it.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The antenna bandwidth is defined as the range of frequencies over which the antenna's performance characteristics (such as input impedance, radiation pattern, gain, and polarization) remain within an acceptable tolerance of those specified for the application.

The three principal parameters used to specify bandwidth are:
1. **Impedance Bandwidth** — typically specified as $VSWR \leq 2:1$ (equivalently, return loss $\geq 9.54$ dB).
2. **Pattern Bandwidth** — main beamwidth and side-lobe level remain stable.
3. **Polarization Bandwidth** — axial ratio (for circularly polarized antennas) stays within a defined bound, typically $AR \leq 3$ dB.

The effective usable bandwidth is the **intersection** of all sub-bandwidths. *(3 Marks)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**What is meant by percentage bandwidth? Compute the percentage bandwidth of an antenna operating from 1.8 GHz to 2.4 GHz.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Percentage Bandwidth (PBW) is the ratio of the absolute bandwidth to the centre (arithmetic-mean) frequency, expressed as a percentage:

$$PBW = \frac{2(f_H - f_L)}{f_H + f_L}\times 100$$

**Computation:**
$$PBW = \frac{2(2.4 - 1.8)}{2.4 + 1.8}\times 100 = \frac{2(0.6)}{4.2}\times 100 = 28.57\%$$

The antenna is therefore **wideband** (since $PBW > 20\%$) by KTU convention. *(3 Marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module-2 Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question in Module-2 carries **14 marks**, internally divided into (a) **7 marks** + (b) **7 marks**. Full step-by-step working is mandatory; final numerical answer alone fetches at most 2 marks.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)**

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **impedance bandwidth** of an antenna with a neat sketch. Derive the relation between the **quality factor (Q)** and the **3-dB bandwidth** for a series RLC antenna equivalent circuit. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A half-wave dipole operating at $f_0 = 300$ MHz has a quality factor $Q = 12$. For a feed-line characteristic impedance of $50\,\Omega$ and the antenna's resonant resistance $R_{rad} = 73\,\Omega$, determine:
1. The 3-dB bandwidth in MHz.
2. The percentage bandwidth.
3. The lower and upper frequency edges of the impedance bandwidth.
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

**Impedance Bandwidth (sketch explanation):** The impedance bandwidth is the frequency range over which the antenna's input impedance, when terminated in the system's characteristic impedance $Z_0$, presents a reflection coefficient whose magnitude satisfies $\vert \Gamma \vert \leq \Gamma_m$ (typically $1/3$, i.e., VSWR $\leq 2$).

A representative plot of $\vert S_{11} \vert$ versus frequency shows a resonance dip at $f_0$ with $\vert S_{11}\vert_{\min} \to -\infty$ for an ideal lossless antenna. The two frequencies $f_L$ and $f_H$ at which $\vert S_{11} \vert$ crosses the $-9.54$ dB line define the **impedance bandwidth** $B = f_H - f_L$.

**Derivation (Q vs. 3-dB bandwidth):**

The input impedance of a series RLC circuit near its resonant angular frequency $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ is:

$$Z(\omega) = R + j\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$

A first-order Taylor expansion of the reactive term around $\omega_0$ gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C} &\approx 2 L (\omega - \omega_0) \\
&= 2L\,\Delta\omega
\end{aligned}
$$

The 3-dB (half-power) bandwidth is defined by the condition $\vert Z \vert^2 = 2R^2$, which implies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(2L\,\Delta\omega)^2 &= R^2 \\
\Delta\omega &= \frac{R}{2L}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the series-RLC quality factor $Q = \omega_0 L / R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\omega_{3dB} &= \frac{\omega_0}{2Q} \\
B_{3dB} = 2\Delta\omega_{3dB} &= \frac{\omega_0}{Q} = \frac{f_0}{Q}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{f_0}{B_{3dB}}}$$

This shows that **Q is inversely proportional to bandwidth**. A high-Q antenna is highly resonant and narrowband; a low-Q antenna is broadband. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $f_0 = 300$ MHz, $Q = 12$, $Z_0 = 50\,\Omega$, $R_{rad} = 73\,\Omega$.

**1) 3-dB bandwidth:**
$$B = \frac{f_0}{Q} = \frac{300}{12} = 25 \text{ MHz}$$

**2) Percentage bandwidth:**
$$PBW = \frac{B}{f_0}\times 100 = \frac{25}{300}\times 100 = 8.33\%$$

**3) Frequency edges:**
$$f_L = f_0 - B/2 = 300 - 12.5 = 287.5 \text{ MHz}$$
$$f_H = f_0 + B/2 = 300 + 12.5 = 312.5 \text{ MHz}$$

**Sanity check (VSWR at edges):**
$$\Gamma = \frac{R_{rad} - Z_0}{R_{rad} + Z_0} = \frac{73 - 50}{73 + 50} = \frac{23}{123} = 0.187$$
$$RL = -20\log_{10}(0.187) = 14.6 \text{ dB} \quad (\text{well below the VSWR} = 2 \text{ threshold of } 9.54 \text{ dB})$$

**Note:** The actual VSWR = 2 bandwidth edges are *closer to* $f_0$ than the 3-dB-RL-point edges; the Q-based estimate (25 MHz) is the **3-dB RL bandwidth**, which is *narrower* than the VSWR = 2 bandwidth in this case. A more accurate VSWR = 2 bandwidth would use the more general impedance-circle model with $X(f)$. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Define **percentage bandwidth** and **fractional bandwidth**. Classify antennas into narrowband, wideband, and ultra-wideband based on percentage bandwidth. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With reference to the **Bode–Fano criterion** for a lossless matching network feeding a **parallel RC load**, derive an expression for the maximum achievable fractional bandwidth. An electrically small antenna has $Q = 50$ and the design allows a maximum tolerable reflection coefficient $\Gamma_m = 0.2$. Calculate the **maximum fractional bandwidth**. Comment on the implications. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

**Fractional Bandwidth (FBW):**
$$FBW = \frac{f_H - f_L}{f_0}$$

**Percentage Bandwidth (PBW):**
$$PBW = \frac{2(f_H - f_L)}{f_H + f_L}\times 100$$

**Classification (per IEEE Std 145 / KTU 2024 syllabus):**

| Class | PBW Range | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Narrowband** | 0 % – 1 % | Microstrip patch, short dipole, helical in normal mode |
| **Moderate Bandwidth** | 1 % – 20 % | Biconical, sleeve dipole, discone |
| **Wideband** | 20 % – 50 % | Log-periodic, ridged horn, Vivaldi |
| **Ultra-Wideband (UWB)** | > 50 % | Spiral, LPDA, log-spiral, Vivaldi with proper feed |

Note: Some texts use FBW = 0.20 as the UWB boundary (FCC regulatory definition). *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (b):**

The Bode–Fano inequality for a lossless matching network feeding a **parallel RC load** is:

$$\Gamma_m \ln\left(\frac{1}{\Gamma_m}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{Q} \geq \frac{\pi}{2}\cdot\frac{B}{\omega_0} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot FBW$$

Rearranging for the **maximum** fractional bandwidth:

$$FBW_{\max} = \frac{2\Gamma_m}{Q\,\ln(1/\Gamma_m)}$$

**Given:** $Q = 50$, $\Gamma_m = 0.2$.

**Step 1.** Compute $\ln(1/\Gamma_m)$:
$$\ln\left(\frac{1}{0.2}\right) = \ln(5) = 1.6094$$

**Step 2.** Compute numerator:
$$2\Gamma_m = 0.4$$

**Step 3.** Compute denominator:
$$Q \cdot \ln(1/\Gamma_m) = 50 \times 1.6094 = 80.47$$

**Step 4.** Compute $FBW_{\max}$:
$$FBW_{\max} = \frac{0.4}{80.47} = 0.004971 \approx 0.497\%$$

$$\boxed{FBW_{\max} \approx 0.5\% \text{ (i.e., } PBW \approx 0.5\% \text{)}}$$

**Comment on implications:**

- The achievable bandwidth is **inversely proportional to Q**. For an electrically small antenna ($Q = 50$), the impedance-matched bandwidth is less than **0.5%**, which classifies the antenna as **narrowband** even by strict standards.
- The result confirms the **Bode–Fano fundamental limit**: there is no lossless matching network that can deliver more than ~0.5% FBW for $\Gamma_m = 0.2$ and $Q = 50$.
- Practical ways to *appear* to increase bandwidth include:
  1. **Allowing higher $\Gamma_m$** (looser VSWR spec) — $\Gamma_m = 0.5$ raises FBW to ~1.8%.
  2. **Tunable / reconfigurable matching** (e.g., varactor-tuned networks) that re-positions the matching band over time — the *instantaneous* bandwidth stays narrow, but the *aggregate* bandwidth is wide.
  3. **Accepting mismatch loss and adding a low-noise amplifier (LNA)** to recover signal-to-noise ratio.
  4. **Multi-mode / multi-resonator antennas** that create multiple adjacent resonances, "stitching" a wider effective band.

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks (Q5b):**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the FBW formula — it is $2\Gamma_m / [Q\ln(1/\Gamma_m)]$, not $\Gamma_m / [Q\ln(1/\Gamma_m)]$.
> 2. **Using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\ln$** in the Bode–Fano formula — the formula is in **natural logarithm**.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert FBW to PBW** at the end by multiplying by 100 (PBW = FBW × 100).
> 4. **Not commenting** on the practical engineering implications — KTU examiners specifically test *engineering judgement*, not just arithmetic. *(7 Marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Generic Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Bandwidth Questions (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
>
> 1. **"Bandwidth" without a parameter specifier** — Full credit only if you write "impedance bandwidth for VSWR ≤ 2:1," "3-dB gain bandwidth," etc. Generic "bandwidth" is **2 marks** out of 7 max.
> 2. **Confusing FBW and PBW** — A common slip. FBW is dimensionless; PBW is FBW × 100. Several past KTU toppers have lost 1 mark on this alone.
> 3. **Forgetting the impedance mismatch effect** — When the antenna's resonant resistance ($73\,\Omega$ for a half-wave dipole) is not exactly $Z_0$ ($50\,\Omega$), there is a **residual reflection** even at $f_0$. This is *not* a mismatch at the band edge — it's a design constraint.
> 4. **Using Bode–Fano for non-canonical loads** — Bode–Fano is exact only for **series R, parallel RC, series RC, parallel RL** canonical loads. For a real antenna, it is a *bound*, not an equality.
> 5. **Stating the Chu limit without stating the "lossless matching" assumption** — In practice, ohmic losses in the matching network further *reduce* the achievable bandwidth by ~20–30%.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Bandwidth** = the **intersection** of impedance, pattern, gain, and polarization sub-bandwidths. Never quote "bandwidth" without specifying the parameter.
- [x] **Absolute bandwidth:** $B = f_H - f_L$ (in Hz).
- [x] **Fractional bandwidth (FBW):** $B/f_0$ (dimensionless).
- [x] **Percentage bandwidth (PBW):** $2(f_H - f_L)/(f_H + f_L) \times 100$ (in %).
- [x] **Quality factor:** $Q = f_0/B_{3dB}$. **High Q = narrowband.**
- [x] **VSWR ≤ 2 ↔ $\vert\Gamma\vert \leq 1/3$ ↔ Return Loss ≥ 9.54 dB** — the most common KTU benchmark.
- [x] **Bode–Fano bound (parallel RC):** $FBW_{\max} = 2\Gamma_m / [Q \ln(1/\Gamma_m)]$.
- [x] **Chu limit:** $Q_{\min} = 1/(ka)^3 + 1/(ka)$ — sets the **fundamental** lower bound on Q for an antenna inside a sphere of radius $a$.
- [x] **Classification:** Narrowband (PBW < 1%) → Moderate (1–20%) → Wideband (20–50%) → UWB (> 50%).
- [x] **FCC UWB:** FBW > 0.20 OR absolute BW > 500 MHz.
- [x] **Microstrip patch** is **narrowband** (1–5% PBW); **LPDA** is **multi-octave** (PBW > 100%).
- [x] **Radar range resolution:** $\delta R = c / (2B)$ — halving the bandwidth **doubles** the range cell.
- [x] **Beamwidth × Bandwidth product** is a fundamental tradeoff (Chu limit).
- [x] **Series RLC derivation:** remember the Taylor expansion $\omega L - 1/(\omega C) \approx 2L\,\Delta\omega$ near resonance.
- [x] **Common exam numbers to memorize:** $VSWR=2 \to \vert\Gamma\vert = 1/3$; $VSWR=1.5 \to \vert\Gamma\vert = 0.2$; $RL=10\,\text{dB} \to VSWR=1.92$.
- [x] **Always** state the matching-network loss assumption (lossless vs. lossy) when applying Bode–Fano.
- [x] **Always** specify whether the bandwidth is *impedance*, *pattern*, *gain*, or *polarization* bandwidth.

---

### 5.5 KTU-2024 Outcome Mapping (For Examiner Reference)

| Course Outcome (CO) | Bloom's Level | Module-2 Topic Coverage | Question Bank Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CO2** — Analyze antenna parameters (directivity, gain, bandwidth, polarization) | **L4 (Analyze)** | Bandwidth definitions, Q, Bode–Fano | Q1, Q2 (Part A); Q5 (Part B) |
| **CO3** — Design matching and feeding structures for specified bandwidth | **L5 (Evaluate/Design)** | Impedance bandwidth, RLC model, LPDA sizing | Q5 (Part B — sub-parts a, b) |
| **CO4** — Apply Chu limit and Bode–Fano to practical antenna selection | **L6 (Create/Justify)** | ESA trade-offs, broadband architectures | Q5b commentary |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Study Tip (Module-2 centric):** Bandwidth questions almost always combine **formula derivation** + **numerical computation** + **one paragraph of engineering commentary**. Memorize the Q-vs-bandwidth derivation cold, and always end with a one-line implication: "*This makes the antenna suitable for X application*." Examiners reward the engineering connect.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Efficiency ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In antenna engineering, **Efficiency** is a fundamental figure-of-merit that quantifies how effectively an antenna converts the available input power into useful radiated electromagnetic energy. Because no practical antenna is a perfect transducer, a portion of the accepted power is invariably dissipated as heat within the antenna structure (ohmic/dielectric losses) and another portion may be reflected back to the source due to impedance mismatch. The concept of efficiency therefore acts as the bridge between the **circuit-level** behavior (feed-point impedance, matching) and the **field-level** behavior (radiation into space) of the antenna. The KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes antenna efficiency into two principal, hierarchical metrics: 1. **Radiation Efficiency ($e_r$ or $\eta_r$):** The ratio of power radiated into free space to the total power accepted by the antenna terminals. It accounts only for **ohmic and dielectric losses** within the antenna itself. 2. **Total Antenna Efficiency ($e_0$ or $\eta_0$):** The product of radiation efficiency and reflection (mismatch) efficiency. It represents the overall effectiveness of the antenna from the source's perspective. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme explicitly distinguishes between *Radiation Efficiency*, *Total Efficiency*, and *Reflection Efficiency*. Students must not use these terms interchangeably in board examinations. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Leaky Water Hose Imagine you are watering a garden with a long rubber hose connected to a tap (the transmitter). Three distinct "losses" can occur: - **Conduction/Dielectric Loss ($\eta_r$ part):** The hose itself is old and porous — some water seeps out through the walls before reaching the nozzle. The water that finally sprays out is less than what entered the hose. This is the **radiation efficiency** — power lost inside the antenna structure ($P_{loss}$) versus what actually radiates out ($P_r$). - **Reflection Loss (mismatch):** The nozzle is too narrow, and the tap pressure is too high. Water bounces back toward the tap, never leaving the hose. This is the **reflection efficiency** ($1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2$) — the power reflected back to the transmitter due to feed-point impedance mismatch. - **Total Efficiency ($\eta_0$):** The amount of water that *actually* lands on the garden compared to the amount the tap tried to deliver. > [!NOTE] > **The Governing Insight:** A perfectly efficient antenna ($e_0 = 1$, or **100 %**) would have **zero ohmic loss** ($\eta_r = 1$) AND **zero reflection loss** ($\vert \Gamma \vert = 0$). In practice, engineers optimize both simultaneously using low-loss conductors, high-Q dielectrics, and matching networks (e.g., stub tuners, baluns). ### 1.3 The Three Efficiencies — Quick Taxonomy | Efficiency Type | Symbol | Accounts For | Range | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Radiation Efficiency | $e_r$ | Ohmic + Dielectric losses | $0 \le e_r \le 1$ | | Reflection Efficiency | $e_{ref}$ or $1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2$ | Feed-point mismatch (VSWR) | $0 \le e_{ref} \le 1$ | | Total Efficiency | $e_0$ | Both loss and mismatch | $0 \le e_0 \le 1$ | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric:** In dB form, $\eta_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(\eta)$. An efficiency of **0.5** corresponds to **−3.0103 dB**, a frequently cited benchmark in KTU board problems. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Decomposition — The Power Flow Logic The journey of electromagnetic power from the source to free space can be described as a four-step cascade: 1. **Power Available from Source ($P_{avs}$):** The maximum power the source can deliver to a perfectly matched load (Conjugate Matching Theorem). 2. **Power Accepted by Antenna ($P_{in}$):** The power that actually enters the antenna feed terminals, reduced by the amount reflected back. Mathematically, $P_{in} = P_{avs} \cdot (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2)$. 3. **Power Dissipated as Loss ($P_{loss}$):** The power converted to heat in the antenna's finite-conductivity conductors and lossy dielectric substrates. $P_{loss} = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert I_0 \vert^2 R_L$, where $R_L$ is the loss resistance. 4. **Power Radiated ($P_{rad}$):** The power that successfully escapes into space as a propagating electromagnetic wave. $P_{rad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \vert I_0 \vert^2 R_{rad}$, where $R_{rad}$ is the radiation resistance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" Behind the Formula:** Notice that $P_{rad}$ and $P_{loss}$ share the same current dependence ($\vert I_0 \vert^2$). This is why the efficiency ratio simplifies to a ratio of resistances — the current terms cancel algebraically. This is a **favorite KTU board question** (asked for 7 marks in 2019 and 2022). ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Mastery Mandate:** The following table consolidates every efficiency-related equation assessable under the KTU 2024 PEECT868 syllabus. Memorize units and Greek symbols — they are checked explicitly in Part A (3-mark) questions. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Radiation Efficiency** | $e_r = \dfrac{P_{rad}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{R_{rad}}{R_{rad} + R_{L}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le e_r \le 1$ | | 2 | **Reflection Efficiency** | $e_{ref} = 1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2$ | Dimensionless, depends on mismatch | | 3 | **Total Efficiency** | $e_0 = e_r \cdot e_{ref} = e_r (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2)$ | Dimensionless, overall figure of merit | | 4 | **Reflection Coefficient (Voltage)** | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_{in} - Z_0}{Z_{in} + Z_0}$ | Complex, $\vert \Gamma \vert^2$ is return loss factor | | 5 | **Return Loss** | $RL = -10 \log_{10}(\vert \Gamma \vert^2)$ | dB; higher is better | | 6 | **VSWR in terms of $\Gamma$** | $\rho = \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$ | Dimensionless ratio, $\rho \ge 1$ | | 7 | **$\vert \Gamma \vert$ in terms of VSWR** | $\vert \Gamma \vert = \dfrac{\rho - 1}{\rho + 1}$ | Used when VSWR is given | | 8 | **Reflection Efficiency in VSWR** | $e_{ref} = \dfrac{4\rho}{(\rho + 1)^2}$ | Direct VSWR-to-efficiency conversion | | 9 | **Loss Resistance Term** | $R_L = R_{cond} + R_{dielectric}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | | 10 | **Efficiency in dB** | $\eta_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(\eta)$ | Negative dB indicates loss | > **LaTeX Safety Note:** All absolute-value bars in the table above use `\vert` (e.g., `\vert \Gamma \vert`) to prevent markdown table-parser collisions. Do not write `|Gamma|` in any KTU answer script markup. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The efficiency concept is not a theoretical luxury — it dictates **practical engineering trade-offs** in: - **Satellite Payloads:** Even a 5 % efficiency drop on a 2 GHz downlink antenna can mean **megawatts** of wasted DC power over a satellite's 15-year lifespan, directly impacting thermal budgets. - **Mobile Handset Antennas:** With only ~1 W of input power, every fraction of a dB matters for battery life. Modern PIFA (Planar Inverted-F Antenna) designs target $e_0 > 50 \,\%$. - **RFID Tags:** Passive UHF tags harvest energy from the reader. A reflection-efficient (well-matched) tag antenna is critical for read-range. - **Phased Array Radars:** Thousands of T/R modules mean the cumulative efficiency loss multiplies — a 0.5 dB loss per element can collapse system SNR by tens of dB. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Industrial Context Question (Frequently Asked):** "Why is the efficiency of a small loop antenna typically much lower than that of a half-wave dipole at the same frequency?" — *Model Answer:* Small loops have very low radiation resistance $R_{rad}$ (often $< 1\,\Omega$). Since $e_r = R_{rad}/(R_{rad}+R_L)$ and $R_L$ (ohmic loss) does not scale down proportionally, the ratio is severely degraded. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Radiation Efficiency from Power Balance **Statement to Prove:** $$e_r = \frac{R_{rad}}{R_{rad} + R_L}$$ **Given:** - Input current to antenna: $I_0$ (RMS, at feed-point) - Radiation Resistance: $R_{rad}$ - Loss Resistance: $R_L$ **Step 1 — Express Radiated Power.** The power radiated into space is modeled as the dissipation in an equivalent resistance $R_{rad}$: $$P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2} \vert I_0 \vert^2 R_{rad}$$ **Step 2 — Express Power Lost as Heat.** Similarly, ohmic and dielectric losses are modeled as dissipation in $R_L$: $$P_{loss} = \frac{1}{2} \vert I_0 \vert^2 R_L$$ **Step 3 — Express Total Accepted Power.** By conservation of energy at the antenna terminals (ignoring mismatch for now): $$P_{in} = P_{rad} + P_{loss}$$ **Step 4 — Form the Efficiency Ratio.** $$e_r = \frac{P_{rad}}{P_{in}} = \frac{P_{rad}}{P_{rad} + P_{loss}}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute and Cancel.** $$e_r = \frac{\frac{1}{2} \vert I_0 \vert^2 R_{rad}}{\frac{1}{2} \vert I_0 \vert^2 R_{rad} + \frac{1}{2} \vert I_0 \vert^2 R_L}$$ The factor $\frac{1}{2} \vert I_0 \vert^2$ appears in every term and cancels: $$e_r = \frac{R_{rad}}{R_{rad} + R_L} \quad \blacksquare$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This derivation proves that efficiency is independent of the input current amplitude — it is an *intrinsic* property of the antenna geometry and material. Doubling the input power does not change $e_r$. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Efficiency as Product of Two Terms **Statement to Prove:** $$e_0 = e_r \cdot (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2)$$ **Given:** - Reflection coefficient at the feed-point: $\Gamma$ - Antenna radiation efficiency: $e_r$ **Step 1 — Power Available from Source.** For a source with internal impedance matched to the transmission line ($Z_s = Z_0$), the power the source *intends* to deliver is $P_{avs}$. **Step 2 — Power Accepted by the Antenna.** When the antenna presents an impedance $Z_{in} \neq Z_0$, a portion is reflected. The accepted power is: $$P_{in} = P_{avs} \cdot (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2)$$ This is the definition of **reflection efficiency**: $$e_{ref} = 1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2$$ **Step 3 — Power Radiated.** From the accepted power, a fraction $e_r$ is radiated and the rest is lost internally: $$P_{rad} = P_{in} \cdot e_r = P_{avs} \cdot (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2) \cdot e_r$$ **Step 4 — Define Total Efficiency.** Total efficiency compares radiated power to source-available power: $$e_0 = \frac{P_{rad}}{P_{avs}}$$ **Step 5 — Combine.** $$e_0 = e_r \cdot (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2) \quad \blacksquare$$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Reflection Efficiency in Terms of VSWR **Statement to Prove:** $$e_{ref} = \frac{4\rho}{(\rho + 1)^2}$$ **Step 1 — Start with VSWR Definition.** $$\rho = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $\vert \Gamma \vert$.** Multiply both sides by $(1 - \vert \Gamma \vert)$: $$\rho (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert) = 1 + \vert \Gamma \vert$$ $$\rho - \rho \vert \Gamma \vert = 1 + \vert \Gamma \vert$$ Collect $\vert \Gamma \vert$ terms: $$\rho - 1 = \vert \Gamma \vert (\rho + 1)$$ $$\vert \Gamma \vert = \frac{\rho - 1}{\rho + 1}$$ **Step 3 — Square $\vert \Gamma \vert$.** $$\vert \Gamma \vert^2 = \left(\frac{\rho - 1}{\rho + 1}\right)^2 = \frac{(\rho - 1)^2}{(\rho + 1)^2}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into Reflection Efficiency.** $$e_{ref} = 1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2 = 1 - \frac{(\rho - 1)^2}{(\rho + 1)^2}$$ **Step 5 — Combine Over Common Denominator.** $$e_{ref} = \frac{(\rho + 1)^2 - (\rho - 1)^2}{(\rho + 1)^2}$$ **Step 6 — Expand the Difference of Squares.** Recall: $(a+b)^2 - (a-b)^2 = 4ab$. Here $a = \rho$, $b = 1$: $$(\rho + 1)^2 - (\rho - 1)^2 = 4\rho$$ **Step 7 — Final Result.** $$e_{ref} = \frac{4\rho}{(\rho + 1)^2} \quad \blacksquare$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quick Sanity Check:** For a perfectly matched antenna, $\rho = 1$, giving $e_{ref} = 4/4 = 1$ ✓. For an open or short circuit, $\rho \to \infty$, giving $e_{ref} \to 0$ ✓. The formula is **monotonically decreasing** in $\rho$ — exactly the physical expectation. ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A half-wave dipole antenna at 100 MHz has $R_{rad} = 73\,\Omega$ and $R_L = 7\,\Omega$. It is fed by a source through a transmission line with VSWR = 1.5. Find (a) Radiation Efficiency, (b) Reflection Efficiency, (c) Total Efficiency, and (d) Total Efficiency in dB. **Part (a) — Radiation Efficiency:** $$e_r = \frac{73}{73 + 7} = \frac{73}{80} = 0.9125$$ **Part (b) — Reflection Efficiency:** $$e_{ref} = \frac{4 \times 1.5}{(1.5 + 1)^2} = \frac{6.0}{6.25} = 0.96$$ **Part (c) — Total Efficiency:** $$e_0 = 0.9125 \times 0.96 = 0.876$$ **Part (d) — In Decibels:** $$\eta_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(0.876) = 10 \times (-0.0574) = -0.574 \text{ dB}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Board Pattern:** This is a 14-mark KTU-style Part B question. Always present the final answer in **both linear and dB forms** — examiners explicitly award 2 marks for the dB conversion. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Power Flow Through an Antenna System The diagram below maps the **signal-flow architecture** of power from the source through the antenna into free space, explicitly showing the points at which each efficiency is computed. ```mermaid flowchart LR srcA[RF SOURCE
Pavs available]:::srcClass refA[REFLECTION POINT
Mismatch Loss]:::refClass accA[ACCEPTED POWER
Pin into antenna]:::accClass lossA[INTERNAL LOSS
Ohmic + Dielectric]:::lossClass radA[RADIATED POWER
Prad into space]:::radClass refE[REFLECTION EFFICIENCY
1 minus Gamma squared]:::effClass radE[RADIATION EFFICIENCY
Rrad over Rrad plus RL]:::effClass totE[TOTAL EFFICIENCY
e0 equals er times eref]:::totClass srcA --> refA refA --> accA accA --> lossA accA --> radA refA -.-> refE accA -.-> radE radA -.-> totE refE -.-> totE radE -.-> totE classDef srcClass fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1 classDef refClass fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#B71C1C classDef accClass fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F9A825,stroke-width:2px,color:#F57F17 classDef lossClass fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#4A148C classDef radClass fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20 classDef effClass fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F,stroke-width:2px,color:#263238 classDef totClass fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:3px,color:#880E4F ``` ### 4.2 Hierarchical Relationship Between Efficiencies ```mermaid flowchart TD A[AVAILABLE POWER FROM SOURCE Pavs]:::topClass B[REFLECTION EFFICIENCY eref]:::midClass C[ACCEPTED POWER Pin]:::midClass D[RADIATION EFFICIENCY er]:::midClass E[RADIATED POWER Prad]:::botClass F[TOTAL EFFICIENCY e0 equals er times eref]:::resultClass A -->|Multiply by eref| B B -->|Yields| C C -->|Multiply by er| D D -->|Yields| E A -.->|Direct Path| F C -.->|Contributes| F D -.->|Contributes| F classDef topClass fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1 classDef midClass fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20 classDef botClass fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#BF360C classDef resultClass fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:3px,color:#880E4F ``` ### 4.3 Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Efficiency Formula in Board Problems ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{What quantity is given?}:::questionClass Q2{Given Rrad and RL?}:::questionClass Q3{Given VSWR rho?}:::questionClass Q4{Given Return Loss in dB?}:::questionClass A1[Use er equals Rrad over Rrad plus RL]:::answerClass A2[Use eref equals 4 rho over rho plus 1 squared]:::answerClass A3[Convert RL to Gamma then use 1 minus Gamma squared]:::answerClass A4[Use e0 equals er times 1 minus Gamma squared]:::answerClass Q1 -->|Resistances| Q2 Q1 -->|VSWR value| Q3 Q1 -->|Return Loss dB| Q4 Q2 --> A1 Q3 --> A2 Q4 --> A3 A1 --> A4 A2 --> A4 A3 --> A4 classDef questionClass fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100 classDef answerClass fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Solid arrows represent the *flow* of power. Dotted arrows represent the *mathematical definition* of each efficiency metric. This dual representation is essential for KTU Part B derivations where students must show both the physical power split and the algebraic formulation. --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define the term *radiation efficiency* of an antenna. Why is it considered an intrinsic property independent of the input power level? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Radiation efficiency is defined as the ratio of power radiated by the antenna to the total power accepted at its input terminals, expressed as $e_r = P_{rad}/P_{in} = R_{rad}/(R_{rad}+R_L)$. It is intrinsic because the current-dependent terms ($\tfrac{1}{2}\vert I_0 \vert^2$) cancel out in the numerator and denominator — leaving a ratio dependent only on the antenna's geometric and material properties, not on the excitation amplitude. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Distinguish between *radiation efficiency* and *total antenna efficiency*. Under what condition do they become numerically equal? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Radiation efficiency ($e_r$) accounts only for ohmic and dielectric losses within the antenna. Total efficiency ($e_0 = e_r \cdot e_{ref}$) additionally accounts for impedance mismatch at the feed-point. They are numerically equal when there is no mismatch loss, i.e., when the reflection coefficient $\vert \Gamma \vert = 0$ (VSWR = 1:1), making $e_{ref} = 1$. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern) > **Examination Note:** The KTU 2024 Scheme mandates that every Part B question from a module offer an internal choice between two parallel sub-questions (e.g., "OR" pattern). Both alternatives are presented below for comprehensive preparation. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2023]* **(a)** Derive the expression for the **radiation efficiency** of an antenna in terms of its radiation resistance and loss resistance. Explain why the efficiency decreases as antenna size shrinks below $\lambda/50$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — State the Definition.** Radiation efficiency is the ratio of radiated power to accepted power: $$e_r = \frac{P_{rad}}{P_{in}} = \frac{P_{rad}}{P_{rad} + P_{loss}}$$ **Step 2 — Express Powers Using Equivalent Resistances.** The antenna is modeled as a current $I_0$ flowing through a series combination of $R_{rad}$ and $R_L$: $$P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2} \vert I_0 \vert^2 R_{rad}, \quad P_{loss} = \frac{1}{2} \vert I_0 \vert^2 R_L$$ **Step 3 — Form the Ratio and Cancel.** $$e_r = \frac{\frac{1}{2} \vert I_0 \vert^2 R_{rad}}{\frac{1}{2} \vert I_0 \vert^2 (R_{rad} + R_L)} = \frac{R_{rad}}{R_{rad} + R_L}$$ **Step 4 — Explain the Size Dependence.** For an electrically small antenna (e.g., a loop with circumference $\ll \lambda$), the radiation resistance scales as $R_{rad} \propto (L/\lambda)^4$ or $(A/\lambda^2)^2$, dropping rapidly toward fractions of an ohm. However, the loss resistance $R_L$ is governed by conductor skin depth and geometry — it does **not** shrink proportionally. As $R_{rad} \to 0$ while $R_L$ remains finite, the ratio $R_{rad}/(R_{rad}+R_L) \to 0$. Hence efficiency collapses. **(7 marks)** ✓ --- **(b)** An antenna with $R_{rad} = 36\,\Omega$ and $R_L = 4\,\Omega$ is connected to a feed line of characteristic impedance $50\,\Omega$ via a network presenting a feed-point impedance of $Z_{in} = 75 + j25\,\Omega$. Calculate the **total antenna efficiency** in linear and dB form. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute the Reflection Coefficient.** $$\Gamma = \frac{Z_{in} - Z_0}{Z_{in} + Z_0} = \frac{(75 + j25) - 50}{(75 + j25) + 50} = \frac{25 + j25}{125 + j25}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $\vert \Gamma \vert^2$.** Numerator magnitude: $\vert 25 + j25 \vert = \sqrt{25^2 + 25^2} = 25\sqrt{2}$ Denominator magnitude: $\vert 125 + j25 \vert = \sqrt{125^2 + 25^2} = 25\sqrt{26}$ $$\vert \Gamma \vert^2 = \frac{(25\sqrt{2})^2}{(25\sqrt{26})^2} = \frac{1250}{16250} = 0.0769$$ **Step 3 — Compute Reflection Efficiency.** $$e_{ref} = 1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2 = 1 - 0.0769 = 0.9231$$ **Step 4 — Compute Radiation Efficiency.** $$e_r = \frac{36}{36 + 4} = \frac{36}{40} = 0.9$$ **Step 5 — Compute Total Efficiency.** $$e_0 = 0.9 \times 0.9231 = 0.8308$$ **Step 6 — Convert to dB.** $$e_0 \, (\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(0.8308) = -0.804 \text{ dB}$$ **Final Answer:** $e_0 = 0.831$ or equivalently **−0.804 dB**. **(7 marks)** ✓ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]* **(a)** Starting from the definition of the **voltage standing wave ratio (VSWR)**, derive an expression for the **reflection efficiency** of an antenna in terms of VSWR. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define VSWR.** $$\rho = \frac{V_{max}}{V_{min}} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $\vert \Gamma \vert$.** Rearranging: $\rho (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert) = 1 + \vert \Gamma \vert$, giving: $$\vert \Gamma \vert = \frac{\rho - 1}{\rho + 1}$$ **Step 3 — Square.** $$\vert \Gamma \vert^2 = \frac{(\rho - 1)^2}{(\rho + 1)^2}$$ **Step 4 — Reflection Efficiency Definition.** $$e_{ref} = 1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2 = \frac{(\rho+1)^2 - (\rho-1)^2}{(\rho+1)^2}$$ **Step 5 — Difference of Squares Expansion.** $(\rho+1)^2 - (\rho-1)^2 = [(\rho+1)+(\rho-1)][(\rho+1)-(\rho-1)] = (2\rho)(2) = 4\rho$ **Step 6 — Final Result.** $$\boxed{e_{ref} = \frac{4\rho}{(\rho+1)^2}} \quad \blacksquare$$ **Verification:** $\rho = 1 \Rightarrow e_{ref} = 4/4 = 1$ (matched) ✓; $\rho \to \infty \Rightarrow e_{ref} \to 0$ (total reflection) ✓. **(7 marks)** --- **(b)** A microwave antenna operates at 2.4 GHz with measured VSWR of 1.8 and total efficiency of 0.7. The antenna's radiation resistance is $50\,\Omega$. Determine: (i) the reflection efficiency, (ii) the radiation efficiency, and (iii) the loss resistance $R_L$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Reflection Efficiency:** $$e_{ref} = \frac{4 \times 1.8}{(1.8 + 1)^2} = \frac{7.2}{7.84} = 0.9184$$ **(ii) Radiation Efficiency:** Given $e_0 = e_r \cdot e_{ref}$: $$e_r = \frac{e_0}{e_{ref}} = \frac{0.7}{0.9184} = 0.7622$$ **(iii) Loss Resistance:** From $e_r = R_{rad}/(R_{rad} + R_L)$: $$0.7622 = \frac{50}{50 + R_L}$$ $$50 + R_L = \frac{50}{0.7622} = 65.60$$ $$R_L = 65.60 - 50 = 15.60\,\Omega$$ **Final Answers:** $e_{ref} = 0.918$, $e_r = 0.762$, $R_L = 15.6\,\Omega$. **(7 marks)** ✓ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–3 Marks Each):** > 1. **Unit Confusion:** Forgetting to convert percentages (e.g., 87.6 %) to decimals (0.876) before log computation. Always check. > 2. **Misnaming Efficiencies:** Calling total efficiency "radiation efficiency" or vice versa. The 2024 scheme has separate CO mappings for each — terminology is graded. > 3. **Skipping the dB Step:** KTU mandates dB representation whenever efficiency is involved. Omitting it costs a full mark in the "presentation" component. > 4. **Arithmetic Error in Complex Reflection Coefficient:** Mistaking $\vert a + jb \vert^2$ for $a^2 + b^2$ (correct: $a^2 + b^2$ — but forgetting the magnitude form before squaring). Always compute $\vert a+jb \vert = \sqrt{a^2+b^2}$ first, then square. > 5. **VSWR Direction Error:** Writing $\vert \Gamma \vert = (\rho+1)/(\rho-1)$ — this is the *inverse*. Memorize the correct form via dimensional check. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Three Efficiencies — Never Confuse:** - **Radiation Efficiency** ($e_r$): Internal loss only — function of $R_{rad}$ and $R_L$. - **Reflection Efficiency** ($e_{ref}$): Mismatch only — function of VSWR or $\vert \Gamma \vert$. - **Total Efficiency** ($e_0$): Product of the two. - **Key Formula Triad (must memorize):** - $e_r = \dfrac{R_{rad}}{R_{rad} + R_L}$ - $e_{ref} = 1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2 = \dfrac{4\rho}{(\rho+1)^2}$ - $e_0 = e_r \cdot e_{ref}$ - **Boundary Conditions to Verify Answers:** - Perfect match ($\rho = 1$, $\Gamma = 0$): $e_{ref} = 1$. - Lossless antenna ($R_L = 0$): $e_r = 1$. - Ideal antenna: $e_0 = 1$ (or 0 dB). - **Electrically Small Antennas:** $e_r \to 0$ as $L \to 0$ because $R_{rad}$ collapses faster than $R_L$. - **dB Conversion is Mandatory:** $e_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(e_{linear})$; remember efficiency in dB is always $\le 0$ dB. - **VSWR ↔ $\Gamma$ Conversion:** $\vert \Gamma \vert = (\rho-1)/(\rho+1)$ — derivable from first principles in 2 lines, frequently asked in 3-mark Part A questions. - **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** VSWR $\le 2$ corresponds to $e_{ref} \ge 8/9 \approx 88.9\%$ — the standard design target in most commercial RF systems.

topics.title: Input Impedance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Input Impedance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — INPUT IMPEDANCE

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$)** of an antenna is defined as the **impedance presented by the antenna at its feed point (terminals)**. Mathematically, it is the complex ratio of the voltage to the current at the pair of terminals where the feeder (transmission line) is connected to the antenna.

$$
Z_{in} = \frac{V_{in}}{I_{in}} = R_{in} + jX_{in} \quad (\Omega)
$$

It consists of two physically meaningful parts:

| Component | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|--------|-------------------|
| Input Resistance | $R_{in}$ | $R_{in} = R_{rad} + R_{loss}$ |
| Input Reactance | $X_{in}$ | Energy stored in the antenna's near field |

> [!NOTE]
> - **$R_{rad}$** → Radiation Resistance: Represents the energy that the antenna **actually radiates into space**.
> - **$R_{loss}$** → Loss Resistance: Represents the energy dissipated as heat (conductor loss, dielectric loss, ground loss).
> - **$X_{in}$** → Reactance: Represents the **stored energy** in the reactive near-field. Positive $X_{in}$ = inductive; Negative $X_{in}$ = capacitive.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Water-Pipe Model 💧

Imagine a complex water-pumping system:

- A water pump (the **transmitter**) pushes water into a thin pipe (the **transmission line**) which ends at a **nozzle** (the **antenna**).
- The **input impedance** is exactly like the **"resistance to flow"** that the nozzle presents to the water coming through the pipe.
  - If the nozzle is **wide open and smooth** → water flows out easily → low resistance → good radiation.
  - If the nozzle is **narrow or partially blocked** → water pushes back → some water bounces back as a wave (just like a **standing wave / VSWR** on a transmission line).
  - If the nozzle has a **bulge or a constriction** → extra reactive "springiness" (water sloshes back and forth) → **reactance**.
- The **radiation resistance** corresponds to water that *actually exits* the nozzle; the **loss resistance** corresponds to water that *heats up the pipe walls*. Anything not transmitted or radiated *bounces back*.

This is exactly why **impedance matching** at the feed point is so critical — just like matching the pipe diameter to the nozzle size minimizes turbulence and maximizes water throughput.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner Heuristic:** Whenever a question asks for a numerical answer, always remember that for a *standard half-wave dipole in free space*, the **expected input impedance is approximately $73 + j42.5\ \Omega$**. This single number can earn partial marks if quoted correctly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 Why Input Impedance Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Input impedance is the *single most important parameter* in antenna engineering** because it governs:
> 1. **Power transfer** from the transmission line to the antenna.
> 2. **Standing Wave Ratio (SWR / VSWR)** on the feed line.
> 3. **Bandwidth** of the antenna (frequency range over which matching holds).
> 4. **Array behaviour** through mutual coupling.
> 5. **Transmitter stability** (mismatch can damage the PA stage).

For maximum power transfer (conjugate matching):
$$
Z_{source} = Z_{in}^{*}
$$
The reflection coefficient at the feed point is:
$$
\Gamma = \frac{Z_{in} - Z_0}{Z_{in} + Z_0}
$$
where $Z_0$ is the characteristic impedance of the feed line (typically $50\ \Omega$ or $75\ \Omega$).

## 2.2 Physical Origin of Each Component

### (a) Radiation Resistance ($R_{rad}$)
Defined as a **fictitious resistance** which, when carrying the antenna input current $I_{in}$, would dissipate exactly the same total radiated power $P_{rad}$ as the antenna actually radiates:
$$
P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2} \vert I_{in} \vert^{2}\, R_{rad}
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
R_{rad} = \frac{2 P_{rad}}{\vert I_{in}\vert^{2}}
$$

### (b) Loss Resistance ($R_{loss}$)
Represents ohmic and dielectric dissipation:
$$
P_{loss} = \frac{1}{2}\vert I_{in}\vert^{2} R_{loss}
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
R_{loss} = \frac{2 P_{loss}}{\vert I_{in}\vert^{2}}
$$

### (c) Reactance ($X_{in}$)
Purely reactive — represents **stored energy** in the evanescent near-field:
$$
W_{stored} = \frac{\vert I_{in}\vert^{2} X_{in}}{4\omega}
$$
If $W_{stored} > 0$ → inductive ($X_{in} > 0$); if $W_{stored} < 0$ → capacitive ($X_{in} < 0$).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-ready** and appear directly in KTU ESE solutions.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | KTU Application |
|---|---------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | $Z_{in} = R_{in} + jX_{in}$ | Complex input impedance | Foundation equation |
| 2 | $R_{in} = R_{rad} + R_{loss}$ | Real part | Decomposition |
| 3 | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_{in}-Z_0}{Z_{in}+Z_0}$ | Reflection coefficient | Matching / SWR |
| 4 | $\text{VSWR} = \dfrac{1+\vert\Gamma\vert}{1-\vert\Gamma\vert}$ | Voltage Standing Wave Ratio | Matching quality |
| 5 | $R_{rad} = \dfrac{2 P_{rad}}{\vert I_{in}\vert^{2}}$ | Radiated power equivalent | Defining $R_{rad}$ |
| 6 | $Z_{A} = -\dfrac{1}{I_{in}}\displaystyle\int_{-h}^{h}E_{z}(z)\,dz$ | Induced-EMF self-impedance | Thin dipole derivation |
| 7 | $Z_{21} = -\dfrac{1}{I_{1}}\displaystyle\int_{-h_{2}}^{h_{2}}E_{z_{21}}(z)\,dz$ | Mutual impedance | Antenna arrays |
| 8 | $Z_{in}^{(\lambda/2)} \approx 73 + j42.5\ \Omega$ | Half-wave dipole | Numerical question |
| 9 | $P_{rad} = \dfrac{1}{2}\vert I_{0}\vert^{2} R_{rad}$ | Average radiated power | Power calculations |
| 10 | $Z_{in}^{total} = Z_{11} + Z_{12} + Z_{21} + Z_{22}$ | Array element impedance | Mutual coupling |

> [!TIP]
> The formulas in rows **6 and 7** are the **induced-EMF method integrals** (Carter's method) — these almost always carry **7 marks** in a 14-mark KTU ESE question on dipole impedance.

## 2.4 Behavioural Trends (Highly Tested in Viva & Short Notes)

1. **Resonant length**: At slightly less than $\lambda/2$ (≈ $0.47\lambda$ for a wire dipole), $X_{in} = 0$ — this is the **first resonance**. The dipole then looks purely resistive (~73 Ω).
2. **Shorter dipole** ($L \ll \lambda$): $R_{rad}$ becomes very small, $X_{in}$ becomes large and **negative (capacitive)**.
3. **Longer dipole** ($L \approx 0.6\lambda$): $X_{in}$ swings to large positive (inductive).
4. **Multiple resonances** occur near $L \approx \lambda/2$, $\lambda$, $3\lambda/2$, ...
5. **Thicker dipole** → lower reactance slope → wider bandwidth (Balanis relation).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Self Impedance of a Linear Dipole via the Induced-EMF Method (Carter, 1932)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most important derivation** for KTU Module 2. Memorise every step.

### Step 1 — Model the Antenna and Current Distribution

Consider a **center-fed thin linear dipole** of total length $L = 2h$, oriented along the z-axis, with centre at the origin.

Assume a **sinusoidal current distribution** (valid for a thin dipole, $a \ll \lambda$):
$$
I(z') = I_{in}\,\frac{\sin\!\bigl[k(h - \vert z'\vert)\bigr]}{\sin(kh)}
$$

where:
- $I_{in}$ = current at the feed point (input terminals),
- $h$ = half-length,
- $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ = free-space wavenumber.

> *Reason:* A thin dipole behaves like an open-circuited transmission line stub; its boundary conditions force a sine variation with a maximum at the centre for $h \approx \lambda/4$.

### Step 2 — Write the E-field on the Dipole Surface

Using the standard Hertzian-dipole integration (Balanis Eq. 8-26), the z-component of the electric field at a point $z$ on the surface of the dipole (radius $a \ll \lambda$) due to the entire current distribution is:
$$
E_{z}(z) = -\frac{j\eta I_{in}}{4\pi}\left[\frac{e^{-jkr_{1}}}{r_{1}} + \frac{e^{-jkr_{2}}}{r_{2}} - 2\cos(kh)\,\frac{e^{-jkr_{0}}}{r_{0}}\right]
$$

where the three distances are:
$$
r_{1} = \sqrt{(z-h)^{2}+a^{2}}, \quad r_{2} = \sqrt{(z+h)^{2}+a^{2}}, \quad r_{0} = \sqrt{z^{2}+a^{2}}
$$

> *Reason:* The first two terms are contributions from the upper and lower halves; the third term is the image-mode correction that cancels the discontinuity at $z = \pm h$.

### Step 3 — Express the Back-EMF (Faraday / Reciprocity)

The field $E_{z}$ acts back on the antenna current and induces a "back-EMF". Integrating along the dipole length:
$$
V_{emf} = -\int_{-h}^{h} E_{z}(z')\,dz'
$$

### Step 4 — Define the Self-Impedance (Input Impedance)

By definition:
$$
Z_{in} = Z_{A} = \frac{V_{emf}}{I_{in}}
$$

Substituting:
$$
Z_{A} = -\frac{1}{I_{in}}\int_{-h}^{h} E_{z}(z')\,dz'
$$

This is the **induced-EMF self-impedance** of the dipole.

### Step 5 — Substitute the Sinusoidal Current (Carter's Result)

Multiplying numerator and denominator by the current profile:
$$
Z_{A} = -\frac{1}{I_{in}^{2}}\int_{-h}^{h} I(z')\,E_{z}(z')\,dz'
$$

Plugging in:
$$
I(z') = I_{in}\,\frac{\sin[k(h-\vert z'\vert)]}{\sin(kh)}
$$

gives the **canonical Carter integral**:
$$
Z_{A} = -\frac{1}{I_{in}\,\sin^{2}(kh)}\int_{-h}^{h} \sin\!\bigl[k(h-\vert z'\vert)\bigr]\,E_{z}(z')\,dz'
$$

### Step 6 — Split into Real (Resistance) and Imaginary (Reactance) Parts

The real part is the **radiation + loss resistance** and the imaginary part is the **input reactance**. After lengthy (but standard) integration (see Balanis Section 8.2.2):

$$
R_{rad}(h) = \frac{\eta}{2\pi}\Biggl\{ C + \ln(2kh) - \text{Ci}(2kh) + \tfrac{1}{2}\sin(2kh)\bigl[\text{Si}(4kh)-2\,\text{Si}(2kh)\bigr] + \tfrac{1}{2}\cos(2kh)\bigl[C + \ln(kh) + \text{Ci}(4kh)-2\,\text{Ci}(2kh)\bigr] \Biggr\}
$$

$$
X_{A}(h) = \frac{\eta}{4\pi}\Biggl\{ 2\,\text{Si}(2kh) + \cos(2kh)\bigl[2\,\text{Si}(2kh)-\text{Si}(4kh)\bigr] - \sin(2kh)\bigl[2\,\text{Ci}(2kh)-\text{Ci}(4kh)-\text{Ci}(2ka_{eq})\bigr] \Biggr\}
$$

where:
- $\text{Si}(x) = \int_{0}^{x}\dfrac{\sin u}{u}\,du$ — sine integral,
- $\text{Ci}(x) = -\int_{x}^{\infty}\dfrac{\cos u}{u}\,du$ — cosine integral,
- $C = 0.5772\ldots$ — Euler–Mascheroni constant,
- $a_{eq}$ — equivalent radius (for a cylindrical wire of radius $a$, $a_{eq} \approx a/2$),
- $\eta = 120\pi \approx 377\ \Omega$ — free-space intrinsic impedance.

### Step 7 — Evaluate for the Special Case $h = \lambda/4$ (Half-Wave Dipole)

Substituting $kh = \pi/2$ into the above integrals (or using the closed forms for $L = \lambda/2$):

$$
R_{rad}\Bigl(\tfrac{\lambda}{2}\Bigr) \approx 73.08\ \Omega
$$

$$
X_{A}\Bigl(\tfrac{\lambda}{2}\Bigr) \approx 42.54\ \Omega
$$

Therefore:
$$
\boxed{Z_{in}^{(\lambda/2)} \approx 73 + j42.5\ \Omega}
$$

> *Reason:* A pure $\lambda/2$ dipole is slightly **non-resonant**; true resonance (where $X_{in}=0$) occurs at about $L \approx 0.47\lambda$ — this is why a practical dipole is often cut *slightly shorter* than $\lambda/2$.

### Step 8 — Numerical Verification (For an Exam-Style Problem)

> **Example:** Find the input impedance of a half-wave dipole operating at $600\ \text{MHz}$ in free space. Compute the matching efficiency with a $50\ \Omega$ feed line.

**Given:** $f = 600\ \text{MHz} \Rightarrow \lambda = c/f = 0.5\ \text{m}$, so $L = \lambda/2 = 0.25\ \text{m}$.

From Step 7:
$$
Z_{in} = 73 + j42.5\ \Omega
$$

Reflection coefficient with $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$:
$$
\Gamma = \frac{(73+j42.5) - 50}{(73+j42.5) + 50} = \frac{23 + j42.5}{123 + j42.5}
$$

Computing magnitude:
$$
\Gamma = \frac{\sqrt{23^{2} + 42.5^{2}}}{\sqrt{123^{2} + 42.5^{2}}} = \frac{\sqrt{529 + 1806}}{\sqrt{15129 + 1806}} = \frac{\sqrt{2335}}{\sqrt{16935}} = \frac{48.32}{130.13} \approx 0.371
$$

VSWR:
$$
\text{VSWR} = \frac{1 + 0.371}{1 - 0.371} \approx \frac{1.371}{0.629} \approx 2.18
$$

Mismatch loss (return loss in dB):
$$
\text{RL} = -20\log_{10}(0.371) \approx 8.6\ \text{dB}
$$

> ✅ This is why practical dipole antennas are almost always **tuned (shortened)** to bring $X_{in}$ to zero before impedance-matching networks are applied.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Mutual Impedance Between Two Parallel Dipoles

### Step 1 — Geometry

Two thin linear dipoles (1 and 2), each of length $2h$, are placed parallel to the z-axis and separated by distance $d$ in the x-direction. Dipole 1 is excited with current $I_1$, dipole 2 is open-circuited.

### Step 2 — Mutual Impedance Definition (Open-Circuit Voltage Method)

$$
Z_{21} = \frac{V_{21}}{I_{1}}\bigg|_{I_{2}=0}
$$

where $V_{21}$ is the open-circuit voltage induced at the terminals of antenna 2 by the current $I_1$ in antenna 1.

### Step 3 — Field from Antenna 1 at Antenna 2

Using the same $E_{z}$ expression as in §3.1 Step 2 but evaluated at the location of antenna 2 (offset by $d$ along x):
$$
E_{z_{21}}(z) = -\frac{j\eta I_{1}}{4\pi}\left[\frac{e^{-jkr_{1d}}}{r_{1d}} + \frac{e^{-jkr_{2d}}}{r_{2d}} - 2\cos(kh)\,\frac{e^{-jkr_{0d}}}{r_{0d}}\right]
$$

where now the distances include the lateral offset:
$$
r_{1d} = \sqrt{(z-h)^{2}+d^{2}}, \quad r_{2d} = \sqrt{(z+h)^{2}+d^{2}}, \quad r_{0d} = \sqrt{z^{2}+d^{2}}
$$

### Step 4 — Induced Voltage (Induced-EMF Method, Reciprocity)

$$
V_{21} = -\int_{-h}^{h} E_{z_{21}}(z)\,dz
$$

### Step 5 — Mutual Impedance Integral

$$
\boxed{Z_{21} = -\frac{1}{I_{1}}\int_{-h}^{h} E_{z_{21}}(z)\,dz}
$$

Equivalently, using the current distribution of antenna 2 (assumed to be sinusoidal with input $I_{2}$):
$$
Z_{21} = -\frac{1}{I_{1}\,I_{2}}\int_{-h}^{h} I_{2}(z)\,E_{z_{21}}(z)\,dz
$$

### Step 6 — Useful Special Cases (Tabulated)

For two **side-by-side parallel half-wave dipoles**, the mutual impedance $Z_{21} = R_{21} + jX_{21}$ depends only on separation $d/\lambda$:

| $d/\lambda$ | $R_{21}\ (\Omega)$ | $X_{21}\ (\Omega)$ |
|:-----------:|:------------------:|:------------------:|
| 0.0  |  73.08 |  42.54 |
| 0.1  |  53.3  |  18.6  |
| 0.25 |   5.8  | –25.0  |
| 0.5  | –12.7  | –29.6  |
| 1.0  |   1.9  |   1.4  |

> *Physical Insight:* The imaginary part of $Z_{21}$ dominates at small separations — this is why **closely-spaced antenna arrays** exhibit strong reactive mutual coupling that drastically alters their input impedance.

---

## 3.3 Master Equation Summary

$$
Z_{in}^{(\text{array element})} = Z_{11} + \sum_{m\neq 1} \frac{I_{m}}{I_{1}}\,Z_{1m}
$$

For a uniform linear array with identical excitation:
$$
Z_{in}^{(\text{each element})} = Z_{11} + Z_{12}\left(\frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}\right) + Z_{13}\left(\frac{I_{3}}{I_{1}}\right) + \cdots
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Active Element Pattern Theorem (KTU Favourite):** The driving-point impedance of *any element in a finite array* depends on the **scan angle** — this is the basis of the "scan blindness" phenomenon that examiners love to ask in viva.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Decomposition of Antenna Input Impedance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ANTENNA INPUT IMPEDANCE Zin"]:::main
    B["REAL PART Rin"]:::real
    C["IMAGINARY PART jXin"]:::imag
    D["R_rad = Radiated Power Equivalent"]:::real_sub
    E["R_loss = Ohmic plus Dielectric Dissipation"]:::real_sub
    F["Energy Stored in Near Field"]:::imag_sub
    G["Inductive if X greater than 0"]:::imag_sub
    H["Capacitive if X less than 0"]:::imag_sub
    I["Feed Point Terminals"]:::feed
    J["Transmission Line Z0 = 50 ohm"]:::line

    A --> B
    A --> C
    B --> D
    B --> E
    C --> F
    F --> G
    F --> H
    J --> I
    I --> A

    classDef main fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef real fill:#A8D5BA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef real_sub fill:#D4F1D4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef imag fill:#FFB6B9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef imag_sub fill:#FFD3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef feed fill:#C7CEEA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef line fill:#E8E8E8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The transmission line presents its characteristic impedance ($Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$). At the feed point it sees the antenna input impedance, which is the *parallel-series combination* of a radiation resistance, a loss resistance, and a reactive component that stores energy.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Induced-EMF Method Algorithm (KTU Workflow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STEP 1 Assume sinusoidal current I of z = Iin sin k of h minus mod z divided by sin kh"]:::step
    S2["STEP 2 Compute Ez on dipole surface using Hertzian dipole integration"]:::step
    S3["STEP 3 Integrate Ez over dipole length to find induced back EMF"]:::step
    S4["STEP 4 Compute Zin as minus 1 divided by Iin times the integral"]:::step
    S5["STEP 5 Split Zin into Rrad and jXin via real and imaginary parts"]:::step
    S6["STEP 6 Evaluate Rrad and XA at desired h using Si and Ci functions"]:::step
    S7["FINAL RESULT Zin equals 73 plus j42.5 ohm for half wave dipole"]:::result

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7

    classDef step fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#FFD54F,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Feed Point Matching Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX["Transmitter PA Stage Zs"]:::tx
    LINE["Transmission Line Z0 = 50 ohm"]:::line
    MATCH["Impedance Matching Network"]:::match
    ANT["Antenna Input Terminals Zin"]:::ant
    RAD["Radiated Power Rrad"]:::radiate
    LOSS["Lost Heat Rloss"]:::heat
    STORED["Stored Reactive Energy jXin"]:::store

    TX --> LINE --> MATCH --> ANT
    ANT --> RAD
    ANT --> LOSS
    ANT --> STORED

    classDef tx fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef line fill:#CFD8DC,stroke:#263238,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef match fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ant fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef radiate fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef heat fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef store fill:#B39DDB,stroke:#311B92,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The matching network's purpose is to transform $Z_{in}$ into a value **conjugate-matched to $Z_0$** so that maximum power flows into $R_{rad}$ (radiated) while $R_{loss}$ (heat) and $X_{in}$ (stored energy) are minimised at the feed point.

## 4.4 Frequency Response Block — Impedance vs Dipole Length

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["Very Short L much less than lambda"]:::short
    L2["Half Wave L equals lambda by 2"]:::half
    L3["Full Wave L equals lambda"]:::full
    L4["Long L equals 1.5 lambda"]:::long

    L1 --> R1["Rrad very small approx 20 ohm squared"]:::out
    L1 --> X1["Xin large negative Capacitive"]:::out

    L2 --> R2["Rrad equals 73 ohm"]:::out
    L2 --> X2["Xin equals 42.5 ohm Slightly inductive"]:::out

    L3 --> R3["Rrad very large approx 200 ohm"]:::out
    L3 --> X3["Xin near zero Resonant"]:::out

    L4 --> R4["Rrad about 105 ohm"]:::out
    L4 --> X4["Xin large positive Inductive"]:::out

    classDef short fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef half fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef full fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef long fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```
