<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define input impedance of an antenna. Mention its real and imaginary components. *(Module 2, Module-level CO)* `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** The input impedance of an antenna is the ratio of the complex voltage to the complex current at the antenna's feed point terminals:
> $$Z_{in} = \frac{V_{in}}{I_{in}}$$

> **Components (2 Marks):** It is a complex quantity:
> $$Z_{in} = R_{in} + jX_{in}$$
> where:
> - $R_{in} = R_{rad} + R_{loss}$ represents power dissipated — *radiated* through $R_{rad}$ and *lost as heat* through $R_{loss}$.
> - $X_{in}$ represents energy stored in the antenna's reactive near field; positive $X_{in}$ indicates inductive behaviour, negative $X_{in}$ indicates capacitive behaviour.

---

### Q2. What is mutual impedance? Why is it important in antenna arrays? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 Marks):** Mutual impedance $Z_{21}$ between two antennas is the **open-circuit voltage induced in antenna 2 per unit current flowing in antenna 1**:
> $$Z_{21} = \left.\frac{V_{21}}{I_{1}}\right|_{I_{2}=0}$$
> It quantifies the electromagnetic coupling between two antennas through the surrounding field.

> **Importance (1 Mark):** In antenna arrays, the driving-point impedance of each element is modified by the mutual impedances of all other elements:
> $$Z_{in}^{(\text{element})} = Z_{11} + \sum_{m\neq 1} Z_{1m}\left(\frac{I_{m}}{I_{1}}\right)$$
> Ignoring $Z_{21}$ in array design leads to severe impedance mismatch and "scan blindness" — a phenomenon frequently examined in KTU viva.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A — Self-Impedance via Induced-EMF Method** *(Carry 14 marks; internal choice)* `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

> **"Derive the expression for the input (self) impedance of a thin linear centre-fed dipole using the induced-EMF method. Hence evaluate it for a half-wave dipole in free space."**

**Step-by-step Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Geometry and Current Assumption (1 Mark)**
Consider a centre-fed thin linear dipole of half-length $h$ along the z-axis. For a thin wire ($a \ll \lambda$), the current distribution is approximately sinusoidal:
$$
I(z') = I_{in}\,\frac{\sin\!\bigl[k(h-\vert z'\vert)\bigr]}{\sin(kh)}
$$

**Step 2 — Electric Field on the Dipole Surface (2 Marks)**
By integrating the contributions of infinitesimal current elements over the dipole, the z-component of the electric field on the dipole surface is:
$$
E_{z}(z) = -\frac{j\eta I_{in}}{4\pi}\left[\frac{e^{-jkr_{1}}}{r_{1}} + \frac{e^{-jkr_{2}}}{r_{2}} - 2\cos(kh)\,\frac{e^{-jkr_{0}}}{r_{0}}\right]
$$
with the three distances $r_{1}, r_{2}, r_{0}$ as defined in §3.1.

**Step 3 — Induced Back-EMF (1 Mark)**
Integrating the field over the antenna gives the back-EMF (Carter 1932):
$$
V_{emf} = -\int_{-h}^{h} E_{z}(z')\,dz'
$$

**Step 4 — Self-Impedance Definition (1 Mark)**
$$
Z_{A} = \frac{V_{emf}}{I_{in}} = -\frac{1}{I_{in}}\int_{-h}^{h} E_{z}(z')\,dz'
$$
This is the **induced-EMF self-impedance** of the dipole.

**Step 5 — Decomposition into Real and Imaginary Parts (1 Mark)**
The integral splits into a **real part = radiation + loss resistance** and an **imaginary part = input reactance**:
$$
Z_{A} = R_{rad}(h) + jX_{A}(h)
$$
with $R_{rad}$ and $X_{A}$ given by the Si/Ci expressions in §3.1.

**Step 6 — Half-Wave Dipole Result (1 Mark)**
For $L = \lambda/2$ ($kh = \pi/2$), substituting and evaluating the special functions:
$$
\boxed{R_{rad} = 73.08\ \Omega, \quad X_{A} = 42.54\ \Omega, \quad Z_{A} = 73 + j42.5\ \Omega}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Variation & Significance (7 Marks)

> **"Explain the variation of input reactance of a dipole with its electrical length. Discuss the practical importance of self-impedance in antenna matching."**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Variation with Length (3 Marks):**
- For a **very short dipole** ($L \ll \lambda$): $X_{in}$ is large and **negative (capacitive)**. The antenna behaves like a small capacitor.
- As $L$ increases toward $\lambda/2$, $X_{in}$ crosses zero — this is the **first resonant length**, occurring at $L \approx 0.47\lambda$ for a thin wire (slightly less than $\lambda/2$ because of end-effect capacitance).
- At $L = \lambda/2$ exactly: $X_{in} \approx +j42.5\ \Omega$ (slightly inductive).
- Between $L = 0.5\lambda$ and $L = 0.65\lambda$: $X_{in}$ reaches a large **positive (inductive)** peak.
- At $L \approx 0.7\lambda$: second resonance (anti-resonance) — $X_{in} = 0$ but $R_{rad}$ is large.
- The pattern of resonance/anti-resonance repeats near $L \approx \lambda$, $1.5\lambda$, etc.

**2. Self-Impedance Significance (4 Marks):**
- **Power transfer:** Maximum power is delivered only when the source impedance equals the **conjugate** of the antenna input impedance.
- **VSWR / Return Loss:** Mismatch leads to standing waves and reflected power. A typical design target is VSWR ≤ 2 (≈ 90% power transfer).
- **Bandwidth:** The frequency range over which $|X_{in}|$ stays below a threshold (e.g., 50 Ω matching) defines the antenna's usable bandwidth.
- **Array design:** Each element's self-impedance is needed to compute its total driving-point impedance including mutual coupling.
- **Practical tuning:** A dipole is normally trimmed slightly shorter than $\lambda/2$ so that the residual positive reactance cancels the small negative reactance from feed-line discontinuities and the balun.

---

### **Question B — Mutual Impedance & Self-Impedance Concepts** *(Alternative 14-mark question)* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — Self-Impedance Components (7 Marks)

> **"Discuss the components of input impedance of an antenna. Explain the physical significance of radiation resistance and loss resistance. How is the input impedance of a half-wave dipole measured in practice?"**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Components (3 Marks):**
$$
Z_{in} = R_{rad} + R_{loss} + jX_{in}
$$
- **Radiation resistance $R_{rad}$** is a *fictitious* resistance such that the power it would dissipate when carrying $I_{in}$ equals the **actual radiated power**:
$$
P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2}|I_{in}|^{2}\,R_{rad}
$$
- **Loss resistance $R_{loss}$** accounts for ohmic conductor losses, dielectric losses in any insulation, and ground losses (for ground-based antennas).
- **Reactance $X_{in}$** represents stored reactive energy; it can be positive (inductive) or negative (capacitive).

**2. Physical Significance (2 Marks):**
- A **high $R_{rad}$ relative to $R_{loss}$** is the mark of an *efficient* antenna:
$$
e_{rad} = \frac{R_{rad}}{R_{rad}+R_{loss}}
$$
- For a half-wave dipole, $R_{rad} \approx 73\ \Omega$ dominates $R_{loss} < 1\ \Omega$, giving efficiencies above 95%.

**3. Measurement (2 Marks):**
- In practice, the input impedance is measured using a **Vector Network Analyser (VNA)**.
- The antenna is connected at the calibrated test port; the VNA directly displays $Z_{in}(f)$, $|S_{11}|$, and VSWR.
- Alternatively, **slotted-line measurements** with a movable probe (older method) detect standing-wave patterns from which $|Z_{in}|$ and the reflection coefficient angle are computed.

---

#### Part (b) — Mutual Impedance Derivation (7 Marks)

> **"Define mutual impedance between two coupled dipoles. Derive its expression using the induced-EMF method. Mention any two practical applications of mutual impedance in antenna engineering."**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 Mark):**
$$
Z_{21} = \left.\frac{V_{21}}{I_{1}}\right|_{I_{2}=0}
$$
where $V_{21}$ is the open-circuit voltage induced at the terminals of antenna 2 by current $I_{1}$ flowing in antenna 1.

**2. Geometry (1 Mark):**
Two thin dipoles of half-length $h$ lie parallel to the z-axis, separated laterally by distance $d$.

**3. Field from Antenna 1 (2 Marks):**
Using the standard Hertzian-dipole integration:
$$
E_{z_{21}}(z) = -\frac{j\eta I_{1}}{4\pi}\left[\frac{e^{-jkr_{1d}}}{r_{1d}} + \frac{e^{-jkr_{2d}}}{r_{2d}} - 2\cos(kh)\,\frac{e^{-jkr_{0d}}}{r_{0d}}\right]
$$
with $r_{1d}, r_{2d}, r_{0d}$ defined in §3.2.

**4. Induced-EMF Integral (2 Marks):**
$$
V_{21} = -\int_{-h}^{h} E_{z_{21}}(z)\,dz
$$
$$
\therefore\;\; Z_{21} = -\frac{1}{I_{1}}\int_{-h}^{h} E_{z_{21}}(z)\,dz
$$

**5. Practical Applications (1 Mark — any two):**
- **Antenna array design:** Element driving-point impedance is $Z_{in} = Z_{11} + Z_{21}(I_{2}/I_{1}) + \ldots$
- **Scan blindness analysis:** At certain scan angles, mutual impedances create destructive interference, reducing array gain.
- **MIMO antenna decoupling:** Mutual impedance between closely-packed MIMO elements must be minimised (target $|S_{21}| < -10$ dB) for channel independence.
- **Beam steering in Yagi-Uda arrays:** Mutual coupling between driven element, reflector, and directors shapes the radiation pattern.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks on Input Impedance Questions:**
> 1. **Mixing up $R_{rad}$ and $R_{loss}$:** $R_{rad}$ is NOT a physical resistor — it is an *equivalent* resistance that represents radiated power. Many students write it as if it were a literal wire resistance. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Forgetting to write the $j$ in $jX_{in}$:** The reactance is always imaginary; omitting $j$ or writing just $X_{in}$ as the full reactive term is wrong. **Penalty: 0.5 mark.**
> 3. **Quoting $Z_{in} = 73\ \Omega$ alone for half-wave dipole:** This is the *resonant* dipole at $L \approx 0.47\lambda$; for a true $L = \lambda/2$ you **must** include $j42.5\ \Omega$. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Using Cartesian $Z_{in}$ instead of polar form** when computing VSWR or $\Gamma$ in numerical problems. Always convert carefully:
>    $$Z_{in} = R + jX \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \vert Z_{in}\vert = \sqrt{R^{2}+X^{2}}, \;\;\angle Z_{in} = \tan^{-1}(X/R)$$
> 5. **Omitting the **"minus"** sign** in the induced-EMF integral $Z = -\dfrac{1}{I}\int E\,dz$. The negative sign comes from Lenz's law — examiners specifically check for this. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 6. **Stating that mutual impedance is always real:** $Z_{21}$ has both real and imaginary parts. Only in the special case $d = \lambda/2$ does it become purely real. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 7. **Confusing self-impedance with driving-point impedance:** The two are equal *only* for an isolated antenna. In an array, the driving-point impedance includes mutual terms.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Before walking into the KTU ESE hall, mentally tick off every item below.

- [x] **Definition:** $Z_{in} = V_{in}/I_{in} = R_{in} + jX_{in}$ at the antenna feed terminals.
- [x] **Real part:** $R_{in} = R_{rad} + R_{loss}$.
- [x] **$R_{rad}$:** Equivalent resistance corresponding to radiated power; for $\lambda/2$ dipole ≈ **73 Ω**.
- [x] **$X_{in}$:** Stored near-field energy; positive = inductive, negative = capacitive.
- [x] **Standard result to memorise:** Half-wave dipole $Z_{in} \approx 73 + j42.5\ \Omega$ in free space.
- [x] **Resonant length** of a thin wire dipole: ≈ $0.47\lambda$ (slightly less than $\lambda/2$).
- [x] **Induced-EMF integral** (Carter's method):
  $$Z_{A} = -\frac{1}{I_{in}}\int_{-h}^{h} E_{z}(z)\,dz$$
- [x] **Mutual impedance:**
  $$Z_{21} = \left.\frac{V_{21}}{I_{1}}\right|_{I_{2}=0}$$
- [x] **Reflection coefficient:** $\Gamma = (Z_{in} - Z_0)/(Z_{in} + Z_0)$.
- [x] **VSWR:** $(1 + |\Gamma|)/(1 - |\Gamma|)$.
- [x] **Efficiency:** $e_{rad} = R_{rad}/(R_{rad} + R_{loss})$.
- [x] **Thicker dipole → wider bandwidth** (lower reactance slope).
- [x] **Mutual coupling** makes array element impedances depend on scan angle.
- [x] **Conjugate matching** ($Z_s = Z_{in}^*$) is needed for maximum power transfer.
- [x] **Measurement tools:** VNA (modern); slotted line (legacy).
- [x] **Watch for the minus sign** in the induced-EMF formula.
- [x] **For arrays:** Total element impedance = self-impedance + weighted sum of mutual impedances.

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — INPUT IMPEDANCE**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coupling
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Coupling

# Coupling in Antenna Arrays

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of antenna engineering, **mutual coupling** is defined as the electromagnetic interaction that occurs between two or more radiating elements positioned in close proximity to one another, typically within an antenna array configuration. When current flows through one antenna element (the *excited* or *driven* element), it radiates electromagnetic fields that impinge upon neighboring elements. These neighboring elements, although not directly excited by their own feed networks, re-radiate a portion of the intercepted energy and scatter the rest. This energy exchange modifies the effective excitation (amplitude and phase) of every element relative to its *isolated* (free-space, independent) operating condition.

Mathematically, mutual coupling manifests as an alteration of the **self-impedance** $Z_{ii}$ of each element and introduces non-zero **mutual impedances** $Z_{ij}$ between element pairs, where $i \neq j$. The driven element "sees" a different terminal impedance than it would in isolation because of the currents induced in the parasitic or adjacent driven elements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Coupling* is the phenomenon by which energy is transferred between adjacent antenna elements due to near-field interactions, and it directly modifies the array's input impedance, radiation pattern, and polarization purity.

### 1.2 Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

The principal measurable quantities associated with antenna coupling are:

- **Mutual Impedance ($Z_{m}$ or $Z_{ij}$)**: Measured in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**; represents the complex voltage induced in one antenna per unit current flowing in another.
- **Coupling Coefficient ($C$)**: A dimensionless, complex quantity, often expressed in **decibels (dB)** as $\vert C \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\vert C \vert$, that quantifies the amplitude ratio of the coupled wave to the incident wave between two ports.
- **S-Parameter ($S_{21}$)**: The scattering transmission coefficient (in **dB**), directly measured on a Vector Network Analyzer (VNA) to characterize inter-port coupling.
- **Active Element Pattern (AEP)**: The radiation pattern of an element *with all other elements present and excited*, as opposed to its *isolated element pattern (IEP)*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Symphony Orchestra:**
> Imagine a row of violinists, each playing their own individual note in isolation. When placed together as an orchestra, each violinist *hears* the sound produced by the others. This acoustic feedback subtly alters each violinist's perception of pitch, timing, and resonance, forcing them to instinctively adjust their bowing pressure and finger placement. The resulting collective sound is *not* simply the algebraic sum of individual notes — it is a modified, coupled performance.
> 
> Similarly, each antenna element in an array "hears" the electromagnetic fields of its neighbors. The element's input impedance, current distribution, and resulting radiation pattern are all *mutually adjusted* by these near-field interactions. The array's behavior is therefore not a simple superposition of isolated-element patterns; it is the result of a coupled, multi-port electromagnetic system.

### 1.4 Where Coupling Arises in Engineering Practice

Coupling is a first-order design concern in:

- **Phased Arrays** (radar, 5G/6G Massive MIMO base stations)
- **Adaptive / Smart Antennas**
- **Antenna arrays on satellite payloads**
- **Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO) systems**
- **Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)** — where unwanted coupling between unrelated antennas and circuits causes interference.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physics of Mutual Coupling — Operational Breakdown

When two antennas, $A_1$ and $A_2$, are placed within each other's reactive or radiating near-field regions, the following chain of events unfolds:

- **Step 1 — Excitation of Source Element:** A time-varying current $\tilde{I}_1$ is driven into antenna $A_1$, producing a radiating electromagnetic field $\vec{E}_1, \vec{H}_1$ in the surrounding medium (typically free space, $\eta_0 \approx 377\ \Omega$).
- **Step 2 — Field Incidence on Neighboring Element:** The far-reaching components of this field impinge on the conducting surfaces of $A_2$. According to the **Equivalence Principle**, the induced surface currents on $A_2$ are equivalent to a re-radiating source distribution.
- **Step 3 — Re-radiation and Scattering:** Element $A_2$ acts as a *secondary radiator*. Part of the intercepted energy is re-radiated back into space (scattered field), and part is delivered to the termination of $A_2$ (often a $50\ \Omega$ matched load, in which case it is absorbed).
- **Step 4 — Back-Reaction on the Source:** The scattered field from $A_2$ propagates back to $A_1$, modifying the boundary conditions at $A_1$'s terminals. This changes the apparent input impedance of $A_1$ — a phenomenon called *impedance loading*.
- **Step 5 — Generalized Array Behavior:** In an $N$-element array, this process is *not* a simple pairwise interaction. The current on each element is the result of mutual coupling to *all* $N-1$ other elements simultaneously, leading to a fully coupled $N$-port network.

## 2.2 Why Coupling Cannot Be Ignored — The "Why"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **infinite-array** limit (a theoretical construct), each element "sees" an identical environment. The array can be analyzed by considering only *one element* inside an equivalent waveguide with periodic boundary conditions. In any **finite, practical array**, edge and corner elements experience *asymmetric* coupling, which causes their driving-point impedances to differ significantly from those of central elements. This is a critical failure mode in real-world antenna systems if neglected.

## 2.3 Mutual Impedance — The Mathematical Foundation

Consider two center-fed, thin, linear dipole antennas $A_1$ and $A_2$, oriented parallel to the z-axis, separated by a distance $d$. Let $l_1$ and $l_2$ be their respective lengths. Using the **induced EMF method** (or the more general **reaction theorem** by Rumsey), the mutual impedance $Z_{21}$ between them is defined by:

$$Z_{21} = \frac{V_{21}}{I_{1}}$$

where $V_{21}$ is the open-circuit voltage induced at the terminals of antenna 2 by the current $I_1$ flowing at the terminals of antenna 1.

The terminal voltage equations for the two-port coupled network are:

$$V_1 = Z_{11}\, I_1 + Z_{12}\, I_2$$

$$V_2 = Z_{21}\, I_1 + Z_{22}\, I_2$$

The matrix form is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

By the **reciprocity theorem** of electromagnetics, $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$. For an $N$-element array, this generalizes to an $N \times N$ symmetric impedance matrix $Z$.

## 2.4 Driving-Point (Active) Impedance

For the $i$-th element in an $N$-element array, the input impedance *as seen at its feed* (called the **active** or **driving-point impedance** $Z_{a,i}$) is:

$$Z_{a,i} = \frac{V_i}{I_i} = Z_{ii} + \sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{N} \frac{I_j}{I_i} Z_{ij}$$

This shows explicitly how the currents of all other elements ($I_j$) feed back into the impedance of element $i$ through the mutual coupling terms $Z_{ij}$. For a uniformly excited linear array with progressive phase shift $\beta$, the active impedance becomes identical for all elements and can be computed using the array's scan impedance.

## 2.5 Coupling Coefficient

For a two-port network with matched ports ($Z_0$), the **voltage coupling coefficient** is:

$$C = \frac{V_{\text{coupled}}}{V_{\text{incident}}}$$

In terms of S-parameters:

$$\vert S_{21} \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert S_{21} \vert = 20 \log_{10} \vert C \vert$$

> [!NOTE]
> In phased arrays, an **element gain** (or **active element pattern**) is often computed as the product of the isolated element gain and an embedded element pattern that includes coupling. The pattern degradation due to coupling is quantified by the *scan loss*, typically expressed in **dB** at the array's broadside and scan extremes.

## 2.6 Effects of Mutual Coupling

| # | Effect | Engineering Consequence |
|---|--------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **Input Impedance Variation** | Mismatch with feed network; reflection coefficient $\Gamma$ rises; reduced radiated power. |
| 2 | **Pattern Distortion** | Main beam broadening, raised sidelobe levels, grating lobes in extreme cases. |
| 3 | **Scan Blindness** | At certain scan angles, mutual coupling causes $\Gamma \rightarrow 1$; total reflection (no radiation). |
| 4 | **Polarization Distortion** | Cross-polarization components increase; axial ratio degrades for circularly polarized arrays. |
| 5 | **Reduced Gain** | Power dissipated in nearby parasitic or mismatched elements lowers effective array gain. |
| 6 | **Correlation in MIMO** | In communications, high coupling raises envelope correlation, reducing channel capacity. |

## 2.7 Methods to Reduce Mutual Coupling

| # | Technique | Mechanism |
|---|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | **Increased Element Spacing** | Reduces near-field overlap (most basic, but increases grating-lobe risk). |
| 2 | **Decoupling Networks** | Lumped or distributed circuits cancel mutual admittance between ports. |
| 3 | **Electromagnetic Bandgap (EBG) Structures** | Periodic high-impedance surfaces suppress surface-wave coupling. |
| 4 | **Defected Ground Structures (DGS)** | Slot patterns in the ground plane alter surface current paths to reduce coupling. |
| 5 | **Metamaterial Shields** | Near-zero-permittivity or negative-permeability sheets between elements. |
| 6 | **Orthogonal Polarization** | Placing adjacent elements on orthogonal polarizations exploits polarization isolation. |
| 7 | **Parasitic Elements / Directors** | Engineering deliberate coupling for desired radiation control (e.g., Yagi-Uda). |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mutual Impedance of Two Parallel Dipoles (Induced EMF Method)

> **Assumption:** Two identical, center-fed, thin linear dipoles, each of half-length $h$ (so total length $L = 2h$), oriented parallel to the z-axis, separated laterally by a distance $d$ along the x-axis, both centered at the origin of their local coordinate systems.

**Step 1 — Define the assumed current distribution.**

For a center-fed dipole of half-length $h \approx 0.25\lambda$ (resonant length), the current distribution is approximately sinusoidal:

$$\tilde{I}_1(z') = I_{0} \sin\!\left[ k(h - \vert z' \vert) \right]$$

where $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ is the free-space wavenumber and $z' \in [-h, +h]$ parameterizes the position along antenna 1.

**Step 2 — Compute the vector potential at antenna 2.**

The magnetic vector potential at a point $(x, y, z)$ due to antenna 1 is:

$$\tilde{A}_{z1}(x, y, z) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int_{-h}^{+h} \tilde{I}_1(z') \frac{e^{-jkR}}{R}\, dz'$$

where $R = \sqrt{(x-d)^2 + y^2 + (z - z')^2}$ is the distance from the source point on antenna 1 to the field point on antenna 2.

**Step 3 — Compute the induced electric field.**

The z-component of the electric field from the vector potential is:

$$\tilde{E}_{z1} = -j\omega \tilde{A}_{z1} - \frac{j}{\omega \mu_0 \epsilon_0} \frac{\partial^2 \tilde{A}_{z1}}{\partial z^2}$$

For thin-wire antennas, the dominant contribution is from $\tilde{A}_{z1}$.

**Step 4 — Compute the open-circuit mutual voltage $V_{21}$.**

Using the **induced EMF method**, the mutual voltage is the line integral of $\tilde{E}_{z1}$ along antenna 2, weighted by the assumed current distribution on antenna 2:

$$V_{21} = -\frac{1}{I_{2}} \int_{-h}^{+h} \tilde{I}_2(z)\, \tilde{E}_{z1}(d, 0, z)\, dz$$

For identical antennas with sinusoidal current $\tilde{I}_2(z) = I_{0}' \sin[k(h - \vert z \vert)]$, the mutual impedance becomes:

$$Z_{21} = \frac{V_{21}}{I_{1}}$$

**Step 5 — Final expression for $Z_{21}$ for half-wave dipoles ($h = \lambda/4$).**

For two parallel half-wave dipoles, separated by distance $d$ and offset laterally, the classical **Carter's formula** (derived via rigorous integration of the above integrals) yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{21} &= R_{21} + j X_{21} \\[6pt]
R_{21} &= \frac{\eta_0}{4\pi} \left[ 2\, \mathrm{Ci}(k d) - \mathrm{Ci}(k\sqrt{d^2 + h^2} - kh) - \mathrm{Ci}(k\sqrt{d^2 + h^2} + kh) \right] \\[6pt]
X_{21} &= -\frac{\eta_0}{4\pi} \left[ 2\, \mathrm{Si}(k d) - \mathrm{Si}(k\sqrt{d^2 + h^2} - kh) - \mathrm{Si}(k\sqrt{d^2 + h^2} + kh) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

where:

- $\mathrm{Ci}(x) = -\int_{x}^{\infty} \dfrac{\cos t}{t}\, dt$ is the **cosine integral**
- $\mathrm{Si}(x) = \int_{0}^{x} \dfrac{\sin t}{t}\, dt$ is the **sine integral**
- $\eta_0 \approx 376.73\ \Omega$ is the **intrinsic impedance of free space**
- $h = \lambda/4$ for half-wave dipoles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Carter's formula allows a designer to compute the mutual impedance between two dipoles as a function of separation $d$. Substituting these $Z_{ij}$ values into the $N$-port impedance matrix then yields the active impedance of each element in a finite array, which is essential for matching network design and predicting scan blindness.

## 3.2 Derivation: Active Impedance of a Uniformly Excited Linear Array

Consider an $N$-element uniformly spaced linear array with element spacing $d$ and progressive phase shift $\beta = -k_0 d \cos\theta_0$, scanned to angle $\theta_0$.

**Step 1 — Element current assumption.**

By the principle of uniform excitation and array symmetry, the currents are:

$$I_n = I_0 e^{-jn\beta} = I_0 e^{jnk_0 d \cos\theta_0}, \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

**Step 2 — Mutual impedance matrix.**

By translation symmetry, $Z_{ij} = Z_{m}(\vert i - j \vert d)$, depending only on the distance between elements $i$ and $j$. Define:

$$Z_{p} = Z_{m}(p d), \quad p = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

with $Z_0 = Z_{ii}$ (self-impedance).

**Step 3 — Active impedance of the $i$-th element.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{a,i} &= Z_{ii} + \sum_{j \neq i} \frac{I_j}{I_i} Z_{ij} \\[4pt]
&= Z_{ii} + \sum_{j \neq i} e^{j(j - i)k_0 d \cos\theta_0}\, Z_{\vert i - j \vert} \\[4pt]
&= Z_{ii} + \sum_{p=1}^{N-1} \left[ e^{jpk_0 d \cos\theta_0} + e^{-jpk_0 d \cos\theta_0} \right] Z_p \quad \text{(for symmetric arrays)} \\[4pt]
&= Z_{ii} + 2 \sum_{p=1}^{N-1} Z_p \cos(p k_0 d \cos\theta_0)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Scan impedance.**

For an infinite array (or as $N \rightarrow \infty$), the active impedance becomes *identical for every element* and depends only on the scan angle. This limiting value is called the **scan impedance** $Z_{s}(\theta_0)$:

$$Z_{s}(\theta_0) = Z_{ii} + 2 \sum_{p=1}^{\infty} Z_p \cos(p k_0 d \cos\theta_0)$$

**Step 5 — Scan blindness condition.**

Scan blindness occurs when the reflection coefficient magnitude $\vert \Gamma(\theta_0) \vert = 1$, i.e., when $\mathrm{Re}\{Z_{s}(\theta_0)\} = 0$ and the imaginary part dominates. This typically arises when a **surface wave mode** of the periodic structure matches the scan condition:

$$k_{\text{sw}} = k_0 \sin\theta_0 + m \frac{2\pi}{d}, \quad m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \dots$$

When this equality is satisfied, energy is bound to the array surface rather than radiated, producing total reflection.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** Two parallel half-wave dipoles ($h = \lambda/4$) are separated by $d = 0.5\lambda$ broadside. Compute the mutual impedance using Carter's formula. Take $\eta_0 = 377\ \Omega$.

**Step 1 — Compute argument values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k d &= \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot 0.5\lambda = \pi \approx 3.1416 \\[4pt]
k h &= \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot 0.25\lambda = \frac{\pi}{2} \approx 1.5708 \\[4pt]
k\sqrt{d^2 + h^2} &= \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \sqrt{(0.5\lambda)^2 + (0.25\lambda)^2} \\[4pt]
&= 2\pi \sqrt{0.25 + 0.0625} = 2\pi \sqrt{0.3125} \approx 3.511
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate Ci and Si functions (using standard tables or software).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Ci}(\pi) &\approx -0.0737 \\[4pt]
\mathrm{Ci}(3.511 - 1.571) = \mathrm{Ci}(1.940) &\approx 0.2367 \\[4pt]
\mathrm{Ci}(3.511 + 1.571) = \mathrm{Ci}(5.082) &\approx -0.1899 \\[4pt]
\mathrm{Si}(\pi) &\approx 1.852 \\[4pt]
\mathrm{Si}(1.940) &\approx 1.633 \\[4pt]
\mathrm{Si}(5.082) &\approx 1.633 \quad \text{(approaches } \pi/2 \text{ asymptotically)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $R_{21}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{21} &= \frac{377}{4\pi} \big[ 2(-0.0737) - 0.2367 - (-0.1899) \big] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{377}{12.566} \big[ -0.1474 - 0.2367 + 0.1899 \big] \\[4pt]
&= 30.0 \times (-0.1942) \approx -5.83\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $X_{21}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{21} &= -\frac{377}{4\pi} \big[ 2(1.852) - 1.633 - 1.633 \big] \\[4pt]
&= -30.0 \times (3.704 - 3.266) \\[4pt]
&= -30.0 \times 0.438 \approx -13.14\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Mutual impedance result.**

$$\boxed{Z_{21} = R_{21} + j X_{21} \approx -5.83 - j\, 13.14\ \Omega}$$

> **Interpretation:** The negative real part indicates that the coupled dipole *delivers* (rather than dissipates) power back into the source — a hallmark of reactive mutual coupling in the near field. The magnitude $\vert Z_{21} \vert \approx 14.4\ \Omega$ is comparable to the self-impedance of a half-wave dipole ($\approx 73 + j\,42.5\ \Omega$), confirming that at $d = 0.5\lambda$, coupling is *significant* and must be accounted for in array design.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Mutual Coupling in a Two-Element Array

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Source Generator 1] --> portA1[Port A Terminals]
    srcB[Source Generator 2] --> portB1[Port B Terminals]
    portA1 --> antA[Antenna Element A Driven]
    portB1 --> antB[Antenna Element B Driven]
    antA -- radiated near field --> coupling1[Coupling Path Z21]
    antA -- radiated near field --> coupling2[Coupling Path Z11 self term]
    antB -- back radiated field --> coupling3[Coupling Path Z12 back to A]
    antB -- re radiated energy --> coupling4[Coupling Path Z22 self term]
    coupling1 --> antB
    coupling3 --> antA
    coupling2 --> zmat[Z Matrix N port Network]
    coupling4 --> zmat
    zmat --> v1[Active Impedance at A]
    zmat --> v2[Active Impedance at B]
    v1 --> patA[Active Element Pattern A]
    v2 --> patB[Active Element Pattern B]
    patA --> farfield[Combined Far Field Pattern]
    patB --> farfield
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Sources of Coupling and Mitigation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    a1[Antenna Element 1] --> n1{Near field region}
    n1 --> n2[Inductive coupling B field dominant]
    n1 --> n3[Capacitive coupling E field dominant]
    n1 --> n4[Surface wave coupling ground plane]
    n1 --> n5[Direct radiation coupling far near boundary]
    a2[Antenna Element 2] --> n1
    n2 --> zmatrix[Z parameter matrix modification]
    n3 --> zmatrix
    n4 --> zmatrix
    n5 --> zmatrix
    zmatrix --> eff1[Effect: Impedance mismatch]
    zmatrix --> eff2[Effect: Pattern distortion]
    zmatrix --> eff3[Effect: Scan blindness]
    zmatrix --> eff4[Effect: Polarization impurity]
    eff1 --> mit1[Mitigation: Decoupling network]
    eff2 --> mit2[Mitigation: EBG or DGS structures]
    eff3 --> mit3[Mitigation: Element spacing optimization]
    eff4 --> mit4[Mitigation: Orthogonal polarization layout]
```

## 4.3 Signal Flow: Active Impedance Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp[Input Geometry and Excitation] --> getZ[Compute Mutual Impedances Zij]
    getZ --> getC[Compute Currents using Network Equation V equals Z times I]
    getC --> getIa[Compute Active Impedance Z active equals Vi divided by Ii]
    getIa --> getPat[Compute Active Element Pattern by superposition]
    getPat --> out[Far field Pattern, Gain, SLL]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Schematic of Two Coupled Dipoles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gen1[Generator V1] --- termA1[Terminal A1]
    termA1 --- dip1[Dipole 1 length L oriented along z axis]
    dip1 --- space[Separation d in free space]
    space --- dip2[Dipole 2 length L oriented along z axis]
    dip2 --- termA2[Terminal A2]
    termA2 --- load[Matched Load Z0]
    space -- mutual Z21 --> dip1
    space -- mutual Z12 --> dip2
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define mutual coupling between antenna elements. List two of its effects on antenna array performance.

**Model Answer:**

**Mutual Coupling Definition:** Mutual coupling is the electromagnetic interaction between two or more antenna elements placed in close proximity, where the current and fields of one element induce currents and modify the boundary conditions on neighboring elements. Mathematically, it is represented by the off-diagonal terms $Z_{ij}$ ($i \neq j$) of the array's impedance matrix.

**Two Effects:**
1. **Input impedance variation** — the driving-point (active) impedance of each element differs from its self-impedance, causing mismatch with the feed network.
2. **Pattern distortion** — the radiation pattern deviates from the ideal pattern obtained by superposition of isolated element patterns, including raised sidelobes and scan blindness at certain scan angles.

*(Any two valid effects accepted; full 3 marks for clear definition + correctly named effects.)*

---

### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** What is the significance of the mutual impedance $Z_{21}$ between two antenna elements? State its unit.

**Model Answer:**

**Significance:** $Z_{21}$ represents the open-circuit voltage induced at the terminals of antenna 2 per unit current flowing at the terminals of antenna 1, when antenna 2 is open-circuited. It quantifies the strength of electromagnetic coupling between the two elements and is essential for computing the active (driving-point) impedance of array elements, predicting scan blindness, and designing matching networks.

**Unit:** The mutual impedance is measured in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**, the same as self-impedance.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q(a).** With the aid of a neat equivalent circuit, derive the expression for mutual impedance between two coupled antenna elements. Explain how the impedance matrix is constructed for an $N$-element array. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Two-Antenna Equivalent Circuit.**

Two coupled antennas form a two-port network. The terminal voltages $V_1$, $V_2$ and terminal currents $I_1$, $I_2$ are related through the self-impedances $Z_{11}$, $Z_{22}$ and mutual impedances $Z_{12}$, $Z_{21}$.

**Step 2 — Definition of Mutual Impedance.**

By definition, the mutual impedance $Z_{21}$ is:

$$Z_{21} = \left. \frac{V_{21}}{I_1} \right|_{I_2 = 0}$$

where $V_{21}$ is the open-circuit voltage induced at antenna 2's terminals when antenna 1 carries current $I_1$ and antenna 2 is open-circuited.

**Step 3 — Derivation via Induced EMF Method.**

Using the vector potential approach, the magnetic vector potential at antenna 2 due to antenna 1's current distribution $\tilde{I}_1(z')$ is:

$$\tilde{A}_{z1} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \tilde{I}_1(z') \frac{e^{-jkR}}{R}\, dz'$$

The corresponding induced electric field is $\tilde{E}_{z1} = -j\omega \tilde{A}_{z1} - \dfrac{j}{\omega \mu_0 \epsilon_0}\dfrac{\partial^2 \tilde{A}_{z1}}{\partial z^2}$.

The open-circuit voltage is:

$$V_{21} = -\frac{1}{I_2} \int \tilde{I}_2(z)\, \tilde{E}_{z1}\, dz$$

Substituting and dividing by $I_1$ yields the explicit formula:

$$Z_{21} = -\frac{1}{I_1 I_2} \int_{-h}^{+h} \int_{-h}^{+h} \tilde{I}_1(z') \tilde{I}_2(z)\, \mathcal{G}(z, z')\, dz'\, dz$$

where $\mathcal{G}$ is the free-space Green's function.

**Step 4 — Specialization to Half-Wave Dipoles.**

For two parallel half-wave dipoles with sinusoidal currents and lateral separation $d$, this integral reduces to Carter's formula (as derived in Section 3.1 above).

**Step 5 — N-Element Impedance Matrix.**

For an $N$-element array, the terminal equations generalize to:

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \\ \vdots \\ V_N \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} & \cdots & Z_{1N} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} & \cdots & Z_{2N} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ Z_{N1} & Z_{N2} & \cdots & Z_{NN} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \\ \vdots \\ I_N \end{bmatrix}$$

By **reciprocity**, $\mathbf{Z}$ is symmetric: $Z_{ij} = Z_{ji}$.

**Step 6 — Active Impedance.**

The active impedance of the $i$-th element is:

$$Z_{a,i} = \frac{V_i}{I_i} = Z_{ii} + \sum_{j \neq i} \frac{I_j}{I_i}\, Z_{ij}$$

**Conclusion:** The mutual impedance matrix fully characterizes inter-element coupling and is the foundation of coupled-array analysis.

---

**Q(b).** For a uniformly excited 4-element linear array with element spacing $d = \lambda/2$ and progressive phase shift $\beta = 90^\circ$, the mutual impedances are given by:
$Z_{11} = 73 + j42.5\ \Omega$,
$Z_{12} = Z_{14} = -5.8 - j13.1\ \Omega$ (adjacent elements),
$Z_{13} = 12.5 - j22.0\ \Omega$ (end-to-end elements).

Compute the active impedance of the central elements (2 and 3). Comment on whether feed network mismatch is a concern if all elements are connected to $50\ \Omega$ feeds. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Current Ratios.**

The element currents follow the uniform excitation $I_n = I_0 e^{-jn\beta}$ with $\beta = 90^\circ = \pi/2$. Thus:

$$\frac{I_2}{I_2} = 1,\quad \frac{I_3}{I_3} = 1,\quad \frac{I_1}{I_2} = e^{+j\pi/2} = j,\quad \frac{I_4}{I_2} = e^{-j\pi/2} = -j$$

**Step 2 — Active Impedance of Element 2.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{a,2} &= Z_{22} + \frac{I_1}{I_2} Z_{21} + \frac{I_3}{I_2} Z_{23} + \frac{I_4}{I_2} Z_{24} \\[4pt]
&= (73 + j42.5) + (j)(-5.8 - j13.1) + (1)(12.5 - j22.0) + (-j)(-5.8 - j13.1) \\[4pt]
&= 73 + j42.5 + (-j5.8 + 13.1 \cdot j^2 / \text{wait}) \dots \text{recompute carefully}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 (corrected) — Active Impedance of Element 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{a,2} &= Z_{22} + \frac{I_1}{I_2} Z_{21} + \frac{I_3}{I_2} Z_{23} + \frac{I_4}{I_2} Z_{24} \\[6pt]
\frac{I_1}{I_2} Z_{21} &= (j)(-5.8 - j13.1) = -j5.8 - j^2 13.1 = +13.1 - j5.8 \\[6pt]
\frac{I_3}{I_2} Z_{23} &= (1)(12.5 - j22.0) = 12.5 - j22.0 \\[6pt]
\frac{I_4}{I_2} Z_{24} &= (-j)(-5.8 - j13.1) = +j5.8 + j^2 13.1 = -13.1 + j5.8
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{a,2} &= (73 + 13.1 + 12.5 - 13.1) + j(42.5 - 5.8 - 22.0 + 5.8) \\[4pt]
&= 85.5 + j20.5\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Active Impedance of Element 3.**

By symmetry, $Z_{a,3} = Z_{a,2} = 85.5 + j20.5\ \Omega$.

**Step 4 — Reflection Coefficient with $50\ \Omega$ Feed.**

$$\Gamma = \frac{Z_{a,2} - 50}{Z_{a,2} + 50} = \frac{(85.5 - 50) + j20.5}{(85.5 + 50) + j20.5} = \frac{35.5 + j20.5}{135.5 + j20.5}$$

$$\vert \Gamma \vert \approx \frac{\sqrt{35.5^2 + 20.5^2}}{\sqrt{135.5^2 + 20.5^2}} = \frac{\sqrt{1680}}{\sqrt{18770}} \approx \frac{41.0}{137.0} \approx 0.299$$

Return loss $\approx 20 \log_{10}(0.299) \approx -10.5\ \text{dB}$, and VSWR $\approx 1.85$.

**Step 5 — Comment.**

The active impedance of the central elements ($85.5 + j20.5\ \Omega$) deviates significantly from $50\ \Omega$. A return loss of only about $-10.5\ \text{dB}$ implies roughly **11% power reflected** at each feed. Although this is sometimes acceptable, **for high-performance phased arrays**, a matching network (e.g., a small L-section LC network) should be added at each central element to bring $\Gamma$ below $-20\ \text{dB}$. Therefore, **feed network mismatch is a real concern** and must be addressed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q(a).** Discuss in detail the effects of mutual coupling on antenna array performance. Explain the phenomenon of scan blindness with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction.**

Mutual coupling modifies the current distribution, input impedance, and radiation characteristics of each element relative to its isolated state.

**Step 2 — Effect 1: Input Impedance Variation.**

The driving-point (active) impedance of element $i$ in a coupled array is:

$$Z_{a,i} = Z_{ii} + \sum_{j \neq i} \frac{I_j}{I_i}\, Z_{ij}$$

For a uniformly excited array scanned to angle $\theta_0$, the active impedance is a function of scan angle. At scan angles where $\mathrm{Re}\{Z_a\} = 0$, the mismatch is severe.

**Step 3 — Effect 2: Pattern Distortion.**

The far-field pattern of an array is the superposition of **active element patterns** (AEPs), not isolated element patterns. The AEP includes the effect of all other elements being present. Sidelobe levels rise, nulls fill in, and main-beam gain decreases compared to the ideal pattern.

**Step 4 — Effect 3: Scan Blindness.**

Scan blindness is the catastrophic phenomenon where, at a specific scan angle, the array reflection coefficient $\vert \Gamma \vert \rightarrow 1$ and essentially no power is radiated.

**Physical Mechanism:** Mutual coupling excites a **surface wave** along the array structure. When the scan condition satisfies:

$$k_0 \sin\theta_0 = k_{\text{sw}} - m \frac{2\pi}{d}$$

where $k_{\text{sw}}$ is the surface-wave wavenumber and $m$ is an integer, energy is bound to the surface rather than radiated.

**Diagram (conceptual):** A phased array, when scanned toward $\theta_0$, normally beams energy outward. At scan blindness, the energy is trapped as a slow surface wave traveling along the array aperture, never leaving the structure — visually analogous to total internal reflection in optics.

**Step 5 — Effect 4: Polarization Distortion.**

Coupling introduces cross-polarized components. For circularly polarized arrays, the axial ratio degrades.

**Step 6 — Effect 5: Gain and Efficiency Reduction.**

Power absorbed in parasitic elements or lost to surface waves reduces the radiated power and hence the array gain.

**Conclusion:** Mutual coupling is not a secondary concern; it can render a carefully designed array completely non-functional at certain scan angles (scan blindness) and is a fundamental design driver in phased-array engineering.

---

**Q(b).** Two parallel half-wave dipoles of length $L = \lambda/2$ are placed broadside at a separation of $d = 0.25\lambda$. Using Carter's mutual impedance formula, evaluate the mutual impedance $Z_{21}$. Take $\eta_0 = 377\ \Omega$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometry Setup.**

Half-length: $h = L/2 = \lambda/4$, so $kh = \pi/2 \approx 1.5708$.
Separation: $kd = (2\pi/\lambda)(0.25\lambda) = \pi/2 \approx 1.5708$.

**Step 2 — Auxiliary Quantity.**

$$
\sqrt{d^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{(0.25\lambda)^2 + (0.25\lambda)^2} = 0.25\lambda \sqrt{2} \approx 0.3536\lambda
$$

So:

$$k\sqrt{d^2 + h^2} = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot 0.3536\lambda \approx 2.221$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Two Composite Arguments.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k\sqrt{d^2 + h^2} - kh &\approx 2.221 - 1.571 = 0.650 \\[4pt]
k\sqrt{d^2 + h^2} + kh &\approx 2.221 + 1.571 = 3.792
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Ci and Si at these arguments (using standard numerical tables).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Ci}(kd) = \mathrm{Ci}(1.571) &\approx 0.472 \\
\mathrm{Ci}(0.650) &\approx 0.241 \\
\mathrm{Ci}(3.792) &\approx -0.066 \\
\mathrm{Si}(kd) = \mathrm{Si}(1.571) &\approx 1.467 \\
\mathrm{Si}(0.650) &\approx 0.614 \\
\mathrm{Si}(3.792) &\approx 1.629
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $R_{21}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{21} &= \frac{377}{4\pi} \big[ 2(0.472) - 0.241 - (-0.066) \big] \\[4pt]
&= 30.0 \times (0.944 - 0.241 + 0.066) \\[4pt]
&= 30.0 \times 0.769 \approx 23.07\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $X_{21}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{21} &= -\frac{377}{4\pi} \big[ 2(1.467) - 0.614 - 1.629 \big] \\[4pt]
&= -30.0 \times (2.934 - 0.614 - 1.629) \\[4pt]
&= -30.0 \times 0.691 \approx -20.73\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Final Result.**

$$\boxed{Z_{21} = 23.07 - j\,20.73\ \Omega \approx 30.97 \angle -42.0^\circ\ \Omega}$$

**Interpretation:** At $d = 0.25\lambda$ (very close spacing), the mutual coupling is *very strong* — the magnitude $\vert Z_{21} \vert \approx 31\ \Omega$ is comparable to the self-impedance of the dipole ($73 + j42.5\ \Omega$). The negative reactance indicates a *capacitive* (E-field-dominated) coupling regime typical of the near field. Such strong coupling would severely distort the array pattern and prevent independent element excitation; this is why practical arrays usually operate with $d \geq 0.5\lambda$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing self-impedance with active impedance:** The self-impedance $Z_{ii}$ is the impedance of one element in isolation; the *active* (driving-point) impedance $Z_{a,i}$ includes the loading effect of all other elements. Examiners frequently pose questions that require the latter — failure to use the correct formula costs **3 marks** easily.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting reciprocity:** In the impedance matrix, $Z_{ij} = Z_{ji}$. Failing to exploit symmetry in numerical problems leads to unnecessary computation and is often penalized.
> 
> 3. **Sign error in Carter's formula:** The mutual impedance for two parallel dipoles has a *negative* reactance in the broadside, near-field configuration. Students frequently misquote the formula with the wrong sign for $X_{21}$. **Memorize Carter's formula with signs intact.**
> 
> 4. **Ignoring scan blindness:** When asked about "effects of mutual coupling," many students list only pattern distortion and skip scan blindness — this is a guaranteed **2-mark loss** because scan blindness is the most dramatic and exam-favored consequence.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting units:** Mutual impedance is in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**, coupling coefficient in **dB** or dimensionless. Mismatch of units in numerical answers is a recurring penalty.
> 
> 6. **Treating $S_{21}$ and coupling coefficient interchangeably:** $S_{21}$ is the matched-port transmission coefficient; coupling coefficient in the antenna-array sense often refers to embedded element pattern overlap. State definitions explicitly to avoid ambiguity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Coupling in Antenna Arrays**

### **Core Concepts**
- ☐ Mutual coupling is the electromagnetic interaction between nearby antenna elements due to overlapping near-fields.
- ☐ It is mathematically represented by the **off-diagonal terms** $Z_{ij}$ ($i \neq j$) of the $N \times N$ impedance matrix.
- ☐ By **reciprocity**, the matrix $\mathbf{Z}$ is symmetric: $Z_{ij} = Z_{ji}$.
- ☐ The **active (driving-point) impedance** of element $i$ is $Z_{a,i} = Z_{ii} + \sum_{j \neq i} (I_j/I_i)\, Z_{ij}$.
- ☐ The **active element pattern (AEP)** — *not* the isolated element pattern — is the correct building block for array far-field analysis when coupling is present.

### **Key Formulae (Memorize for KTU ESE)**
- ☐ Two-port coupled equations: $V_1 = Z_{11}I_1 + Z_{12}I_2$, $V_2 = Z_{21}I_1 + Z_{22}I_2$.
- ☐ Definition of mutual impedance: $Z_{21} = V_{21}/I_1 \big\vert_{I_2 = 0}$.
- ☐ **Carter's formula** for two parallel half-wave dipoles (Section 3.1).
- ☐ Scan impedance: $Z_s(\theta_0) = Z_{ii} + 2 \sum_{p=1}^{\infty} Z_p \cos(pk_0 d \cos\theta_0)$.
- ☐ Scan blindness condition: $k_0 \sin\theta_0 = k_{\text{sw}} - m(2\pi/d)$.
- ☐ Coupling in dB: $\vert S_{21} \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\vert S_{21}\vert$.

### **Effects of Mutual Coupling (Memorize All Five)**
- ☐ Input impedance variation
- ☐ Radiation pattern distortion (sidelobe rise, main-beam shift)
- ☐ **Scan blindness** at critical angles
- ☐ Polarization distortion (axial ratio degradation)
- ☐ Gain / efficiency reduction

### **Mitigation Techniques (Memorize at Least Three)**
- ☐ Element spacing optimization ($d \geq 0.5\lambda$ typical)
- ☐ Decoupling networks (lumped/distributed)
- ☐ EBG structures (periodic high-impedance surfaces)
- ☐ Defected Ground Structures (DGS)
- ☐ Orthogonal polarization between adjacent elements
- ☐ Metamaterial shields

### **Practical Engineering Numbers to Remember**
- ☐ Free-space intrinsic impedance: $\eta_0 \approx 377\ \Omega$ (or $\approx 120\pi\ \Omega$).
- ☐ Half-wave dipole self-impedance: $Z_{11} \approx 73 + j\,42.5\ \Omega$.
- ☐ At $d = 0.5\lambda$, mutual impedance of half-wave dipoles: $Z_{21} \approx -5.8 - j\,13.1\ \Omega$.
- ☐ At $d = 0.25\lambda$, mutual impedance is *comparable* to self-impedance — strongly coupled.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circular Polarisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Circular Polarisation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Circular Polarisation** is a special, degenerate case of *elliptical polarisation* in which the tip of the instantaneous electric field vector $\vec{E}(z,t)$ of a uniform plane wave, observed at a fixed spatial point, traces a **perfect circle** in the plane perpendicular to the direction of propagation as time progresses. For a wave travelling along the $+z$ direction, the two orthogonal spatial components of the electric field must satisfy **two simultaneous conditions** for the polarisation to be strictly circular: 1. **Equal Magnitudes (Amplitude Condition):** $$\vert E_{0x} \vert \;=\; \vert E_{0y} \vert \;=\; E_0$$ 2. **Quadrature Phase Condition:** $$\Delta\phi \;=\; \phi_y - \phi_x \;=\; \pm 90^{\circ} \;=\; \pm \dfrac{\pi}{2}\ \text{rad}$$ The "+" sign yields **Right-Hand Circular Polarisation (RHCP)**, and the "–" sign yields **Left-Hand Circular Polarisation (LHCP)**, as per the **IEEE Standard for Letter Designations for Polarisation (IEEE Std 145-2013)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** A common error is to call any rotating E-field "circular." Board examiners specifically check whether you state **BOTH** the amplitude-equal AND quadrature-phase conditions explicitly. Skipping either condition costs marks. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spinning Clock Hand" Intuition Imagine you are standing in front of a giant analogue wall clock, looking straight at the face. The clock's **second hand** is like the tip of the electric field vector at a fixed point in space. - If the second hand moves at a **constant angular speed** and its **length never changes**, it traces a perfect circle — this is **circular polarisation**. - The clock's face represents the $xy$-plane (perpendicular to the wave's direction of travel). - Whether the hand spins clockwise or anticlockwise determines **Right-Hand** or **Left-Hand** circular polarisation. In contrast, an **elliptical** polarisation is like a clock whose second hand changes length as it spins, and **linear** polarisation is like a pendulum that only swings left-right along a single line. > [!NOTE] > **Why is this useful in real engineering?** The spinning nature of the E-field makes the wave **immune to Faraday rotation** in the ionosphere and to orientation mismatches between the transmitting and receiving antennas — a property heavily exploited in **satellite TV (DTH), GPS, and deep-space telemetry** (e.g., ISRO's INSAT and NASA's deep-space network). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Component-Wave Decomposition — The "Building Block" Logic Any arbitrarily polarised plane wave propagating along $+z$ can be written as the **superposition of two orthogonal linearly polarised TEM waves**: $$ \vec{E}(z,t) \;=\; \hat{a}_x\, E_{0x}\cos\!\bigl(\omega t - kz + \phi_x\bigr) \;+\; \hat{a}_y\, E_{0y}\cos\!\bigl(\omega t - kz + \phi_y\bigr) $$ Step-by-step logical interpretation: - **Step 1 — Spatial Axes:** The wave uses the $\hat{a}_x$ and $\hat{a}_y$ basis because these are mutually orthogonal unit vectors lying in the plane transverse to propagation (the $xy$-plane). - **Step 2 — Independent Amplitudes:** $E_{0x}$ and $E_{0y}$ are the *peak* magnitudes of the $x$ and $y$ components, generally unequal. - **Step 3 — Independent Phases:** $\phi_x$ and $\phi_y$ are the *initial* phase angles of the two components, which can differ. - **Step 4 — The Polarisation State Encodes the Difference:** It is **only the difference** $\Delta\phi = \phi_y - \phi_x$ that determines the polarisation shape, not the absolute phases. > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** Antenna designers (e.g., at ISRO's Space Applications Centre or Boeing's antenna division) use the relative-phase control of two orthogonal feeds (such as crossed dipoles or a turnstile junction) to *engineer* any desired polarisation — linear, circular, or elliptical — on demand. ### 2.2 The Three Polarisation Regimes — Decision Flowchart | Parameter | Linear | Circular | Elliptical (general) | |---|---|---|---| | Amplitude ratio $E_{0y}/E_{0x}$ | Any value | $\textbf{1}$ (equal) | Any $\neq 1$ | | Phase difference $\Delta\phi$ | $0$ or $\pi$ (integer multiple of $180^{\circ}$) | $\pm \pi/2$ ($\pm 90^{\circ}$) | Any other value | | E-field tip traces | Straight line | **Perfect circle** | Tilted ellipse | | Axial Ratio (AR) | $\infty$ | $\textbf{1}$ (ideal) | $> 1$ | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready) | # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_x = E_0 \cos(\omega t - kz)$ | $x$-component, no initial phase | Reference component | | 2 | $E_y = E_0 \cos(\omega t - kz \pm \pi/2)$ | $y$-component, $\pm 90^{\circ}$ shift | Quadrature excitation | | 3 | $E_y = \mp E_0 \sin(\omega t - kz)$ | After trig. identity | Eliminating $t$ for tip locus | | 4 | $E_x^2 + E_y^2 = E_0^2$ | Equation of a **circle** of radius $E_0$ | Proof of circularity | | 5 | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{\text{major axis}}{\text{minor axis}} = 1$ | Axial Ratio for ideal CP | Polarisation purity metric | | 6 | RHCP: $\Delta\phi = -90^{\circ}$ (E_y **lags** E_x) | Per IEEE convention | US satellite (GPS) RHCP | | 7 | LHCP: $\Delta\phi = +90^{\circ}$ (E_y **leads** E_x) | Per IEEE convention | Indian DTH (DD Direct+) LHCP | | 8 | Instantaneous Poynting vector $\vec{S} = \hat{a}_z \dfrac{E_0^2}{\eta_0}$ | Time-averaged $\langle S \rangle = \dfrac{E_0^2}{2\eta_0}$ | Constant magnitude ⇒ CP carries constant power | | 9 | Linear-to-Circular (LTC) network: hybrid-tee + $\lambda/4$ line | Hardware implementation | Practical CP feed network | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** The handedness convention in the IEEE standard is **opposite** to the optics/physics convention. In KTU examinations, always follow the **IEEE/engineering convention** unless explicitly told otherwise. Writing the wrong handedness can lose up to **2 marks** in a 14-mark problem. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Proving the E-Field Tip Traces a Circle **Starting Statement:** Consider a uniform plane wave propagating in the $+z$ direction whose electric field is the superposition of two orthogonal linearly polarised components with equal amplitude $E_0$ and a phase difference of $\pm 90^{\circ}$. **Step 1:** Write the two component fields explicitly. $$ E_x(z,t) \;=\; E_0 \cos(\omega t - kz) $$ $$ E_y(z,t) \;=\; E_0 \cos(\omega t - kz + \Delta\phi), \quad \Delta\phi = \pm \dfrac{\pi}{2} $$ **Step 2:** Choose a fixed observation point. Without loss of generality, set $z = 0$ (the wavefront at the origin), so the spatial term $-kz$ vanishes. $$ E_x(0,t) \;=\; E_0 \cos(\omega t) $$ $$ E_y(0,t) \;=\; E_0 \cos(\omega t \pm \pi/2) \;=\; \mp E_0 \sin(\omega t) $$ *(Reasoning: $\cos(\theta \pm \pi/2) = \mp\sin\theta$ — the standard trig. complementary-angle identity.)* **Step 3:** Define the new shorthand $\theta \equiv \omega t$. Then we have the parametric equations of the field tip: $$ E_x(\theta) \;=\; E_0 \cos\theta $$ $$ E_y(\theta) \;=\; \mp E_0 \sin\theta $$ **Step 4:** Eliminate the parameter $\theta$ using the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$: $$ \left(\dfrac{E_x}{E_0}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{E_y}{E_0}\right)^2 \;=\; \cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta \;=\; 1 $$ **Step 5:** Therefore, in the $E_x$–$E_y$ plane, the locus satisfies: $$ \boxed{\,E_x^2 + E_y^2 \;=\; E_0^2\,} $$ **Step 6 — Geometric Interpretation:** This is the **standard equation of a circle** of radius $E_0$ centred at the origin. Hence the E-field tip traces a perfect circle of radius $E_0$. $\quad\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Direction of Rotation (Handedness) We need to determine *which way* the tip rotates for a given sign of $\Delta\phi$. Evaluate the E-field at four quarter-cycle instants ($\theta = 0, \pi/2, \pi, 3\pi/2$) at the fixed plane $z = 0$. | Time / $\theta = \omega t$ | $E_x = E_0\cos\theta$ | $E_y = E_0\cos(\theta + \pi/2) = -E_0\sin\theta$ | $E_y = E_0\cos(\theta - \pi/2) = +E_0\sin\theta$ | |---|---|---|---| | $0$ | $+E_0$ | $0$ | $0$ | | $\pi/2$ | $0$ | $-E_0$ | $+E_0$ | | $\pi$ | $-E_0$ | $0$ | $0$ | | $3\pi/2$ | $0$ | $+E_0$ | $-E_0$ | **Column A — $\Delta\phi = +\pi/2$ (E_y leads E_x by $90^{\circ}$):** The tip moves from $(E_0, 0) \to (0, -E_0) \to (-E_0, 0) \to (0, +E_0) \to (E_0, 0)$. Observing the wave coming **towards you** (looking in the $-\hat{a}_z$ direction), this is a **clockwise** rotation. By the **IEEE handedness rule** (thumb in propagation direction, fingers curl in rotation sense), this is **LHCP**. **Column B — $\Delta\phi = -\pi/2$ (E_y lags E_x by $90^{\circ}$):** The tip moves from $(E_0, 0) \to (0, +E_0) \to (-E_0, 0) \to (0, -E_0) \to (E_0, 0)$. Counter-clockwise rotation when viewed looking into the wave. This is **RHCP**. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Time-Average Poynting Vector (Why CP Carries Constant Power) For a linearly polarised wave, the instantaneous Poynting vector $S(z,t) = E_x^2/\eta_0$ *pulses* (it is zero twice per cycle). For CP, the *sum* of the squared components is constant. $$ \vec{S}(t) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\eta_0}\,\bigl(E_x^2 + E_y^2\bigr)\,\hat{a}_z \;=\; \dfrac{E_0^2}{\eta_0}\,\bigl(\cos^2\omega t + \sin^2\omega t\bigr)\,\hat{a}_z \;=\; \dfrac{E_0^2}{\eta_0}\,\hat{a}_z $$ The instantaneous Poynting vector is **constant** — no pulsing. This is one of the engineering reasons CP is favoured in **pulsed radar and digital communication systems** where power consistency is critical. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — What Happens When Conditions Are Violated | Violation | Resulting Polarisation | Engineering Implication | |---|---|---| | $E_{0x} = E_{0y}$, $\Delta\phi = 0$ | **Linear** (along $45^{\circ}$ axis) | No rotation; classic linear feed | | $E_{0x} = E_{0y}$, $\Delta\phi = \pi$ | **Linear** (along $-45^{\circ}$ axis) | Crossed-dipole anti-phase feed | | $E_{0x} \neq E_{0y}$, $\Delta\phi = \pi/2$ | **Elliptical** (axis-aligned ellipse) | Quadrature feed with unequal arms | | $E_{0x} = E_{0y}$, $\Delta\phi = \pi/3$ | **Elliptical** (tilted ellipse) | Partial quadrature; bad purity | | $E_{0x} = E_{0y}$, $\Delta\phi = \pi/2$ | **Circular** (ideal) | Desired outcome | | $E_{0x} = E_{0y}$, $\Delta\phi = \pi/2 \pm \delta$ | **Elliptical** with AR = 1/cos $\delta$ | Phase error degrades AR | > [!TIP] > **Practical Insight:** In real feeds, the phase error $\delta$ is rarely zero. A common KTU numerical question is: *"If the feed phase error is $5^{\circ}$, what is the resulting Axial Ratio?"* Answer: $\text{AR} = 1/\cos 5^{\circ} \approx 1.0038$ dB $\approx 0.033$ dB — usually acceptable for satellite work. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generation of Circular Polarisation from Two Orthogonal Linear Feeds ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeS[Source RF Signal
f0 reference oscillator] --> nodeSP[90 Degree Power Splitter
Hybrid Tee or Branch Line Coupler] nodeSP --> nodeD1[Branch A
0 degree phase path] nodeSP --> nodeD2[Branch B
Quadrature phase shifted path] nodeD1 --> nodeA1[Dipole 1
orientated along X axis
radiates E_x field] nodeD2 --> nodeA2[Dipole 2
orientated along Y axis
radiates E_y field] nodeA1 --> nodeR[Radiated Field in Far Zone
superposition point on z axis] nodeA2 --> nodeR nodeR --> nodeP{Handedness Selection} nodeP --> nodeL[LHCP output
if E_y leads E_x by 90 deg] nodeP --> nodeN[RHCP output
if E_y lags E_x by 90 deg] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Analytical Path from Wave Equation to Circular Locus ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeW[Uniform Plane Wave
propagating in plus z direction] --> nodeD[Decompose into
orthogonal E_x and E_y components] nodeD --> nodeC1{Check Amplitude
E_0x equals E_0y?} nodeC1 -->|No| nodeEL[Result is Elliptical Polarisation
with axis aligned to x and y] nodeC1 -->|Yes| nodeC2{Check Phase
delta phi equals 90 deg?} nodeC2 -->|No| nodeE2[Result is Elliptical
tilted from x y axes] nodeC2 -->|Yes| nodeC3{Which sign
of phase shift?} nodeC3 -->|negative ninety deg| nodeRHCP[Result is RHCP
Right Hand Circular Polarisation] nodeC3 -->|positive ninety deg| nodeLHCP[Result is LHCP
Left Hand Circular Polarisation] ``` ### 4.3 Physical Schematic Description (Text-Based Rendering) Since Mermaid cannot directly render the rotating E-field vector, the following block diagrams describe the physical structure of a **crossed-dipole turnstile antenna** — the canonical CP radiator: - **Element 1:** Half-wave dipole along the $x$-axis, fed at its centre. - **Element 2:** Identical half-wave dipole along the $y$-axis, fed at its centre. - **Feed Network:** A 90° hybrid ring (or rat-race coupler) splits the input RF into two equal-amplitude, $90^{\circ}$ phase-shifted outputs, one to each dipole. - **Resulting Aperture:** The two crossed dipoles radiate orthogonal linear fields; their superposition in the broadside direction ($+z$) produces a circularly polarised wave. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to *"sketch a CP antenna arrangement"*, the standard answer is either a **crossed-dipole with quadrature feed** or a **helical antenna** (axial-mode helix with circumference $\approx \lambda$ and pitch $\approx \lambda/4$). Both are board-favourite diagrams. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define circular polarisation. State the two necessary conditions for a wave to be circularly polarised.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Circular polarisation is the polarisation state of a plane wave in which the tip of the electric field vector traces a perfect circle in the plane transverse to the direction of propagation, at a constant angular velocity. The two necessary conditions are: 1. The two orthogonal components must have **equal amplitudes**: $E_{0x} = E_{0y} = E_0$. 2. The two orthogonal components must be in **time-phase quadrature** (i.e., $90^{\circ}$ out of phase): $\Delta\phi = \phi_y - \phi_x = \pm \pi/2 \ \text{rad}$. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 each for the two conditions)** --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between RHCP and LHCP. State one application where circular polarisation is preferred over linear.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **RHCP (Right-Hand Circular Polarisation):** The E-field vector rotates **clockwise** when viewed from the direction *towards which the wave is propagating* (i.e., the receiver looks *into* the oncoming wave). Per IEEE convention, $E_y$ **lags** $E_x$ by $90^{\circ}$. - **LHCP (Left-Hand Circular Polarisation):** The E-field vector rotates **counter-clockwise** when viewed from the same direction. Per IEEE convention, $E_y$ **leads** $E_x$ by $90^{\circ}$. - **Application:** **Satellite communication / GPS** — CP is preferred because it eliminates the need to align the polarisation of the ground antenna with that of the satellite, and it is immune to Faraday rotation in the ionosphere. **(3 marks — 1 each for the two definitions, 1 for the application with justification)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Forgetting to specify the IEEE handedness convention** — Examiners in Kerala frequently mark the convention as a *separate sub-step*. Always state *"as per IEEE convention"* explicitly. > - **Confusing LHCP/RHCP direction with the observer's viewpoint** — Specify whether you are looking *with* or *against* the propagation direction. This is a 2-mark trap. > - **Skipping the $\cos^2 + \sin^2 = 1$ derivation step** — The board expects to see the elimination of the time parameter, not just the final circle equation. Loss of up to 3 marks if skipped. > - **Using the optics handedness convention** — If you use the physics convention (where LHCP has $E_y$ lagging $E_x$), you will be marked *incorrect* on a 2024-scheme paper unless explicitly instructed. > - **Failing to draw the unit circle / E-field tip diagram** — A missing figure can cost 2 marks even if the maths is perfect. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Starting from the general expression of a plane wave with two orthogonal electric field components, derive the conditions for **circular polarisation** and show that the E-field tip traces a circle of radius $E_0$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two crossed half-wave dipoles are oriented along the $x$ and $y$ axes and fed with equal-amplitude signals. The $y$-dipole is fed with a phase that is $+90^{\circ}$ ahead of the $x$-dipole. Determine the sense of polarisation (LHCP or RHCP per IEEE) and justify with a tip-rotation table. **(7 marks)** ##### **Model Solution to Question A:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — General wave expression (1 mark):** $$ \vec{E}(z,t) = \hat{a}_x E_{0x}\cos(\omega t - kz + \phi_x) + \hat{a}_y E_{0y}\cos(\omega t - kz + \phi_y) $$ **Step 2 — Apply conditions for circular polarisation (2 marks):** - Condition 1: $E_{0x} = E_{0y} = E_0$ (equal amplitudes) - Condition 2: $\Delta\phi = \phi_y - \phi_x = \pm \pi/2$ (quadrature phase) **Step 3 — Evaluate at $z = 0$ (1 mark):** $$ E_x(t) = E_0\cos(\omega t), \qquad E_y(t) = E_0\cos(\omega t \pm \pi/2) = \mp E_0\sin(\omega t) $$ **Step 4 — Eliminate $t$ to obtain the locus (2 marks):** $$ \left(\dfrac{E_x}{E_0}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{E_y}{E_0}\right)^2 = \cos^2\omega t + \sin^2\omega t = 1 $$ **Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):** $$ \boxed{\,E_x^2 + E_y^2 = E_0^2\,} $$ This is the equation of a circle of radius $E_0$ centred at the origin in the $E_x$–$E_y$ plane, confirming circular polarisation. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Identify the phase relationship (1 mark):** $\phi_y - \phi_x = +90^{\circ}$ (E_y leads E_x by $90^{\circ}$). **Step 2 — Write the field components at $z = 0$ (1 mark):** $$ E_x(t) = E_0\cos(\omega t), \quad E_y(t) = E_0\cos(\omega t + \pi/2) = -E_0\sin(\omega t) $$ **Step 3 — Construct the tip-rotation table (3 marks):** | $\omega t$ | $E_x$ | $E_y$ | Tip position | |---|---|---|---| | $0$ | $+E_0$ | $0$ | $(+E_0, 0)$ — start | | $\pi/2$ | $0$ | $-E_0$ | $(0, -E_0)$ | | $\pi$ | $-E_0$ | $0$ | $(-E_0, 0)$ | | $3\pi/2$ | $0$ | $+E_0$ | $(0, +E_0)$ | **Step 4 — Determine rotation direction (1 mark):** The tip moves in the sequence $(+E_0, 0) \to (0, -E_0) \to (-E_0, 0) \to (0, +E_0) \to$ back. When an observer looks *into* the oncoming wave (i.e., looking in the $-\hat{a}_z$ direction, towards the source), this path traces **clockwise** rotation. **Step 5 — Conclude using IEEE convention (1 mark):** By the IEEE handedness rule, a clockwise rotation viewed looking *into* the wave corresponds to **LHCP (Left-Hand Circular Polarisation)**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]** **(a)** With reference to the IEEE convention, define **RHCP** and **LHCP**. Explain the role of the **Axial Ratio (AR)** in characterising polarisation purity, and state its ideal value for circular polarisation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A crossed-dipole antenna radiates a wave whose E-field components at $z = 0$ are $E_x = 5\cos(\omega t)\ \text{V/m}$ and $E_y = 5\cos(\omega t - 60^{\circ})\ \text{V/m}$. Determine the polarisation state, the Axial Ratio, and the orientation angle of the ellipse. **(7 marks)** ##### **Model Solution to Question B:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — RHCP definition (1.5 marks):** Per IEEE convention, **RHCP** is the polarisation state in which the E-field vector, observed at a fixed point and looking *into* the direction from which the wave is coming (i.e., against the propagation direction), rotates in the **counter-clockwise** sense. Equivalently, the E-field traces a right-handed helix along the propagation direction. **Step 2 — LHCP definition (1.5 marks):** Per IEEE convention, **LHCP** is the polarisation state in which the E-field vector, observed at a fixed point looking into the oncoming wave, rotates in the **clockwise** sense. Equivalently, the E-field traces a left-handed helix along the propagation direction. **Step 3 — Axial Ratio definition (2 marks):** The Axial Ratio (AR) is the ratio of the major axis to the minor axis of the polarisation ellipse: $$ \text{AR} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{major axis length}}{\text{minor axis length}} $$ It is expressed in linear units or in decibels: $\text{AR (dB)} = 20\log_{10}(\text{AR})$. **Step 4 — Role in purity characterisation (1 mark):** AR = 1 indicates a perfect circle (ideal circular polarisation); AR = $\infty$ indicates linear polarisation; $1 < \text{AR} < \infty$ indicates elliptical polarisation. Thus AR is the *figure of merit* for polarisation purity. **Step 5 — Ideal value (1 mark):** For *ideal* circular polarisation, $\boxed{\text{AR} = 1 \;(0\ \text{dB})}$. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** $E_{0x} = E_{0y} = 5\ \text{V/m}$, $\Delta\phi = -60^{\circ}$ (E_y lags E_x by $60^{\circ}$). **Step 1 — Check polarisation type (1 mark):** Amplitudes are equal, but phase difference is $-60^{\circ}$, **not** $\pm 90^{\circ}$. Hence the polarisation is **elliptical**, not circular. **Step 2 — Axial Ratio calculation (3 marks):** For equal amplitudes ($E_{0x} = E_{0y} = E_0$) and arbitrary phase $\Delta\phi$, the AR is given by: $$ \text{AR} \;=\; \left\vert \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\pi/2 - \vert \Delta\phi \vert}{2}\right) \right\vert $$ Wait — for *equal amplitudes* the major and minor axes correspond to the half-sum and half-difference evaluated at the ellipse extremes. The standard textbook formula for AR is: $$ \text{AR} \;=\; \dfrac{E_{0x} + E_{0y}}{\vert E_{0x} - E_{0y} \vert} \quad \text{only if } \Delta\phi = \pm\pi/2 $$ Since $\Delta\phi \neq \pm\pi/2$, we must use the **general AR formula** for equal amplitudes: $$ \text{AR} \;=\; \cot\!\left(\dfrac{\vert \Delta\phi \vert}{2}\right) \;=\; \cot\!\left(\dfrac{60^{\circ}}{2}\right) \;=\; \cot(30^{\circ}) \;=\; \sqrt{3} \;\approx\; 1.732 $$ In dB: $\text{AR (dB)} = 20\log_{10}(1.732) \approx 4.77\ \text{dB}$. **Step 3 — Orientation angle (tilt of ellipse) (2 marks):** For equal amplitudes, the orientation (tilt) of the ellipse's major axis is given by: $$ \tau \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2E_{0x}E_{0y}\cos\Delta\phi}{E_{0x}^2 - E_{0y}^2}\right) $$ Since $E_{0x}^2 = E_{0y}^2$, the denominator is zero, so $\tau = \pm 45^{\circ}$ (the ellipse is tilted at $\pm 45^{\circ}$ to the $x$-axis, with the sign depending on whether $\Delta\phi$ is positive or negative). For $\Delta\phi = -60^{\circ}$, $\tau = +45^{\circ}$. **Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):** The wave is **left-tilted elliptical** with major-axis orientation $45^{\circ}$, axial ratio $1.732$ ($4.77$ dB). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Circular polarisation requires BOTH equal amplitudes AND $90^{\circ}$ phase difference** between two orthogonal components — never one without the other. - ✅ **IEEE Handedness Rule:** Thumb points in the direction of propagation; fingers curl in the direction of E-field rotation. This is the **engineering convention** used in all KTU 2024-scheme papers. - ✅ **Equation of the locus:** $E_x^2 + E_y^2 = E_0^2$ — the standard circle of radius $E_0$ in the $E_x$–$E_y$ plane. - ✅ **Axial Ratio = 1 (0 dB)** is the ideal CP figure of merit; any deviation from $1$ means the polarisation is *elliptical*, not strictly circular. - ✅ **E_y leads E_x by $+90^{\circ}$** $\Rightarrow$ **LHCP** (per IEEE). - ✅ **E_y lags E_x by $-90^{\circ}$** $\Rightarrow$ **RHCP** (per IEEE). - ✅ **Poynting vector is constant** for CP — no power pulsing, unlike linear polarisation. - ✅ **Applications:** GPS, satellite DTH, deep-space communication, weather radar, RFID, mobile base stations — all prefer CP for *orientation insensitivity* and *ionospheric immunity*. - ✅ **Canonical CP antennas:** Crossed-dipole with $90^{\circ}$ hybrid feed, axial-mode helical antenna, and patch antenna with two orthogonal feeds (used widely in the 2.4 GHz / 5.8 GHz Wi-Fi and ISM bands). - ✅ **Polarisation mismatch loss:** A CP receive antenna will lose 3 dB of signal if it has the *opposite* handedness to the incoming CP wave — a critical KTU numerical concept. - ✅ **Faraday rotation effect:** The ionosphere rotates the plane of linear polarisation but **does not affect** CP — this is the historical reason CP is used in satellite links. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Board Tip:** Always draw a **labelled E-field tip diagram** (showing the four cardinal points of the circle and the direction of rotation) whenever the question asks you to "show" or "prove" the polarisation. Examiners award up to **2 marks** for a clear, correctly-labelled figure alone.

topics.title: Substrates for Microstrip Antennas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Substrates for Microstrip Antennas # Module 2: Substrates for Microstrip Antennas ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** A **substrate** in a microstrip antenna is the dielectric material layer sandwiched between the radiating metallic patch (top conductor) and the **ground plane** (bottom conductor). It is the foundational physical medium that determines the electrical, mechanical, and electromagnetic performance of the printed antenna element. The substrate is characterized by three primary electrical parameters that govern every aspect of microstrip antenna behavior: - **Relative Permittivity (Dielectric Constant), $\varepsilon_r$** — typically ranging from **$\varepsilon_r = 1.0$ to $12.0$**. - **Loss Tangent, $\tan \delta$** — typically between **$0.0001$ and $0.025$**. - **Substrate Thickness, $h$** — typically between **$0.5$ mm and $3.0$ mm**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The selection of the substrate is the single most critical design decision in microstrip antenna engineering, because it simultaneously fixes the **resonant frequency**, **bandwidth**, **radiation efficiency**, **surface wave losses**, and the **physical dimensions** of the patch. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Foundation Layer" View Imagine you are building a **musical speaker membrane** on a wooden frame. The thickness and density of the wood (the substrate) dictates how freely the membrane (patch) can vibrate to produce sound. A thick, light wooden frame allows rich, deep resonance; a thin, heavy frame restricts vibration and produces muffled sound. In a microstrip antenna, the substrate plays this exact role: - **Thick, low-$\varepsilon_r$ substrate** = Large antenna, rich bandwidth, freely radiating waves → analogous to a thin wooden frame. - **Thin, high-$\varepsilon_r$ substrate** = Compact antenna, narrow bandwidth, tightly bound waves → analogous to a heavy dense frame. > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of the substrate as a **swimming pool** in which electromagnetic waves swim. A **shallow, viscous pool** (thin, high $\varepsilon_r$) traps the wave tightly to the patch and limits its escape (poor radiation). A **deep, water-clear pool** (thick, low $\varepsilon_r$) lets the wave glide outward freely, producing strong radiation into free space. --- ### 1.3 Physical Construction Role A microstrip antenna is a three-layer sandwich: | Layer | Material | Function | |-------|----------|----------| | Top | Conducting patch (Copper/Gold) | Radiating element | | Middle | **Dielectric Substrate** | **Traps and guides fringing fields** | | Bottom | Ground plane (Copper) | Reflector / return path | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the patch material (always a conductor) with the substrate (always a **dielectric**). The substrate is **never conductive**; otherwise, the patch would be short-circuited to ground and no radiation could occur. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Substrate Parameter Influence on Microstrip Antenna Behavior ### 2.1.1 Effect of Dielectric Constant ($\varepsilon_r$) The relative permittivity controls **three fundamental trade-offs**: 1. **Resonant Length of Patch ($L$)** - The patch length is inversely proportional to $\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$. - Higher $\varepsilon_r$ → physically smaller patch → good for **miniaturized antennas** (mobile phones, IoT). 2. **Fringing Field Concentration** - High $\varepsilon_r$ substrates confine the electric field lines tightly between the patch and ground plane. - This **reduces radiation efficiency** because the fields cannot escape easily into free space. 3. **Input Impedance Bandwidth** - Bandwidth is **inversely proportional** to $\varepsilon_r$. - Higher $\varepsilon_r$ → narrower bandwidth → unsuitable for broadband applications. ### 2.1.2 Effect of Substrate Thickness ($h$) Thickness trades bandwidth against surface wave losses: - **Thicker substrate** ($h \uparrow$): - Wider bandwidth (proportional to $h$). - Increased **surface wave excitation** (especially $TM_0$ mode for $h > 0.09 \lambda_0$). - Reduced radiation efficiency due to trapped power in surface waves. - Greater probe-feed inductance (problematic for coaxial feeding). - **Thinner substrate** ($h \downarrow$): - Narrower bandwidth. - Better surface wave suppression. - Higher conductor loss (skin-effect losses increase relative to radiated power). - Easier impedance matching at microstrip feed line. ### 2.1.3 Effect of Loss Tangent ($\tan \delta$) Loss tangent determines dielectric dissipation: - The **dielectric loss** is directly proportional to $\tan \delta$. -
