High $\tan \delta$ (e.g., FR-4: $\tan \delta \approx 0.025$) → poor efficiency at microwave/millimeter-wave frequencies. - Low $\tan \delta$ (e.g., PTFE: $\tan \delta \approx 0.0002$) → required for satellite, radar, and millimeter-wave systems. --- ## 2.2 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the high-yield formula sheet you must memorize for KTU 2024 Scheme University Examinations.** | # | Formula | Description / Use | |---|---------|-------------------| | 1 | $W = \frac{c}{2f_r}\sqrt{\frac{2}{\varepsilon_r + 1}}$ | Patch width calculation | | 2 | $L = \frac{c}{2f_r\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}} - 2\Delta L$ | Resonant patch length | | 3 | $\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12h/W}}\right]$ | Effective dielectric constant (Hammerstad) | | 4 | $\frac{\Delta L}{h} = 0.412\,\frac{(\varepsilon_{eff} + 0.3)(W/h + 0.264)}{(\varepsilon_{eff} - 0.258)(W/h + 0.813)}$ | Fringing length extension | | 5 | $BW (\%) = \frac{A \cdot h}{\lambda_0 \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \cdot 100$ | Approximate bandwidth (Schaubert) | | 6 | $\eta_{rad} = \frac{R_{rad}}{R_{rad} + R_{loss}}$ | Radiation efficiency | | 7 | $R_{loss} = \frac{1}{G_{dielectric}} = \frac{\tan \delta}{\omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$ | Dielectric loss resistance (inverse conductance) | | 8 | $h_{crit} = \frac{0.3\,c}{2\pi f_r \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$ | Critical thickness (surface wave onset) | | 9 | $f_{TM_0} = \frac{n c}{4h\sqrt{\varepsilon_r - 1}}$ | $TM_0$ surface wave cutoff | | 10 | $\varepsilon_{r}^{eff} \approx \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2 \sqrt{1 + 10h/W}}$ | Simplified effective $\varepsilon_r$ | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Rule for KTU Board Exams:** In the formula sheet above, **all vertical bars use $\vert$** instead of the literal pipe `|` symbol. This preserves the integrity of the markdown table. Always wrap multi-line formulas with `$\begin{aligned} \ldots \end{aligned}$` outside the table. --- ## 2.3 The Five Trade-Offs of Substrate Selection (Engineering Decision Matrix) The substrate parameter space imposes **five simultaneous design compromises** that every microstrip antenna engineer must balance: 1. **Size vs Bandwidth** Higher $\varepsilon_r$ minimizes size but reduces bandwidth. 2. **Efficiency vs Bandwidth** Thicker substrates increase bandwidth but lower efficiency via surface waves. 3. **Cost vs Performance** Low-loss ceramic/PTFE substrates are expensive; FR-4 is cheap but lossy. 4. **Mechanical Robustness vs Frequency** Thin substrates are mechanically delicate but minimize surface waves at high frequencies. 5. **Polarization Purity vs Conductor Loss** Thick substrates with high $\varepsilon_r$ degrade polarization purity at mm-wave bands. > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight:** A substrate selection is rarely a single-parameter optimization. KTU board questions typically test your ability to **justify a substrate choice** based on multiple criteria simultaneously. Always list *both* the advantage and the limitation in your answers. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Dielectric Constant of a Microstrip Line The patch antenna is analyzed using the **transmission line model**, where the substrate behaves like a non-uniform dielectric medium because some of the electric field lines travel in the substrate while others travel in the air above it. ### 3.1.1 Physical Setup Consider a microstrip line of width $W$ over a substrate of thickness $h$ and permittivity $\varepsilon_r$. Part of the field (fraction $q$) lies in the dielectric and the rest (fraction $1 - q$) lies in air. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation The total capacitance per unit length of the microstrip line is the parallel combination of the air-region capacitance and the dielectric-region capacitance: $$C = C_{air} + C_{dielectric} = \varepsilon_0 \frac{W}{h} + \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \frac{W}{h}$$ But because the fringing fields only partially fill the dielectric, the **effective capacitance** is defined as: $$\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{C}{C_{air}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 (W/h) + \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r (W/h) \cdot q}{\varepsilon_0 (W/h)} = 1 + q(\varepsilon_r - 1)$$ where the **filling factor** $q$ is empirically given by Hammerstad's formula. Substituting $q$: $$q = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12h/W}}\right]$$ We now substitute $q$ into the effective permittivity expression: $$\varepsilon_{eff} = 1 + \left[\frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12h/W}}\right)\right](\varepsilon_r - 1)$$ Distributing $\varepsilon_r - 1$: $$\varepsilon_{eff} = 1 + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2\sqrt{1 + 12h/W}}$$ Combining the constants: $$\boxed{\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12h/W}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Behavior Verification (Mandatory in KTU Valuation):** > 1. **Limit $W/h \to \infty$** (very wide line): $\sqrt{1 + 12h/W} \to 1$, so $\varepsilon_{eff} \to \varepsilon_r$. Physically correct — wide line has nearly all field inside the dielectric. > 2. **Limit $W/h \to 0$** (very narrow line): $\sqrt{1 + 12h/W} \to \infty$, so $\varepsilon_{eff} \to (\varepsilon_r + 1)/2$. Physically correct — narrow line has equal field split between air and dielectric. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Substrate Thickness for Surface Wave Excitation Surface waves are bound modes that propagate along the air-dielectric interface of the substrate and trap RF energy that would otherwise radiate into free space. ### 3.2.1 Threshold Condition The lowest-order **$TM_0$ surface wave** begins to propagate when the substrate electrical thickness satisfies: $$\frac{h}{\lambda_0} > \frac{0.3}{2\pi\sqrt{\varepsilon_r - 1}}$$ This can be rearranged to give the **critical substrate thickness** $h_{crit}$: $$\boxed{h_{crit} = \frac{0.3 \cdot \lambda_0}{2\pi\sqrt{\varepsilon_r - 1}} = \frac{0.3 \cdot c}{2\pi f_r \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - 1}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** When $h > h_{crit}$, the antenna begins to trap energy in the substrate as a guided wave. Beyond this threshold, **further increasing $h$ hurts efficiency** despite the bandwidth increasing. This is one of the most counter-intuitive aspects of microstrip antenna design. ### 3.2.2 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A microstrip antenna is designed for $f_r = 2.4$ GHz on a substrate with $\varepsilon_r = 4.4$. Compute the critical thickness at which surface waves begin to propagate. **Step 1:** Compute the free-space wavelength. $$\lambda_0 = \frac{c}{f_r} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.125 \text{ m} = 125 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2:** Compute the denominator term. $$\sqrt{\varepsilon_r - 1} = \sqrt{4.4 - 1} = \sqrt{3.4} = 1.844$$ **Step 3:** Apply the critical thickness formula. $$h_{crit} = \frac{0.3 \times 0.125}{2\pi \times 1.844}$$ **Step 4:** Evaluate denominator. $$2\pi \times 1.844 = 11.585$$ **Step 5:** Final division. $$h_{crit} = \frac{0.0375}{11.585} = 3.236 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 3.236 \text{ mm}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Insight:** A substrate thickness above **$h_{crit} \approx 3.24$ mm at 2.4 GHz** for FR-4 ($\varepsilon_r = 4.4$) means the antenna will launch surface waves. This is why FR-4 patches are typically designed with $h \approx 1.6$ mm — to stay safely below the surface-wave threshold. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Bandwidth as a Function of Substrate Thickness A well-known approximate formula for the impedance bandwidth of a rectangular microstrip patch antenna is: $$\boxed{BW (\%) \approx \frac{3.77\,(\varepsilon_r - 1) \cdot h}{\varepsilon_r^{1.5} \cdot W} \times 100}$$ But a more common form (Schaubert / Bahl & Bhartia) uses: $$BW (\%) = \frac{A \cdot h}{\lambda_0 \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \times 100$$ where $A$ is an empirical constant depending on patch geometry ($A \approx 180$ for rectangular patch). **Step-by-step logic:** $$\frac{h}{\lambda_0} = \frac{h f_r}{c}$$ So substituting: $$BW (\%) = \frac{A \cdot h f_r}{c \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \times 100$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Trade-off demonstrated:** $BW \propto h$ but $BW \propto 1/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$. To get a wideband patch, you must use a **thick, low-$\varepsilon_r$ substrate** — at the cost of antenna size. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Substrate Materials for KTU Reference | Substrate Material | $\varepsilon_r$ | $\tan \delta$ | Typical Use | |-------------------|----------------|---------------|-------------| | Air / Foam | $1.00 - 1.10$ | $\approx 0$ | Wideband, high-efficiency research | | PTFE (Teflon) | $2.10$ | $0.0002$ | Satellite, low-loss microwave | | Rogers RT/duroid 5880 | $2.20$ | $0.0009$ | Radomes, broadband | | Rogers RT/duroid 5870 | $2.33$ | $0.0012$ | Microwave circuits | | RO4003C | $3.38$ | $0.0021$ | Commercial microwave | | **FR-4 (epoxy fiberglass)** | $4.20 - 4.50$ | $0.020 - 0.025$ | **Low-cost consumer electronics** | | RO4350B | $3.48$ | $0.0037$ | Mid-cost wireless | | Alumina (Ceramic) | $9.80$ | $0.0004$ | High-frequency, miniaturized | | GaAs (semiconductor) | $12.90$ | $0.006$ | Monolithic mm-wave | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** FR-4 is **the most commonly used substrate in KTU board problems** because it is cheap and well-known. However, its $\tan \delta = 0.025$ makes it **unsuitable for frequencies above ~3 GHz** for high-efficiency designs. Always mention this limitation in answers. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Physical Cross-Section of a Microstrip Antenna (Mermaid Schematic) ```mermaid graph TD A["Top: Conducting Patch (Copper)"] --> B["Middle: Dielectric Substrate"] B --> C["Bottom: Ground Plane (Copper)"] B --> D["E-field lines: confined mostly in substrate"] D --> E["Fringing fields at patch edges"] E --> F["Radiation into free space"] style A fill:#F9C74F,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#90BE6D,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#577590,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#F8961E,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style E fill:#F3722C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style F fill:#43AA8B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The **green layer** is the substrate under study. The yellow patch is the radiator, and the dark blue layer is the ground plane. Orange arrows depict the **fringing E-field** at the patch edges — these are the actual sources of radiation. The teal arrows show energy escaping into free space. --- ## 4.2 Substrate Parameter Decision Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD Start["Design Requirements Input"] --> Q1["Operating Frequency?"] Q1 --> Q2["Required Bandwidth?"] Q2 --> Q3["Size Constraint?"] Q3 --> Q4["Efficiency Target?"] Q4 --> LowEps["Low eps_r substrate
PTFE, Foam, Air"] Q4 --> MidEps["Mid eps_r substrate
FR4, Rogers RO4003"] Q4 --> HighEps["High eps_r substrate
Alumina, GaAs"] LowEps --> LowResult["Wideband
Large size
High efficiency"] MidEps --> MidResult["Moderate BW
Compact
Moderate loss"] HighEps --> HighResult["Narrowband
Very compact
Miniaturized"] style Start fill:#43AA8B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style LowResult fill:#90BE6D,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style MidResult fill:#F9C74F,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style HighResult fill:#F3722C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.3 Effect of Substrate Thickness on Surface Waves (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph LR Thin["Thin Substrate h less than h_crit"] --> ThinOut["Suppressed Surface Waves
High Radiation Efficiency
Narrow Bandwidth"] Thick["Thick Substrate h greater than h_crit"] --> ThickOut["Surface Wave Excitation
Lower Radiation Efficiency
Wider Bandwidth"] style Thin fill:#90BE6D,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Thick fill:#F3722C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style ThinOut fill:#43AA8B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style ThickOut fill:#F8961E,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.4 Equivalent Circuit Representation of Patch on Substrate ```mermaid graph TD Patch["Patch Capacitance C"] --> Rad["Radiation Resistance Rrad"] Patch --> Diel["Dielectric Loss Rloss"] Patch --> Surf["Surface Wave Loss Rsw"] Feed["Feed Probe Inductance Lfeed"] --> Patch style Patch fill:#F9C74F,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Rad fill:#90BE6D,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Diel fill:#F3722C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Surf fill:#577590,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Feed fill:#F8961E,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading the Equivalent Circuit:** The patch is modeled as a lossy capacitor in parallel with three resistance terms: $R_{rad}$ (desired radiation), $R_{loss}$ (dielectric heating — determined by $\tan \delta$), and $R_{sw}$ (surface wave loss — determined by $h$ and $\varepsilon_r$). A well-designed substrate minimizes $R_{loss}$ and $R_{sw}$ while maximizing $R_{rad}$. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **"List any four characteristics of substrates used for microstrip antennas."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The four essential characteristics of microstrip antenna substrates are: 1. **Low dielectric loss tangent** ($\tan \delta < 0.005$ preferred) to maximize radiation efficiency. 2. **Stable dielectric constant** ($\varepsilon_r$) over frequency and temperature to ensure resonant frequency stability. 3. **Low relative permittivity** ($\varepsilon_r < 3$ preferred) for wider bandwidth and better radiation efficiency. 4. **Mechanical rigidity and uniformity** to support patch definition and reproducibility. > [!NOTE] > **Additional acceptable characteristics:** Low moisture absorption, good thermal conductivity, isotropic dielectric properties, and compatibility with PCB fabrication processes. --- ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **"Why is a thick substrate preferred for broadband microstrip antennas?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A thicker substrate is preferred for broadband microstrip antennas because: 1. **Bandwidth proportionality:** The impedance bandwidth $BW (\%)$ is directly proportional to substrate thickness $h$. Increasing $h$ enlarges the antenna's operational bandwidth. 2. **Reduced Q-factor:** Thicker substrates lower the antenna's quality factor $Q$, resulting in wider impedance matching and radiation bandwidths. 3. **Improved impedance matching:** A thicker substrate increases the patch-to-ground-plane separation, which relaxes the feed-line matching constraint. > [!WARNING] > **Important caveat (often missed by students):** Increasing $h$ beyond the **critical thickness** $h_{crit}$ excites **surface waves**, which *reduces* radiation efficiency. Hence, the increase in thickness must be kept below the surface-wave threshold for best performance. --- ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) ### **`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`** **Question A(a) [7 marks]:** *"Explain the role of the dielectric substrate in determining the performance of a microstrip antenna. Discuss the effect of dielectric constant on the resonant length, bandwidth, and radiation efficiency of the patch."* **Model Solution (7 marks):** The dielectric substrate plays a fundamental role in the operation of a microstrip patch antenna by providing the medium in which fringing electromagnetic fields are confined between the patch and the ground plane. **Effect on Resonant Length ($L$):** The resonant length of the patch is given by: $$L = \frac{c}{2 f_r \sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}} - 2\Delta L$$ Since $\varepsilon_{eff}$ increases with $\varepsilon_r$, an increase in $\varepsilon_r$ **decreases the physical length** $L$ of the patch. This is why high-$\varepsilon_r$ substrates (e.g., alumina with $\varepsilon_r = 9.8$) are preferred for **miniaturized** antennas. **Effect on Bandwidth ($BW$):** $$BW (\%) \propto \frac{h}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$$ Bandwidth is **inversely proportional to** $\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$. Hence, **higher** $\varepsilon_r$ leads to **narrower** bandwidth, making high-$\varepsilon_r$ substrates unsuitable for broadband applications. **Effect on Radiation Efficiency:** High-$\varepsilon_r$ substrates confine the electric field lines between the patch and ground plane, reducing the fringing field intensity at the patch edges. Since radiation in a microstrip antenna is produced primarily by these fringing fields, a high $\varepsilon_r$ substrate reduces radiation efficiency. **Summary Table:** | Parameter | Effect of increasing $\varepsilon_r$ | |-----------|--------------------------------------| | Patch size $L$ | **Decreases** | | Bandwidth $BW$ | **Decreases** | | Radiation efficiency | **Decreases** | | Surface wave loss | **Increases** | --- ### **Question A(b) [7 marks]:** *"A rectangular microstrip patch antenna operating at 2.4 GHz uses an FR-4 substrate ($\varepsilon_r = 4.4$, $h = 1.6$ mm). Calculate (i) the patch width $W$, (ii) the effective dielectric constant $\varepsilon_{eff}$, and (iii) the fringing extension $\Delta L$."* **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $f_r = 2.4$ GHz, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $\varepsilon_r = 4.4$, $h = 1.6 \times 10^{-3}$ m. **Step (i): Patch Width $W$:** $$W = \frac{c}{2 f_r}\sqrt{\frac{2}{\varepsilon_r + 1}}$$ $$W = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 2.4 \times 10^9}\sqrt{\frac{2}{4.4 + 1}}$$ $$W = 0.0625 \sqrt{\frac{2}{5.4}} = 0.0625 \sqrt{0.3704}$$ $$W = 0.0625 \times 0.6086 = 0.0380 \text{ m} = 38.0 \text{ mm}$$ **Step (ii): Effective Dielectric Constant $\varepsilon_{eff}$:** $$\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12h/W}}\right]$$ Computing the term $\frac{h}{W}$: $$\frac{h}{W} = \frac{1.6}{38.0} = 0.0421$$ Computing the denominator: $$1 + 12 \times 0.0421 = 1 + 0.5053 = 1.5053$$ $$\sqrt{1.5053} = 1.2270$$ $$\frac{1}{1.2270} = 0.8150$$ Now substituting: $$\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{4.4 + 1}{2} + \frac{4.4 - 1}{2}(0.8150)$$ $$\varepsilon_{eff} = 2.7 + \frac{3.4}{2}(0.8150)$$ $$\varepsilon_{eff} = 2.7 + 1.7 \times 0.8150 = 2.7 + 1.3855$$ $$\boxed{\varepsilon_{eff} = 4.086}$$ **Step (iii): Fringing Extension $\Delta L$:** $$\frac{\Delta L}{h} = \frac{0.412(\varepsilon_{eff} + 0.3)(W/h + 0.264)}{(\varepsilon_{eff} - 0.258)(W/h + 0.813)}$$ Compute $W/h$: $$\frac{W}{h} = \frac{38.0}{1.6} = 23.75$$ Compute numerator: $$0.412 \times (4.086 + 0.3) \times (23.75 + 0.264) = 0.412 \times 4.386 \times 24.014$$ $$= 0.412 \times 105.32 = 43.39$$ Compute denominator: $$(4.086 - 0.258) \times (23.75 + 0.813) = 3.828 \times 24.563 = 94.03$$ Therefore: $$\frac{\Delta L}{h} = \frac{43.39}{94.03} = 0.4615$$ $$\Delta L = 0.4615 \times 1.6 = 0.738 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta L \approx 0.74 \text{ mm}}$$ --- ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice ### **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`** **Question B(a) [7 marks]:** *"Explain the mechanism of surface wave excitation in microstrip antennas due to the substrate. Derive the critical substrate thickness condition that suppresses surface wave propagation."* **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Mechanism of Surface Wave Excitation:** When a microstrip patch antenna is excited, in addition to the desired space wave radiation, electromagnetic energy can be trapped along the **air-dielectric interface** as a **surface wave**. This occurs because the substrate acts as a dielectric slab waveguide with air above and ground plane below. Surface waves propagate along the interface with a phase velocity between that of free space and that of the substrate. The most significant surface wave mode is the **$TM_0$ mode**, which has **no low-frequency cutoff** — meaning it can be excited at almost any thickness above the threshold. **Effect of Surface Waves:** - Power trapped in surface waves does not radiate, reducing **radiation efficiency**. - Surface waves diffract from substrate edges, increasing **side-lobe levels** and **mutual coupling** in arrays. - Surface waves cause **scan blindness** in phased arrays. **Derivation of Critical Thickness:** The propagation constant of the dominant $TM_0$ surface wave is: $$\beta_{sw} = \beta_0 \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - 1}$$ where $\beta_0 = 2\pi/\lambda_0$ is the free-space propagation constant. Surface waves begin to propagate when the electrical thickness satisfies: $$\frac{h}{\lambda_0} > \frac{0.3}{2\pi\sqrt{\varepsilon_r - 1}}$$ Solving for $h_{crit}$: $$\boxed{h_{crit} = \frac{0.3 \lambda_0}{2\pi\sqrt{\varepsilon_r - 1}} = \frac{0.3\,c}{2\pi f_r\sqrt{\varepsilon_r - 1}}}$$ **Boundary Condition Summary:** | Condition | Effect | |-----------|--------| | $h < h_{crit}$ | Surface wave suppressed; efficient radiation | | $h = h_{crit}$ | Threshold; surface wave onset | | $h > h_{crit}$ | Surface waves excited; efficiency drops | --- ### **Question B(b) [7 marks]:** *"Compare the properties of FR-4, PTFE (Teflon), and Alumina ceramic substrates for microstrip antenna applications. Justify which substrate is most appropriate for (i) a 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi antenna and (ii) a 60 GHz millimeter-wave antenna."* **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Comparative Table:** | Property | FR-4 | PTFE (Teflon) | Alumina Ceramic | |----------|------|---------------|-----------------| | $\varepsilon_r$ | $4.2 - 4.5$ | $2.1$ | $9.8$ | | $\tan \delta$ | $0.020 - 0.025$ | $0.0002$ | $0.0004$ | | Cost | Very low | Moderate to high | High | | Mechanical strength | Moderate | Soft, flexible | Excellent | | Max usable frequency | $\sim 3$ GHz | $\sim 30$ GHz | $\sim 100$ GHz | | Thermal conductivity | Low | Low | High | | Moisture absorption | High | Very low | None | **Justification (i): 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi Antenna:** **Recommended: FR-4** is acceptable because: 1. Wi-Fi bands are narrow, and the modest bandwidth requirement (~80 MHz) is achievable with FR-4. 2. Cost is critical for consumer Wi-Fi products (routers, laptops, smartphones). 3. At 2.4 GHz, FR-4 loss is manageable (efficiency ~70-80%). 4. FR-4 is compatible with standard PCB etching processes. **Justification (ii): 60 GHz Millimeter-Wave Antenna:** **Recommended: Alumina Ceramic** (or PTFE-based laminates like Rogers RT/duroid 5880) is appropriate because: 1. At 60 GHz, FR-4's high $\tan \delta = 0.025$ causes severe dielectric loss (efficiency drops below 30%). 2. Alumina has $\tan \delta = 0.0004$ at mm-wave, sustaining high efficiency. 3. Alumina's $\varepsilon_r = 9.8$ enables miniaturization needed for compact 60 GHz antennas. 4. Alumina's thermal conductivity aids heat dissipation in mm-wave power amplifiers. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. **Always** show units in every step of numerical problems. Marks are deducted for missing units in 70% of KTU answer sheets. > 2. In comparison questions, **tabular presentation fetches higher marks** than prose. KTU examiners actively reward clarity. > 3. For substrate choice justifications, **mention both the chosen material's advantage AND the rejected materials' limitation**. > 4. Always verify the formula's **boundary conditions** when deriving — KTU examiners specifically test this. > 5. **Never** confuse $\varepsilon_r$ (relative permittivity) with $\varepsilon_{eff}$ (effective permittivity) — they are distinct quantities. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print and Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE):** ### A. Core Conceptual Points - [ ] A **substrate** is the dielectric layer between patch and ground plane. - [ ] Substrate parameters: **$\varepsilon_r$, $\tan \delta$, $h$**. - [ ] The substrate determines **bandwidth, efficiency, and size**. ### B. Formula Memorization Checklist - [ ] Patch width: $W = \dfrac{c}{2f_r}\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon_r + 1}}$ - [ ] Effective permittivity: $\varepsilon_{eff} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2\sqrt{1 + 12h/W}}$ - [ ] Fringing length: $\dfrac{\Delta L}{h} = \dfrac{0.412(\varepsilon_{eff}+0.3)(W/h+0.264)}{(\varepsilon_{eff}-0.258)(W/h+0.813)}$ - [ ] Critical thickness: $h_{crit} = \dfrac{0.3\,c}{2\pi f_r\sqrt{\varepsilon_r - 1}}$ - [ ] Bandwidth formula: $BW (\%) = \dfrac{A \cdot h}{\lambda_0 \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \times 100$ ### C. Trade-Off Matrix (Must Memorize) - [ ] **Higher** $\varepsilon_r$ → **smaller** patch, **narrower** bandwidth, **lower** efficiency. - [ ] **Higher** $h$ → **wider** bandwidth, but **surface waves** if $h > h_{crit}$. - [ ] **Lower** $\tan \delta$ → **higher** efficiency. ### D. Substrate Material Memory Aids - [ ] **FR-4**: Cheap, $\varepsilon_r \approx 4.4$, lossy at $>3$ GHz. - [ ] **PTFE**: $\varepsilon_r \approx 2.1$, very low loss, satellite-grade. - [ ] **Alumina**: $\varepsilon_r \approx 9.8$, ideal for miniaturization and mm-wave. ### E. Common Pitfalls to Avoid - [ ] Do not confuse $\varepsilon_r$ and $\varepsilon_{eff}$. - [ ] Do not state that thicker is always better — surface waves limit thickness. - [ ] Always include **units** in numerical answers. - [ ] Always state the **assumed feeding method** when quoting bandwidth formulas. ### F. Frequently Asked KTU Concept Questions - **Q: Why can't FR-4 be used at 60 GHz?** **A:** $\tan \delta = 0.025$ causes severe dielectric loss at mm-wave, reducing radiation efficiency below usable levels. - **Q: Why do satellite antennas use low-$\varepsilon_r$ substrates?** **A:** Low $\varepsilon_r$ minimizes surface wave losses and maximizes radiation efficiency, which is critical for long-distance, low-power satellite links. - **Q: How does the substrate affect antenna polarization?** **A:** Anisotropic substrates (e.g., woven fibers) introduce cross-polarization. Isotropic substrates like PTFE and Alumina maintain linear polarization purity. > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "justify a substrate choice" in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, structure your answer as: **(i) Frequency of operation, (ii) Bandwidth needed, (iii) Cost constraint, (iv) Size constraint, (v) Final selection with both advantage and limitation stated**. Examiners reward structured, multi-criterion reasoning over single-factor justifications.

topics.title: Antenna Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Antenna Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📡 Antenna Measurements — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Antenna Measurements form the practical backbone of validating theoretical antenna parameters. As per Module 2 of PEECT868, students must master the experimental determination of **Radiation Pattern, Gain, Directivity, Polarization, Impedance (VSWR), and Efficiency**, along with the instrumentation used (Vector Network Analyzers, Anechoic Chambers, Test Ranges).

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Antenna Measurement** is defined as the systematic, quantitative experimental characterization of an antenna's electrical and radiation parameters under controlled electromagnetic environments. It involves transmitting a known, calibrated electromagnetic signal to a Device Under Test (DUT) or Antenna Under Test (AUT) and accurately measuring the response in terms of field strength, phase, impedance, and polarization across space, frequency, and time.

The principal parameters measured are:

| Parameter | Definition | Standard Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Radiation Pattern** | Spatial distribution of radiated energy | Relative power (dB) or field (V/m) |
| **Gain ($G$)** | Ability to concentrate energy in a given direction | **dBi or dBd** |
| **Directivity ($D$)** | Ratio of max intensity to average intensity | Dimensionless / dB |
| **Polarization** | Orientation of the E-field vector | Linear / Circular / Elliptical |
| **VSWR** | Impedance match to transmission line | Ratio (1 to $\infty$) |
| **Return Loss (RL)** | Power reflected due to mismatch | **dB (negative)** |
| **Efficiency ($\eta$)** | Ratio of radiated to input power | Percentage (%) |
| **Bandwidth (BW)** | Usable frequency range | **Hz** or % |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lighthouse Test"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of an antenna measurement setup as a doctor testing a patient's vision (the antenna) in a controlled, dark room (anechoic chamber).
>
> - The **transmitting antenna** is the eye chart — it sends a known, standardized signal.
> - The **AUT (Antenna Under Test)** is the patient rotating on a turntable.
> - The **anechoic chamber walls** are the dark room — they absorb all stray reflections so the doctor measures *only* what the patient truly sees.
> - The **rotating positioner** turns the patient slowly while the doctor records how well they "see" in every direction — this builds the **radiation pattern**.
> - Just as a doctor compares the patient to a known healthy person, we compare our AUT to a **standard gain horn** to find gain.

Without a controlled environment, you'd be measuring echoes and reflections, not the antenna itself — just as measuring vision in a sunny, mirror-filled room would give false results.

## 1.3 Why Antenna Measurements are Crucial

- **Design Verification:** Simulations (HFSS, CST, FEKO) must be experimentally validated before deployment.
- **Compliance:** Regulatory bodies (FCC, ETSI, DoT) mandate certified measurements for licensed bands.
- **Quality Control (QC):** Mass-produced antennas (e.g., mobile phone PIFAs) must be sampled and tested.
- **Defect Detection:** Manufacturing tolerances, connector issues, and substrate variations can only be caught by measurement.
- **Real-World Performance:** Cable losses, ground effects, and near-field coupling cannot be modeled perfectly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask: *"Why can't we measure antennas in a regular lab?"* The answer is **multipath interference** from walls, floor, and metallic objects that contaminate the true radiation pattern. This justifies the need for anechoic chambers and outdoor test ranges.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Antenna Range — The Foundation of All Measurements

An **antenna range** is a specialized facility designed to simulate **Free-Space (Far-Field) conditions** for accurate antenna characterization. The Fraunhofer distance establishes the minimum range length:

$$d_{\min} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}$$

where:
- $D$ = largest dimension of the AUT (in meters)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of test signal (in meters)

> [!NOTE]
> **Fraunhofer (Far-Field) Condition:** Beyond this distance, the wavefront can be considered **planar** (flat) and the angular field distribution becomes independent of range. This is the universal rule for all outdoor and indoor far-field measurements.

### Types of Antenna Ranges

**1. Elevated Range (Free-Space Range)**
- AUT and source antenna are mounted on towers at sufficient height to avoid ground reflections.
- Tilted geometry is used to direct the first null of the source pattern toward the ground.
- Suitable for medium-to-high gain antennas.

**2. Ground Reflection Range**
- AUT and source placed close to a flat, smooth ground plane.
- Constructive interference (image principle) is exploited to double the effective aperture.
- Cost-effective; suitable for low-gain antennas.

**3. Slant Range**
- Hybrid; one antenna elevated, the other at ground level.
- Reduces ground bounce at the elevated end.

**4. Compact (Collapsed) Range**
- Uses a parabolic reflector to convert a spherical wave from a feed into a planar wave in a small chamber.
- Ideal for testing large antennas indoors (e.g., satellite payloads).

**5. Near-Field Range (Planar / Spherical / Cylindrical)**
- Probe scans the near field; far-field pattern is computed via FFT-based transformation.
- Allows indoor testing of electrically massive antennas.

## 2.2 Radiation Pattern Measurement

The AUT is mounted on a **rotating positioner** controlled by a stepper/servo motor. As it rotates in azimuth ($\phi$) and elevation ($\theta$), a **receiving antenna (or probe)** records the signal strength at discrete angular positions.

The **pattern recorder** plots the received power (or voltage) versus angle. Two key indicators:

- **Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW):** Angular width between -3 dB points.
- **First Sidelobe Level (SLL):** Difference (in dB) between main beam peak and first sidelobe peak.

## 2.3 Gain Measurement

### (a) Absolute Gain Method (Two-Antenna Method)

$$G_{AUT} = G_{TX} = \frac{4\pi d}{\lambda} \sqrt{\frac{P_{r}}{P_{t}}}$$

If two identical antennas are used (assumed equal gain $G$):

$$G = \frac{4\pi d}{\lambda} \sqrt{\frac{P_{r}}{P_{t}}}$$

where $P_r$ = received power, $P_t$ = transmitted power, $d$ = range distance.

### (b) Gain Comparison (Gain-Transfer) Method

$$\frac{G_{AUT}}{G_{STD}} = \frac{P_{AUT}}{P_{STD}}$$

Expressed in dB:

$$G_{AUT}(\text{dBi}) = G_{STD}(\text{dBi}) + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{AUT}}{P_{STD}}\right)$$

where $G_{STD}$ is a calibrated standard gain horn.

### (c) Three-Antenna Method

Used when **no standard gain horn is available**. Three antennas (1, 2, 3) are measured pairwise:

$$G_1 G_2 = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 \frac{P_{r12}}{P_{t1}}$$

Three equations are solved simultaneously for $G_1$, $G_2$, $G_3$.

## 2.4 Directivity from Pattern Integration

Directivity is computed from the **measured radiation pattern** by numerical integration:

$$D_0 = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A} = \frac{4\pi}{\int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} F_n(\theta, \phi) \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi}$$

where $F_n(\theta, \phi) = \frac{U(\theta,\phi)}{U_{max}}$ is the normalized radiation intensity.

In practice, the pattern is discretized into $N$ samples:

$$D_0 \approx \frac{4\pi}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{M} F_n(\theta_i, \phi_j) \sin\theta_i \, \Delta\theta \, \Delta\phi}$$

## 2.5 Polarization Measurement

**Method — Linear Component / Polarization Pattern:**
- A linearly polarized probe antenna is rotated about its boresight axis.
- The received signal varies sinusoidally.
- For **linear polarization**: received power is constant with probe rotation.
- For **circular polarization**: received power is constant, but a phase shift indicates LHCP or RHCP.
- For **elliptical polarization**: received power varies; AR is computed from min/max ratio.

**Axial Ratio (AR):**

$$AR = \frac{E_{max}}{E_{min}} \quad \text{(for the polarization ellipse)}$$

- AR = 1 → Circular
- AR = $\infty$ → Linear

## 2.6 Impedance / VSWR Measurement

Measured using a **Vector Network Analyzer (VNA)** or **Slotted Line / Smith Chart**:

$$VSWR = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}, \quad \Gamma = \frac{Z_{in} - Z_0}{Z_{in} + Z_0}$$

$$RL \; (\text{dB}) = -20 \log_{10} \vert \Gamma \vert$$

The **bandwidth** is the frequency range over which VSWR $\leq$ 2 (a common KTU exam benchmark).

## 2.7 Antenna Efficiency Measurement

**Wheeler Cap Method** (popular for small antennas):

$$\eta = \frac{R_{rad}}{R_{rad} + R_{loss}} = \frac{R_{cap}}{R_{air}}$$

where $R_{cap}$ = input resistance with cap placed, $R_{air}$ = input resistance in free space.

**Radiometric / Q-Factor Method** is also used for electrically small antennas.

## 2.8 Phase Measurement

Using a **Vector Network Analyzer**, the phase of $S_{21}$ (transmission coefficient) is recorded as the AUT rotates. This gives the **phase pattern**, which is essential for:
- Array antenna beam-forming validation.
- Near-Field to Far-Field transformation.

## 2.9 The Anechoic Chamber

A **shielded, RF-anechoic room** whose inner walls are lined with pyramidal or wedge-shaped **absorbers** (carbon-loaded foam or ferrite tiles) that provide:
- Reflection coefficient $\leq$ -40 dB (typical) over the operating band.
- Quiet zone purity: $\pm 0.5$ dB amplitude, $\pm 5^\circ$ phase variation.
- EMI shielding for sensitive low-noise measurements.

## 2.10 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_{\min} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}$ | Fraunhofer far-field distance | m |
| 2 | $RL = -20\log_{10}\vert\Gamma\vert$ | Return Loss | dB |
| 3 | $VSWR = \frac{1+\vert\Gamma\vert}{1-\vert\Gamma\vert}$ | Voltage Standing Wave Ratio | Ratio |
| 4 | $\Gamma = \frac{Z_{in}-Z_0}{Z_{in}+Z_0}$ | Reflection coefficient | Unitless |
| 5 | $G_{AUT} = G_{STD} + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{AUT}}{P_{STD}}\right)$ | Gain-transfer (dB form) | dBi |
| 6 | $G = \frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\sqrt{\frac{P_r}{P_t}}$ | Two-antenna absolute gain | Linear |
| 7 | $D_0 = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A}$ | Directivity from beam solid angle | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\Omega_A \approx \Omega_M + \Omega_m$ | Beam solid angle (approx) | sr |
| 9 | $\eta = \frac{G}{D_0}$ | Antenna efficiency | Ratio |
| 10 | $AR = \frac{E_{max}}{E_{min}}$ | Axial ratio (polarization) | Ratio |
| 11 | $BW \approx \frac{VSWR-1}{Q\sqrt{VSWR}}$ | Bandwidth from Q-factor | Hz |
| 12 | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | Friis transmission equation | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These measurements underpin telecom (5G/6G base station antenna validation), radar cross-section testing, satellite payload qualification (where the AUT cannot be sent to free space easily), and EMC compliance. Industries like **ISRO, Boeing, Ericsson, Nokia, and Qualcomm** maintain in-house ranges that routinely cost **$5–50 million USD**.

## 2.11 Instrumentation Block Summary

- **Signal Source:** Synthesized RF signal generator (HP/Keysight 83650 series).
- **Receiver:** Spectrum analyzer or VNA.
- **Positioner:** Computer-controlled turntable + elevation mast.
- **AUT Mount:** Low-RCS foam column to avoid scattering.
- **Control Software:** MATLAB-based or vendor-specific (e.g., NSI-MI, Orbit/FR, Antenna Measurement Software).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fraunhofer (Far-Field) Distance $d_{\min} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Find the minimum distance beyond which the wavefront at the AUT can be approximated as a **plane wave** (constant phase across the aperture).

### Step 1 — Define the maximum phase error
Consider two extreme points on the AUT aperture separated by dimension $D$. The difference in path length from a point source at distance $d$ is:

$$\Delta R = \sqrt{d^2 + D^2} - d$$

### Step 2 — Apply binomial expansion
For $d \gg D$, use the Taylor series:

$$\Delta R = d\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{D}{d}\right)^2} - 1\right]$$

$$\Delta R \approx d\left[1 + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{D}{d}\right)^2 - 1\right] = \frac{D^2}{2d}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to maximum phase error
The corresponding phase difference is:

$$\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{D^2}{2d} = \frac{\pi D^2}{\lambda d}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the standard criterion
For the Fraunhofer approximation, the maximum phase error is conventionally limited to $\pi/8$ radians (22.5°):

$$\frac{\pi D^2}{\lambda d} \leq \frac{\pi}{8}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $d$

$$d \geq \frac{2D^2}{\lambda} \implies d_{\min} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}$$

This is the celebrated **Fraunhofer distance**, a cornerstone of every antenna range design. ✅

## 3.2 Derivation of the Two-Antenna Gain Formula

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Express antenna gain in terms of measurable quantities ($P_t$, $P_r$, $d$, $\lambda$).

### Step 1 — Friis Transmission Equation (Recall)
For two antennas separated by distance $d$ in free space:

$$P_r = P_t \, G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

### Step 2 — Assume identical antennas
For the two-antenna method, two identical AUTs are used:
- $G_t = G_r = G$

$$P_r = P_t \, G^2 \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $G^2$

$$G^2 = \frac{P_r}{P_t} \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2$$

### Step 4 — Take the square root

$$G = \frac{4\pi d}{\lambda} \sqrt{\frac{P_r}{P_t}}$$

This is the **standard two-antenna gain formula** widely tested in KTU examinations. ✅

## 3.3 Derivation of Directivity from Numerical Pattern Data

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Compute $D_0$ when only sampled $F_n(\theta_i, \phi_j)$ values are available.

### Step 1 — Beam solid angle integral
By definition:

$$\Omega_A = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} F_n(\theta,\phi) \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

### Step 2 — Discretize the sphere
Sample uniformly: $\theta_i = i\Delta\theta$, $\phi_j = j\Delta\phi$:

$$\Omega_A \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{M} F_n(\theta_i, \phi_j) \sin\theta_i \, \Delta\theta \, \Delta\phi$$

### Step 3 — Apply $D_0 = 4\pi / \Omega_A$

$$D_0 \approx \frac{4\pi}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{M} F_n(\theta_i, \phi_j) \sin\theta_i \, \Delta\theta \, \Delta\phi}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** A common mistake students make is omitting the $\sin\theta$ weighting. Always remember that the **element of solid angle** on a sphere is $d\Omega = \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$, **not** $d\theta \, d\phi$. This is a guaranteed 2-mark trap!

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A horn antenna with aperture $D = 0.5$ m is to be tested at $f = 10$ GHz.
> Compute: (a) Far-field distance, (b) VSWR if $Z_{in} = (75 + j25) \, \Omega$, $Z_0 = 50 \, \Omega$.

### Part (a) — Far-field distance

**Step 1:** Compute wavelength.
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^9} = 0.03 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 2:** Apply formula.
$$d_{\min} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times (0.5)^2}{0.03} = \frac{0.5}{0.03} \approx 16.67 \, \text{m}$$

**Answer:** The minimum range length is **16.67 m**. ✅

### Part (b) — VSWR

**Step 1:** Reflection coefficient.
$$\Gamma = \frac{Z_{in} - Z_0}{Z_{in} + Z_0} = \frac{(75 + j25) - 50}{(75 + j25) + 50} = \frac{25 + j25}{125 + j25}$$

**Step 2:** Magnitude.

$$\vert \Gamma \vert = \frac{\sqrt{25^2 + 25^2}}{\sqrt{125^2 + 25^2}} = \frac{25\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{16250}} \approx \frac{35.36}{127.48} \approx 0.2774$$

**Step 3:** VSWR.
$$VSWR = \frac{1 + 0.2774}{1 - 0.2774} = \frac{1.2774}{0.7226} \approx 1.768$$

**Answer:** $VSWR \approx 1.77$. ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** A perfectly matched antenna ($Z_{in} = 50 \, \Omega$) would give VSWR = 1. Our $Z_{in}$ is mismatched (real part higher + reactive part), so VSWR > 1 is physically consistent. Good engineering intuition!

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Measurement Methods

| Method | Quantity Measured | Accuracy | Complexity | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Two-Antenna** | Absolute Gain | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Gain-Transfer** | Gain (vs. standard) | High | Low | Low |
| **Three-Antenna** | Gain (no standard) | Medium | Medium | Low |
| **Wheeler Cap** | Efficiency | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Pattern Integration** | Directivity | High | Medium | Medium |
| **Near-Field Scan** | Full pattern, phase | Very High | Very High | Very High |
| **Slotted Line** | VSWR, Impedance | Medium | High | Low |
| **VNA** | S-parameters, VSWR, Phase | Very High | Low | High |
| **Polarization Pattern** | AR, Tilt angle | Medium | Low | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Far-Field Antenna Measurement Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[Source Side]
        sigGen[Synthesized Signal Generator]
        txAmp[Power Amplifier]
        txAnt[Transmitting Antenna]
    end
    subgraph RCV[Receive Side]
        rxAnt[AUT on Positioner]
        lna[Low Noise Amplifier]
        recvr[Spectrum Analyzer or VNA]
        pc[Pattern Recorder and PC]
    end
    sigGen --> txAmp --> txAnt
    txAnt -.Free space path.-> rxAnt
    rxAnt --> lna --> recvr --> pc
    pc -. Stepper control .-> rxAnt
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The signal generator produces a clean CW or swept-frequency tone, which is amplified and radiated by the transmitting antenna. The AUT receives this signal, which is then amplified (if needed) and recorded. The PC controls the positioner rotation and stores the received power vs. angle — building the **radiation pattern** in real time.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Anechoic Chamber Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    outer[Shielded Metallic Enclosure] --> ferrite[Ferrite Tile Inner Lining]
    ferrite --> foam[Pyramidal RF Absorber Wedges]
    foam --> qz[Quiet Zone Region]
    qz --> aut[AUT on Positioner Tower]
    qz --> src[Source Antenna on Tower]
    aut --> ctrl[Computer Controlled Positioner]
    ctrl --> vna[Vector Network Analyzer]
    vna --> mon[Display and Data Logging]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Gain Measurement (Comparison Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Connect STD Horn to VNA] --> B[Calibrate VNA at Test Frequency]
    B --> C[Measure Pr STD as Reference]
    C --> D[Replace STD with AUT Antenna]
    D --> E[Measure Pr AUT at Same Frequency]
    E --> F[Compute GAUT in dB]
    F --> G[Report GAUT dBi]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Three-Antenna Gain Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Measure Pr12 using Antennas 1 and 2] --> B[Measure Pr23 using Antennas 2 and 3]
    B --> C[Measure Pr13 using Antennas 1 and 3]
    C --> D[Form Three Friis Equations]
    D --> E[Solve Simultaneously]
    E --> F[Obtain G1, G2, G3]
```

## 4.5 Decision Flow — Choosing an Antenna Range

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need to Measure AUT] --> B{AUT Size vs Wavelength}
    B -->|D small| C[Use Indoor Anechoic Chamber]
    B -->|D very large| D[Use Compact Range or Near Field]
    C --> E{Test Frequency Band}
    E -->|1 to 40 GHz| F[Standard Pyramidal Absorber Chamber]
    E -->|Below 1 GHz| G[Ferite Loaded Chamber]
    E -->|mmWave above 40 GHz| H[Compact Range or Near Field Scanner]
```

## 4.6 Schematic — Impedance / VSWR Measurement (Slotted Line)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tx[TX Signal Source] --> sl[Slotted Line Section with Probe]
    sl --> dut[Antenna Under Test]
    sl --> probe[Movable Probe Detector]
    probe --> meter[VSWR Meter or Detector]
    meter --> chart[Smith Chart Plot]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
> **Define an antenna range. List any three types of antenna ranges used for antenna measurements.**

**Model Answer:**
An **antenna range** is a specialized measurement facility designed to simulate free-space, far-field conditions for accurate radiation pattern and gain characterization of an antenna.

Three common types are:
1. **Elevated (Free-Space) Range** — both antennas mounted on tall towers.
2. **Ground Reflection Range** — antennas placed over a smooth, flat ground plane.
3. **Compact (Collapsed) Range** — uses a parabolic reflector to produce planar waves in a small chamber.

Other valid types: Slant Range, Near-Field Range (Planar/Spherical/Cylindrical). ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
> **What is the significance of the Fraunhofer distance in antenna measurements? Write the formula.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Fraunhofer distance** ($d_{\min}$) defines the minimum range length beyond which the wavefront incident on the Antenna Under Test (AUT) can be approximated as a **planar wavefront**, ensuring the AUT is in the **far-field region**. It is essential for accurate radiation pattern, gain, and phase measurements because:
- Phase variation across the AUT aperture is limited to $\pi/8$ rad (22.5°).
- The angular field distribution becomes independent of range.

**Formula:**
$$d_{\min} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}$$

where $D$ is the largest AUT dimension and $\lambda$ is the wavelength. ✅

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

> **(a)** Explain the **two-antenna method** and the **three-antenna method** for measurement of antenna gain with neat block diagrams. Discuss the relative merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A pyramidal horn antenna with aperture $0.4 \times 0.3$ m is to be tested at 6 GHz using an outdoor elevated range. Compute: (i) the Fraunhofer distance, (ii) the VSWR if $Z_{in} = 60 + j30 \, \Omega$ and $Z_0 = 50 \, \Omega$, and (iii) the reflection coefficient magnitude. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Gain Measurement Methods

**Two-Antenna Method:**
Two identical antennas (assumed to have equal gain $G$) are placed on the antenna range at the far-field distance. The Friis transmission equation is used:

$$G = \frac{4\pi d}{\lambda} \sqrt{\frac{P_r}{P_t}}$$

**Block diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)

**Merits:**
- Simple, no calibrated standard horn required.
- Quick field measurement.

**Demerits:**
- Requires two **identical** antennas (a strong assumption).
- Cable losses and connector mismatches introduce error.
- Inaccurate if antennas are not truly identical.

**Three-Antenna Method:**
Used when no calibrated standard gain horn is available. Three unknown antennas (1, 2, 3) are paired in three combinations, and Friis equation is written for each:

$$G_1 G_2 = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 \frac{P_{r12}}{P_{t1}}$$

$$G_2 G_3 = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 \frac{P_{r23}}{P_{t2}}$$

$$G_1 G_3 = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 \frac{P_{r13}}{P_{t1}}$$

Multiplying all three and taking the cube root:

$$G_1 = \left[\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^6 \frac{P_{r12} P_{r13}}{P_{t1}^2 P_{r23}}\right]^{1/6}$$

Similarly for $G_2$ and $G_3$.

**Merits:** No standard horn needed; only range length, frequency, and power measurements.

**Demerits:** Three separate measurements → cumulative error; assumes reciprocity ($G_t = G_r$). ✅

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:**
- Aperture $D = \max(0.4, 0.3) = 0.4$ m
- Frequency $f = 6$ GHz
- $Z_{in} = 60 + j30 \, \Omega$, $Z_0 = 50 \, \Omega$

**(i) Fraunhofer Distance:**

Step 1 — Wavelength:
$$\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^9} = 0.05 \, \text{m}$$

Step 2 — Apply formula:
$$d_{\min} = \frac{2 \times (0.4)^2}{0.05} = \frac{0.32}{0.05} = 6.4 \, \text{m}$$

**Answer:** $d_{\min} = 6.4$ m. ✅

**(ii) Reflection Coefficient:**

$$\Gamma = \frac{Z_{in} - Z_0}{Z_{in} + Z_0} = \frac{(60 + j30) - 50}{(60 + j30) + 50} = \frac{10 + j30}{110 + j30}$$

$$\vert \Gamma \vert = \frac{\sqrt{10^2 + 30^2}}{\sqrt{110^2 + 30^2}} = \frac{\sqrt{1000}}{\sqrt{13000}} = \frac{31.62}{114.02} \approx 0.2774$$

**Answer:** $\vert \Gamma \vert \approx 0.2774$. ✅

**(iii) VSWR:**

$$VSWR = \frac{1 + 0.2774}{1 - 0.2774} = \frac{1.2774}{0.7226} \approx 1.768$$

**Answer:** $VSWR \approx 1.77$. ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> - **Forgetting $\sin\theta$** in pattern integration → loses 2 marks.
> - **Confusing $D$ (aperture) with wavelength** in $d_{\min}$ formula → entire answer wrong.
> - **Using $\max$ dimension** of the aperture (not both) — always use the **largest** dimension $D$ for the worst-case far-field criterion.
> - **Skipping the square root** in the two-antenna gain formula — examiners are very strict here.

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the measurement of **radiation pattern** of an antenna using an **anechoic chamber**. Mention the role of pyramidal RF absorbers. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the measurement of **polarization** of an antenna. Define **Axial Ratio (AR)** and compute the AR for a polarization ellipse with major axis $E_{max} = 6$ mV/m and minor axis $E_{min} = 2$ mV/m. Comment on the polarization state. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Radiation Pattern in Anechoic Chamber

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes.)

**Procedure:**
1. The **AUT** is mounted on a positioner tower inside the anechoic chamber.
2. A **source (transmitting) antenna** is placed at the far-field distance on the opposite tower.
3. The transmitting antenna radiates a CW signal at the test frequency from a signal generator.
4. The AUT rotates in **azimuth** (and/or **elevation**) under computer control.
5. A **receiving chain** (LNA + Spectrum Analyzer / VNA) records the received power at each angular step.
6. Data is plotted as **Received Power (dB) vs Angle** to obtain the radiation pattern.

**Role of Pyramidal RF Absorbers:**
- Pyramid-shaped carbon-loaded foam wedges are mounted on the chamber walls, floor, and ceiling.
- They absorb incident EM waves by progressive impedance tapering, reducing reflections by **40 dB or more**.
- This creates a **"simulated free-space"** environment — equivalent to anechoic free space.
- The **Quiet Zone** — the volume where the AUT is placed — has amplitude variation $\leq \pm 0.5$ dB and phase variation $\leq \pm 5^\circ$. ✅

#### Part (b) — Polarization Measurement & AR Computation

**Method (Polarization Pattern Method):**
1. A linearly polarized probe antenna is aligned with the AUT's boresight.
2. The probe is rotated about its boresight axis in $10^\circ$ or $15^\circ$ increments.
3. The received power is recorded as a function of probe rotation angle.
4. For a **circularly polarized** AUT, the received power is **constant** at all probe angles; the phase indicates LHCP or RHCP.
5. For a **linearly polarized** AUT, the received power follows a $\cos^2$ pattern.
6. For an **elliptically polarized** AUT, the received power varies between $E_{max}^2$ and $E_{min}^2$.

**Axial Ratio (AR) Definition:**
$$AR = \frac{\text{Major axis of polarization ellipse}}{\text{Minor axis of polarization ellipse}} = \frac{E_{max}}{E_{min}}$$

**Numerical Computation:**
- $E_{max} = 6$ mV/m, $E_{min} = 2$ mV/m
- $AR = \frac{6}{2} = 3$

**Comment on Polarization State:**
- AR = 1 → Circular polarization
- AR = $\infty$ → Linear polarization
- $1 < AR < \infty$ → **Elliptical polarization**

Since $AR = 3$ (finite, not 1, not $\infty$), the antenna is **elliptically polarized**. Specifically, it is closer to linear (AR = 3 is a "moderately elongated" ellipse) than to circular. ✅

**Conversion to dB (optional, often asked):**
$$AR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(3) \approx 9.54 \, \text{dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> - **Not commenting on the polarization state** from AR value → loses 1–2 marks.
> - **Forgetting that anechoic chamber walls MUST be covered with absorbers on all six sides** — partial coverage ruins the quiet zone.
> - **Confusing LHCP with RHCP** — examiners expect students to state the **IEEE convention**: RHCP = wave whose E-field rotates **clockwise when viewed from the direction of propagation**.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Antenna Measurements**

- ✅ **Fraunhofer Distance:** $d_{\min} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}$ — Always use the **largest** AUT dimension.
- ✅ **Friis Equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ — Foundation of all radiated power measurements.
- ✅ **Two-Antenna Gain:** $G = \frac{4\pi d}{\lambda} \sqrt{P_r/P_t}$ — Assumes **identical** antennas.
- ✅ **Three-Antenna Gain:** Use when no standard horn is available; solve three Friis equations simultaneously.
- ✅ **Gain-Transfer (Comparison):** $G_{AUT} = G_{STD} + 10\log_{10}(P_{AUT}/P_{STD})$ — Most accurate when a calibrated horn is available.
- ✅ **VSWR:** $VSWR = (1 + \vert\Gamma\vert)/(1 - \vert\Gamma\vert)$ — VSWR = 1 is a perfect match.
- ✅ **Return Loss:** $RL = -20\log_{10}\vert\Gamma\vert$ — Always a **negative** dB value.
- ✅ **Directivity from Pattern:** $D_0 = 4\pi / \Omega_A$ — Don't forget the $\sin\theta$ weighting in numerical integration.
- ✅ **Efficiency:** $\eta = G / D_0$ — Wheeler Cap or Q-factor methods for small antennas.
- ✅ **Polarization:** AR = 1 → Circular; AR = $\infty$ → Linear; otherwise → Elliptical.
- ✅ **Anechoic Chamber:** Quiet zone, RF absorbers (ferrite for low freq, pyramidal foam for high freq), and EMI shielding are the three pillars.
- ✅ **Anechoic Chamber vs Outdoor Range:** Indoor chambers are more reliable but smaller; outdoor ranges handle larger DUTs but are weather-dependent.
- ✅ **Compact Range:** Uses a parabolic reflector to **collapse** the far-field distance — great for indoor testing of large antennas.
- ✅ **Near-Field to Far-Field Transformation:** Probe-scan the near field, apply FFT or modal expansion to compute far-field — used for electrically large antennas.
- ✅ **Common Pitfall:** Multipath reflections in a non-anechoic lab cause **± 3 to 10 dB errors** in pattern measurements — the #1 reason to use a chamber.
- ✅ **VNA-based Setup:** Modern measurements (S-parameters, VSWR, phase, gain) are dominated by Vector Network Analyzers — single instrument replaces multiple legacy tools.
- ✅ **KTU Frequency:** Always quote the **test frequency** explicitly; Fraunhofer distance depends on it.
- ✅ **Cross-Verification:** For any KTU 14-mark question, always cross-check units (Hz, m, dBi, dB) and provide a final boxed answer.

> 📌 **One-Line Mnemonic for Quick Recall:**
> **"**F**ar-field = 2D²/λ; **G**ain = (4πd/λ)√(Pr/Pt); **P**attern uses sinθ; **V**SWR uses Γ; **A**R = 3 means **E**lliptical."** 🎯
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Antenna Range
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Antenna Range

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Antenna Range** is a precisely engineered outdoor or indoor measurement facility used to characterize the radiation properties of an Antenna Under Test (AUT) under controlled, reproducible, and quantified electromagnetic conditions that emulate free-space propagation. It is the laboratory equivalent of a "frictionless surface" in classical mechanics — a controlled environment designed so that the *only* electromagnetic contribution reaching the probe comes from the AUT itself, free from the corrupting influence of ground bounce, surrounding scatterers, and unwanted multipath reflections.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Antenna ranges are classified by their geometry, the location of the source and the test antenna, and the method used to suppress or eliminate the ground reflection. The two principal categories are **Reflection-Free Ranges (Indoor)** and **Reflection-Ranges (Outdoor)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — "The Echo-Free Concert Hall":**
> Imagine trying to measure exactly how loud a single violinist sounds in an empty valley. If the violin is faint and you are far away, the echoes bouncing off the valley walls and the floor are often *louder* than the violin itself. Your measurement is ruined.
>
> An **antenna range** solves the exact same problem for invisible electromagnetic waves. The walls of the range are lined with **anechoic foam (radiation-absorbent material — RAM)** that swallows the echoes the way soft velvet drapes swallow sound in a recording studio. The geometry is set so that the AUT behaves *exactly* as if it were suspended in infinite free space, far from anything. Then, and only then, can we trust the numbers we read from the receiver.

### 1.3 Why Antenna Ranges Are Necessary

The radiation pattern of an antenna is, by definition, the **far-field** spatial distribution of its radiated power density. To measure this faithfully we must satisfy three conditions simultaneously:

1. **Far-Field Condition:** The probe (receiving antenna) must be located in the Fraunhofer region of the AUT.
2. **Illumination Uniformity:** The AUT must be illuminated by a plane wave (or equivalently, the source must be far enough that its wavefront is essentially planar at the AUT).
3. **Reflection Suppression:** All unwanted echoes from the surroundings must be reduced to a negligible level (typically **−40 dB** or lower relative to the direct signal for a high-precision range).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Threshold for Echo Suppression:**
> A well-designed range typically requires that spurious signals (reflections) be at least **30 dB to 40 dB below** the desired direct signal. For ultra-precise gain measurements, the standard is **−50 dB** or stricter.

### 1.4 Antenna Range — Block Overview

A complete antenna range consists of:

- **Source (transmitting) antenna** — radiates a stable, known signal.
- **AUT (receiving) antenna** — the device under test.
- **Range geometry** — outdoor elevated, ground-reflection, compact, or anechoic chamber.
- **Positioner / rotator** — orients the AUT for pattern cut.
- **RF instrumentation** — signal generator, receiver, cabling, recording.
- **Absorbers / RAM** — quash reflections.
- **Quiet zone** — the volume in which measurements are valid.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Field Regions of an Antenna

Before we can design a range, we must respect the three well-defined field regions of a radiating aperture of largest dimension $D$ operating at wavelength $\lambda$:

| Region | Boundary | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Reactive Near-Field | $R < 0.62 \sqrt{D^3/\lambda}$ | Stored energy dominates; field has reactive (imaginary) components. |
| Radiating Near-Field (Fresnel) | $0.62 \sqrt{D^3/\lambda} \le R < 2D^2/\lambda$ | Radiation exists, but angular distribution is *not yet* fully formed; pattern varies with distance. |
| Far-Field (Fraunhofer) | $R \ge 2D^2/\lambda$ | Pattern shape is independent of distance; power falls as $1/R^2$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fraunhofer Distance:**
> $$R_{\min} \;=\; \frac{2D^2}{\lambda} \quad \text{(standard criterion, } D \gg \lambda\text{)}$$
> is the *minimum* test distance at which an aperture antenna of size $D$ can be considered to be in its far field. This single number dictates the physical length of an outdoor range.

For an *electrically small* antenna ($D < \lambda$), the criterion is replaced by a fixed distance rule, e.g. $R \ge 10\lambda$ or $R \ge 3\lambda$, but for almost all KTU problems $D \gg \lambda$ applies.

### 2.2 Classification of Antenna Ranges

> [!NOTE]
> The classification used by KTU (Balanis, Antenna Theory) splits ranges into two fundamental families:

**A. Reflection-Free (Indoor) Ranges**
- **Anechoic Chamber** — fully lined with RAM; rectangular or tapered.
- **Compact (CATR) Range** — uses a parabolic or reflector collimator to synthesize a plane wave at short distance.

**B. Reflection (Outdoor) Ranges**
- **Elevated Range** — both antennas are mounted on tall towers so ground bounce is geometrically blocked.
- **Ground-Reflection Range** — ground bounce is *allowed* but its phase is engineered to add in-phase to the direct ray.
- **Slant Range** — the test antenna is mounted on a low hill or slope.

### 2.3 The Quiet Zone

The **Quiet Zone (QZ)** is the cylindrical (or rectangular) volume within the range over which the field distribution is acceptable for measurements. Typical QZ specifications:

| Parameter | Typical Spec |
|---|---|
| Amplitude taper | $\le \pm 0.5$ dB |
| Phase deviation | $\le \pm 5^\circ$ |
| Cross-polarization isolation | $\le -25$ dB |
| Reflectivity (residual) | $\le -40$ dB |

### 2.4 Ground-Reflection Range — Two-Ray Model

In a ground-reflection range, the AUT receives a direct ray plus a ray reflected from the ground. Using image theory, the reflection coefficient for a flat earth is:

$$\Gamma \;=\; \frac{\sin\psi - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi}}{\sin\psi + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi}}$$

where $\psi$ is the grazing angle and $\varepsilon_r$ is the relative permittivity of the ground. For a *perfectly conducting* ground ($\varepsilon_r \to \infty$, $\sigma \to \infty$), $\Gamma = -1$ (magnitude 1, phase 180°).

The total received field is the phasor sum of direct and reflected waves:

$$E_{\text{total}} \;=\; E_d \left[ 1 + \Gamma \, \rho \, e^{j(\Delta\phi)} \right]$$

where $\rho$ is the spherical wave divergence factor and $\Delta\phi$ is the phase difference between the two paths.

**Path difference for two-ray geometry** (transmitter height $h_t$, receiver height $h_r$, range $d$):

$$\Delta R \;\approx\; \frac{2 h_t h_r}{d}$$

**Phase difference:**

$$\Delta\phi \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\Delta R \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\frac{2 h_t h_r}{d}$$

For in-phase reinforcement (constructive interference) at the receiving antenna at a broadside position, we need the two rays to arrive in phase, which is engineered by the geometry of the tower heights and the test distance.

### 2.5 Gain Measurement Methods

**A. Two-Antenna (Comparison) Method:**
$$G_t G_r \;=\; \frac{P_r}{P_t} \left( \frac{4\pi R}{\lambda} \right)^2$$
If $G_t = G_r = G$ (identical antennas):
$$G \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{P_r}{P_t}} \cdot \frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}$$

**B. Three-Antenna Method** (used when no two antennas are identical):
$$G_1 G_2 = \left( \frac{4\pi R_{12}}{\lambda} \right)^2 \frac{P_{r12}}{P_{t1}}, \quad
G_2 G_3 = \left( \frac{4\pi R_{23}}{\lambda} \right)^2 \frac{P_{r23}}{P_{t2}}, \quad
G_1 G_3 = \left( \frac{4\pi R_{13}}{\lambda} \right)^2 \frac{P_{r13}}{P_{t3}}$$

Solving:
$$G_1 = \sqrt{\frac{(G_1G_2)(G_1G_3)}{G_2G_3}}$$

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fraunhofer distance | $R_{\min} = 2D^2/\lambda$ | For $D \gg \lambda$; distance in metres |
| 2 | Reactive near-field bound | $R = 0.62\sqrt{D^3/\lambda}$ | Boundary in metres |
| 3 | Fresnel-region upper bound | $R = 2D^2/\lambda$ | Same as Fraunhofer distance |
| 4 | Friis power-transfer | $P_r/P_t = G_t G_r (\lambda/4\pi R)^2$ | Far-field only |
| 5 | Two-ray path difference | $\Delta R \approx 2h_t h_r / d$ | Valid for $d \gg h_t, h_r$ |
| 6 | Phase difference | $\Delta\phi = (2\pi/\lambda)\,\Delta R$ | In radians |
| 7 | Reflection coeff. (ground) | $\Gamma = (\sin\psi - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi})/(\sin\psi + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi})$ | Pol: vertical |
| 8 | Perfect-ground reflection | $\Gamma = -1$ | $\varepsilon_r \to \infty$ |
| 9 | Two-antenna gain | $G = (1/2)\sqrt{(P_r/P_t)}\,(4\pi R/\lambda)$ | $G_t = G_r = G$ |
| 10 | Three-antenna gain (one of three) | $G_1 = \sqrt{(G_1G_2)(G_1G_3)/(G_2G_3)}$ | Symmetric expression |
| 11 | Quiet-zone taper (dB) | $\Delta_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}(1 \pm \Gamma_{\text{residual}})$ | Residual error |
| 12 | Far-field pattern solid angle | $\Omega_A = 4\pi/\text{Directivity}$ | Steradians |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Use of the Friis Equation in a Range:**
> In a real range, the Friis equation is the *theoretical baseline* of received power. Any deviation between *measured* and *theoretical* $P_r$ is direct evidence of range errors (reflections, misalignment, near-field contamination). Engineers routinely use this deviation as a *figure of merit* of the range itself.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Fraunhofer Distance

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Show that the maximum phase error across an aperture of size $D$, when the observation point is at finite distance $R$, becomes acceptably small ($\le \pi/8$ rad or $22.5^\circ$) when $R \ge 2D^2/\lambda$.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the aperture.**
Consider a linear aperture of length $D$, centred at the origin, lying along the $x$-axis. The observation point $P$ is on the $z$-axis at distance $R_0$, and a generic source point on the aperture is at $x$ where $-D/2 \le x \le D/2$.

The distance from the source point at $x$ to $P$ is:

$$R_x \;=\; \sqrt{R_0^2 + x^2}$$

**Step 2 — Binomial expansion.**
For $R_0 \gg x$ (far observation), use the binomial expansion:

$$R_x \;=\; R_0 \sqrt{1 + \frac{x^2}{R_0^2}} \;\approx\; R_0 \left[ 1 + \frac{x^2}{2R_0^2} - \frac{x^4}{8R_0^4} + \cdots \right]$$

**Step 3 — Difference between edge and centre paths.**
The path difference between the ray from the aperture edge ($x = D/2$) and the ray from the centre ($x = 0$) is:

$$\Delta R \;=\; R_{D/2} - R_0 \;\approx\; \frac{(D/2)^2}{2R_0} \;=\; \frac{D^2}{8R_0}$$

**Step 4 — Convert path difference to phase.**

$$\Delta\phi \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\Delta R \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{D^2}{8 R_0} \;=\; \frac{\pi D^2}{4 \lambda R_0}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the $\pi/8$ phase-error criterion.**
Setting $\Delta\phi \le \pi/8$:

$$\frac{\pi D^2}{4 \lambda R_0} \;\le\; \frac{\pi}{8}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad R_0 \;\ge\; \frac{2 D^2}{\lambda}$$

**Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{\,R_{\min} \;=\; \frac{2 D^2}{\lambda}\,}$$

**Numerical sanity check:** For $D = 1$ m and $f = 3$ GHz ($\lambda = 0.1$ m):

$$R_{\min} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 1^2}{0.1} \;=\; 20 \text{ m}$$

This is why a 1 GHz Wi-Fi antenna (10 cm) needs at least ~2 cm, but a 1 m satellite-dish test needs 20 m of clear indoor space — a direct motivation for **compact ranges**!

### 3.2 Derivation of Two-Antenna Gain Formula

**Starting from the Friis transmission equation (far field):**

$$\frac{P_r}{P_t} \;=\; G_t G_r \left( \frac{\lambda}{4\pi R} \right)^2$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange for the product of gains:**

$$G_t G_r \;=\; \frac{P_r}{P_t} \left( \frac{4\pi R}{\lambda} \right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Impose the two-antenna condition.** When the two antennas are *identical* ($G_t = G_r = G$):

$$G^2 \;=\; \frac{P_r}{P_t} \left( \frac{4\pi R}{\lambda} \right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Take the positive square root (gain is positive):**

$$\boxed{\,G \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{P_r}{P_t}} \cdot \frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example.**
Suppose $P_t = 1$ W, $P_r = 10 \mu$W, $R = 50$ m, $f = 1$ GHz ($\lambda = 0.3$ m):

$$G \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{10 \times 10^{-6}} \cdot \frac{4\pi \cdot 50}{0.3}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 3.162 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \frac{628.32}{0.3}$$

$$= 0.5 \cdot 3.162 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 2094.4$$

$$\approx 3.31 \;\; \text{(linear, i.e. } 5.2 \text{ dBi)}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Range Types vs. Properties

| Range Type | Geometry | Typical Use | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Outdoor Elevated** | Towers ≥ 1 wave; clear LOS | HF, VHF, UHF, large antennas | Real free-space, no ground bounce | Weather, RFI, large land |
| **Ground-Reflection** | Both antennas low; flat ground | UHF, SHF | Compact, real ground used | Sensitive to terrain, weather, weeds |
| **Rectangular Anechoic** | Fully RAM-lined box | Microwave, mm-wave | All-weather, full RF quiet | Expensive, RAM, low-frequency limit |
| **Tapered Anechoic** | Pyramidal RAM + horn feed | EMC, broadband | Wideband | Lower QZ at low freq |
| **Compact (CATR)** | Parabolic reflector + feed | mm-wave, sub-mm, large $D$ | Short physical length | Expensive reflector, edge diffraction |
| **Near-Field (Planar/Spherical/Cylindrical)** | Probe scans over AUT | Phased arrays, large $D$ | Indoor, full 3D pattern via FFT | Complex software, careful calibration |

### 3.4 Compact (CATR) Range — Conceptual Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A CATR uses a parabolic reflector to convert a spherical wave from a feed horn into a *collimated plane wave* at the AUT. The AUT, though only meters away, "sees" a wavefront as if it had come from infinity.

The geometric constraint is:

$$D_{\text{reflector}} \;\ge\; 2R_{\min} \cdot \tan(\theta_{\text{feed}}/2) + D_{\text{AUT}}$$

This guarantees that the AUT sits in the geometric shadow of the reflector across the full angular extent of the feed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics
