<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define radiation efficiency of an antenna. How is it different from total antenna efficiency?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Radiation efficiency is the ratio of the total power radiated by the antenna to the total power accepted by the antenna at its input terminals:

$$\eta_{rad} = \frac{P_{rad}}{P_{in}} = \frac{R_{rad}}{R_{rad} + R_{loss}}$$

**Difference (1 Mark):**
Total antenna efficiency $e_0$ also includes the reflection (mismatch) loss at the feed:

$$e_0 = \eta_{rad} \cdot (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2)$$

Hence, $e_0 \leq \eta_{rad}$ always, with equality only when the antenna is perfectly matched ($\Gamma = 0$).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*A half-wave dipole has a radiation resistance of $73 \, \Omega$ and a loss resistance of $7 \, \Omega$ at its resonant frequency. Calculate the radiation efficiency in dB.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply the resistance formula:**

$$\eta_{rad} = \frac{R_{rad}}{R_{rad} + R_{loss}} = \frac{73}{73 + 7} = \frac{73}{80} = 0.9125$$

**Step 2 — Convert to dB:**

$$\eta_{rad} \, (\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(0.9125) = -0.397 \, \text{dB}$$

**Answer:** $\eta_{rad} = 0.9125$ or equivalently $-0.397$ dB.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style]**

**Q.A.** *(a)* Derive the expression for radiation efficiency in terms of radiation resistance and loss resistance. *(7 Marks)*

*(b)* An antenna has $R_{rad} = 50 \, \Omega$ and $R_{loss} = 10 \, \Omega$ when measured at the feed point. If 100 W is delivered to the antenna terminals, find (i) radiation efficiency, (ii) radiated power, (iii) power lost as heat, and (iv) efficiency in dB. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

The input current at the antenna terminals is $I_0$. By the definition of radiation resistance, the radiated power is:

$$P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2} I_0^2 R_{rad}$$

By the definition of loss resistance, the dissipated power is:

$$P_{loss} = \frac{1}{2} I_0^2 R_{loss}$$

By conservation of energy:

$$P_{in} = P_{rad} + P_{loss} = \frac{1}{2} I_0^2 (R_{rad} + R_{loss})$$

Therefore:

$$\eta_{rad} = \frac{P_{rad}}{P_{in}} = \frac{R_{rad}}{R_{rad} + R_{loss}}$$

This shows that when both resistances are referenced to the same current (the input current $I_0$), the efficiency depends only on their ratio. (Conclusion — 1 Mark)

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $R_{rad} = 50 \, \Omega$, $R_{loss} = 10 \, \Omega$, $P_{in} = 100$ W.

**(i) Radiation Efficiency (2 Marks):**

$$\eta_{rad} = \frac{50}{50 + 10} = \frac{50}{60} = 0.8333 \quad \text{or} \quad 83.33\%$$

**(ii) Radiated Power (2 Marks):**

$$P_{rad} = \eta_{rad} \cdot P_{in} = 0.8333 \times 100 = 83.33 \, \text{W}$$

**(iii) Power Lost as Heat (2 Marks):**

$$P_{loss} = P_{in} - P_{rad} = 100 - 83.33 = 16.67 \, \text{W}$$

**Cross-check:** $P_{loss}/P_{in} = 10/60 = 0.1667$ ✓

**(iv) Efficiency in dB (1 Mark):**

$$\eta_{rad} \, (\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(0.8333) = -0.791 \, \text{dB}$$

**Final Answers:** $\eta_{rad} = 83.33\%$, $P_{rad} = 83.33$ W, $P_{loss} = 16.67$ W, $-0.791$ dB.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B.** *(a)* With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the equivalent circuit representation of a practical antenna, distinguishing between radiation resistance and loss resistance. *(7 Marks)*

*(b)* A short dipole of length $0.05\lambda$ has a loss resistance of $1.5 \, \Omega$. The current at the feed point is $I_0 = 2$ A (peak). Calculate the total input power, radiated power, and radiation efficiency. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Explanation (7 Marks):**

**Equivalent Circuit (3 Marks):**

A practical transmitting antenna, when viewed from its input terminals, can be modeled as a series combination of two resistances:

- A **radiation resistance** $R_{rad}$ — a fictitious (virtual) resistance whose value is defined such that the power it would dissipate, when carrying the antenna input current $I_0$, equals the actual radiated power:

$$P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2} I_0^2 R_{rad}$$

- A **loss resistance** $R_{loss}$ — a real, physical resistance representing the cumulative ohmic losses in the antenna conductor ($I^2R$ heating), the lossy dielectric insulators, and (for monopoles) the finite-conductivity ground plane:

$$P_{loss} = \frac{1}{2} I_0^2 R_{loss}$$

**Key Distinctions (3 Marks):**

| Property | $R_{rad}$ | $R_{loss}$ |
|----------|-----------|------------|
| Nature | Virtual / fictitious | Real / physical |
| Power transfer | Radiated as EM waves | Dissipated as heat |
| Frequency dependence | Strong (e.g., $\propto (l/\lambda)^2$ for short dipole) | Weak (mostly via skin effect) |
| Affected by surroundings | Yes — nearby objects change it | No — intrinsic to the conductor |

The **total input resistance** of the antenna is $R_{in} = R_{rad} + R_{loss}$ (for a resonant antenna, ignoring reactance). The radiation efficiency then follows directly as $\eta_{rad} = R_{rad}/(R_{rad} + R_{loss})$.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $l = 0.05\lambda$, $R_{loss} = 1.5 \, \Omega$, $I_0 = 2$ A (peak), short dipole.

**Step 1 — Compute $R_{rad}$ for the short dipole (3 Marks):**

$$R_{rad} = 80\pi^2 \left( \frac{l}{\lambda} \right)^2 = 80 \pi^2 (0.05)^2$$

$$R_{rad} = 80 \times 9.8696 \times 0.0025 = 1.974 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Total input power (1 Mark):**

$$P_{in} = \frac{1}{2} I_0^2 (R_{rad} + R_{loss}) = \frac{1}{2} (2)^2 (1.974 + 1.5)$$

$$P_{in} = 2 \times 3.474 = 6.948 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Radiated power (1 Mark):**

$$P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2} I_0^2 R_{rad} = 2 \times 1.974 = 3.948 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Radiation efficiency (2 Marks):**

$$\eta_{rad} = \frac{P_{rad}}{P_{in}} = \frac{3.948}{6.948} = 0.568$$

**Verification via resistance ratio:**

$$\eta_{rad} = \frac{1.974}{1.974 + 1.5} = \frac{1.974}{3.474} = 0.568 \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answers:** $P_{in} = 6.948$ W, $P_{rad} = 3.948$ W, $\eta_{rad} = 56.8\%$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up $P_{in}$ and $P_{avail}$** — $P_{in}$ is the power *accepted* by the antenna (after mismatch), not the power available from the source. Total efficiency uses $P_{avail}$; radiation efficiency uses $P_{in}$.
> 2. **Using $I_{rms}$ vs $I_{peak}$ incorrectly** — The formula $P = \tfrac{1}{2} I_0^2 R$ uses **peak** current $I_0$. If a problem gives $I_{rms}$, convert via $I_0 = \sqrt{2} \, I_{rms}$.
> 3. **Forgetting that $R_{rad}$ and $R_{loss}$ must be referenced to the same current** — If $R_{rad}$ is given at the maximum current point of a non-uniform distribution, you must either refer both to the same point or use the generalized form $\eta_{rad} = P_{rad}/(P_{rad} + P_{loss})$.
> 4. **Conflating radiation efficiency with total efficiency** — The factor $(1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2)$ is **not** part of radiation efficiency. Adding it makes the quantity a total efficiency.
> 5. **Ignoring units in numerical answers** — Always state $P_{rad}$ in watts and efficiency as a percentage or in dB; examiners deduct marks for ambiguous units.
> 6. **Wrong sign in dB conversion** — Since $\eta_{rad} < 1$, the dB value is always **negative**. Writing "+0.79 dB" instead of "-0.79 dB" will be marked wrong.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Radiation Efficiency:**
>
> - ☐ Radiation efficiency $\eta_{rad} = P_{rad}/P_{in}$ is a **dimensionless** number between 0 and 1.
> - ☐ Two equivalent forms: $\eta_{rad} = P_{rad}/(P_{rad} + P_{loss}) = R_{rad}/(R_{rad} + R_{loss})$.
> - ☐ **$R_{rad}$ is virtual**; $R_{loss}$ is real. Both must be at the same reference current.
> - ☐ **$R_{rad} \propto (l/\lambda)^2$** for short dipoles — efficiency collapses for electrically small antennas.
> - ☐ Half-wave dipole: $R_{rad} \approx 73 \, \Omega$. Quarter-wave monopole: $R_{rad} \approx 36.5 \, \Omega$.
> - ☐ Radiation efficiency **excludes mismatch loss**; total efficiency = $\eta_{rad} \cdot (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2)$.
> - ☐ Conversion: $\eta_{rad} \, (\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(\eta_{rad})$ — always **negative** when $< 1$.
> - ☐ Antenna losses come from: conductor (ohmic) losses, dielectric losses, and ground-plane losses (for monopoles).
> - ☐ Skin effect at high frequencies raises $R_{loss}$, reducing efficiency.
> - ☐ At resonance (no reactive part), the antenna input impedance is purely resistive: $R_{in} = R_{rad} + R_{loss}$.
> - ☐ **For the Hertzian dipole derivation:** remember the final compact form $R_{rad} = 80\pi^2 (dl/\lambda)^2 \, \Omega$.
> - ☐ In KTU 14-mark derivations, always **start from Joule's law**, **apply energy conservation**, and **end with the resistance ratio form** — this satisfies the marking scheme's logical-flow check.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impedance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Impedance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In antenna engineering, **Impedance** characterizes the complex opposition that an antenna presents to the radio-frequency (RF) signal at its feed terminals. It is the cornerstone parameter that governs how effectively RF power is delivered from the transmitter (or received by the receiver) to (or from) free space.

The **Antenna Input Impedance** $Z_{in}$ is formally defined as the ratio of the complex voltage to the complex current at the antenna's feed point (or any specified reference plane):

$$Z_{in} = \frac{V_{in}}{I_{in}} = R_{in} + jX_{in} \quad [\Omega]$$

Where:
- $R_{in}$ = Input Resistance (in Ohms, $\Omega$)
- $X_{in}$ = Input Reactance (in Ohms, $\Omega$)
- $j = \sqrt{-1}$ represents the imaginary operator

The **Input Resistance** further decomposes into two physically meaningful components:

$$R_{in} = R_{rad} + R_{loss}$$

Where:
- $R_{rad}$ = **Radiation Resistance** — the equivalent resistance that would dissipate the same average power as the antenna radiates into space
- $R_{loss}$ = **Ohmic Loss Resistance** — represents power dissipated as heat in the antenna's conductors and nearby lossy dielectrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept**: Radiation resistance is NOT a real resistor! It is a *fictional* resistance that quantifies the antenna's ability to convert conduction currents into electromagnetic radiation. A *higher* $R_{rad}$ generally implies *better* radiation efficiency for the same feed current.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine trying to push water through a complex pipe system:
- **Pure Resistance** is like the friction of the pipe walls (energy lost as heat)
- **Reactance** is like a spring or flywheel connected to the water — it stores energy temporarily and gives it back, but resists rapid changes
- **Impedance** is the *total* opposition — friction + springiness combined
- **Radiation Resistance** is like a nozzle at the end that *uses* the water pressure to do useful work (spray the water outward)

For the antenna, the "useful work" is launching electromagnetic waves into free space. If the impedance is mismatched, RF power bounces back toward the transmitter (like water hammer in a pipe), causing **Standing Waves** and potentially damaging the source.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Impedance Matters in KTU Problems**: Every antenna problem in Module 2 — dipoles, monopoles, arrays — ends with impedance calculations because impedance determines (1) matching network design, (2) bandwidth, (3) array driving-point currents, and (4) radiation efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Antenna Impedance Concepts

The topic "Impedance" in Module 2 spans four interconnected sub-concepts. Each is essential for solving KTU board problems.

### 2.1.1 Self-Impedance ($Z_{ii}$)
- The input impedance of an antenna *when all other antennas in the vicinity are open-circuited* (no current flows in them).
- Represents the antenna's *intrinsic* impedance characteristic.
- For a center-fed half-wave dipole: $Z_{11} \approx 73.1 + j42.5 \ \Omega$

### 2.1.2 Mutual Impedance ($Z_{ij}, \ i \neq j$)
- The complex ratio of the voltage induced in antenna *i* to the current flowing in antenna *j* (with antenna *i* open-circuited):

$$Z_{ij} = \frac{V_{i,\ open}}{I_{j}} \quad [\Omega]$$

- This is the *coupling mechanism* between elements of an antenna array.
- **Reciprocity Theorem** guarantees $Z_{ij} = Z_{ji}$.

### 2.1.3 Driving-Point Impedance (in an array)
- For an element in an array, the actual terminal impedance is the **sum of self-impedance and the contributions of all other elements**:

$$Z_{i,\ drive} = Z_{ii} + \sum_{j \neq i} \frac{I_{j}}{I_{i}} Z_{ij}$$

- The current ratios $\frac{I_{j}}{I_{i}}$ are dictated by the array excitation law.

### 2.1.4 Radiation Resistance of Common Antennas
| Antenna Type | Length / Mode | Radiation Resistance ($R_{rad}$) |
|---|---|---|
| Hertzian (Infinitesimal) Dipole | $L \ll \lambda$ | $R_{rad} \approx 80\pi^2 (L/\lambda)^2$ |
| Half-Wave Dipole | $L = \lambda/2$ | $R_{rad} \approx 73.1 \ \Omega$ |
| Quarter-Wave Monopole | $L = \lambda/4$ over ground | $R_{rad} \approx 36.5 \ \Omega$ |
| Full-Wave Dipole | $L = \lambda$ | $R_{rad} \approx 199 \ \Omega$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Fact**: The quarter-wave monopole has *half* the radiation resistance of the half-wave dipole because the ground plane effectively doubles the radiated power for the same feed current — making it ideal for mobile and base-station antennas.

## 2.2 Operational Logic: How Impedance Affects Antenna Performance

1. **Power Transfer Maximum** occurs when antenna input impedance equals the complex conjugate of the source/feed-line impedance (conjugate matching).
2. **Reflection Coefficient** at the feed point:

$$\Gamma = \frac{Z_{in} - Z_0}{Z_{in} + Z_0}$$

Where $Z_0$ is the characteristic impedance of the feed line (commonly $50 \ \Omega$ or $75 \ \Omega$).

3. **Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR)**:

$$\text{VSWR} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$$

4. **Return Loss**:

$$RL = -20 \log_{10} \vert \Gamma \vert \quad [dB]$$

5. **Impedance Bandwidth** is typically defined as the frequency range over which VSWR $\leq 2$ (equivalently, $\vert \Gamma \vert \leq 1/3$).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Input Impedance | $Z_{in} = R_{in} + jX_{in}$ | $\Omega$ | Complex feed-point opposition |
| 2 | Input Resistance Decomposition | $R_{in} = R_{rad} + R_{loss}$ | $\Omega$ | Radiated + Ohmic losses |
| 3 | Self-Impedance | $Z_{ii}$ | $\Omega$ | Impedance with all neighbors open |
| 4 | Mutual Impedance | $Z_{ij} = V_{i,\ open}/I_{j}$ | $\Omega$ | Coupling between elements |
| 5 | Driving-Point Impedance | $Z_{i} = Z_{ii} + \sum_{j \neq i} (I_{j}/I_{i}) Z_{ij}$ | $\Omega$ | In-array element impedance |
| 6 | Half-Wave Dipole | $Z_{11} \approx 73.1 + j42.5$ | $\Omega$ | Self-impedance |
| 7 | Qtr-Wave Monopole | $Z_{11} \approx 36.5 + j21.25$ | $\Omega$ | Self-impedance over ground |
| 8 | Hertzian Radiation Resistance | $R_{rad} = 80\pi^2 (L/\lambda)^2$ | $\Omega$ | Small-dipole case |
| 9 | Reflection Coefficient | $\Gamma = (Z_{in} - Z_0)/(Z_{in} + Z_0)$ | unitless | Mismatch metric |
| 10 | VSWR | $\text{VSWR} = (1+\vert\Gamma\vert)/(1-\vert\Gamma\vert)$ | ratio | Standing wave magnitude |
| 11 | Return Loss | $RL = -20\log_{10}\vert\Gamma\vert$ | dB | Mismatch loss |
| 12 | Antenna Efficiency | $\eta = R_{rad}/(R_{rad}+R_{loss})$ | ratio (0–1) | Power radiated / power input |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder**: All impedances are in Ohms ($\Omega$). Reactance $X_{in}$ is positive (inductive) for an antenna slightly longer than resonance and negative (capacitive) for slightly shorter.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Impedance matching networks** (L-section, quarter-wave transformer, stub tuner) are designed using the input impedance of the antenna.
- **Antenna analyzers** measure $Z_{in}$ directly across a swept frequency range for tuning.
- **Array design** relies on mutual impedance tables to compute array currents and the resultant driving-point impedances.
- **Mobile handset antennas** must be impedance-matched to $50 \ \Omega$ across multiple cellular bands — a problem that would be impossible without precise impedance characterization.
- **Vector Network Analyzers (VNAs)** in RF labs directly display $Z_{in}$ on Smith charts for real-time matching.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Self-Impedance of a Center-Fed Thin-Wire Antenna (Induced EMF Method)

This is a **classic KTU Module 2 derivation** that examiners frequently expect. The method was formalized by P. S. Carter in 1932.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions
- A thin, perfectly conducting cylindrical wire antenna of length $L$ and radius $a \ll L, \lambda$.
- Fed at its center by a delta-gap voltage source $V_t$.
- The wire lies along the $z$-axis, centered at the origin.
- **Current Distribution Assumption** (sinusoidal, valid for $a \ll \lambda$):

$$I(z) = I_0 \sin\left[k\left(\frac{L}{2} - \vert z \vert\right)\right]$$

where $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the wavenumber and $I_0$ is the input (feed-point) current.

### 3.1.2 Induced EMF Relation

By definition of input impedance:

$$Z_{in} = \frac{V_t}{I_0}$$

The voltage $V_t$ must overcome the back-EMF induced by the antenna's own radiation. Equating the work done by the source to the energy radiated:

$$\frac{1}{2} \vert I_0 \vert^2 Z_{in} = -\frac{1}{2} \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I^*(z) E_z(z) \ dz$$

Solving for $Z_{in}$:

$$Z_{in} = -\frac{1}{\vert I_0 \vert^2} \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I^*(z) E_z(z) \ dz$$

This integral involves the $z$-component of the scattered (or tangential) electric field on the surface of the wire.

### 3.1.3 Tangential Electric Field from Vector Potential

The vector potential due to the wire current is:

$$A_z = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \frac{I(z') e^{-jkR}}{R} \ dz'$$

where $R = \sqrt{(z - z')^2 + a^2}$ is the distance from source point $z'$ on the axis to a surface field point $(z, a)$.

The tangential electric field at the wire surface (assuming perfect conductor, the scattered field equals the negative of the incident field) involves both the vector potential and scalar potential contributions. Under the thin-wire approximation, the **far-zone (radiation) contribution dominates**, and we obtain the *standard induced-EMF result*:

$$Z_{in} = \frac{j\eta_0}{4\pi \sin^2(kL/2)} \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \sin\left[k\left(\frac{L}{2} - \vert z' \vert\right)\right] \left[ \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \frac{I(z) e^{-jkR}}{R} \ dz \right] dz'$$

### 3.1.4 Specialization to Half-Wave Dipole ($L = \lambda/2$)

Set $kL/2 = \pi/2$, so $\sin(kL/2) = 1$. The current distribution simplifies:

$$I(z) = I_0 \cos(kz), \quad -L/2 \leq z \leq L/2$$

Substituting and evaluating the double integral (using the standard far-field approximation with $\int \cos(kz) \cdot ... \, dz$ procedures), the result is the **well-known closed-form expression** for the half-wave dipole's self-impedance:

$$\boxed{Z_{11} \approx 73.1 + j42.5 \ \Omega}$$

The real part ($73.1 \ \Omega$) is the radiation resistance, and the imaginary part ($42.5 \ \Omega$) is the residual inductive reactance (since the dipole is *slightly* longer than electrical half-wave at its physical half-wave length).

### 3.1.5 Final Summary of the Derivation Pipeline

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & I(z) = I_0 \sin\left[k\left(\tfrac{L}{2} - \vert z \vert\right)\right] \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & Z_{in} = -\dfrac{1}{\vert I_0 \vert^2} \displaystyle\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I^*(z) E_z(z) \, dz \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & E_z \text{ derived from } A_z \text{ via retarded potential} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Evaluate integral with } L = \lambda/2 \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & Z_{11} \approx 73.1 + j42.5 \ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip**: If a derivation problem appears, students should write the integral form and then directly state the numerical result, showing the sinusoidal current assumption clearly.

## 3.2 Derivation: Mutual Impedance Between Two Parallel Half-Wave Dipoles

Consider two parallel center-fed half-wave dipoles, both aligned with the $z$-axis, separated laterally by distance $d$. Both are center-driven.

### 3.2.1 Definition
The mutual impedance $Z_{21}$ is the voltage $V_{21}$ induced at the open-circuited terminals of antenna 2 per unit current $I_1$ flowing in antenna 1:

$$Z_{21} = \frac{V_{21}}{I_1}$$

### 3.2.2 Far-Field Approximation (Most Useful for KTU Problems)

At distances $d$ comparable to $\lambda$, we can approximate the field of antenna 1 at the location of antenna 2 by its far-field expression. The far-field $E$-plane component radiated by antenna 1 is:

$$E_{\theta}^{(1)} = j \eta_0 \frac{I_0 e^{-jkr}}{2\pi r} \frac{\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2} \cos\theta\right)}{\sin\theta}$$

where $r = d$ for broadside observation. For broadside ($\theta = \pi/2$):

$$E_{\theta}^{(1)} \approx j \eta_0 \frac{I_0}{2\pi d}$$

The induced EMF at antenna 2's terminals (using the same effective length concept) is:

$$V_{21} \approx h_{e,2} \cdot E_{\theta}^{(1)}$$

where $h_{e,2} \approx \lambda / \pi$ for a half-wave dipole.

Therefore:

$$Z_{21} \approx \frac{h_{e,2} \cdot E_{\theta}^{(1)}}{I_0} = \frac{(\lambda/\pi) \cdot j\eta_0 I_0 / (2\pi d)}{I_0} = j \frac{\eta_0 \lambda}{2\pi^2 d}$$

Since $\eta_0 \approx 377 \ \Omega$ and $\lambda = c/f$, this gives a *purely imaginary* approximate value (only valid at large $d$):

$$Z_{21} \approx j \frac{60}{d/\lambda} \quad \Omega$$

### 3.2.3 Exact Values (Tabulated — used in KTU numericals)

| Separation $d$ | $Z_{21}$ (broadside, parallel) |
|---|---|
| $\lambda / 4$ | $\approx 40 - j20 \ \Omega$ |
| $\lambda / 2$ | $\approx -12.5 - j30 \ \Omega$ |
| $\lambda$ | $\approx -1 - j12 \ \Omega$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Tip**: For $d = \lambda/2$, the high *capacitive* reactance ($-j30$) means significant reactive coupling — array element currents will have a noticeable phase lag.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Hertzian Dipole | Half-Wave Dipole | Quarter-Wave Monopole |
|---|---|---|---|
| Length | $L \ll \lambda$ | $L = \lambda/2$ | $L = \lambda/4$ |
| $R_{rad}$ | $80\pi^2(L/\lambda)^2$ | $73.1 \ \Omega$ | $36.5 \ \Omega$ |
| $X_{in}$ | $\approx 0$ (small) | $+42.5 \ \Omega$ | $+21.25 \ \Omega$ |
| Directivity | $1.5$ (linear) | $1.64$ | $1.64$ (with ground) |
| Real-world use | Reference only | TV/radio, base stations | Vehicle, mobile, base |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map: Impedance Decomposition

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the hierarchical structure of antenna impedance concepts from Module 2:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[ANTENNA INPUT IMPEDANCE Zin] --> B1[Resistance Part Rin]
    A0 --> B2[Reactance Part jXin]
    B1 --> C1[Radiation Resistance Rrad]
    B1 --> C2[Ohmic Loss Resistance Rloss]
    B2 --> D1[Inductive if Antenna Too Long]
    B2 --> D2[Capacitive if Antenna Too Short]
    C1 --> E1[Hertzian 80 pi squared L by lambda squared]
    C1 --> E2[Half Wave 73 point 1 ohm]
    C1 --> E3[Quarter Wave 36 point 5 ohm]
    A0 --> F1[Self Impedance Zii]
    A0 --> F2[Mutual Impedance Zij]
    F1 --> G1[Measured with All Neighbors Open]
    F2 --> G2[Coupling Between Array Elements]
    F2 --> G3[Reciprocity Zij equals Zji]
    F1 --> H1[Driving Point in Array]
    F2 --> H1
    H1 --> H2[Zdrive = Zii plus sum of Ij over Ii times Zij]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Impedance Matching Workflow

The following block diagram depicts the engineering workflow for using antenna impedance in practice:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Transmitter RF Source] --> S2[Feedline Z0 equals 50 ohm]
    S2 --> S3[Matching Network]
    S3 --> S4[Antenna Terminals]
    S4 --> S5[Antenna Input Impedance Zin]
    S5 --> S6{Radiation Efficiency Check}
    S6 -->|Matched| S7[Maximum Power Transfer]
    S6 -->|Mismatched| S8[Reflected Power and VSWR]
    S8 --> S9[Return to Matching Network Design]
    S5 --> S10[Mutual Coupling in Arrays]
    S10 --> S11[Mutual Impedance Zij Lookup]
    S11 --> S12[Array Current Distribution]
    S12 --> S4
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block: Self vs Mutual Impedance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Self Impedance Zii] --> N2[Single Antenna Isolated]
    N2 --> N3[All Neighbors Open Circuited]
    N3 --> N4[Example Half Wave equals 73 plus j42]

    N5[Mutual Impedance Zij] --> N6[Two or More Antennas Coupled]
    N6 --> N7[Neighbors Carry Current]
    N7 --> N8[Voltage Induced in Open Antenna i]
    N8 --> N9[Example Z21 at half lambda separation equals minus 12 minus j30]

    N4 --> P1[Used For Matching and Resonance Tuning]
    N9 --> P2[Used For Array Driving Point Impedance]
    P1 --> P3[VSWR less than 2 over Bandwidth]
    P2 --> P4[Array Gain Pattern Control]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation**: The blocks in `N1`–`N9` represent functional states. In KTU board exams, when asked to "draw the block diagram," a similar functional architecture with clear labels is fully acceptable even if it is not a physical schematic.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define antenna input impedance. What is the significance of its real and imaginary parts?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *(1 mark)* **Definition**: The antenna input impedance $Z_{in}$ is defined as the impedance presented by the antenna at its feed terminals. It is the ratio of the complex voltage to the complex current at the feed point: $Z_{in} = V_{in}/I_{in} = R_{in} + jX_{in}$.
- *(1 mark)* **Real Part Significance**: The real part $R_{in} = R_{rad} + R_{loss}$ accounts for power radiated into space ($R_{rad}$) and power dissipated as heat ($R_{loss}$). It is the *active* power-handling component.
- *(1 mark)* **Imaginary Part Significance**: The imaginary part $X_{in}$ represents reactive (stored) energy near the antenna — inductive if positive (antenna too long), capacitive if negative (antenna too short). It must be tuned to zero for resonance.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define mutual impedance between two antennas. State the property of reciprocity applicable to it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *(1.5 marks)* **Definition**: Mutual impedance $Z_{21}$ between two antennas is the ratio of the open-circuit voltage induced at the terminals of antenna 2 to the current flowing in antenna 1: $Z_{21} = V_{2,open}/I_1$.
- *(1 mark)* **Physical Meaning**: It quantifies the *electromagnetic coupling* between nearby antennas and is essential for analyzing antenna arrays.
- *(0.5 marks)* **Reciprocity**: The reciprocity theorem guarantees that $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$. The mutual impedance is symmetric in antenna indices.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**(a) Discuss the concept of self-impedance and mutual impedance of antennas with suitable examples.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

- *(2 marks)* **Self-Impedance $Z_{ii}$**: Self-impedance is the input impedance of an antenna measured at its terminals when all surrounding antennas are open-circuited (carrying zero current). It represents the antenna's intrinsic impedance, which depends on its geometry, length, and proximity to any ground plane.
  - **Example**: A center-fed half-wave dipole in free space has a self-impedance of $Z_{11} \approx 73.1 + j42.5 \ \Omega$.

- *(2 marks)* **Mutual Impedance $Z_{ij}$**: When two or more antennas are in proximity, the current in one induces a voltage at the terminals of the other. This coupling effect is quantified by the mutual impedance:

$$Z_{ij} = \frac{V_i \text{ (with antenna } i \text{ open-circuited)}}{I_j}$$

- *(1.5 marks)* **Driving-Point Impedance**: In an array, the actual terminal impedance of element $i$ becomes:

$$Z_{i, drive} = Z_{ii} + \sum_{j \neq i} \frac{I_j}{I_i} Z_{ij}$$

- *(1 mark)* **Reciprocity**: The mutual impedance matrix is symmetric: $Z_{ij} = Z_{ji}$. This significantly simplifies array analysis.
- *(0.5 marks)* **Example**: Two parallel half-wave dipoles separated by $d = \lambda/2$ have $Z_{21} \approx -12.5 - j30 \ \Omega$.

---

**(b) Derive the input impedance of a half-wave dipole antenna using the induced EMF method.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

- *(1 mark)* **Setup**: Consider a thin cylindrical wire of length $L = \lambda/2$ and radius $a \ll \lambda$, center-fed by a delta-gap voltage source $V_t$. Let $I_0$ be the input current.

- *(1 mark)* **Current Distribution Assumption**: For a thin wire, the current distribution is approximately sinusoidal:

$$I(z) = I_0 \cos(kz), \quad -L/2 \leq z \leq L/2$$

with $k = 2\pi/\lambda$.

- *(1 mark)* **Induced EMF Definition**: The input impedance is defined by the integral relation:

$$Z_{in} = -\frac{1}{\vert I_0 \vert^2} \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I^*(z) E_z(z) \, dz$$

- *(1 mark)* **Vector Potential**: The retarded vector potential along the wire is:

$$A_z = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \frac{I(z') e^{-jkR}}{R} \, dz', \quad R = \sqrt{(z-z')^2 + a^2}$$

- *(1 mark)* **Tangential Electric Field**: Using $E_z = -j\omega A_z - \partial \Phi/\partial z$ and applying the far-field/radiation approximation:

$$E_z(z) = -j \omega A_z \big\vert_{r=a}$$

- *(1 mark)* **Final Integration**: Substituting and evaluating the double integral with the sinusoidal current assumption:

$$Z_{in} \approx 73.1 + j42.5 \ \Omega$$

- *(1 mark)* **Conclusion**: The radiation resistance is $73.1 \ \Omega$ (this is the *real part*), and the inductive reactance $42.5 \ \Omega$ indicates the antenna is slightly off resonance at physical half-wavelength. Resonance can be achieved by shortening the dipole by a few percent.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a) Explain the concept of radiation resistance. Calculate the radiation resistance of a half-wave dipole and a quarter-wave monopole over an infinite ground plane.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

- *(2 marks)* **Concept of Radiation Resistance**: Radiation resistance $R_{rad}$ is a fictitious resistance that would dissipate the same average power as the antenna actually radiates into free space when the feed-point current is the same. It quantifies the antenna's radiation capability:

$$P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2} \vert I_0 \vert^2 R_{rad}$$

- *(1.5 marks)* **Half-Wave Dipole**: Using the induced EMF method or direct integration of the far-field Poynting vector:

$$P_{rad} = \frac{\eta_0 I_0^2}{2\pi} \int_0^\pi \frac{\cos^2\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta\right)}{\sin\theta} d\theta$$

Evaluating the integral numerically gives $P_{rad}/I_0^2 \approx 36.55$, so:

$$R_{rad, \text{half-wave}} = 73.1 \ \Omega$$

- *(2 marks)* **Quarter-Wave Monopole over Ground Plane**: A quarter-wave monopole over an infinite perfectly conducting ground plane radiates into the upper half-space only. By image theory, it behaves like a half-wave dipole radiating into full space. Hence its radiation resistance is *half*:

$$R_{rad, \text{quarter-wave}} = \frac{73.1}{2} \approx 36.5 \ \Omega$$

- *(1.5 marks)* **Conclusion**: Both antennas have the same radiation efficiency for the same input current, but the monopole impedance is half, making it easier to match to $50 \ \Omega$ feed systems.

---

**(b) Two identical half-wave dipoles are placed parallel to each other with a broadside separation of $d = \lambda/2$. Compute the driving-point impedance of each dipole when they are fed with equal in-phase currents.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

- *(1 mark)* **Given Data**:
  - Two parallel half-wave dipoles, separation $d = \lambda/2$
  - Both fed in phase, equal currents: $I_1 = I_2 = I_0$

- *(1 mark)* **Self-Impedance**:
$$Z_{11} = Z_{22} = 73.1 + j42.5 \ \Omega$$

- *(1 mark)* **Mutual Impedance from Standard Tables** (broadside, $d = \lambda/2$):
$$Z_{12} = Z_{21} \approx -12.5 - j30 \ \Omega$$

- *(2 marks)* **Driving-Point Impedance Calculation**:

$$Z_{1,\ drive} = Z_{11} + \frac{I_2}{I_1} Z_{12} = Z_{11} + Z_{12}$$

$$Z_{1,\ drive} = (73.1 + j42.5) + (-12.5 - j30) = 60.6 + j12.5 \ \Omega$$

- *(1 mark)* **Result Interpretation**: The driving-point impedance is changed from the isolated self-impedance due to mutual coupling. The resistance drops from $73.1 \ \Omega$ to $60.6 \ \Omega$, and the reactance drops from $j42.5 \ \Omega$ to $j12.5 \ \Omega$. This shows that array elements must be retuned after assembly.

- *(1 mark)* **Symmetry Note**: By symmetry, $Z_{2,\ drive} = Z_{1,\ drive} = 60.6 + j12.5 \ \Omega$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Forgetting the current ratio** in the driving-point formula. Always write $Z_i = Z_{ii} + \sum (I_j/I_i) Z_{ij}$ — students often drop the ratio and just add $Z_{ij}$, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Quoting $73 \ \Omega$ for monopoles** — remember the monopole has *half* the radiation resistance of a dipole due to image theory.
> 3. **Confusing $R_{rad}$ and $R_{in}$** — for a lossless antenna $R_{in} = R_{rad}$, but in general $R_{in} = R_{rad} + R_{loss}$.
> 4. **Skipping units** — Ohms ($\Omega$) must accompany every impedance value or marks are deducted.
> 5. **Mutual impedance sign errors** — for $d = \lambda/2$, $Z_{12}$ has a *negative* real part; do not assume positive resistance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU Board Exams)**

- ☐ **Input Impedance** = $Z_{in} = R_{in} + jX_{in}$, with $R_{in} = R_{rad} + R_{loss}$.
- ☐ **Half-Wave Dipole Self-Impedance**: $Z_{11} \approx 73.1 + j42.5 \ \Omega$.
- ☐ **Quarter-Wave Monopole** (over infinite ground): $Z_{11} \approx 36.5 + j21.25 \ \Omega$.
- ☐ **Hertzian Dipole**: $R_{rad} = 80\pi^2 (L/\lambda)^2$ (very small).
- ☐ **Mutual Impedance Definition**: $Z_{ij} = V_{i,open}/I_j$, with $Z_{ij} = Z_{ji}$ by reciprocity.
- ☐ **Driving-Point Impedance**: $Z_i = Z_{ii} + \sum_{j \neq i} (I_j/I_i) Z_{ij}$.
- ☐ **Reflection Coefficient**: $\Gamma = (Z_{in} - Z_0)/(Z_{in} + Z_0)$.
- ☐ **VSWR**: $(1 + \vert\Gamma\vert)/(1 - \vert\Gamma\vert)$.
- ☐ **Conjugate Matching**: Maximum power transfer when $Z_{source} = Z_{in}^*$.
- ☐ **Mutual Impedance at $d = \lambda/2$**: $\approx -12.5 - j30 \ \Omega$ (broadside parallel).
- ☐ **Image Theory**: Monopole impedance = Dipole impedance / 2.
- ☐ **Inductive Reactance**: Positive $X_{in}$ means the antenna is *electrically longer* than resonance.
- ☐ **Capacitive Reactance**: Negative $X_{in}$ means the antenna is *electrically shorter* than resonance.
- ☐ **Bandwidth Definition**: Frequency range over which VSWR $\leq 2$.
- ☐ **Standard Feed-Line Impedances**: $50 \ \Omega$ (RF systems), $75 \ \Omega$ (TV/CATV).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Current Polarization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Current Polarization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Polarization of an antenna** is defined as the orientation of the electric field vector of the electromagnetic (EM) wave radiated by the antenna in the far-field region, observed along the direction of maximum radiation (typically the +z direction by convention). It is fundamentally determined by the **time-varying current distribution** on the radiating structure. Since a time-varying current produces a time-varying magnetic field, which in turn generates a time-varying electric field (via Maxwell's equations), the spatial pattern of the current on the antenna directly dictates the spatial orientation of the radiated E-field. The term **Current Polarization** explicitly emphasizes that the physical current vector $\vec{I}(t)$ flowing on the antenna conductor is the source and governing parameter of the polarization state of the transmitted wave.

The polarization state of a plane wave traveling in the +z direction can be completely described by tracing the locus traced by the tip of the instantaneous electric field vector $\vec{E}(t)$ as a function of time at a fixed point in space (z = 0). This locus generally forms an **ellipse** in the x-y plane.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The IEEE definition of antenna polarization (Std 145-2013) is the standard reference. Polarization is a property of the *radiated wave* in the far field, not a property of the antenna structure itself — although it is *caused* by the antenna's geometry and current distribution.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are **swinging a rope tied to a wall**:
- If you swing the rope **up and down** (in a single vertical plane), the wave on the rope is **vertically polarized** (analogous to a **linearly polarized** antenna oriented vertically).
- If you swing the rope in **horizontal circles** (like stirring a pot), the rope's free end traces a **circle** — this is **circular polarization**.
- If you swing it in a **tilted ellipse**, you get **elliptical polarization**.

The direction in which you *initiate the swing* and *the shape* of motion you impart is like the **current direction** and **phase relationship** in the antenna. The current along the antenna is the "hand" that wiggles the rope; whatever wiggle pattern is fed in determines the polarization of the wave going out.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Receiver–Transmitter Polarization Match:** Maximum power transfer between two antennas occurs only when both are identically polarized. A **polarization mismatch loss** of 3 dB occurs between a linearly polarized and a circularly polarized antenna, and theoretically 100% loss occurs between RHCP and LHCP antennas (or two orthogonal linear polarizations).

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Constant Reminder)

- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Intrinsic impedance of free space:** $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377$ **Ω**
- **Operating frequency band:** Determines wavelength $\lambda = c/f$
- **IEEE Reference Wave Direction:** +z (outward normal from the antenna)
- **Far-field condition:** $R \geq 2D^2/\lambda$ (Fraunhofer distance)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic Steps — How Current Creates Polarization

The chain of cause and effect is as follows:

1. **Time-Varying Excitation:** A high-frequency alternating current $I(t)$ is fed to the antenna terminals (typically via a transmission line).
2. **Surface Current Distribution:** This current distributes along the conducting surface of the antenna according to Maxwell's boundary conditions and the geometry. For a thin linear dipole, the current is approximately sinusoidal: $I(z') = I_0 \sin\left[k(\ell/2 - |z'|)\right]$.
3. **Vector Decomposition of Current:** The current vector $\vec{I}$ at any point on the antenna can be decomposed into orthogonal components (e.g., $I_x$ and $I_y$ for crossed-dipoles). If the antenna is a single straight wire, only one component exists.
4. **Far-Field Generation:** Each infinitesimal current element $\vec{I}dl'$ acts as a Hertzian dipole and radiates an E-field parallel to $\vec{I}$ in the far zone.
5. **Superposition:** The total E-field in the far field is the vector sum (superposition) of the contributions from all current elements. If only one current direction exists → **linear polarization**. If two orthogonal currents with **equal amplitude** and **90° phase difference** exist → **circular polarization**.
6. **Wave Propagation:** The resulting E-field propagates outward as a TEM (Transverse ElectroMagnetic) wave, with $\vec{E}$, $\vec{H}$, and $\hat{k}$ mutually perpendicular (orthogonality of plane waves in free space).

### 2.2 The Three Fundamental Polarization States

| Polarization Type | E-field Locus | Current Configuration | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | Straight line | Single direction of current (or two in-phase orthogonal currents) | TV antennas, AM broadcast, dipoles |
| **Circular** | Circle | Two orthogonal currents, equal magnitude, 90° phase difference | Satellite comms (because it ignores Faraday rotation) |
| **Elliptical** | Tilted ellipse | Two orthogonal currents, arbitrary amplitude & phase | Most general case (includes linear & circular as special cases) |

### 2.3 Mathematical Description (Plane Wave Along +z)

A general plane wave traveling along the +z direction has its E-field in the transverse (x-y) plane:

$$\vec{E}(z,t) = \hat{x}\,E_x(z,t) + \hat{y}\,E_y(z,t)$$

The two orthogonal components are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_x(z,t) &= E_{x0}\,\cos(\omega t - kz + \phi_x) \\
E_y(z,t) &= E_{y0}\,\cos(\omega t - kz + \phi_y)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $E_{x0}$ and $E_{y0}$ are the **amplitudes** of the x and y components.
- $\phi_x$ and $\phi_y$ are the **initial phases**.
- $\omega$ is the angular frequency, $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the wavenumber.
- The **relative phase** $\delta = \phi_y - \phi_x$ is the key determinant of polarization state.

### 2.4 The Polarization Ellipse — Universal Description

At a fixed point in space (say $z = 0$), eliminating time $t$ yields the **polarization ellipse equation**:

$$\left(\frac{E_x}{E_{x0}}\right)^2 - 2\left(\frac{E_x\,E_y}{E_{x0}E_{y0}}\right)\cos\delta + \left(\frac{E_y}{E_{y0}}\right)^2 = \sin^2\delta$$

This is the equation of an ellipse, and it is the **most general description** of antenna polarization. The orientation and shape of this ellipse are governed by the amplitude ratio $E_{y0}/E_{x0}$ and the phase difference $\delta$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Ellipse?** Any two sinusoids at the same frequency, with arbitrary amplitudes and phases, when added vectorially trace an ellipse. This is a fundamental result from the **Lissajous figure** theory applied to degenerate (equal-frequency) cases. Linear and circular are merely degenerate ellipses.

### 2.5 The Three Special Cases

| Case | Condition | Resulting Locus | Sense of Rotation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear | $\delta = 0$ or $\delta = \pm\pi$ | Straight line at angle $\tau = \arctan(E_{y0}/E_{x0})$ | None (degenerate) |
| Circular | $E_{x0} = E_{y0}$ and $\delta = \pm\pi/2$ | Perfect circle | RHCP if $\delta = -\pi/2$; LHCP if $\delta = +\pi/2$ |
| Elliptical | All other combinations | Tilted ellipse | RH or LH depending on $\delta$ sign |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **Use $\vert$ inside tables to avoid markdown conflicts.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Far-field condition (Fraunhofer) | $R_f = 2D^2/\lambda$ | $R \geq R_f$, meters |
| 2 | E-field general components | $E_x = E_{x0}\cos(\omega t - kz + \phi_x)$, $E_y = E_{y0}\cos(\omega t - kz + \phi_y)$ | V/m |
| 3 | Phase difference | $\delta = \phi_y - \phi_x$ | radians |
| 4 | Polarization ellipse | $\left(\frac{E_x}{E_{x0}}\right)^2 - 2\left(\frac{E_x E_y}{E_{x0}E_{y0}}\right)\cos\delta + \left(\frac{E_y}{E_{y0}}\right)^2 = \sin^2\delta$ | Universal |
| 5 | Orientation angle $\tau$ | $\tau = \frac{1}{2}\arctan\!\left[\frac{2E_{x0}E_{y0}\cos\delta}{E_{x0}^2 - E_{y0}^2}\right]$ | radians |
| 6 | Axial Ratio (AR) | $AR = \frac{\text{Major axis}}{\text{Minor axis}} = \frac{E_{x0}^2 + E_{y0}^2 + \sqrt{E_{x0}^4 + E_{y0}^4 + 2E_{x0}^2 E_{y0}^2\cos(2\delta)}}{\text{denominator}}$ | $1 \leq AR \leq \infty$ |
| 7 | AR simplified form | $AR = \frac{E_{x0}}{E_{y0}}$ (when one component dominates) | dimensionless |
| 8 | Circular polarization requirement | $E_{x0} = E_{y0}$ AND $\delta = \pm\pi/2$ | Equal amp, 90° phase |
| 9 | Sense of rotation (RHCP) | $\delta = -\pi/2$ (observer looks into direction of propagation) | Right-Hand |
| 10 | Sense of rotation (LHCP) | $\delta = +\pi/2$ | Left-Hand |
| 11 | Polarization Loss Factor (PLF) | $PLF = \vert \hat{\rho}_w \cdot \hat{\rho}_a^* \vert^2$ | $0 \leq PLF \leq 1$ |
| 12 | Polarization Efficiency | $\eta_p = PLF \times 100\%$ | Percent |
| 13 | Stokes Parameters | $S_0 = E_{x0}^2 + E_{y0}^2$, $S_1 = E_{x0}^2 - E_{y0}^2$, $S_2 = 2E_{x0}E_{y0}\cos\delta$, $S_3 = 2E_{x0}E_{y0}\sin\delta$ | W/m² |
| 14 | Ellipticity angle $\chi$ | $\sin(2\chi) = \frac{S_3}{S_0}$, with $-\pi/4 \leq \chi \leq \pi/4$ | radians |
| 15 | Poincaré sphere radius | $r = \sqrt{S_1^2 + S_2^2 + S_3^2}/S_0$ | $0 \leq r \leq 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stokes Parameters (4 real numbers)** completely characterize the polarization state of a quasi-monochromatic wave, including the degree of polarization. They are used to map the polarization state onto the **Poincaré sphere** (radius = 1 for fully polarized waves). This is a favorite KTU 2-mark question.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Satellite Communication:** Circular polarization is used because the ionosphere's **Faraday rotation** rotates the polarization of linearly polarized signals, causing signal loss. Circular polarization is immune to this effect.
- **Radar Systems:** Polarization diversity (using different polarizations on transmit and receive) can suppress rain clutter (rain echoes are more strongly depolarized) and help in target classification.
- **Mobile Communications (MIMO):** Cross-polarization discrimination (XPD) is a key parameter; typically 6–10 dB in cellular systems.
- **GPS:** Uses RHCP at L1 (1575.42 MHz) and L2 (1227.60 MHz) bands. Receiver antennas must also be RHCP to achieve the maximum 3-dB link budget benefit.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Polarization Ellipse Equation

**Starting Point:** We have two orthogonal field components at $z = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_x(t) &= E_{x0}\,\cos(\omega t + \phi_x) \\
E_y(t) &= E_{y0}\,\cos(\omega t + \phi_y)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Define the relative phase for clarity.**
Let $\delta = \phi_y - \phi_x$. Then we can rewrite $E_y$ using the identity $\cos(\omega t + \phi_y) = \cos(\omega t + \phi_x + \delta)$.

**Step 2 — Normalize by dividing by amplitudes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{E_x}{E_{x0}} &= \cos(\omega t + \phi_x) \\
\frac{E_y}{E_{y0}} &= \cos(\omega t + \phi_x + \delta)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand $\cos(\omega t + \phi_x + \delta)$ using the angle addition formula.**

$$\frac{E_y}{E_{y0}} = \cos(\omega t + \phi_x)\cos\delta - \sin(\omega t + \phi_x)\sin\delta$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\cos(\omega t + \phi_x) = E_x/E_{x0}$.**

$$\frac{E_y}{E_{y0}} = \frac{E_x}{E_{x0}}\cos\delta - \sin(\omega t + \phi_x)\sin\delta$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\sin(\omega t + \phi_x)$ from Step 4.**

$$\sin(\omega t + \phi_x) = \frac{1}{\sin\delta}\left[\frac{E_x}{E_{x0}}\cos\delta - \frac{E_y}{E_{y0}}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Pythagorean identity.**

$$\sin^2(\omega t + \phi_x) + \cos^2(\omega t + \phi_x) = 1$$

**Step 7 — Substitute both expressions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\left[\frac{1}{\sin\delta}\left(\frac{E_x}{E_{x0}}\cos\delta - \frac{E_y}{E_{y0}}\right)\right]^2 + \left[\frac{E_x}{E_{x0}}\right]^2 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Multiply through by $\sin^2\delta$ and expand.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{E_x}{E_{x0}}\right)^2\cos^2\delta \;-\; 2\left(\frac{E_x}{E_{x0}}\right)\left(\frac{E_y}{E_{y0}}\right)\cos\delta \;+\; \left(\frac{E_y}{E_{y0}}\right)^2 \;=\; \sin^2\delta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Rearrange to standard ellipse form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{E_x}{E_{x0}}\right)^2 - 2\left(\frac{E_x E_y}{E_{x0}E_{y0}}\right)\cos\delta + \left(\frac{E_y}{E_{y0}}\right)^2 = \sin^2\delta
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Polarization Ellipse Equation**, valid for all polarization states.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Orientation Angle $\tau$

**Step 1 — Differentiate the ellipse equation implicitly w.r.t. $E_x$ to find the axes.**

Setting $dE_y/dE_x = 0$ at the major axis endpoints:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{2E_x}{E_{x0}^2} - \frac{2E_y\cos\delta}{E_{x0}E_{y0}} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving:

$$E_y = E_x \cdot \frac{E_{y0}}{E_{x0}\cos\delta}$$

**Step 2 — The slope of the major axis is $\tan\tau$.**

For axes in the rotated coordinate system, the standard result (from Balanis, *Antenna Theory*, 4th ed., ch. 2) is:

$$\tan(2\tau) = \frac{2E_{x0}E_{y0}\cos\delta}{E_{x0}^2 - E_{y0}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\tau$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau = \frac{1}{2}\arctan\!\left[\frac{2E_{x0}E_{y0}\cos\delta}{E_{x0}^2 - E_{y0}^2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

This angle is measured from the +x axis to the **major axis** of the ellipse, in the range $-\pi/2 \leq \tau \leq \pi/2$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Axial Ratio (AR)

**Step 1 — Axial Ratio definition.**

$$AR = \frac{\text{Length of major axis}}{\text{Length of minor axis}}$$

For a fully polarized wave, $AR \in [1, \infty]$. For circular polarization, $AR = 1$. For linear polarization, $AR = \infty$.

**Step 2 — Use the parametric equations of the rotated ellipse.**

In the rotated (tilted) coordinate frame aligned with the major/minor axes, the ellipse becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_\xi(t) &= E_0 \cos\tau \cos(\omega t + \phi) + E_0 \sin\tau \sin(\omega t + \phi) \quad \text{(along major axis)} \\
E_\eta(t) &= E_0 \sin\tau \cos(\omega t + \phi) - E_0 \cos\tau \sin(\omega t + \phi) \quad \text{(along minor axis)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Standard result (from Kraus / Balanis):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AR = \frac{E_{x0}^2 + E_{y0}^2 + \sqrt{E_{x0}^4 + E_{y0}^4 + 2E_{x0}^2 E_{y0}^2\cos(2\delta)}}{E_{x0}^2 + E_{y0}^2 - \sqrt{E_{x0}^4 + E_{y0}^4 + 2E_{x0}^2 E_{y0}^2\cos(2\delta)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Special Case: Pure linear polarization.** When $\delta = 0$, $\cos(2\delta) = 1$, and the numerator equals $E_{x0}^2 + E_{y0}^2$ while the denominator equals $\vert E_{x0}^2 - E_{y0}^2 \vert$. For a single-component field (say $E_{y0} = 0$), $AR \to \infty$, confirming the degeneracy.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A plane wave has $E_x = 3\cos(\omega t)\,$ V/m and $E_y = 4\cos(\omega t + 90°)\,$ V/m. Determine:
1. The sense of rotation.
2. The axial ratio.
3. The orientation angle of the polarization ellipse.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**

$E_{x0} = 3$ V/m, $E_{y0} = 4$ V/m, $\delta = \phi_y - \phi_x = 90° - 0° = +\pi/2$.

**Step 2 — Sense of rotation.**

Since $\delta = +\pi/2$, $E_y$ **leads** $E_x$ by 90°. Tracing the E-field tip at $z = 0$:
- $t = 0$: $E_x = 3$, $E_y = 0$
- $t = T/4$: $E_x = 0$, $E_y = +4$
- $t = T/2$: $E_x = -3$, $E_y = 0$
- $t = 3T/4$: $E_x = 0$, $E_y = -4$

Motion is from +x → +y → −x → −y, which is **counter-clockwise** when viewed from the −z direction (wave traveling +z). This is **LHCP only if** $E_{x0} = E_{y0}$. Here $E_{x0} \neq E_{y0}$, so it is **Left-Hand Elliptical Polarization (LHEP)**.

**Step 3 — Axial Ratio calculation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AR &= \frac{3^2 + 4^2 + \sqrt{3^4 + 4^4 + 2(3^2)(4^2)\cos(180°)}}{3^2 + 4^2 - \sqrt{3^4 + 4^4 + 2(3^2)(4^2)\cos(180°)}} \\
&= \frac{9 + 16 + \sqrt{81 + 256 + 2(9)(16)(-1)}}{9 + 16 - \sqrt{81 + 256 - 288}} \\
&= \frac{25 + \sqrt{337 - 288}}{25 - \sqrt{337 - 288}} \\
&= \frac{25 + \sqrt{49}}{25 - \sqrt{49}} \\
&= \frac{25 + 7}{25 - 7} = \frac{32}{18} = \frac{16}{9} \approx 1.778
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Orientation angle.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(2\tau) &= \frac{2(3)(4)\cos(90°)}{3^2 - 4^2} = \frac{0}{9 - 16} = 0 \\
\Rightarrow 2\tau &= 0 \Rightarrow \tau = 0°
\end{aligned}
$$

The major axis is aligned with the **+x axis** (since $E_{y0} > E_{x0}$, the y-direction is actually the major axis... let me re-verify):

Actually with $E_{y0} > E_{x0}$ and $\delta = 90°$, the convention is that the ellipse is oriented with its major axis along the y-direction. The formula gives $\tau = 0$ as the angle of the line from origin to *one of the axes*; we use the quadrant and physical reasoning to identify the major axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation Rule:** $\tau$ is the angle of the line that goes through the two **endpoints of the major axis**. In our case, the major axis lies along $\hat{y}$ (because $E_{y0}$ is the larger component, and with 90° phase difference, the E-field reaches its peak along y first).

**Final Answer Summary:**
- Sense: **Left-Hand Elliptical Polarization (LHEP)**
- $AR = 16/9 \approx 1.78$ (or **5 dB** in dB scale: $20\log_{10}(1.78) = 5$ dB)
- Major axis orientation: along $\hat{y}$ axis (i.e., the ellipse is "tall" not "tilted")

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Linear | Circular | Elliptical |
|---|---|---|---|
| Two orthogonal components? | No (or in-phase only) | Yes | Yes |
| Amplitude ratio $E_{y0}/E_{x0}$ | 0 or $\infty$ | 1 | Any value |
| Phase difference $\delta$ | $0$ or $\pm\pi$ | $\pm\pi/2$ | Anything else |
| Axial Ratio | $\infty$ | 1 | $1 < AR < \infty$ |
| Sense of rotation | N/A | RHCP or LHCP | RH or LH |
| Sense ambiguity? | No | Yes (RH/LH by IEEE) | Yes (RH/LH) |
| Used in | Broadcast TV, AM | Satellite, GPS | Most realistic antennas |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How Current Becomes Polarization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source / Transmitter] --> B[Transmission Line Feed]
    B --> C[Antenna Current Distribution I z,t]
    C --> D[Vector Decomposition of Current]
    D --> E1[Horizontal Current Component Ix]
    D --> E2[Vertical Current Component Iy]
    E1 --> F[Far-Field Radiation via Maxwell Eqs]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G{E_x0 vs E_y0 and phase delta}
    G -->|delta equals 0 or pi| H1[LINEAR POLARIZATION]
    G -->|equal amplitude, delta equals plus or minus pi by 2| H2[CIRCULAR POLARIZATION]
    G -->|all other cases| H3[ELLIPTICAL POLARIZATION]
    H1 --> I1[E-field traces a straight line]
    H2 --> I2[E-field traces a circle, RH or LH sense]
    H3 --> I3[E-field traces a tilted ellipse]
    I1 --> J[Receiver Polarization Match Check]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K[Power Transfer to Receiver]
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree: Identifying Polarization from Given E-Field

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: E_x and E_y given] --> Q1{Is there only one component or are they in phase 0 or 180 deg?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| LIN[Linear Polarization]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Are amplitudes equal E_x0 equals E_y0?}
    Q2 -->|No| ELL[Elliptical Polarization]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Is phase difference delta equals plus or minus 90 deg?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| CIR[Circular Polarization]
    Q3 -->|No| ELL
    CIR --> Q4{Sign of delta}
    Q4 -->|delta negative pi by 2| RH[Right-Hand Circular Polarization RHCP]
    Q4 -->|delta positive pi by 2| LH[Left-Hand Circular Polarization LHCP]
```

### 4.3 Crossed-Dipole Physical Realization of Circular Polarization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[RF Source 90 deg Hybrid] --> DIV[Power Divider with 90 deg phase shift]
    DIV --> HOR[Horizontal Dipole]
    DIV --> VER[Vertical Dipole]
    HOR --> RAD[Radiated Wave]
    VER --> RAD
    RAD --> CIR[Circularly Polarized Wave]
    note1[Equal amplitude to both dipoles] -.-> DIV
    note2[Vertical dipole leads horizontal by 90 deg for RHCP] -.-> DIV
```

### 4.4 Poincaré Sphere Conceptual Map (Logical Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    STOKES[Input: Stokes Parameters S0 S1 S2 S3] --> NORM[Normalize to unit sphere]
    NORM --> POS[Compute 2 chi and 2 psi]
    POS --> MAP[Map to point on sphere]
    MAP --> N[North Pole: LHCP]
    MAP --> S2[South Pole: RHCP]
    MAP --> E[Equator: All Linear Polarizations]
    MAP --> NH[Northern Hemisphere: LH Elliptical]
    MAP --> SH[Southern Hemisphere: RH Elliptical]
    N --> OUT[Polarization State Identified]
    S2 --> OUT
    E --> OUT
    NH --> OUT
    SH --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Poincaré Sphere?** Every point on the unit sphere represents a unique polarization state. The **angular position** $2\chi$ (latitude) gives the ellipticity, and $2\psi$ (longitude) gives the orientation. **Antipodal points** represent **orthogonal polarizations** (perfect cross-polarization rejection).
