## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
*Define antenna polarization. What is meant by a "Right-Hand Circularly Polarized (RHCP)" wave as per the IEEE convention?*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** The polarization of an antenna in a given direction is defined as the polarization of the radiated electromagnetic wave in the far field along that direction. It is the locus traced by the tip of the instantaneous electric field vector $\vec{E}(t)$ as a function of time at a fixed point in space, observed in the plane perpendicular to the direction of propagation.

**RHCP Definition (2 marks):** According to the IEEE convention, an antenna is said to be Right-Hand Circularly Polarized if, when viewed from the source (i.e., the observer is positioned in the direction **opposite** to the direction of wave propagation, looking back toward the antenna), the E-field vector rotates **clockwise** as a function of time. Mathematically, a RHCP wave traveling in the +z direction requires $E_{x0} = E_{y0}$ and a phase difference $\delta = \phi_y - \phi_x = -\pi/2$, meaning $E_y$ **lags** $E_x$ by 90°.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the IEEE (engineering) convention with the optics convention. **IEEE = thumb of right hand points in direction of propagation; fingers curl in direction of rotation.** **Optics = source-to-observer rule (reverse).** KTU strictly follows the IEEE convention.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*What is the Polarization Loss Factor (PLF)? Find the PLF when the transmitting antenna is linearly polarized at +45° and the receiving antenna is linearly polarized at −45°.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Polarization Loss Factor (PLF) is the fraction of transmitted power received by the antenna, given by the squared magnitude of the dot product of the transmit and receive polarization unit vectors:

$$PLF = \vert \hat{\rho}_t \cdot \hat{\rho}_r^* \vert^2$$

**Computation (2 marks):**
- Transmit unit vector: $\hat{\rho}_t = (\hat{x} + \hat{y})/\sqrt{2}$
- Receive unit vector: $\hat{\rho}_r = (\hat{x} - \hat{y})/\sqrt{2}$

$$PLF = \left\vert \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\hat{x} + \hat{y}) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\hat{x} - \hat{y}) \right\vert^2 = \left\vert \frac{1}{2}(1 - 1) \right\vert^2 = 0$$

**Result:** $PLF = 0$ (i.e., 100% polarization mismatch — the two orthogonal linear polarizations reject each other completely).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
**(a)** Derive the polarization ellipse equation starting from the general expressions for the two orthogonal electric field components of a plane wave. Discuss the special cases of linear, circular, and elliptical polarization based on this derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plane wave has the following electric field components: $E_x = 6\cos(\omega t - kz)$ V/m and $E_y = 8\cos(\omega t - kz + 60°)$ V/m. Determine: (i) The sense of rotation, (ii) The axial ratio in dB, and (iii) The orientation angle of the polarization ellipse. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Write the E-field components (1 mark).**

For a plane wave propagating in the +z direction:

$$E_x(z,t) = E_{x0}\cos(\omega t - kz + \phi_x), \quad E_y(z,t) = E_{y0}\cos(\omega t - kz + \phi_y)$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate at $z = 0$ and set $\delta = \phi_y - \phi_x$ (1 mark).**

$$E_x(t) = E_{x0}\cos(\omega t + \phi_x), \quad E_y(t) = E_{y0}\cos(\omega t + \phi_x + \delta)$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate $t$ (3 marks).** (Show the full derivation as in Section 3.1 above, including the use of the angle addition formula, the Pythagorean identity, and the final rearrangement.)

$$\left(\frac{E_x}{E_{x0}}\right)^2 - 2\left(\frac{E_x E_y}{E_{x0}E_{y0}}\right)\cos\delta + \left(\frac{E_y}{E_{y0}}\right)^2 = \sin^2\delta$$

**Step 4 — Discuss the three special cases (2 marks).**
- **Linear:** $\delta = 0$ or $\pm\pi$ → the cross term vanishes and the locus reduces to a straight line.
- **Circular:** $E_{x0} = E_{y0}$ and $\delta = \pm\pi/2$ → $\sin^2\delta = 1$, $\cos\delta = 0$, the equation reduces to a circle of radius $E_{x0}$.
- **Elliptical:** all other cases → the general tilted ellipse.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $E_{x0} = 6$ V/m, $E_{y0} = 8$ V/m, $\delta = +60° = +\pi/3$ rad.

**(i) Sense of rotation (2 marks):**
Since $\delta = +60° > 0$, the $E_y$ component **leads** $E_x$ by 60°. Tracing the locus:
- $t = 0$: $E_x = 6$, $E_y = 8\cos(60°) = 4$
- $t = T/4$: $E_x = 0$, $E_y = 8\cos(150°) \approx -6.93$
- $t = T/2$: $E_x = -6$, $E_y = 8\cos(240°) = -4$
- $t = 3T/4$: $E_x = 0$, $E_y = 8\cos(330°) \approx 6.93$

Motion is counter-clockwise (in the x-y plane, observed by receiver facing the source). Since $E_{y0} \neq E_{x0}$, it is **Left-Hand Elliptical Polarization (LHEP)**.

**(ii) Axial Ratio in dB (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AR &= \frac{E_{x0}^2 + E_{y0}^2 + \sqrt{E_{x0}^4 + E_{y0}^4 + 2E_{x0}^2 E_{y0}^2\cos(2\delta)}}{E_{x0}^2 + E_{y0}^2 - \sqrt{E_{x0}^4 + E_{y0}^4 + 2E_{x0}^2 E_{y0}^2\cos(2\delta)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Plug in: $E_{x0}^2 = 36$, $E_{y0}^2 = 64$, $E_{x0}^4 = 1296$, $E_{y0}^4 = 4096$, $2\delta = 120°$, $\cos(120°) = -0.5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Discriminant} &= 1296 + 4096 + 2(36)(64)(-0.5) \\
&= 5392 - 2304 = 3088 \\
\sqrt{3088} &\approx 55.57
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
AR &= \frac{36 + 64 + 55.57}{36 + 64 - 55.57} = \frac{155.57}{44.43} \approx 3.50
\end{aligned}
$$

In dB: $AR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(3.50) \approx 10.88$ dB.

**(iii) Orientation angle (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(2\tau) &= \frac{2(6)(8)\cos(60°)}{6^2 - 8^2} = \frac{96 \times 0.5}{36 - 64} = \frac{48}{-28} = -1.714 \\
2\tau &= \arctan(-1.714) \approx -59.74° \\
\tau &\approx -29.87° \approx -29.9°
\end{aligned}
$$

The major axis of the ellipse is tilted at **−29.9° from the +x axis** (i.e., rotated clockwise into the fourth quadrant).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Alternative Module 2 QP]**
**(a)** Explain the three types of antenna polarization with neat diagrams. What is meant by the **Axial Ratio** and how does it classify the polarization state? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two linearly polarized antennas are oriented with their E-field vectors at $\theta_t = +30°$ and $\theta_r = -15°$ with respect to the horizontal. Compute the polarization loss factor (PLF) and the polarization efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Theory (7 marks)

**The three types (6 marks):**

1. **Linear Polarization (2 marks):**
   The E-field vector oscillates along a single fixed direction. A simple Hertzian dipole or a half-wave dipole radiates a linearly polarized wave whose E-field is parallel to the dipole axis. Example: a vertical monopole produces a vertically polarized wave. The current direction is along a single axis, so the radiated E-field has only one component.

2. **Circular Polarization (2 marks):**
   The E-field vector rotates at constant angular velocity, tracing a circle. Requires **two orthogonal linear currents of equal amplitude** and a **±90° phase difference**. Can be generated by a crossed-dipole fed through a 90° hybrid coupler. Sub-divided into RHCP and LHCP per IEEE convention.

3. **Elliptical Polarization (2 marks):**
   The general case. The E-field traces a tilted ellipse in the transverse plane. The **Axial Ratio (AR)** is the ratio of major to minor axis of this ellipse:
   - $AR = 1$ → Circular
   - $AR = \infty$ → Linear
   - $1 < AR < \infty$ → Elliptical

**Axial Ratio classification (1 mark):**

$$AR = \frac{E_{\text{major}}}{E_{\text{minor}}} \in [1, \infty]$$

In dB: $AR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(AR)$. A high-quality circularly polarized antenna has $AR \leq 3$ dB (i.e., $AR \leq \sqrt{2}$).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** Two linearly polarized antennas; the angle between their E-field vectors is:

$$\Delta\theta = \theta_t - \theta_r = +30° - (-15°) = 45°$$

**Step 1 — Recall the PLF formula for linear-to-linear case (2 marks).**

$$PLF = \cos^2(\Delta\theta) = \cos^2(45°) = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Polarization efficiency (1 mark):**

$$\eta_p = PLF \times 100\% = 50\%$$

**Step 3 — Loss in dB (1 mark):**

$$\text{Loss}_{dB} = -10\log_{10}(PLF) = -10\log_{10}(0.5) = 3 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (3 marks):**
- Only 50% of the transmitted power is delivered to the receiver load.
- 3 dB of signal is lost purely due to polarization mismatch.
- For two identical polarizations ($\Delta\theta = 0$): $PLF = 1$, 0 dB loss.
- For orthogonal polarizations ($\Delta\theta = 90°$): $PLF = 0$, $-\infty$ dB loss (total rejection).
- Engineering fix: use a **polarizer** (a device that rotates the polarization), or use **circular polarization** on both ends to avoid orientation sensitivity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign of phase difference:** A common 1-mark deduction comes from confusing $\delta = +\pi/2$ (LHCP) with $\delta = -\pi/2$ (RHCP). **Always trace the rotation direction explicitly** in your answer; don't just state "circular" — say LHCP or RHCP.
> 2. **Axial Ratio formula:** Plugging values into the AR formula without simplifying $\cos(2\delta)$ correctly. For $\delta = 60°$, $2\delta = 120°$, $\cos(120°) = -0.5$ — students often wrongly write $+0.5$.
> 3. **Units confusion in PLF:** The PLF is dimensionless (a ratio between 0 and 1), but the loss in dB is negative or expressed as a positive number depending on convention. State both clearly.
> 4. **Major vs. minor axis in orientation angle:** Students forget to specify whether $\tau$ is the angle to the **major** or **minor** axis. The standard definition is the angle to the major axis.
> 5. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $\tau$:** The formula is $\tau = (1/2)\arctan(\ldots)$. Omitting the ½ loses 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Polarization** is a property of the *radiated wave* in the far field, not a property of the antenna structure itself.
- [ ] The **current direction** on the antenna **dictates** the E-field direction in the far field (a single wire → linear polarization parallel to wire).
- [ ] The **polarization ellipse** is the universal description; **linear** and **circular** are degenerate special cases.
- [ ] **Linear polarization** → $\delta = 0$ or $\pm\pi$.
- [ ] **Circular polarization** → $E_{x0} = E_{y0}$ AND $\delta = \pm\pi/2$.
- [ ] **IEEE Convention:** RHCP requires $\delta = -\pi/2$ (for a wave going +z). **Thumb of right hand** in propagation direction, fingers curl in direction of E-field rotation.
- [ ] **Axial Ratio** $AR \in [1, \infty]$; $AR = 1$ dB → 0 dB; $AR = \infty$ → Linear.
- [ ] **PLF** for two linear polarizations: $PLF = \cos^2(\Delta\theta)$.
- [ ] **PLF** for linear ↔ circular: $PLF = 0.5$ (−3 dB loss).
- [ ] **PLF** for RHCP ↔ LHCP: $PLF = 0$ (total loss).
- [ ] **Stokes Parameters** fully characterize polarization (4 real numbers); the **Poincaré sphere** is a 3D visualization of these parameters.
- [ ] **Far-field condition:** $R_f = 2D^2/\lambda$ — required to observe "pure" polarization.
- [ ] **Crossed-dipole + 90° hybrid** is the standard physical realization of circular polarization.
- [ ] **Satellite & GPS** prefer **circular polarization** (immune to Faraday rotation).
- [ ] **Engineering tip:** Always design the receive antenna's polarization to **match** the transmit antenna for maximum power transfer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** The above derivation of the polarization ellipse and the numerical example follow the standard Balanis treatment (Chapter 2 of *Antenna Theory: Analysis and Design*). For the KTU 2024 ESE, expect 1 question (3 marks) and 1 question (up to 14 marks) directly from this topic, often combined with array theory in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of an Antenna Array

An **antenna array** is a system of two or more radiating elements (antennas) that are spatially arranged and electrically interconnected so as to produce a directional radiation pattern that is the result of the vector addition of the individual fields produced by each element.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> An antenna array is an assembly of radiating elements arranged in a specific geometrical configuration (linear, planar, or circular) and excited with specific amplitude and phase relationships to obtain a desired radiation pattern that cannot be achieved by a single element alone.

Mathematically, if each element produces a far-field pattern $E_n(\theta, \phi)$, the total field of an N-element array is:

$$E_{total}(\theta, \phi) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} E_n(\theta, \phi)$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Megaphone Effect

Imagine you are trying to call your friend across a noisy playground. A single shout may not reach them. But if **10 friends stand in a line**, each shouting the same word **at the exact same instant**, with carefully timed delays, the sound will combine constructively in the forward direction and cancel out in other directions.

This is precisely how an **antenna array** works:
- Each **antenna element** is one of your friends.
- The **spacing and phase excitation** are the timing of the shouts.
- The combined directional pattern is the focused beam you create.

> [!NOTE]
> A single isotropic radiator spreads its energy **uniformly in all directions** (like a bare light bulb). An array focuses that energy like a **flashlight beam**, giving high **directivity** and **gain**.

## 1.3 Why Are Antenna Arrays Needed?

| Limitation of Single Element | How Array Solves It |
|------------------------------|---------------------|
| Low directivity | Increases directive gain |
| Fixed, broad radiation pattern | Steerable beam electronically |
| Limited gain | Gain scales with number of elements |
| Poor signal-to-noise ratio | Better spatial filtering |
| Cannot suppress interference | Creates nulls in unwanted directions |

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Free-space impedance:** $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377 \text{ }\Omega$
- **Wavelength:** $\lambda = c/f$
- **Element spacing (typical):** $d = \lambda/2$ to avoid grating lobes
- **Directivity (single element):** $D \approx 1.5$ (half-wave dipole)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

The directional properties of an antenna array arise from **constructive and destructive interference** of the radiated fields from individual elements. By controlling two key parameters, the radiation pattern can be precisely shaped:

1. **Geometric configuration** — the physical arrangement (linear, circular, planar).
2. **Excitation** — the amplitude and phase of the current fed to each element.

### 2.1.1 The Three Pillars of Array Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Pillar 1 — Geometry (Spatial Distribution):**
> Where the elements are placed in space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pillar 2 — Excitation Amplitude (Tapering):**
> How much current flows in each element (uniform, triangular, binomial).

> [!NOTE]
> **Pillar 3 — Excitation Phase (Progressive Phase Shift):**
> The phase difference $\beta$ between adjacent elements that steers the beam.

## 2.2 Classification of Antenna Arrays

Antenna arrays are classified based on their physical geometry and excitation scheme.

### A. By Geometry
- **Linear Array:** Elements arranged along a straight line (most common in KTU syllabus).
- **Planar Array:** Elements arranged on a 2-D surface (rectangular, circular aperture).
- **Circular Array:** Elements arranged on a circle.
- **Conformal Array:** Elements conform to a curved surface (e.g., aircraft fuselage).

### B. By Excitation Phase
- **Uniform Linear Array (ULA):** Equal amplitudes, uniform progressive phase.
- **Broadside Array:** Main beam perpendicular to array axis ($\beta = 0$).
- **End-Fire Array:** Main beam along the array axis ($\beta = \pm kd$).
- **Phased Array:** Electronically steerable by varying $\beta$.

## 2.3 The Two-Element Array — Foundation of Everything

Every N-element array analysis reduces to the **pattern multiplication theorem** which fundamentally relies on the two-element case.

Consider two identical isotropic radiators separated by distance $d$, fed with currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ having a phase difference $\beta$.

The total field at a far-field point is:

$$E_{total} = E_0 \left[ 1 + \frac{I_2}{I_1} e^{j(kd\cos\theta + \beta)} \right]$$

The total field is the **product** of:
- The **element pattern** (pattern of a single radiator).
- The **array factor** (pattern due purely to array geometry and excitation).

## 2.4 Pattern Multiplication Theorem (Kulikowski Theorem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Statement:**
> *The radiation pattern of an array of identical elements is the product of the radiation pattern of a single element (Element Pattern) and the radiation pattern of an array of isotropic point sources having the same locations and relative excitations (Array Factor).*

$$E_{array}(\theta, \phi) = E_{element}(\theta, \phi) \times AF(\theta, \phi)$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Array Factor (N-element ULA) | $AF = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} a_n e^{j n \psi}$ | Generalized form, $\psi = kd\cos\theta + \beta$ |
| 2 | AF for Uniform Linear Array | $AF = \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}$ | Closed form for equal amplitudes |
| 3 | Progressive Phase | $\psi = kd\cos\theta + \beta$ | Phase difference from element $n$ to $n+1$ |
| 4 | Wave Number | $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ | Spatial frequency |
| 5 | Half-Power Beamwidth (approx) | $HPBW \approx 0.886 \lambda / (Nd \cos\theta_0)$ | For broadside, $\theta_0 = 90°$ |
| 6 | Directivity of N-element ULA | $D \approx 2N d / \lambda$ | For large broadside arrays |
| 7 | First Null Beamwidth | $FNBW = 2 \lambda / (Nd)$ | Broadside, in radians |
| 8 | Grating Lobe Condition | $d > \lambda$ | Causes unwanted secondary maxima |
| 9 | Grating Lobe Angles | $\theta_{grating} = \cos^{-1}\left(\pm \frac{\lambda}{d} - \frac{\beta}{kd}\right)$ | For uniform spacing |
| 10 | Beam Steering Condition | $\beta = -kd \cos\theta_0$ | To point beam at $\theta_0$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> - **5G Massive MIMO base stations** use planar arrays of 64/128/256 elements.
> - **Phased-array radar** (e.g., AN/APG-81 on F-35) uses thousands of elements.
> - **Satellite communication** on aircraft uses conformal arrays.
> - **Radio astronomy** (e.g., VLA in New Mexico) uses Y-shaped arrays of 27 dish antennas.
> - **Wi-Fi routers** use 4×4 MIMO arrays for spatial multiplexing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Two-Element Array Field

**Setup:** Two identical isotropic radiators placed along the z-axis, separated by distance $d$, with the observation point at angle $\theta$ from the z-axis.

**Step 1 — Write the field from element 1.**

Place element 1 at the origin. For an isotropic radiator with input $I_1$:

$$E_1 = \frac{I_1}{r_1} e^{-j k r_1}$$

**Step 2 — Write the field from element 2.**

Element 2 is placed at $z = d$, so the distance to the far-field point is:

$$r_2 = r_1 - d \cos\theta$$

For the far-field region, $1/r_1 \approx 1/r_2$, so the amplitude term is identical. The phase term becomes:

$$E_2 = \frac{I_2}{r_1} e^{-j k (r_1 - d\cos\theta)} \cdot e^{j\beta}$$

where $\beta$ is the phase lead of element 2 over element 1.

**Step 3 — Apply the far-field amplitude approximation.**

Since $r_1 \approx r_2$ in amplitude, factor out the common term:

$$E_{total} = E_0 \left[ 1 + \frac{I_2}{I_1} e^{j(kd\cos\theta + \beta)} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Define the array factor.**

The bracketed term is the **Array Factor (AF)**:

$$AF = 1 + a \, e^{j\psi}, \quad \text{where} \quad a = \frac{I_2}{I_1}, \quad \psi = kd\cos\theta + \beta$$

**Step 5 — Compute the magnitude.**

$$|AF| = \left| 1 + a e^{j\psi} \right| = \sqrt{1 + a^2 + 2a\cos\psi}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: N-Element Uniform Linear Array

**Step 1 — Geometric series.**

Place N elements along the z-axis with uniform spacing $d$ and equal amplitudes. The total field is:

$$E = E_0 \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j n \psi} = E_0 \left[ 1 + e^{j\psi} + e^{j2\psi} + \dots + e^{j(N-1)\psi} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Multiply and divide by the geometric ratio trick.**

Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 - e^{j\psi})$:

$$E = E_0 \cdot \frac{1 - e^{jN\psi}}{1 - e^{j\psi}}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to sine form using Euler's identity.**

Using $1 - e^{jN\psi} = e^{jN\psi/2}(e^{-jN\psi/2} - e^{jN\psi/2}) = -2j \, e^{jN\psi/2} \sin(N\psi/2)$:

$$E = E_0 \cdot \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} \cdot e^{j(N-1)\psi/2}$$

**Step 4 — Drop the phase term (it does not affect magnitude/radiation pattern).**

The **array factor magnitude** is therefore:

$$\boxed{\vert AF \vert = \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}}$$

This is the **most-asked formula in KTU Module 3.**

## 3.3 Derivation: Conditions for Maximum and Minimum Radiation

**Step 1 — Maximum radiation condition.**

$|AF|$ is maximum when $\sin(\psi/2) = 0$, i.e.:

$$\psi = \pm 2m\pi, \quad m = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

The **principal maximum** is at $m = 0$, i.e., $\psi = 0$:

$$kd\cos\theta_{max} + \beta = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\theta_{max} = -\frac{\beta}{kd}$$

**Step 2 — Minimum radiation (null) condition.**

$\sin(N\psi/2) = 0$ while $\sin(\psi/2) \neq 0$:

$$\psi = \frac{\pm 2m\pi}{N}, \quad m = 1, 2, 3, \dots \text{ (excluding multiples of N)}$$

**Step 3 — Half-power beamwidth derivation (sketch).**

Setting $|AF|^2 = 1/2$ of maximum:

$$\frac{\sin^2(N\psi/2)}{\sin^2(\psi/2)} = \frac{N^2}{2}$$

Solving numerically yields the well-known result:

$$HPBW \approx \frac{0.886 \lambda}{Nd \cos\theta_0} \text{ (radians)}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Maximum Directions for Common Arrays

| Array Type | Progressive Phase $\beta$ | Beam Direction $\theta_0$ | Condition |
|------------|---------------------------|---------------------------|-----------|
| **Broadside** | $\beta = 0$ | $\theta_0 = 90°$ | $\psi = 0$ at $\theta = 90°$ |
| **End-fire (ordinary)** | $\beta = -kd$ | $\theta_0 = 0°$ | $\psi = 0$ at $\theta = 0°$ |
| **End-fire (Hansen-Woodyard)** | $\beta \approx -(kd + \pi/N)$ | $\theta_0 = 0°$ | Enhanced directivity |
| **Phased (general)** | $\beta = -kd \cos\theta_0$ | $\theta_0$ arbitrary | Electronically steerable |

## 3.5 Worked Example: 4-Element Broadside Array

**Given:** $N = 4$, $d = \lambda/2$, uniform amplitudes, broadside condition ($\beta = 0$).

**Step 1 — Write $\psi$.**

$$\psi = kd\cos\theta + 0 = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} \cdot \cos\theta = \pi \cos\theta$$

**Step 2 — Identify principal maximum.**

At $\theta = 90°$: $\cos\theta = 0$, so $\psi = 0$. This is the broadside direction. **Maximum radiation** occurs at $\theta = 90°$.

**Step 3 — Find first null.**

Setting $\sin(N\psi/2) = 0$:

$$\sin(2\pi \cos\theta) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2\pi\cos\theta = \pm \pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\theta = \pm 0.5$$

So the first nulls are at $\theta = 60°$ and $\theta = 120°$.

**Step 4 — Compute Half-Power Beamwidth.**

Approximate formula:

$$HPBW \approx \frac{0.886 \times \lambda}{4 \times (\lambda/2)} = \frac{0.886 \times 2}{4} = 0.443 \text{ rad} \approx 25.4°$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Antenna Array System Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[RF Signal Source] --> B[Power Divider Network]
    B --> C1[Phase Shifter 1]
    B --> C2[Phase Shifter 2]
    B --> C3[Phase Shifter 3]
    B --> C4[Phase Shifter N]
    C1 --> D1[Amplitude Tapering Unit 1]
    C2 --> D2[Amplitude Tapering Unit 2]
    C3 --> D3[Amplitude Tapering Units 3]
    C4 --> D4[Amplitude Tapering Unit N]
    D1 --> E1[Radiating Element 1]
    D2 --> E2[Radiating Element 2]
    D3 --> E3[Radiating Element 3]
    D4 --> E4[Radiating Element N]
    E1 --> F[Combined Far Field Pattern]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Desired Directional Beam]
    F --> H[Nulls in Unwanted Directions]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram: Pattern Multiplication

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Single Element Pattern] --> M[Multiplication Operation]
    B[Array Factor Pattern] --> M
    M --> C[Final Array Radiation Pattern]
    A1[Isotropic or Dipole Response] --> A
    B1[Geometry and Excitation Response] --> B
```

## 4.3 Flow Diagram: Array Design Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Define Application Requirement] --> Q1{Need for Directional Pattern?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Number of Elements}
    Q1 -->|No| Single[Use Single Antenna]
    Q2 -->|2 to 5| Small[Small Linear Array]
    Q2 -->|6 to 20| Medium[Medium ULA]
    Q2 -->|More than 20| Large[Large ULA or Planar Array]
    Small --> P1[Choose Broadside or End-Fire]
    Medium --> P2[Apply Tapering for SLL Control]
    Large --> P3[Consider Grating Lobes and Beam Steering]
    P1 --> End[Compute AF and Plot Pattern]
    P2 --> End
    P3 --> End
```

## 4.4 Schematic of a Uniform Linear Array (Geometric Layout)

```mermaid
graph LR
    E1((Element 1)) ---|d| E2((Element 2))
    E2 ---|d| E3((Element 3])
    E3 ---|d| E4((Element 4))
    E4 ---|d| E5((Element N))
    E1 -.->|Beam at 90 deg| B[Far-Field Broadside Direction]
```

## 4.5 Functional Architecture: Pattern Multiplication Process

```mermaid
graph TD
    Input1[Element Pattern E theta phi] --> Stage1[Stage 1: Define Single Element Response]
    Input2[Array Factor AF theta phi] --> Stage2[Stage 2: Compute Geometry-Based Pattern]
    Stage1 --> Stage3[Stage 3: Multiply Element by Array Factor]
    Stage2 --> Stage3
    Stage3 --> Output[Total Array Radiation Pattern]
    Output --> Application1[Antenna Design Validation]
    Output --> Application2[Beam Steering Control]
    Output --> Application3[Sidelobe Level Management]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an antenna array. What are the two main classifications based on geometry?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **antenna array** is an assembly of two or more radiating elements arranged in space and excited with specific amplitudes and phases to produce a desired directional radiation pattern that cannot be obtained from a single element.

**Two main geometric classifications:**
1. **Linear Array** — Elements arranged along a straight line.
2. **Planar Array** — Elements arranged on a 2-D surface (e.g., rectangular, circular aperture).

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark for each classification = 3 marks)*

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the Pattern Multiplication Theorem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Pattern Multiplication Theorem (Kulikowski Theorem):**

The total radiation pattern of an array of identical elements is equal to the **product** of:
1. The radiation pattern of a **single element** (Element Pattern), and
2. The radiation pattern of an array of **isotropic point sources** having the same geometry and relative excitations (Array Factor).

Mathematically:
$$E_{array}(\theta, \phi) = E_{element}(\theta, \phi) \times AF(\theta, \phi)$$

*(2 marks for statement + 1 mark for equation = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Derive the array factor of an N-element uniform linear array. From this, derive the conditions for principal maximum and first minimum. A 4-element ULA with $\lambda/2$ spacing operates in broadside mode. Calculate the HPBW and directivity.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Array Factor (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Consider N isotropic radiators along z-axis with uniform spacing $d$, equal amplitudes, and progressive phase $\beta$.

**Step 2:** The total field is the sum of contributions:

$$E = E_0 \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j n \psi}, \quad \text{where} \quad \psi = kd\cos\theta + \beta$$

**Step 3:** This is a geometric series with first term 1, ratio $e^{j\psi}$, and N terms. Sum:

$$E = E_0 \cdot \frac{1 - e^{jN\psi}}{1 - e^{j\psi}}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by conjugate factor $\frac{e^{-j\psi/2}}{e^{-j\psi/2}}$ and use Euler's identity:

$$\boxed{\vert AF \vert = \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}}$$

**Step 5 — Principal maximum:** Maximum when $\sin(\psi/2) = 0$ and $\sin(N\psi/2) \neq 0$:

$$\psi = \pm 2m\pi, \quad m = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

For $m = 0$: $kd\cos\theta_{max} + \beta = 0$ — gives beam direction.

**Step 6 — First minimum:** When $\sin(N\psi/2) = 0$ but $\sin(\psi/2) \neq 0$:

$$\psi = \frac{\pm 2\pi}{N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\theta_{null} = \frac{1}{kd}\left(\frac{2\pi}{N} - \beta\right)$$

*(7 marks — full step-by-step marks as per KTU valuation key)*

#### Part (b) — HPBW and Directivity Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:** $N = 4$, $d = \lambda/2$, broadside mode ($\beta = 0$).

**Step 1 — HPBW Calculation:**

$$HPBW \approx \frac{0.886 \lambda}{Nd \cos\theta_0}$$

For broadside, $\theta_0 = 90°$, so $\cos\theta_0 = 1$:

$$HPBW = \frac{0.886 \lambda}{4 \times (\lambda/2) \times 1} = \frac{0.886 \times 2}{4} = 0.443 \text{ rad}$$

Converting to degrees: $HPBW \approx 0.443 \times (180/\pi) \approx 25.4°$

**Step 2 — Directivity Calculation:**

For a broadside ULA:

$$D \approx \frac{2Nd}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 4 \times (\lambda/2)}{\lambda} = 4$$

In decibels: $D_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(4) \approx 6.02 \text{ dBi}$

**Final Answers:** $HPBW = 25.4°$, $D = 4 = 6.02 \text{ dBi}$

*(7 marks — 3 for HPBW, 3 for Directivity, 1 for unit conversions)*

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the broadside and end-fire arrays with neat sketches. Compare their directivity and beamwidth. An 8-element end-fire array has spacing $d = \lambda/4$. Find the progressive phase $\beta$ for ordinary end-fire operation and compute the array factor at $\theta = 0°$.**

#### Part (a) — Broadside vs End-Fire Comparison (7 marks)

**Broadside Array:**
- Progressive phase $\beta = 0$.
- Maximum radiation is **perpendicular to the array axis** ($\theta_0 = 90°$).
- Higher directivity for the same number of elements.
- Wider beamwidth in the elevation plane.

**End-Fire Array:**
- Progressive phase $\beta = -kd$ (ordinary end-fire).
- Maximum radiation is **along the array axis** ($\theta_0 = 0°$).
- Lower directivity (about half of broadside) for the same N.
- Narrower beamwidth in the elevation plane.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Broadside | End-Fire |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| $\beta$ | 0 | $-kd$ |
| Beam direction | $\theta_0 = 90°$ | $\theta_0 = 0°$ |
| Directivity (approx) | $2Nd/\lambda$ | $1.333 \times 2Nd/\lambda$ (Hansen-Woodyard enhanced) or $4Nd/\lambda$ (ordinary) |
| Beamwidth | Narrow in azimuth, wide in elevation | Narrow in elevation, wide in azimuth |
| Major application | Broadcasting, base stations | Point-to-point communication, radar |

*(7 marks — 2 for broadside, 2 for end-fire, 3 for comparison table)*

#### Part (b) — End-Fire Array Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:** $N = 8$, $d = \lambda/4$, ordinary end-fire.

**Step 1 — Progressive phase for ordinary end-fire:**

$$\beta = -kd = -\frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{4} = -\frac{\pi}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute array factor at $\theta = 0°$:**

$$\psi = kd\cos\theta + \beta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{4} \cdot \cos 0° + \left(-\frac{\pi}{2}\right) = \frac{\pi}{2} - \frac{\pi}{2} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply array factor formula:**

At $\psi = 0$:

$$|AF| = \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} = \lim_{\psi \to 0} \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} = N = 8$$

This is the **principal maximum** at $\theta = 0°$, confirming the end-fire beam direction.

*(7 marks — 2 for phase, 3 for AF computation, 2 for verification)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the progress of phase $\beta$ in the array factor.** Students often write $AF = \sin(N\psi/2)/\sin(\psi/2)$ without explaining that $\psi = kd\cos\theta + \beta$. **Loss: 2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Confusing $\psi$ with $\theta$.** $\psi$ is the total electrical phase, not the angle. Always express final answer in terms of $\theta$ or specify the principal maximum condition.
>
> 3. **Grating lobe oversight.** When $d > \lambda$, multiple maxima appear. KTU questions often test this. Always state the condition $d \leq \lambda$ to avoid grating lobes (or $d \leq \lambda/(1 + \vert \sin\theta_0 \vert)$ for steered beams).
>
> 4. **Unit errors in beamwidth.** HPBW is dimensionless (radians or degrees) — not in meters. Directivity is dimensionless or in dBi.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Pattern Multiplication Theorem statement** before solving — examiners expect this declared in the first 2-3 lines.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3 Introduction to Antenna Arrays:**

- **Antenna Array Definition:** System of 2 or more radiating elements with controlled amplitude/phase to produce directional patterns.
- **Three Design Pillars:** Geometry, Amplitude Excitation, Phase Excitation.
- **Two-Element Array:** Foundation for understanding all arrays; gives $AF = 1 + a e^{j\psi}$.
- **Pattern Multiplication Theorem:** $E_{array} = E_{element} \times AF$ — must be **stated first** in any derivation question.
- **ULA Array Factor:** $\vert AF \vert = \sin(N\psi/2) / \sin(\psi/2)$ — **the single most important formula in Module 3**.
- **Phase term:** $\psi = kd\cos\theta + \beta$ — defines beam direction.
- **Principal Maximum:** $\psi = 0$ — gives $\cos\theta_0 = -\beta/(kd)$.
- **Broadside Array:** $\beta = 0$, $\theta_0 = 90°$.
- **End-Fire Array:** $\beta = -kd$, $\theta_0 = 0°$.
- **HPBW Formula:** $\approx 0.886\lambda / (Nd\cos\theta_0)$ for ULA broadside.
- **Directivity:** $D \approx 2Nd/\lambda$ for broadside ULA.
- **Grating Lobes Appear When:** $d > \lambda$ (general case) or $d > \lambda/(1 + \vert \sin\theta_0 \vert)$ for steered arrays.
- **No Grating Lobe Condition:** $d \leq \lambda/2$ for broadside; $d \leq \lambda/4$ for end-fire.
- **First Null:** $\psi = \pm 2\pi/N$ — gives angular separation of nulls.
- **Beamwidth × Directivity Tradeoff:** More elements $\Rightarrow$ narrower beam $\Rightarrow$ higher directivity.
- **Pattern Multiplication Caveat:** Valid only when **all elements are identical** in pattern and orientation.
- **Phase Steered Array:** Beam direction controlled electronically via $\beta$ — basis of modern radar and 5G.
- **Common KTU trap:** Forgetting to check that $m \neq 0, N, 2N, \dots$ when finding nulls of ULA.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart-Antenna Analogy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Smart-Antenna Analogy**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smart Antenna (SA):** A multi-element antenna system that dynamically adjusts its radiation pattern (in real-time) toward desired users and places nulls in the direction of interferers, using sophisticated Digital Signal Processing (DSP) algorithms. It exploits the **spatial domain** (in addition to time/frequency) to improve wireless system performance.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (PEECT868, Module 3), a **Smart-Antenna Analogy** refers to the conceptual mapping used to explain how a smart antenna behaves — i.e., comparing its spatial filtering action to a familiar, real-world phenomenon so that students can internalize *beam-steering*, *null-placement*, and *adaptive tracking*.

> [!NOTE]
> The official terminology (Balanis — *Antenna Theory: Analysis and Design*) refers to a "smart antenna" as either a **Switched-Beam Antenna** or an **Adaptive Antenna Array**. Both are sub-classes of the broader **Antenna Array** theory covered earlier in Module 3.

---

## 1.2 The "Smart-Antenna Analogy" — A Real-World Intuition

### Analogy 1 — The Ear-Brain System (Selective Auditory Attention)
> Imagine you are at a noisy cocktail party. **Your two ears** (analogous to two antenna elements) capture sound from every direction. **Your auditory cortex** (analogous to the DSP unit) intelligently processes these signals so that you **focus on one speaker's voice** while *suppressing* the surrounding chatter.

| Human Hearing System | Smart Antenna System |
|---|---|
| Two ears | Antenna array (N elements) |
| Ear spacing (≈ 17 cm) | Element spacing **d** (typically **λ/2**) |
| Brain's auditory processing | DSP beamforming algorithm |
| Head-turn to track speaker | **Electronic beam steering** (no mechanical motion) |
| Ignoring unwanted voices | **Null steering** toward interferers |

### Analogy 2 — The Adjustable Flashlight
A smart antenna is like a **flashlight with a swiveling head** that automatically turns toward a moving person in a dark room. The **beam** is the desired-signal direction; the **darkness around the beam** represents nulls placed on interferers.

### Analogy 3 — The Pinhole Camera
A simple antenna = pinhole camera (sees everything blurred). A **smart antenna** = a camera with a **motorized lens** that **zooms in** on the subject and **dodges** the streetlight flare.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Syllabus Anchors)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Vocabulary for KTU Module 3:**
> - **Spatial Diversity** — Using multiple, spatially separated elements to combat fading.
> - **Beamforming** — Coherent combination of element signals with weighted phase/amplitude to form a directional beam.
> - **Direction of Arrival (DOA)** — The azimuth/elevation angle from which an incoming wave impinges.
> - **Adaptive Beamforming** — Real-time update of weights to track changing signal environments.
> - **Null Steering** — Placing radiation nulls toward unwanted (interfering) sources.
> - **Angle of Arrival (AOA)** — Synonymous with DOA in KTU parlance.
> - **Spatial Filtering** — Selecting signals based on their **angular position** rather than frequency.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$
- **Wavelength:** $\lambda = c/f$
- **Standard inter-element spacing:** $d = \lambda/2$ (avoids grating lobes)
- **Beamwidth (3-dB):** $\theta_{3dB} \approx 0.886 \, \lambda / (N d \cos\theta_0)$ for a uniformly excited broadside array of *N* elements.

---

## 1.5 Why Study the "Analogy"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner rewards answers that **map the abstract antenna math to a physical intuition**. The "smart-antenna analogy" question is often a **3-mark short-answer** in Part A. A textbook-perfect answer that also offers a clean analogy typically earns **full marks** because it satisfies the **CO (Course Outcome) on visualization and engineering reasoning**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition (Step Logic)

A smart-antenna system performs four sequential operations. We will explain the *Why* and *How* of each:

### Step 1 — Signal Capture (Spatial Sampling)
- **Why:** A single omnidirectional element sees *all* directions equally → no spatial selectivity.
- **How:** Use *N* identical elements placed at half-wavelength spacing $d = \lambda/2$ across a straight line (Uniform Linear Array — ULA).
- **Result:** Each element receives a *delayed* copy of the incoming wave.

The progressive phase delay between adjacent elements for a wave arriving at angle $\theta$ from broadside is:

$$
\psi = k d \cos\theta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} d \cos\theta
$$

- **Why this matters:** The relative phase $\psi$ contains the *spatial signature* of the incoming signal — this is the fingerprint used for DOA estimation.

### Step 2 — Weighting (Beamforming Network)
- **Why:** A simple sum of the *N* received signals gives a *broad* pattern. We need **directionality**.
- **How:** Multiply each element's signal by a complex weight $w_n = a_n e^{-j\phi_n}$.
- **Result:** Constructive interference in the desired direction, destructive in others.

### Step 3 — Spatial Filtering
- **Why:** Multiple users share the same frequency (FDMA / TDMA / CDMA). Without spatial filtering, co-channel interference (CCI) is the bottleneck.
- **How:** The Array Factor (AF) of a *N*-element ULA is:

$$
AF(\theta) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} w_n \, e^{j n \psi}
$$

- **Result:** *AF* is a *spatial* transfer function — like a frequency filter, but over angle.

### Step 4 — Adaptive Update
- **Why:** Users move; interferers appear and disappear. Static weights are obsolete in milliseconds.
- **How:** Algorithms (LMS, RLS, MUSIC, ESPRIT) continuously update $\lbrace w_n \rbrace$ to minimize an error cost function — most commonly **MMSE (Minimum Mean Square Error)** or **MV (Minimum Variance)**.

---

## 2.2 The Two Architectural Classes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module 3 explicitly distinguishes between these two classes. Memorize the trade-offs.

| Parameter | **Switched-Beam Antenna** | **Adaptive Antenna Array** |
|---|---|---|
| Beam type | **Finite, fixed** set of pre-defined beams | **Continuously variable** beam |
| Tracking | Switches between beams (discrete) | Steers beam smoothly (continuous) |
| Null placement | No null control | **Active null steering** |
| Complexity | Low (RF switches + lookup table) | High (real-time DSP) |
| Cost | **Low** | **High** |
| Interference rejection | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| Capacity gain | ~ 2–3× | ~ 4–8× |
| Example use | WLL, fixed-WiMAX | 5G NR, military radar |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the minimum formula toolkit for any Module 3 smart-antenna problem.** Do not lose these in the exam hall.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wavenumber | $k = 2\pi / \lambda$ | rad/m |
| 2 | Progressive phase | $\psi = k d \cos\theta$ | radians |
| 3 | Array Factor (ULA) | $AF(\theta) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} w_n e^{j n \psi}$ | complex |
| 4 | Array Factor magnitude (closed form) | $AF(\theta) = \dfrac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}$ | for uniform weights |
| 5 | Beam-steering phase (toward $\theta_0$) | $\beta = -k d \cos\theta_0$ | rad/element |
| 6 | 3-dB Beamwidth | $\theta_{3dB} \approx 0.886 \lambda / (N d \cos\theta_0)$ | radians |
| 7 | First-null beamwidth (FNBW) | $FNBW = 2\lambda/(N d)$ | radians, broadside |
| 8 | Directivity (broadside ULA) | $D_0 \approx 2 N d / \lambda$ | dimensionless (linear) |
| 9 | Grating-lobe-free spacing | $d < \lambda / (1 + \vert \sin\theta_0 \vert)$ | meters |
| 10 | Optimal weight (MVDR / Capon) | $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mu \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{a}(\theta_0)$ | vector, $\mu$ = scalar |
| 11 | Spatial correlation | $\rho = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_n e^{j n \Delta \psi}$ | complex, $0 \le \vert \rho \vert \le 1$ |
| 12 | SNR gain (N elements, coherent) | $G_{SNR} = N$ | linear (10 log₁₀ N in dB) |
| 13 | DOA for two-element baseline | $\theta = \arccos\left(\dfrac{\lambda \Delta \phi}{2\pi d}\right)$ | rad (from broadside) |
| 14 | LMS update | $\mathbf{w}(k+1) = \mathbf{w}(k) + \mu \, e(k) \mathbf{x}^{*}(k)$ | iterative |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the symbol `\vert` is used to denote absolute value / magnitude, as the **vertical pipe** character conflicts with markdown table separators.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Smart antennas are the backbone of:
- **5G NR Massive MIMO** (64–256 elements, hybrid beamforming).
- **Military phased-array radar** (AN/APG-81 on F-35).
- **Satellite-on-the-move (SOTM)** terminals.
- **Adaptive Wi-Fi routers** (Qualcomm's implicit beamforming in 802.11ax/ac).
- **Automotive radar** (77 GHz, 77–81 GHz FMCW with phased arrays).

The **economic value** of the smart-antenna analogy: it converts a 6-figure DSP problem into a 30-second physical insight — *exactly* the depth KTU examiners want to test.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Array Factor of a Uniform Linear Array (ULA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **most repeated** derivation in KTU Module 3. Be ready to reproduce it cold.

### Setup
Consider *N* isotropic elements equally spaced by distance *d* along the *z*-axis. A plane wave arrives from direction $\theta$ measured from broadside (i.e., from the +*x*-axis). The reference is element 0 (at the origin).

### Step 1 — Phase at element *n*
The wavefront reaches element *n* earlier (or later) than element 0 by a path difference:

$$
\Delta r_n = n d \cos\theta
$$

**Logic:** Since element *n* is at $z = nd$, and the wave travels along direction $\theta$, the projection of the segment onto the wave vector is $nd \cos\theta$.

### Step 2 — Convert path difference to phase
The corresponding phase lead/lag is:

$$
\phi_n = k \Delta r_n = n k d \cos\theta = n \psi
$$

where $\psi = kd\cos\theta$ is the **progressive phase**.

### Step 3 — Total field at observer
The far-field is the coherent sum of all element contributions (with equal amplitude $I_0$):

$$
E(\theta) = E_0 \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} I_n \, e^{j n \psi}
$$

For uniform excitation $I_n = 1$:

$$
AF(\theta) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j n \psi}
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the geometric sum
Multiply by $e^{j\psi}$ and subtract:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AF(\theta) \left(1 - e^{j\psi}\right) &= \left(1 - e^{j N \psi}\right) \\[4pt]
AF(\theta) &= \frac{1 - e^{j N \psi}}{1 - e^{j \psi}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to sine form (the "textbook" result)
Using $1 - e^{j\alpha} = e^{j\alpha/2}\left(e^{-j\alpha/2} - e^{j\alpha/2}\right) = -2j e^{j\alpha/2} \sin(\alpha/2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AF(\theta) &= \frac{-2j \, e^{jN\psi/2} \sin(N\psi/2)}{-2j \, e^{j\psi/2} \sin(\psi/2)} \\[4pt]
AF(\theta) &= e^{j(N-1)\psi/2} \cdot \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $e^{j(N-1)\psi/2}$ is the **array phase** (depends on the array's electrical center) and is **dropped** when we look at $\vert AF(\theta) \vert$. The directional behavior is fully captured by:

$$
\boxed{\;\vert AF(\theta)\vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} \right\vert \quad \text{where} \quad \psi = kd\cos\theta\;}
$$

### Step 6 — Maximum condition
The maximum ($\vert AF\vert = N$) occurs when $\psi = 0$, i.e., $\theta = 90^\circ$ (broadside) for a ULA. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Beam Steering by Phase-Only Weights

To steer the main beam to an angle $\theta_0 \ne 90^\circ$, apply weights:

$$
w_n = e^{-j n \beta}, \quad \beta = k d \cos\theta_0
$$

### Step 1 — Substitute into AF

$$
\begin{aligned}
AF(\theta) &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{-j n \beta} e^{j n \psi} \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j n (\psi - \beta)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify the new effective progressive phase
Define $\psi' = \psi - \beta = kd(\cos\theta - \cos\theta_0)$.

The maximum now occurs at $\psi' = 0 \Rightarrow \theta = \theta_0$. ✓

### Step 3 — Final steered expression

$$
\boxed{\;\vert AF(\theta)\vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin\!\left[Nd(\cos\theta - \cos\theta_0)/2\lambda \cdot 2\pi\right]}{\sin\!\left[ \pi d (\cos\theta - \cos\theta_0)/\lambda\right]} \right\vert\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **steered ULA array factor** and is the form required for any "find the array factor for an *N*-element array steered to $\theta_0$" question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — DOA from a Two-Element Sub-Array

### Setup
Two elements at $z = 0$ and $z = d$. A wave arrives from $\theta$ producing a phase difference $\Delta\phi$ between the two received signals.

### Step 1 — Geometry
The path difference is $d \cos\theta$, so the phase difference is:

$$
\Delta\phi = k d \cos\theta = \frac{2\pi d \cos\theta}{\lambda}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $\theta$

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{\lambda \Delta\phi}{2\pi d}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\theta = \arccos\left( \frac{\lambda \Delta\phi}{2 \pi d} \right)\;}
$$

### Step 3 — Validity check
The argument of arccos must be in $[-1, 1]$. This sets the **unambiguous field of view**:

$$
\vert \cos\theta \vert \le 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert \Delta\phi \vert \le \frac{2\pi d}{\lambda}
$$

For $d = \lambda/2$, the unambiguous range is $\theta \in [0^\circ, 180^\circ]$ — i.e., the entire half-space. ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Question Type | Key Formula to Deploy | Substitutions to Remember |
|---|---|---|
| "AF of N-element ULA" | $\vert \sin(N\psi/2)/\sin(\psi/2) \vert$ | $\psi = kd\cos\theta$ |
| "Steer beam to $\theta_0$" | Replace $\psi$ with $(\psi - \beta)$ | $\beta = kd\cos\theta_0$ |
| "Find null direction" | $\sin(N\psi/2) = 0$ | Exclude $\sin(\psi/2) = 0$ branch |
| "Directivity" | $D_0 = 2Nd/\lambda$ | Broadside, uniform, large N |
| "DOA from phase" | $\theta = \arccos(\lambda\Delta\phi/2\pi d)$ | Two-element baseline |
| "Adaptive weight" | $\mathbf{w}_{opt} \propto \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{a}(\theta_0)$ | MVDR / Capon beamformer |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (For Practice)

> **Question (Sample, not in KTU past paper):** A 4-element ULA with $d = \lambda/2$ is steered to $\theta_0 = 60^\circ$. Find the **null directions** closest to broadside.

### Solution
**Step 1** — Zeros of the numerator $\sin(N\psi/2) = 0$:

$$
N\psi/2 = m\pi, \quad m = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots
$$

**Step 2** — Excluding $m = 0$ (which is the main beam) and the grating-lobe values, $m = \pm 1, \pm 2, \pm 3$ give the first three nulls on each side.

**Step 3** — For steered array, the null condition is:

$$
\frac{N}{2}(\psi - \beta) = m\pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \psi = \beta + \frac{2m\pi}{N}
$$

**Step 4** — Compute $\beta$:

$$
\beta = kd\cos\theta_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} \cdot \cos 60^\circ = \pi \cdot 0.5 = 0.5\pi \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 5** — First null ($m = 1$):

$$
\psi_1 = 0.5\pi + \frac{2\pi}{4} = 0.5\pi + 0.5\pi = \pi
$$

**Step 6** — Recover $\theta$:

$$
\cos\theta_1 = \frac{\psi_1}{kd} = \frac{\pi}{\pi} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_1 = 0^\circ
$$

**Verification:** The first null lies at endfire ($0^\circ$) — physically correct for a $60^\circ$-steered half-wavelength 4-element array. ✓

**Second null ($m = -1$):**

$$
\psi_{-1} = 0.5\pi - 0.5\pi = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = 90^\circ
$$

Wait — this is a principal maximum, not a null. Re-evaluate:

For $m = -2$:

$$
\psi_{-2} = 0.5\pi - \pi = -0.5\pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\theta = -0.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = 120^\circ
$$

**Final nulls (closest to main beam):** $\theta = 0^\circ$ and $\theta = 120^\circ$ (with the latter being the *first* lower-side null).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Smart-Antenna Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RF_FRONT_END["RF FRONT END"]
        A1["Antenna Element 1"] --> B1["LNA + Filter"]
        A2["Antenna Element 2"] --> B2["LNA + Filter"]
        A3["Antenna Element N"] --> B3["LNA + Filter"]
    end

    subgraph ADC_BANK["ADC BANK"]
        B1 --> C1["ADC 1"]
        B2 --> C2["ADC 2"]
        B3 --> C3["ADC N"]
    end

    subgraph DSP_CORE["DSP BEAMFORMING CORE"]
        C1 --> D["Complex Weight Multiplier W1 W2 WN"]
        C2 --> D
        C3 --> D
        D --> E["Adaptive Algorithm LMS RLS MUSIC"]
        E --> F["Beamformed Output y of t"]
    end

    F --> G["Decision / Demodulation"]

    E -. feedback .-> D

    style RF_FRONT_END fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style ADC_BANK fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style DSP_CORE fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
```

**Reading the diagram (left → right):**
- The *N* antenna elements sample the **spatial domain**.
- Each branch is digitized by its own ADC.
- The DSP core multiplies each branch by a complex weight, sums, and **feeds back** the error to the adaptive algorithm.

---

## 4.2 Smart-Antenna Analogy Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HUMAN["HUMAN AUDITORY SYSTEM"]
        H1["Two Ears Spaced 17 cm"]
        H2["Auditory Nerve"]
        H3["Brainstem Spatial Processor"]
        H4["Cortex Selective Attention"]
        H5["Recognized Speaker Voice"]
        H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> H4 --> H5
    end

    subgraph SMART["SMART ANTENNA SYSTEM"]
        S1["N Elements Spaced lambda over 2"]
        S2["RF Front End LNA Mixer"]
        S3["DSP Beamforming Engine"]
        S4["Adaptive Weight Update"]
        S5["Beamformed Desired Signal"]
        S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5
    end

    H1 -. maps to .-> S1
    H2 -. maps to .-> S2
    H3 -. maps to .-> S3
    H4 -. maps to .-> S4
    H5 -. maps to .-> S5

    style HUMAN fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#c9184a
    style SMART fill:#e2eafc,stroke:#3a86ff
```

**Purpose:** This is the *single most important* visual for KTU — it directly supports the "smart-antenna analogy" question by mapping the **cocktail-party effect** to the engineering subsystem.

---

## 4.3 Switched-Beam vs. Adaptive Array — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SB["SWITCHED BEAM ANTENNA"]
        SB1["N Element ULA"] --> SB2["RF Switch Matrix"]
        SB2 -->|"Beam 1"| SB3["Predefined Sector 1"]
        SB2 -->|"Beam 2"| SB4["Predefined Sector 2"]
        SB2 -->|"Beam K"| SB5["Predefined Sector K"]
        SB6["Lookup Table Best SNR"] --> SB2
    end

    subgraph AA["ADAPTIVE ANTENNA ARRAY"]
        AA1["N Element ULA"] --> AA2["Adaptive Weight Network W1 W2 WN"]
        AA2 --> AA3["Steered Main Beam Toward User"]
        AA2 --> AA4["Nulls Toward Interferers"]
        AA5["MMSE or MVDR Algorithm"] -->|"Update W"| AA2
    end

    style SB fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#f08c00
    style AA fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44
```

**Key takeaway:** The *Switched-Beam* antenna *selects* one of *K* fixed beams (like a lighthouse with discrete sectors). The *Adaptive* antenna *synthesizes* any beam shape in real time and **simultaneously** places nulls.

---
