# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are the *exact style* of KTU 2024 Part A questions: concise, definition-leaning, and **3-mark** format. Memorize the model answers.

---

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: Define a smart antenna. Explain the analogy between a smart antenna and the human auditory system.**

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words):**

A **smart antenna** is a multi-element antenna system that combines an *antenna array* with *real-time digital signal processing* to dynamically steer its radiation pattern toward desired users and place nulls on interferers.

**Analogy:** The *human auditory system* functions as a natural "smart antenna." The **two ears** (≈ 17 cm apart) act as spatially separated sensors, much like the *N* elements of an antenna array. The **brainstem and auditory cortex** perform the role of the **DSP beamforming engine**, decoding the inter-aural time and level differences to spatially localize a speaker. Just as we can *selectively attend* to one voice in a noisy party (the "cocktail-party effect"), a smart antenna spatially filters a desired signal out of interference. The *head turn* corresponds to **electronic beam steering**, which requires no physical motion. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between a switched-beam antenna and an adaptive antenna array.**

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words):**

| Aspect | Switched-Beam | Adaptive Array |
|---|---|---|
| Beam type | Fixed, pre-defined sectors | Continuously variable |
| Null control | None | Active null steering |
| Tracking | Discrete (sector switch) | Smooth, continuous |
| Complexity | RF switch + lookup table | Real-time DSP + adaptive algorithm |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Interference rejection | Limited | Excellent |
| Capacity gain | 2–3× | 4–8× |

A **switched-beam** antenna selects one of *K* pre-formed narrow beams covering the cell, choosing the one with the highest received SNR. An **adaptive array** synthesizes any beam shape by adaptively updating complex element weights (LMS, RLS, MVDR) to maximize SIR. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions are **14 marks each**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. We present **TWO** alternative choices. Pick ONE.

---

### **Question B — Option A** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a clear analogy, explain the working principle of a **smart antenna**. List **any four** advantages over a conventional omnidirectional antenna. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **4-element uniform linear array** with inter-element spacing $d = \lambda/2$ is excited with progressive phase $\beta = -\pi/2$ per element. Determine:
  (i) The direction of the main beam $\theta_0$. **(3 marks)**
  (ii) The first-null directions closest to the main beam. **(4 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Option A

**Part (a) — Working Principle & Analogy (7 marks)**

**Analogy — The Adjustable Spotlight:**
Imagine a **stage spotlight** operated by a sound engineer. The spotlight's *beam direction* is automatically adjusted to follow the lead actor (the desired user), while the *beamwidth* is tightened to *avoid* illuminating the chorus line behind (the interferer). Crucially, **no one physically moves** the spotlight — its motion is electronic.

**Mapping to smart antenna:**
- Theatrical stage → wireless cell
- Spotlight → electronically steered beam
- Sound engineer → DSP beamforming unit
- Lead actor → desired user
- Chorus line → co-channel interferers

**Working Principle:**
1. *N* antenna elements sample the spatial field. **(1 mark)**
2. Each element's signal is multiplied by a complex weight $w_n$. **(1 mark)**
3. The weighted sum forms the array output. **(1 mark)**
4. An adaptive algorithm (LMS / RLS) updates the weights using an error feedback loop. **(1 mark)**

**Four advantages** over an omnidirectional antenna: **(3 marks — 0.75 each)**
1. **Increased range / coverage** for the same transmit power (spatial gain).
2. **Reduced co-channel interference** through null steering.
3. **Higher spectral efficiency** (more users per Hz).
4. **Improved BER (bit-error-rate)** and link reliability through spatial diversity.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:** $N = 4$, $d = \lambda/2$, $\beta = -\pi/2$ per element.

**(i) Direction of main beam:** **(3 marks)**

The main beam occurs when the total progressive phase *vanishes*:

$$
\psi + \beta = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad kd\cos\theta_0 = -\beta
$$

Substitute $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ and $d = \lambda/2$:

$$
\frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} \cos\theta_0 = -(-\pi/2) = \pi/2
$$

$$
\pi \cos\theta_0 = \frac{\pi}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\theta_0 = 0.5
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\theta_0 = 60^\circ\;}
$$

**(ii) First-null directions:** **(4 marks)**

Nulls occur when $\sin\!\left[N(\psi + \beta)/2\right] = 0$, i.e.:

$$
N(\psi + \beta)/2 = m\pi, \quad m = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots \text{ (excluding main beam)}
$$

With $N = 4$:

$$
\psi + \beta = \frac{m\pi}{2}
$$

For $m = \pm 1$ (first nulls closest to the main beam):

$$
\psi + \beta = \pm \pi/2
$$

**Case $m = +1$:**

$$
\psi = \pi/2 - \beta = \pi/2 - (-\pi/2) = \pi
$$

$$
\frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} \cos\theta = \pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\theta = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = 0^\circ
$$

**Case $m = -1$:**

$$
\psi = -\pi/2 - (-\pi/2) = 0
$$

This is the **main beam maximum** ($0\pi$, not a null) — discard.

**Try $m = -2$:**

$$
\psi = -\pi - (-\pi/2) = -\pi/2
$$

$$
\pi \cos\theta = -\pi/2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\theta = -0.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = 120^\circ
$$

**First nulls (closest on either side of the $60^\circ$ main beam):**

$$
\boxed{\;\theta_{\text{null},1} = 0^\circ, \quad \theta_{\text{null},2} = 120^\circ\;}
$$

(Validating: the array factor $\sin\!\left[2(\psi+\beta)\right]/\sin\!\left[(\psi+\beta)/2\right]$ yields the same.) ✓

---

### **Question B — Option B** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **cocktail-party analogy** in the context of smart antennas. Why is it the most widely cited analogy? Discuss **two** engineering insights it provides. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **2-element smart-antenna** receiver has elements spaced $d = \lambda/2$ apart. The receiver measures a **phase difference** $\Delta\phi = \pi/3$ between the two branches. Determine the **direction of arrival (DOA)** $\theta$ from broadside. Comment on the effect of choosing $d > \lambda/2$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Option B

**Part (a) — Cocktail-Party Analogy (7 marks)**

**The Cocktail-Party Effect (Cherry, 1953):**
At a noisy social gathering, a listener can **focus on a single conversation** in one part of the room while simultaneously *ignoring* nearby conversations and background music. This phenomenon demonstrates *selective auditory attention*. **(2 marks)**

**Why this analogy is dominant in smart-antenna literature:**

1. **Spatial filtering is intuitive.** Everyone has experienced focusing on a friend in a noisy room — this maps directly to the *main beam* of a smart antenna. The friends we *ignore* are the *nulls*. **(1 mark)**
2. **Multiple users in the same spectrum.** Both the cocktail party and a wireless cell host *many concurrent transmissions* in overlapping frequency bands; spatial separation is the only way to disentangle them. **(1 mark)**
3. **It motivates adaptive behavior.** Just as we *tilt our head* to track a moving speaker, a smart antenna *electronically steers* its beam — emphasizing the **adaptive, real-time** nature of the system. **(1 mark)**

**Two engineering insights:**

1. **Spatial diversity gain:** Two ears (and *N* elements) provide *N*-fold SNR improvement under coherent combining. **(1 mark)**
2. **Need for beamforming algorithm:** The brain solves the cocktail-party problem using *machine-learning–like* pattern recognition; the smart antenna uses MVDR/LMS/MUSIC. **(1 mark)**

---

**Part (b) — DOA from Two-Element Baseline (7 marks)**

**Given:** $d = \lambda/2$, $\Delta\phi = \pi/3$.

**Step 1 — Use the DOA formula:**

$$
\theta = \arccos\!\left(\frac{\lambda \Delta\phi}{2\pi d}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
\theta = \arccos\!\left( \frac{\lambda \cdot \pi/3}{2\pi \cdot \lambda/2} \right) = \arccos\!\left( \frac{\pi/3}{\pi} \right) = \arccos\!\left( \frac{1}{3} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$
\theta = \arccos(0.3333) \approx 70.53^\circ
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\theta \approx 70.53^\circ \text{ from broadside}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Effect of $d > \lambda/2$:** **(2 marks)**

When $d > \lambda/2$:
- The unambiguous field of view *shrinks*: only angles for which $\vert\cos\theta\vert \le 1$ map unambiguously. The condition $\vert \lambda \Delta\phi/(2\pi d) \vert \le 1$ is violated, and **spatial aliasing** (the spatial analog of aliasing in time-sampling) occurs.
- The array develops **grating lobes** — spurious maxima at undesired angles, identical in height to the true main beam. This *destroys spatial selectivity* and is why $d = \lambda/2$ is the canonical choice. **(2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to drop the $e^{j(N-1)\psi/2}$ phase term.** In AF magnitude problems, write $\vert AF\vert$, not the complex form. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Confusing $\psi$ with $\beta$.** $\psi = kd\cos\theta$ is the *received* progressive phase; $\beta$ is the *applied* steering phase. Set $\psi + \beta = 0$ (or $\psi - \beta = 0$ depending on sign convention) for the main beam. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **In DOA problems, forgetting to take $\arccos$**, leaving the answer as $\cos\theta = 1/3$. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **In switching-beam vs adaptive questions, writing "switched-beam is better" or vice versa.** The question asks for *differentiation*, not preference. Use a side-by-side table. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Citing a non-existent reference** in the analogy answer. KTU penalizes hallucinated citations. Just give the analogy and its engineering mapping. **(−1 mark)**
> 6. **In numericals, not stating units** (radians vs. degrees) — examiners deduct 0.5 mark per instance. **(−0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Smart-Antenna Analogy (Module 3)**

- ☐ **Smart antenna = antenna array + adaptive DSP.** Always state both halves.
- ☐ The **cocktail-party analogy** maps *ears → elements, brain → DSP, head-turn → electronic steering*.
- ☐ **Two architectures:** Switched-Beam (fixed sectors, low cost) and Adaptive Array (real-time weights, high cost).
- ☐ **ULA Array Factor:** $\vert AF(\theta)\vert = \left\vert \dfrac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} \right\vert$, with $\psi = kd\cos\theta$.
- ☐ **Beam steering** uses $w_n = e^{-jn\beta}$, $\beta = kd\cos\theta_0$. Replace $\psi$ with $(\psi - \beta)$ in AF.
- ☐ **Main beam condition:** $\psi - \beta = 0 \Rightarrow \theta = \theta_0$.
- ☐ **Null condition:** $\sin(N\psi/2) = 0 \Rightarrow \psi = 2m\pi/N$, $m \ne 0$ (mod $N$).
- ☐ **Directivity (broadside, uniform, large N):** $D_0 \approx 2Nd/\lambda$.
- ☐ **Grating-lobe-free spacing:** $d < \lambda / (1 + \vert\sin\theta_0\vert)$.
- ☐ **Two-element DOA:** $\theta = \arccos(\lambda\Delta\phi / 2\pi d)$.
- ☐ **Optimal weight (MVDR):** $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mu \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{a}(\theta_0)$.
- ☐ **LMS update rule:** $\mathbf{w}(k+1) = \mathbf{w}(k) + \mu e(k) \mathbf{x}^{*}(k)$.
- ☐ **SNR gain from N elements (coherent combining):** $10\log_{10}(N)$ dB.
- ☐ **3-dB beamwidth (broadside):** $\theta_{3dB} \approx 0.886\lambda/(Nd\cos\theta_0)$.
- ☐ **Standard spacing:** $d = \lambda/2$ — memorize this; it appears in 80% of array problems.
- ☐ **Real-world relevance:** 5G Massive MIMO, military phased arrays, adaptive Wi-Fi (802.11ax).
- ☐ **Examiner favourite:** the *analogy* + the *formula table* + the *steered-AF numerical* — practice these three together.

> **End of Notes — Smart-Antenna Analogy (PEECT868, Module 3, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart Antennas' Benefits and Drawbacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Smart Antennas' Benefits and Drawbacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Smart Antenna** is a multi-element antenna system (typically an array) that intelligently combines the outputs of (or inputs to) multiple antenna elements with sophisticated **adaptive signal processing algorithms** to dynamically optimize its radiation/reception pattern in real-time. The array steers its main beam toward desired signals (users) while simultaneously forming nulls in the directions of unwanted signals (interferers) and noise sources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A smart antenna is a phased array system that exploits the spatial (angular) dimension to improve system performance. The "smart" aspect arises from the embedded **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)** engine that automatically adjusts the **complex weights** $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N]^T$ applied to each array element.

In the KTU 2024 module context, smart antennas are classified into two principal architectures:

| Type | Beamforming Strategy | Adaptability |
|---|---|---|
| **Switched Beam (SBA)** | Selects one of N pre-defined fixed beams | Discrete switching |
| **Adaptive Array (AA)** | Continuously synthesizes arbitrary pattern using DSP | Fully adaptive, real-time |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Hearing Assistant" in a Crowded Restaurant** 🍽️
>
> Imagine you are sitting in a noisy restaurant trying to listen to your friend across the table:
> - **Conventional Omni Antenna** is like having ears that hear *everything equally* — your friend, the kitchen noise, the table beside, the music — all jumbled together. Your brain (the receiver) gets exhausted trying to filter.
> - **Switched Beam Antenna** is like turning your head in **discrete directions** (North, South, East, West) and picking the loudest direction. Useful, but limited to fixed sectors.
> - **Adaptive Smart Antenna** is like having **superhuman ears with active noise-cancellation**: the ears automatically tilt toward your friend's voice AND create a "cone of silence" pointed at the loud guy at the next table. Even if your friend moves, the ears track him instantly.
>
> The **DSP brain** of the smart antenna is what performs this continuous tracking and null-steering — that's where the "intelligence" lives.

### Key Performance Metrics (Standard KTU Metrics)

The principal figure-of-merit by which smart antenna benefits are quantified is the **Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio (SINR)**:

$$\text{SINR} = \frac{P_{\text{desired}}}{\sum_{k=1}^{K} P_{\text{interferer},k} + P_{\text{noise}}}$$

where $P_{\text{desired}}$ is the received power from the intended user, $P_{\text{interferer},k}$ is the power from the $k$-th co-channel interferer, and $P_{\text{noise}}$ is the thermal noise power. Smart antennas maximize this ratio by spatially filtering interferers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Logic — How a Smart Antenna "Decides"

The smart antenna operates as a closed-loop adaptive system. The logic flow in every adaptation cycle is:

1. **Snapshot Acquisition:** The antenna array samples the incoming RF signals at each of the $N$ elements, forming the **input vector** $\mathbf{x}(t) = [x_1(t), x_2(t), \ldots, x_N(t)]^T \in \mathbb{C}^{N \times 1}$.
2. **Reference / Training Generation:** A reference signal $d(t)$ (known training sequence or decision-directed estimate) defines what the "desired" signal looks like.
3. **Weight Computation:** An adaptive algorithm (LMS, RLS, Sample Matrix Inversion, etc.) iteratively updates the complex weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ to satisfy an optimality criterion (e.g., **MMSE** — Minimum Mean Square Error).
4. **Beamforming / Combining:** The array output is formed as a linear combination:
$$y(t) = \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{x}(t) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n^* \, x_n(t)$$
where $H$ denotes Hermitian (conjugate transpose). The beam pattern $B(\theta,\phi)$ is shaped by these weights.
5. **Continuous Tracking:** The loop re-iterates at the symbol/frame rate to track moving users and changing interference.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this "smart"?** Because step 3 happens *automatically and continuously* — there is no human-in-the-loop. The antenna literally *learns* the radio environment in real-time.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Array Output | $y(t) = \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{x}(t)$ | Beamformed signal |
| 2 | SINR (Output) | $\text{SINR}_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{\sigma_s^2 \vert \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{a}(\theta_s) \vert^2}{\mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{R}_{i+n} \mathbf{w}}$ | Measures interference suppression |
| 3 | Optimum Weight (MMSE) | $\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1} \mathbf{r}_{xd}$ | Wiener-Hopf solution |
| 4 | Correlation Matrix | $\mathbf{R}_{xx} = E\{\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^H\}$ | Captures spatial statistics |
| 5 | Steering Vector | $\mathbf{a}(\theta) = [1, e^{j\beta d \cos\theta}, \ldots, e^{j(N-1)\beta d \cos\theta}]^T$ | Geometry of array |
| 6 | Shannon Capacity Gain | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SINR})$ bps | Throughput improvement |
| 7 | Interference Reduction Factor | $\text{IRF} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{SINR}_{\text{smart}}}{\text{SINR}_{\text{omni}}}\right)$ dB | Direct benefit metric |
| 8 | DOF (Degrees of Freedom) | $\text{DOF} = N - 1$ | Max nulls that can be placed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** The **MMSE weight equation** $\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1} \mathbf{r}_{xd}$ is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question**. Know the derivation of the Wiener-Hopf equation and the meaning of $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$ and $\mathbf{r}_{xd}$.

### Benefits of Smart Antennas — The Engineering "Why"

The benefits derive from the ability to exploit the **spatial dimension** that conventional antennas waste.

- **Spatial Filtering / Interference Suppression:** Places deep nulls (typically $-30$ to $-50$ dB) at interferer directions. Can null up to $N-1$ interferers for an $N$-element array.
- **Increased System Capacity:** In cellular systems, frequency reuse distance can shrink because spatial beams isolate users in the same frequency channel. Capacity gains of **$N$-fold to $4N$-fold** are typical.
- **Improved SINR & BER:** Output SINR rises by 6–10 dB (often more) over omni antennas, lowering Bit Error Rate.
- **Extended Range & Coverage:** Array gain of $10 \log_{10}(N)$ dB effectively increases EIRP and receive sensitivity.
- **Reduced Transmit Power:** Because the beam is focused, the same link budget is achieved with **lower PA power** → green, energy-efficient base stations.
- **Multipath Mitigation:** Smart antennas resolve spatially-distinct multipath components, combating Rayleigh fading through spatial diversity.
- **SDMA (Space Division Multiple Access):** Multiple users on the **same frequency, same time, same code** — separated purely by their angle-of-arrival. This is impossible with a single omni antenna.
- **Co-existence & Spectrum Sharing:** Smart nulls allow secondary users to share spectrum with primary users without harmful interference.

### Drawbacks of Smart Antennas — The Engineering "Cost"

The "smartness" comes at tangible cost and complexity.

- **Hardware Cost & Complexity:** Requires $N$ RF chains, $N$ ADCs/DACs (often at high sampling rates), calibration circuitry. Cost scales with $N$.
- **Computational Load:** Real-time matrix inversion ($\mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}$) is $O(N^3)$. With $N = 8$–$16$ at cellular symbol rates, **DSP power consumption is non-trivial**.
- **Calibration Sensitivity:** Even small phase/amplitude errors across RF chains corrupt the beam pattern. Periodic calibration is mandatory.
- **Channel State Information (CSI) Dependence:** Adaptive weights assume an accurate $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$. In fast-fading or high-mobility channels, weights may lag behind the actual channel.
- **Reference Signal Requirement:** Training-based algorithms (LMS, RLS) consume bandwidth; blind algorithms are slower to converge.
- **Physical Size:** For half-wavelength spacing, an 8-element linear array spans $3.5\lambda$ — bulky at UHF/lower bands.
- **Mutual Coupling:** Closely-spaced elements couple electromagnetically, distorting both element patterns and the assumed steering vector $\mathbf{a}(\theta)$.
- **Deployment Skill:** Network planning, initial installation, and troubleshooting require specialized RF/DSP expertise — *opex* is higher.
- **Algorithm Convergence Issues:** LMS is simple but slow; RLS is fast but numerically unstable; each algorithm is a compromise.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often answer "cost" as a single vague drawback. Examiners expect **layered reasoning**: cost is a *symptom*; the *root causes* are hardware complexity, calibration, DSP load, and CSI acquisition. Always name the *technical* sub-issue.

### Real-World Utility

Smart antennas are now mainstream:
- **4G LTE / 5G NR:** Every modern base station uses $4\times4$, $8\times8$, or $64$-element **MIMO** arrays — a direct descendant of smart antenna theory.
- **Military RADAR & EW:** Phased-array radars (AN/TPY-2, Aegis) use adaptive nulling to suppress jamming.
- **Satcom on the move:** Beam-steerable flat-panel terminals (Starlink, in-flight Wi-Fi) are essentially smart antenna arrays.
- **Wi-Fi 6/6E/7:** MU-MIMO APs use smart antenna principles to serve multiple clients simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — From the MMSE Criterion to the Wiener-Hopf Weight Equation

This derivation is a **guaranteed KTU 14-mark sub-question**. We derive the optimal weight vector that minimises the mean-square error between the array output $y(t)$ and a desired reference $d(t)$.

**Step 1 — Define the Cost Function (MSE).**

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\{\, \vert e(t) \vert^2 \,\} = E\{\, \vert d(t) - y(t) \vert^2 \,\}$$

**Step 2 — Expand the cost function.**

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\{d(t) d^*(t)\} - E\{d(t) y^*(t)\} - E\{d^*((t)) y(t)\} + E\{y(t) y^*(t)\}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $y(t) = \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{x}(t)$.**

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\{\vert d(t) \vert^2\} - E\{d(t) \mathbf{x}^H(t)\}\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{w}^H E\{\mathbf{x}(t) d^*(t)\} + \mathbf{w}^H E\{\mathbf{x}(t) \mathbf{x}^H(t)\}\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 4 — Define the second-order statistics.**

$$E\{ \mathbf{x}(t) \mathbf{x}^H(t) \} = \mathbf{R}_{xx} \quad \text{(autocorrelation matrix, } N \times N\text{)}$$

$$E\{ \mathbf{x}(t) d^*(t) \} = \mathbf{r}_{xd} \quad \text{(cross-correlation vector, } N \times 1\text{)}$$

**Step 5 — Rewrite the cost in compact matrix form.**

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{r}_{xd}^H \mathbf{w} - \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{r}_{xd} + \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{R}_{xx} \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 6 — Set the gradient to zero for optimality.**

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = -\mathbf{r}_{xd} + \mathbf{R}_{xx} \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 7 — Solve the Wiener-Hopf equation.**

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1} \, \mathbf{r}_{xd}}$$

This is the **optimum MMSE weight vector** — the heart of every adaptive smart antenna.

### Derivation 2 — Output SINR After Adaptive Beamforming

**Step 1 — Define the input correlation matrix decomposed into signal, interference, and noise components.**

$$\mathbf{R}_{xx} = \sigma_s^2 \, \mathbf{a}(\theta_s)\mathbf{a}^H(\theta_s) + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sigma_{i,k}^2 \, \mathbf{a}(\theta_k)\mathbf{a}^H(\theta_k) + \sigma_n^2 \mathbf{I}$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the output power into signal, interference, and noise terms.**

$$P_{\text{out}} = \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{R}_{xx} \mathbf{w} = P_s + P_i + P_n$$

**Step 3 — Express each term.**

$$P_s = \sigma_s^2 \vert \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{a}(\theta_s) \vert^2$$

$$P_i = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sigma_{i,k}^2 \vert \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{a}(\theta_k) \vert^2$$

$$P_n = \sigma_n^2 \, \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 4 — Form the SINR.**

$$\boxed{\text{SINR}_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{\sigma_s^2 \vert \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{a}(\theta_s) \vert^2}{\sum_{k=1}^{K} \sigma_{i,k}^2 \vert \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{a}(\theta_k) \vert^2 + \sigma_n^2 \, \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{w}}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.** The numerator is maximised when $\mathbf{w}$ aligns with $\mathbf{a}(\theta_s)$. The denominator is minimised when $\mathbf{w}$ is orthogonal to every interferer steering vector $\mathbf{a}(\theta_k)$. The optimum $\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}}$ trades off these two effects.

### Analytical Matrix — Benefits vs Drawbacks (Comparison Table)

| Engineering Dimension | **Benefit** of Smart Antenna | **Drawback** of Smart Antenna |
|---|---|---|
| **Spectrum Efficiency** | $N$-fold capacity gain via SDMA | Requires clean channel estimation to realize |
| **Hardware** | Spatial gain without more spectrum | $N$ RF chains, $N$ ADCs → expensive |
| **Signal Quality (SINR/BER)** | $+6$ to $+10$ dB SINR gain; nulls $-30$ to $-50$ dB | Performance degrades with mutual coupling |
| **Power** | Lower PA power per link | High DSP power for matrix inversion |
| **Mobility Support** | Tracks moving users in real-time | Fast fading can outpace weight update |
| **Size / Form Factor** | Flat-panel possible (e.g., 5G base stations) | Half-$\lambda$ spacing → bulky at low freq. |
| **Multipath** | Spatial diversity combats fading | Steering-vector mismatch in rich scattering |
| **Cost & OpEx** | Long-term capacity revenue | CapEx (hardware) + OpEx (skilled engineers) |
| **Co-existence** | Clean spectrum sharing via nulls | Regulatory/CSI transparency requirements |
| **Algorithm** | Many proven adaptive algorithms | LMS slow, RLS unstable, CMA non-convex |

### Numerical Example — Capacity Gain from Smart Antenna

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU-style):** A cellular base station uses an omni antenna and serves a user with SINR of **$10$ dB** (= 10 linear). An 8-element smart antenna array improves the SINR by **$9$ dB** (typical for $N=8$). Calculate the new capacity and the percentage increase, assuming bandwidth $B = 200$ kHz.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert SINR to linear scale.**

$$\text{SINR}_{\text{old}} = 10^{10/10} = 10$$

$$\text{SINR}_{\text{new}} = 10 \times 10^{9/10} = 10 \times 7.943 = 79.43$$

**Step 2 — Compute old capacity (Shannon).**

$$C_{\text{old}} = 200{,}000 \times \log_2(1 + 10) = 200{,}000 \times \log_2(11)$$

$$\log_2(11) = \dfrac{\log_{10}(11)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \dfrac{1.0414}{0.3010} \approx 3.4594 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

$$C_{\text{old}} \approx 200{,}000 \times 3.4594 \approx 691{,}880 \text{ bps} \approx 692 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 — Compute new capacity.**

$$C_{\text{new}} = 200{,}000 \times \log_2(1 + 79.43) = 200{,}000 \times \log_2(80.43)$$

$$\log_2(80.43) = \dfrac{\log_{10}(80.43)}{0.3010} = \dfrac{1.9054}{0.3010} \approx 6.330 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

$$C_{\text{new}} \approx 200{,}000 \times 6.330 \approx 1{,}266{,}000 \text{ bps} \approx 1.266 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage capacity increase.**

$$\%\text{Gain} = \dfrac{C_{\text{new}} - C_{\text{old}}}{C_{\text{old}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.266 - 0.692}{0.692} \times 100 \approx 83\%$$

A 9 dB SINR improvement yields approximately **$83\%$ more capacity** for the same bandwidth — illustrating the *commercial* benefit of smart antennas.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 — Smart Antenna System Architecture (Adaptive Array)

The Mermaid block below maps the full functional architecture of an adaptive smart antenna system — the "engine" that delivers the benefits discussed in Section 2.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RF_FRONT_END["RF FRONT END"]
        ant1["ANTENNA ELEMENT 1"]
        ant2["ANTENNA ELEMENT 2"]
        antN["ANTENNA ELEMENT N"]
    end

    subgraph LNA_CHAIN["LNA AND DOWN CONVERSION"]
        lna1["LNA FILTER MIXER"]
        lna2["LNA FILTER MIXER"]
        lnaN["LNA FILTER MIXER"]
    end

    subgraph ADC_BLOCK["ADC SUBSYSTEM"]
        adc1["HIGH SPEED ADC"]
        adc2["HIGH SPEED ADC"]
        adcN["HIGH SPEED ADC"]
    end

    subgraph DSP_CORE["ADAPTIVE DSP CORE"]
        snap["SNAPSHOT VECTOR X OF T"]
        est["ESTIMATE R_XX AND R_XD"]
        alg["ADAPTIVE ALGORITHM LMS RLS SMI"]
        wgt["COMPLEX WEIGHT VECTOR W"]
        bf["BEAMFORM OUTPUT Y OF T"]
    end

    subgraph DECIS_BLOCK["DECISION AND OUTPUT"]
        demod["DEMODULATOR"]
        detect["BIT DETECTOR"]
        out["RECOVERED DATA"]
    end

    ant1 --> lna1 --> adc1 --> snap
    ant2 --> lna2 --> adc2 --> snap
    antN --> lnaN --> adcN --> snap
    snap --> est --> alg --> wgt --> bf
    bf --> demod --> detect --> out
    wgt -.feedback.-> alg
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each antenna element produces a voltage that is amplified, down-converted, and digitised. The DSP core assembles the snapshot vector $\mathbf{x}(t)$, estimates the spatial statistics, runs the adaptive algorithm, and produces the weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ that *shapes the beam*. The beamformed output is demodulated into bits.

### Diagram 2 — Switched Beam vs Adaptive Array (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["SMART ANTENNA SYSTEM"]
    q1{"DO YOU NEED REAL TIME INTERFERENCE NULLING?"}
    sba["SWITCHED BEAM ANTENNA SBA"]
    aa["ADAPTIVE ARRAY ANTENNA AA"]
    q2{"IS THE CHANNEL STATIONARY AND DOAS KNOWN?"}
    q3{"IS THE DOA OF INTERFERERS UNKNOWN OR TIME VARYING?"}
    sba1["SELECT BEST OF N FIXED BEAMS"]
    sba2["SWITCHING NETWORK LOWER COMPLEXITY"]
    aa1["DSP COMPUTES W IN REAL TIME"]
    aa2["NULLS PLACED AUTOMATICALLY"]
    aa3["HIGHER COST AND DSP LOAD"]

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|NO NEED JUST COVERAGE| sba
    q1 -->|YES NEED DEEP NULLS| aa
    sba --> q2
    q2 -->|YES FIXED USERS| sba1
    q2 -->|NO MOBILE USERS| sba2
    aa --> q3
    q3 -->|YES MOST REALISTIC| aa1
    aa1 --> aa2
    aa1 --> aa3
    q3 -->|NO RARE IN PRACTICE| aa2
```

### Diagram 3 — Benefits / Drawbacks Trade-off Radar

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BENEFITS["BENEFITS DOMAIN"]
        b1["SPATIAL FILTERING"]
        b2["CAPACITY GAIN SDMA"]
        b3["SINR AND RANGE"]
        b4["MULTIPATH MITIGATION"]
        b5["LOWER TX POWER"]
    end
    subgraph TRADE["TRADE OFF DOMAIN"]
        t1["HARDWARE COST N RF CHAINS"]
        t2["DSP LOAD O OF N CUBED"]
        t3["CALIBRATION OVERHEAD"]
        t4["CSI AND MOBILITY"]
        t5["PHYSICAL SIZE AND COUPLING"]
    end
    subgraph OUTCOME["ENGINEERING OUTCOME"]
        o1["WIDELY ADOPTED IN 4G 5G MIMO"]
        o2["STANDARD IN MILITARY RADAR"]
        o3["ENABLES SDMA IN WI FI 6E 7"]
    end

    b1 --> o1
    b2 --> o1
    b3 --> o1
    b4 --> o2
    b5 --> o3
    t1 --> o1
    t2 --> o1
    t3 --> o2
    t4 --> o3
    t5 --> o2
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip on Diagrams:** When asked "explain benefits/drawbacks," a labelled block diagram of the smart antenna system (similar to Diagram 1) earns easy 2–3 marks even if your prose is incomplete. Always include the *DSP core* and the *feedback loop* in your drawing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Define a smart antenna. Mention any two of its advantages over a conventional antenna.

**Model Answer:**

A **smart antenna** is an antenna array with embedded digital signal processing that adaptively adjusts the complex weights of its elements to dynamically steer its beam toward desired signals and place nulls at interferers, in real time.

**Two advantages:**

1. **Spatial Filtering / Interference Suppression:** Places deep directional nulls (up to $N-1$ simultaneous nulls for an $N$-element array), which is impossible with a single omni antenna.
2. **Increased Capacity through SDMA:** Allows multiple users to share the same frequency, time slot, and code, separated only by their angle-of-arrival, yielding $N$-fold capacity gain.

*(Acceptable 2-mark answer: definition + any 2 advantages; the third mark is for clear phrasing.)*

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample]** List any three drawbacks of smart antenna systems.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Hardware complexity and cost:** Requires $N$ RF chains, $N$ ADCs/DACs, calibration circuits, and a high-performance DSP — CapEx scales with $N$.
2. **Computational load:** Real-time matrix inversion and adaptive algorithms (RLS, LMS) are $O(N^3)$ to $O(N^2)$ per update, demanding significant DSP power.
3. **Sensitivity to calibration and channel estimation errors:** Phase/amplitude mismatches across RF chains and rapidly time-varying channels degrade the assumed steering vector and corrupt beamforming.

*(Acceptable alternative third drawback: mutual coupling between elements, physical bulk at low frequencies, or slow convergence of training-based algorithms.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When answering the long question on smart antennas, students commonly lose marks in three places:
> 1. Writing only "cost" or "complexity" as a drawback — *always* name the *specific* technical sub-issue (calibration, mutual coupling, CSI acquisition, DSP load, convergence speed).
> 2. Confusing **switched beam** with **adaptive array** — they are *not* the same. SBA uses *fixed* beams; AA uses *continuously computed* weights.
> 3. Forgetting to mention that smart antenna benefits require **accurate channel estimation** — without this qualifier, the answer is marked incomplete.

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Adaptive Beamforming Focus **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an adaptive smart antenna system. Label the DSP core and feedback path. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the optimum weight vector $\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}}$ that minimises the mean-square error $E\{|e(t)|^2\}$ between the array output and a reference signal. State the final Wiener-Hopf equation and the physical meaning of $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$ and $\mathbf{r}_{xd}$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**

Refer to **Diagram 1** in Section 4. The explanation must include:

- **Antenna array stage:** $N$ identical elements (linear or planar) receiving signals $\mathbf{x}(t) = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N]^T$.
- **RF front-end:** Each element feeds a Low-Noise Amplifier (LNA), bandpass filter, and down-conversion mixer to bring the RF to baseband/IF.
- **ADC subsystem:** $N$ high-speed Analog-to-Digital Converters digitise the baseband I/Q samples.
- **DSP core (the "smart" part):** Forms the snapshot vector, estimates the autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$ and cross-correlation vector $\mathbf{r}_{xd}$, and runs the adaptive algorithm (LMS/RLS/SMI) to compute weights.
- **Weight application:** Complex weights $w_n$ are applied to each branch and summed to form $y(t) = \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{x}(t)$.
- **Feedback loop:** A decision-directed or training-based reference $d(t)$ closes the loop. The error $e(t) = d(t) - y(t)$ drives the next weight update.
- **Output stage:** Demodulation, detection, and recovered data.

*Examiner's note:* A clear diagram with the DSP feedback loop labelled earns full marks. Students who omit the feedback path lose 2 marks.

**(b) Derivation of the Wiener-Hopf Equation (7 marks):**

The cost function is:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\{ \vert d(t) - \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{x}(t) \vert^2 \}$$

Expanding:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{r}_{xd} - \mathbf{r}_{xd}^H \mathbf{w} + \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{R}_{xx} \mathbf{w}$$

Setting the gradient $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = -2\mathbf{r}_{xd} + 2\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}$ yields:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1} \mathbf{r}_{xd}}$$

**Physical meaning:**

- $\mathbf{R}_{xx} = E\{\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^H\}$ is the $N \times N$ spatial correlation matrix. It captures the second-order statistics of the *combined* signal, interference, and noise environment.
- $\mathbf{r}_{xd} = E\{\mathbf{x} d^*\}$ is the $N \times 1$ cross-correlation vector between the array input and the desired reference — it encodes *where* the desired signal is, in the spatial sense.

The weight vector $\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}}$ therefore simultaneously (i) correlates strongly with the desired signal direction and (ii) decorrelates from interference-plus-noise directions.

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Benefits vs Drawbacks Trade-off Focus **[KTU University Exam – Sample, Module 3]**

**(a)** Discuss the **benefits** of smart antenna systems over conventional antennas in wireless communication. Substantiate your answer with quantitative justification (e.g., array gain, capacity gain). *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **drawbacks** of smart antenna systems. For each drawback, suggest a standard mitigation technique used in practice. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Benefits (7 marks):**

1. **Array Gain:** For an $N$-element array, the coherent combining gives an SNR gain of $10 \log_{10}(N)$ dB at the output. For $N = 8$, this is $10 \log_{10}(8) \approx 9$ dB.
2. **Interference Suppression:** Up to $N - 1$ deep nulls can be placed. This raises output SINR by 6–10 dB in typical urban interference scenarios.
3. **Capacity Gain (SDMA):** Through spatial multiplexing, $N$ users can share the same time-frequency-code resource. Capacity scales as $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SINR})$, so a 9 dB SINR gain yields ~$83\%$ throughput increase (as shown in the worked example in Section 3).
4. **Multipath Mitigation:** Spatial diversity gain combats Rayleigh fading — BER can drop by 1–2 orders of magnitude at the cell edge.
5. **Lower Transmit Power:** Because the beam is focused, the required PA power at the base station is reduced by $10 \log_{10}(N)$ dB for the same link budget.
6. **Extended Range / Coverage:** Cell radius can grow by 50–80% for the same EIRP.

**(b) Drawbacks and Mitigations (7 marks):**

| Drawback | Mitigation in Practice |
|---|---|
| **Hardware cost (N RF chains)** | Use **hybrid beamforming** (analog + digital) in 5G; share RF chains via sub-arrays |
| **DSP load $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$** | Use **partial adaptation** (e.g., diagonal loading), reduced-rank methods, or systolic array processors |
| **Calibration drift** | Built-in **self-calibration routines** with pilot symbols; temperature-compensated analog front-ends |
| **CSI estimation errors** | Use **robust beamforming** (diagonal loading of $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$); exploit training-aided + decision-directed feedback |
| **Mutual coupling** | Use **decoupling networks** or **mutual coupling matrix** $\mathbf{C}$ in the steering vector: $\tilde{\mathbf{a}} = \mathbf{C}^{-1}\mathbf{a}$ |
| **Slow convergence (LMS) / instability (RLS)** | Use **variable-step LMS**, **normalized LMS**, or **leaky LMS**; switch to **recursive least squares (QR-decomposition)** for stability |
| **Physical size at low frequencies** | Use **smart antenna modules** with integrated RFICs; exploit **higher modes / dielectric loading** to shrink $\lambda/2$ spacing |

*Examiner's note:* Award full marks only if *each* drawback is paired with a *specific* mitigation, not a generic statement.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Smart antenna** = adaptive antenna array + real-time DSP. The "smart" lives in the **weight-update algorithm**, not the metal.
- [ ] **Two architectures:** **Switched Beam** (discrete, cheaper) vs **Adaptive Array** (continuous, more powerful). KTU questions often test the difference.
- [ ] **The Wiener-Hopf equation** $\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1} \mathbf{r}_{xd}$ is *the* core formula. Memorise the derivation — it appears every semester.
- [ ] **Array gain** = $10 \log_{10}(N)$ dB. **SINR gain** in practice = 6–10 dB. **Capacity gain** scales with $\log_2(1 + \text{SINR})$.
- [ ] **Degrees of freedom** = $N - 1$ = max number of interferers that can be nulled simultaneously.
- [ ] **Top 4 benefits** to memorise: spatial filtering, capacity (SDMA), SINR/BER improvement, multipath mitigation.
- [ ] **Top 5 drawbacks** to memorise (with technical depth, not vague "cost"): hardware (N RF chains), DSP load $O(N^3)$, calibration drift, CSI/mobility sensitivity, mutual coupling.
- [ ] **Mutual coupling** is the *most-skipped* drawback in KTU answers — it is a *favourite* examiner's follow-up.
- [ ] **Shannon capacity** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SINR})$ is the quantitative link between beamforming benefit and data rate.
- [ ] **Real-world deployment:** 5G massive MIMO, military phased-array radar, satellite comms-on-the-move, Wi-Fi 6/7 MU-MIMO.
- [ ] **Algorithm trade-off triangle:** LMS (simple, slow) ↔ RLS (fast, unstable) ↔ CMA (blind, non-convex). Know one strength + one weakness of each.
- [ ] Always **draw the block diagram** (RF chain → ADC → DSP core → weight update → output) — it earns easy marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Antenna Beamforming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Antenna Beamforming ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) **Antenna Beamforming** (also called **spatial filtering** or **smart antenna processing**) is a signal processing technique employed with an array of antenna elements to produce a directional radiation pattern (beam) that can be electronically steered toward a desired direction, while simultaneously suppressing interference and noise arriving from other directions. It is the foundational principle behind **phased arrays**, **smart antennas**, and **MIMO (Multiple-Input Multiple-Output)** systems used in 5G, radar, sonar, and satellite communications. Mathematically, beamforming is the operation of coherently combining signals from multiple antenna elements (with appropriate amplitude and phase weights) such that signals from a specific angle add constructively, while signals from other angles add destructively. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** The university expects students to master the **Array Factor (AF)**, **Progressive Phase Shift ($\beta$)**, **Electronic Beam Steering Equation**, and the design equations of **N-element linear arrays** (broadside vs. end-fire). These are guaranteed topics for the End Semester Examination (ESE). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bucket Brigade" Imagine a row of people (antenna elements) standing on a beach trying to listen for a swimmer in the ocean (desired signal). If everyone shouts what they hear at slightly different times, the sounds will mix into meaningless noise. But if each person delays their report *precisely* so that the report from the swimmer's direction arrives at the listening station **at the exact same moment**, the messages will combine into one powerful, clear voice. That synchronized "delay-and-add" is **beamforming**. - **Earlier delay** (wave arrives from left) → beam tilts to the **left**. - **No delay** (wave arrives straight ahead) → beam points **forward** (broadside). - **Later delay** (wave arrives from right) → beam tilts to the **right**. By electronically adjusting these delays (or equivalently, the phase shifts), we can sweep the antenna's "ear" across the horizon in microseconds — without physically moving the antenna. > [!NOTE] > **Why It Matters:** A single antenna radiates a fixed, broad pattern. Beamforming allows us to *dynamically reshape and steer* this pattern electronically. This is the cornerstone of modern 5G Massive MIMO base stations, military phased-array radars (e.g., Aegis), and even consumer Wi-Fi routers. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded) The governing physical constant in beamforming is the **wavelength ($\lambda$)** measured in meters, which determines the element spacing. The key performance metrics students must memorize are: - **Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW)**: The angular width of the main lobe between its -3 dB points, typically approximated as $\approx 0.886 \cdot \lambda / (N \cdot d)$ for an $N$-element uniform array. - **First Null Beamwidth (FNBW)**: $\approx 2\lambda / (N \cdot d)$ for a broadside uniform linear array. - **Directivity ($D$)**: Ratio of radiation intensity in the desired direction to the average; for a uniform linear array, $D \approx 2N \cdot d / \lambda$. - **Side-Lobe Level (SLL)**: Measured in **dB** below the main-lobe peak, ideally $\leq -13$ dB for uniform excitation. > [!TIP] > **Examiner Insight:** When asked to "design" a beamformer, always state: (1) number of elements $N$, (2) spacing $d$ (typically $d = \lambda/2$), (3) progressive phase shift $\beta$, and (4) steering angle $\theta_0$. These four parameters fully define any uniform linear array (ULA). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic The beamforming operation is built upon three pillars: **(1) Geometric Setup**, **(2) Progressive Phase**, and **(3) Array Factor Construction**. **Step 1 — Geometry of an N-Element Linear Array** Consider $N$ identical isotropic radiators placed along the z-axis with uniform spacing $d$ between adjacent elements. Each element is fed with the same amplitude $A$ but a progressively advancing phase $\beta$ (radians). The $n$-th element ($n = 0, 1, 2, ..., N-1$) is located at position $z_n = n \cdot d$. **Step 2 — Path Difference & Phase Difference** For a far-field observation point at angle $\theta$ (measured from the broadside direction, i.e., perpendicular to the array axis), the wave arriving at the $n$-th element travels an extra path compared to the reference (0-th) element. The path difference is $\Delta = n \cdot d \cdot \sin\theta$, corresponding to a phase difference of: $$\psi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} d \sin\theta$$ **Step 3 — Element Excitation (Weighting)** Each element $n$ is excited with a complex weight $w_n = A \cdot e^{j n \beta}$, where $\beta$ is the **progressive phase shift** applied electronically to steer the beam. **Step 4 — Total Far-Field (Array Factor)** By the principle of superposition, the total radiated field in the far zone is proportional to the sum of all weighted contributions: $$AF = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} w_n \cdot e^{j n \psi} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} A \cdot e^{j n (\psi + \beta)}$$ This sum is a **finite geometric series**, which we will derive in full in Section 3. **Step 5 — Beam Steering Condition (The Heart of Beamforming)** The principal maximum (main beam) occurs when all the terms in the sum add *in phase*, which requires: $$\psi + \beta = 0 \pmod{2\pi}$$ Substituting $\psi = (2\pi/\lambda) d \sin\theta$ and solving for the steering angle $\theta_0$: $$\boxed{\beta = -\frac{2\pi}{\lambda} d \sin\theta_0}$$ By changing $\beta$ electronically, we change $\theta_0$ — the beam electronically sweeps across space. **Step 6 — Grating Lobe Suppression** To prevent unwanted secondary maxima (grating lobes) from forming, the inter-element spacing must satisfy: $$d < \frac{\lambda}{1 + \vert \sin\theta_0 \vert}$$ For broadside ($\theta_0 = 90°$), this reduces to $d < \lambda$. The standard engineering choice is **$d = \lambda / 2$**, which guarantees no grating lobes for any scan angle up to $\pm 90°$. ### 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Reference) > [!NOTE] > **The table below is the "cheat sheet" you should memorize for the KTU ESE.** Every formula here has appeared in past KTU examinations for this course. | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | |---------------|-------------|-----------------|-----------| | Array Factor (Magnitude, Normalized) | $\vert AF \vert = \dfrac{\sin(N \psi'/2)}{N \sin(\psi'/2)}$ | Sum of $N$ weighted contributions | Dimensionless | | Phase term in AF | $\psi' = \beta + (2\pi d / \lambda)\cos\theta$ (z-axis array) | Total phase between adjacent elements | Radians | | Steering Equation | $\beta = -\dfrac{2\pi d}{\lambda} \cos\theta_0$ | Required progressive phase for steering to $\theta_0$ | Radians | | Main Lobe Condition | $\psi' = 0 \pmod{2\pi}$ | Condition for principal maximum | Radians | | Null Locations | $\psi' = \pm \dfrac{2m\pi}{N}, \quad m = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ (excluding multiples of $N$) | Angles of zero radiation | Radians | | Grating Lobe Condition | $\psi' = \pm 2m\pi$ | Avoided by choosing $d \le \lambda$ | Radians | | HPBW (Broadside ULA) | $\approx 0.886 \dfrac{\lambda}{N d}$ | Approximate half-power beamwidth | Radians | | Directivity (Uniform ULA) | $D_0 \approx 2 \dfrac{N d}{\lambda}$ | For $d = \lambda/2$, gives $D_0 \approx N$ | Dimensionless | | Broadside Condition | $\beta = 0$ | Main beam at $\theta = 90°$ (perpendicular) | Radians | | End-Fire Condition | $\beta = -\pi$ (ordinary) or $-\pi + \dfrac{2.94}{N}$ (Hansen-Woodyard) | Main beam along the array axis | Radians | | First Null Beamwidth (FNBW) | $\approx 2 \dfrac{\lambda}{N d}$ | Angle between first nulls | Radians | | Binomial Amplitude Weights | $w_n = \binom{N-1}{n}$ | Used to eliminate all side lobes | Dimensionless | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **5G Massive MIMO Base Stations:** Modern 5G base stations use **64 to 256 element planar arrays** with digital beamforming. The base station forms a narrow beam pointed directly at each user's smartphone, then tracks them as they move. This **boosts signal strength by 20–30 dB** and **reduces interference to other users**, enabling 5G's gigabit speeds. > > **Military Phased-Array Radars:** Systems like the AN/SPY-1 (Aegis Cruiser Radar) have thousands of elements forming beams that swing across the sky in milliseconds to track hundreds of supersonic missiles simultaneously — without any mechanical rotation. > > **Medical Ultrasound:** Beamforming is used to focus ultrasonic energy at a precise depth in tissue, enabling high-resolution imaging of internal organs. ### 2.4 Types of Beamforming (KTU Sub-Topic) 1. **Fixed (Switched) Beamforming:** A finite set of pre-defined beams is switched between, based on which beam has the strongest signal. Simple and cheap, but with limited accuracy. 2. **Adaptive Beamforming:** Weights $w_n$ are computed in **real-time** using algorithms like **LMS (Least Mean Squares)** or **MVDR (Minimum Variance Distortionless Response)** to automatically null out interference. This is the core of "smart antennas." 3. **Hybrid Beamforming:** A mix of analog (phase shifters) and digital (baseband) beamforming used in 5G mmWave to reduce hardware cost while still allowing spatial multiplexing. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Array Factor of an N-Element Uniform Linear Array (The Single Most Important Derivation for KTU) **Given:** $N$ identical isotropic radiators spaced uniformly by distance $d$ along the z-axis. Element $n$ has complex excitation $w_n = A e^{j n \beta}$, where $A$ is the amplitude and $\beta$ is the progressive phase shift. The observation point is in the far-field at angle $\theta$ (measured from the x-axis perpendicular to the array). **Find:** The normalized Array Factor $AF(\theta)$. --- **STEP 1 — Write the far-field contribution of each element.** The element pattern is unity (isotropic). The far-field from element $n$ is proportional to its weight, multiplied by the phase delay due to the extra path from element $n$ to the observation point. $$E_n \propto w_n \cdot e^{+j k (n d \cos\theta)} = A \cdot e^{j n \beta} \cdot e^{j k n d \cos\theta}$$ We define the **element-to-element phase difference** as: $$\psi = k d \cos\theta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} d \cos\theta$$ > *Note:* Depending on the textbook convention, $\cos\theta$ may be replaced by $\sin\theta$. The KTU standard for z-axis arrays uses $\cos\theta$, while x-axis arrays use $\sin\theta$. We use $\cos\theta$ here. **Always state the convention in your answer script.** **STEP 2 — Sum all contributions to get the total Array Factor.** The total field is the sum of contributions from all $N$ elements: $$AF = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} A \cdot e^{j n (\beta + \psi)}$$ Pull out the constant $A$: $$AF = A \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left[ e^{j(\beta + \psi)} \right]^n$$ **STEP 3 — Recognize the geometric series.** The sum is a finite geometric series of the form $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} r^n$ where $r = e^{j(\beta + \psi)}$. Using the closed-form identity for a finite geometric series: $$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} r^n = \frac{1 - r^N}{1 - r}$$ Therefore: $$AF = A \cdot \frac{1 - e^{j N (\beta + \psi)}}{1 - e^{j (\beta + \psi)}}$$ **STEP 4 — Simplify using the sine identity.** Let $\Phi = \beta + \psi$. Factor out $e^{j N \Phi / 2}$ from the numerator and $e^{j \Phi / 2}$ from the denominator: $$AF = A \cdot \frac{e^{j N \Phi / 2} \left( e^{-j N \Phi / 2} - e^{j N \Phi / 2} \right)}{e^{j \Phi / 2} \left( e^{-j \Phi / 2} - e^{j \Phi / 2} \right)}$$ Using the identity $\sin(x) = (e^{jx} - e^{-jx}) / (2j)$, this becomes: $$AF = A \cdot \frac{\sin(N \Phi / 2)}{\sin(\Phi / 2)} \cdot e^{j (N-1) \Phi / 2}$$ **STEP 5 — Take the magnitude (since the pattern is the magnitude).** The magnitude of the exponential factor is unity, and the maximum of the ratio $\sin(N\Phi/2)/\sin(\Phi/2)$ is $N$ (by L'Hôpital's rule as $\Phi \to 0$). The **normalized** Array Factor is: $$\boxed{\vert AF(\theta) \vert_{\text{norm}} = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \frac{\sin\left( \frac{N}{2} \left[ \beta + \frac{2\pi d}{\lambda} \cos\theta \right] \right)}{\sin\left( \frac{1}{2} \left[ \beta + \frac{2\pi d}{\lambda} \cos\theta \right] \right)}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Maximum Value is Unity:** When $\beta + (2\pi d/\lambda)\cos\theta_0 = 0$, the array factor evaluates to $1$ (the principal maximum). --- ### 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Steering Equation $\beta = -(2\pi d / \lambda) \cos\theta_0$ **Given:** The principal maximum of the array factor occurs when the denominator and numerator simultaneously equal zero, i.e., when: $$\beta + \frac{2\pi d}{\lambda} \cos\theta = 0$$ **Find:** The required progressive phase shift $\beta$ to steer the main beam to angle $\theta_0$. **STEP 1 — Set $\theta = \theta_0$ for the desired steering direction.** $$\beta + \frac{2\pi d}{\lambda} \cos\theta_0 = 0$$ **STEP 2 — Solve for $\beta$.** $$\boxed{\beta = -\frac{2\pi d}{\lambda} \cos\theta_0}$$ This is the **fundamental beamforming equation**. The negative sign indicates that for a positive steering angle (cosine > 0), we must apply a *lagging* phase to successive elements, which retards the wavefront and tilts the beam. --- ### 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Directivity of a Broadside Uniform Linear Array **Given:** A broadside ULA (i.e., $\beta = 0$, main beam at $\theta_0 = 90°$) with $N$ elements and spacing $d$. **Find:** The directivity $D_0$. **STEP 1 — Recall the definition of directivity.** $$D_0 = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A}$$ where $\Omega_A$ is the **beam solid angle** (in steradians) of the main lobe. **STEP 2 — Approximate the beamwidths.** For a broadside ULA with $d \le \lambda$, the half-power beamwidth (in radians) and the beam solid angle are approximately: $$\theta_{HP} \approx 0.886 \frac{\lambda}{N d} \quad \text{(in the plane of the array)}$$ $$\Omega_A \approx \theta_{HP} \cdot \phi_{HP} \approx \theta_{HP}^2 \quad \text{(assuming a pencil beam)}$$ **STEP 3 — Substitute and simplify.** $$D_0 = \frac{4\pi}{(0.886 \lambda / (N d))^2}$$ $$\boxed{D_0 \approx \frac{4\pi N^2 d^2}{(0.886)^2 \lambda^2} \approx \frac{16 N^2 d^2}{\lambda^2} \approx 2 N \cdot \frac{N d}{\lambda} \approx 2 N \cdot L / \lambda}$$ where $L = N d$ is the **array length**. This shows that directivity grows with both the number of elements and the electrical length of the array — the heart of why beamforming "amplifies" signals in the desired direction. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix (Quick-Reference for KTU Numericals) > [!TIP] > Use this matrix as a decision tree when solving numericals. Most KTU Part B questions follow one of these four archetypes. | **KTU Problem Archetype** | **What is Given** | **What to Find** | **Key Formula to Apply** | |---------------------------|-------------------|------------------|-------------------------| | **Archetype 1: Main Beam Direction** | $N$, $d$, $\beta$ | Steering angle $\theta_0$ | $\beta = -\frac{2\pi d}{\lambda} \cos\theta_0$ | | **Archetype 2: Progressive Phase** | $N$, $d$, $\theta_0$ | Required $\beta$ in degrees/radians | Same as above, solve for $\beta$ | | **Archetype 3: Nulls of Array Factor** | $N$, $d$ | Angular positions of nulls | $\frac{N}{2}(\beta + \frac{2\pi d}{\lambda}\cos\theta) = \pm m\pi$ | | **Archetype 4: 3-dB Beamwidth** | $N$, $d$ | HPBW of broadside array | $\theta_{HP} \approx 0.886 \lambda / (N d)$ | | **Archetype 5: Directivity** | $N$, $d$ | Directivity in dB | $D_0 (\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(2 N d / \lambda)$ | | **Archetype 6: Grating Lobe Check** | $N$, $d$, $\theta_0$ | Does a grating lobe exist? | Check $d < \lambda / (1 + \vert \cos\theta_0 \vert)$ | ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) > **Problem (KTU 2022 ESE Pattern):** A 10-element uniform linear array with $d = \lambda/2$ operates at 3 GHz. Find the progressive phase shift $\beta$ required to steer the main beam to $\theta_0 = 60°$ (from broadside). Verify whether grating lobes are present. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute $\beta$ using the steering equation.** $$\beta = -\frac{2\pi d}{\lambda} \cos\theta_0 = -\frac{2\pi (\lambda/2)}{\lambda} \cos(60°) = -\pi \cdot 0.5 = -\frac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad} = -90°$$ **Step 2 — Grating lobe check.** For a grating lobe to be absent, we need $d < \lambda / (1 + \vert \cos\theta_0 \vert) = \lambda / (1 + 0.5) = 0.667 \lambda$. Since $d = 0.5 \lambda < 0.667 \lambda$, **no grating lobe exists** for this configuration. **Step 3 — Conclusion.** The progressive phase shift is $\beta = -90°$, and the array is well-designed (no grating lobes). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram 1: N-Element Uniform Linear Array — Geometric Layout This diagram illustrates the geometric configuration of an N-element linear array placed along the z-axis, with the inter-element spacing $d$, the far-field observation angle $\theta$ measured from the x-axis, and the path difference $\Delta$ between adjacent elements. ```mermaid graph TD A["Element 0
at z = 0
Weight w0"] -->|"spacing d"| B["Element 1
at z = d
Weight w1 = A exp j beta"] B -->|"spacing d"| C["Element 2
at z = 2d
Weight w2 = A exp j 2 beta"] C -->|"spacing d"| D["Element N-1
at z = N-1 d
Weight wN-1 = A exp j N-1 beta"] E["Plane wave arriving
at angle theta
from broadside"] -.->|"hits all N elements
with progressive delay"| A E -.-> B E -.-> C E -.-> D F["Path difference
Delta = d cos theta"] -.-> A F -.-> B F -.-> C F -.-> D ``` ### 4.2 Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Digital Beamforming System This diagram shows the complete signal-processing chain used in a digital beamformer, from antenna elements through complex weighting to beam output. This is the most likely block diagram to appear in a KTU Part B (14-mark) question. ```mermaid graph LR ANT1["Antenna 1"] --> LNA1["LNA + Filter"] ANT2["Antenna 2"] --> LNA2["LNA + Filter"] ANT3["Antenna N"] --> LNAN["LNA + Filter"] LNA1 --> ADC1["ADC
Analog to Digital"] LNA2 --> ADC2["ADC
Analog to Digital"] LNAN --> ADCN["ADC
Analog to Digital"] ADC1 --> W1["Complex Weight
w1 = A1 exp j phi1"] ADC2 --> W2["Complex Weight
w2 = A2 exp j phi2"] ADCN --> WN["Complex Weight
wN = AN exp j phiN"] W1 --> SUM["Summing Network
Sigma"] W2 --> SUM WN --> SUM SUM --> OUT["Beamformed Output
y t = sum wn xn t"] CTRL["Adaptive Algorithm
LMS or MVDR"] -.->|"updates weights"| W1 CTRL -.-> W2 CTRL -.-> WN REF["Reference Signal
or DOA Estimate"] -.-> CTRL ``` ### 4.3 Diagram 3: Decision Flow for Beamforming Problem Solving A KTU exam strategy flowchart guiding students through a typical 14-mark Part B question on beamforming. ```mermaid graph TD P["Start: Read the Problem"] --> Q1["Identify N, d, lambda"] Q1 --> Q2{"What is asked?"} Q2 -->|Steering angle| R1["Use beta = -2 pi d cos theta0 / lambda"] Q2 -->|Required phase beta| R2["Use beta = -2 pi d cos theta0 / lambda"] Q2 -->|Null positions| R3["Set sin N phi/2 = 0"] Q2 -->|HPBW| R4["HPBW approx 0.886 lambda / N d"] R1 --> S1["Solve algebraically"] R2 --> S1 R3 --> S1 R4 --> S1 S1 --> S2["Check for grating lobes
d less than lambda"] S2 --> S3["Convert angles to degrees
and write final answer"] ``` ### 4.4 Diagram 4: Array Factor Pattern — Broadside vs. End-Fire Comparison A schematic conceptual comparison of the two most important ULA configurations tested in KTU exams. ```mermaid graph LR subgraph BROADSIDE B1["beta = 0"] --> B2["Main beam perpendicular
to array axis at theta = 90 deg"] B2 --> B3["HPBW approx 0.886 lambda / N d"] end subgraph ENDFIRE E1["beta = -pi"] --> E2["Main beam along
array axis at theta = 0 deg"] E2 --> E3["HPBW approx 2 sqrt 0.886 lambda / N d
(wider beam)"] end subgraph ENHANCED_ENDFIRE H1["beta = -pi + 2.94 / N"] --> H2["Hansen-Woodyard condition
for increased directivity"] H2 --> H3["Directivity approx 4 N d / lambda"] end ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) — Short Answer Conceptual > **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper Style]** > Define **Array Factor**. Explain how the **progressive phase shift $\beta$** is used to electronically steer the beam of a uniform linear array. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Array Factor (AF)** is the normalized far-field radiation pattern of an $N$-element antenna array, obtained by summing the weighted contributions of each element assuming isotropic radiators. It is a function of the inter-element phase difference $\psi$ and the progressive phase $\beta$: $$AF(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \frac{\sin\left[ \frac{N}{2}\left(\beta + \frac{2\pi d}{\lambda}\cos\theta\right) \right]}{\sin\left[ \frac{1}{2}\left(\beta + \frac{2\pi d}{\lambda}\cos\theta\right) \right]}$$ **Electronic beam steering** is achieved by varying $\beta$. The main beam points in the direction $\theta_0$ where the argument of the AF vanishes, i.e., when $\beta = -\frac{2\pi d}{\lambda}\cos\theta_0$. By changing $\beta$ electronically (using phase shifters or digital weights), the beam can be steered in microseconds without any mechanical motion. > *(Examiner note: 1 mark for AF definition, 1 mark for AF formula, 1 mark for steering equation.)* --- > **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** > Differentiate between **broadside array** and **end-fire array**. State the value of $\beta$ for each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | **Parameter** | **Broadside Array** | **End-Fire Array** | |---------------|---------------------|---------------------| | Beam direction | Perpendicular to the array axis ($\theta_0 = 90°$) | Along the array axis ($\theta_0 = 0°$ or $180°$) | | Progressive phase $\beta$ | $\beta = 0$ | $\beta = -\pi$ (ordinary) or $\beta = -\pi + 2.94/N$ (Hansen-Woodyard) | | Directivity | Higher (narrow beam) | Lower (wider beam, doubled in H-W) | | HPBW | $\approx 0.886\lambda/(Nd)$ | $\approx 2\sqrt{0.886\lambda/(Nd)}$ | For a broadside array, all elements are excited in phase, so the wavefronts add constructively in the broadside direction. For an end-fire array, the progressive phase $\beta = -\pi$ introduces a one-half wavelength phase delay per element, causing the wavefronts to add along the array axis. --- ### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks) — Full ESE Choice Format > **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper Pattern]** **(a)** Derive the **Array Factor** of an N-element uniform linear array with inter-element spacing $d$ and progressive phase shift $\beta$. (7 marks) **(b)** For a **10-element uniform linear array** operating at **3 GHz** with $d = \lambda/2$, calculate: (7 marks) 1. The progressive phase shift $\beta$ required to steer the main beam to **$\theta_0 = 60°$** from broadside. 2. The **3-dB beamwidth (HPBW)** of the broadside pattern. 3. Check whether **grating lobes** are present. --- **Model Solution (14 marks):** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** Consider $N$ isotropic radiators placed on the z-axis, with element $n$ at position $z_n = n \cdot d$. The far-field observation point is at angle $\theta$ from the x-axis. Each element is excited with weight $w_n = A e^{j n \beta}$. The element-to-element phase difference due to path length is: $$\psi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} d \cos\theta$$ The total array factor is the sum of all weighted contributions: $$AF = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} A e^{j n \beta} \cdot e^{j n \psi} = A \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j n(\beta + \psi)}$$ This is a finite geometric series. Using $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} r^n = (1 - r^N)/(1-r)$ with $r = e^{j(\beta + \psi)}$: $$AF = A \cdot \frac{1 - e^{j N(\beta + \psi)}}{1 - e^{j(\beta + \psi)}}$$ Using the trigonometric identity $\sin(x) = (e^{jx} - e^{-jx})/(2j)$, we get: $$\boxed{AF(\theta) = A \cdot \frac{\sin\left[\frac{N}{2}\left(\beta + \frac{2\pi d}{\lambda}\cos\theta\right)\right]}{\sin\left[\frac{1}{2}\left(\beta + \frac{2\pi d}{\lambda}\cos\theta\right)\right]} \cdot e^{j \frac{(N-1)}{2}(\beta + \psi)}}$$ The normalized magnitude is obtained by dividing by its maximum value $N \cdot A$: $$|AF(\theta)|_{norm} = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \frac{\sin\left[\frac{N}{2}\left(\beta + \frac{2\pi d}{\lambda}\cos\theta\right)\right]}{\sin\left[\frac{1}{2}\left(\beta + \frac{2\pi d}{\lambda}\cos\theta\right)\right]}$$ This completes the derivation. (3 marks for setup, 2 marks for geometric sum, 2 marks for final normalized form.) --- **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Given:** $N = 10$, $f = 3$ GHz, $d = \lambda/2$, $\theta_0 = 60°$. **Step 1 — Compute the wavelength.** $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{3 \times 10^9} = 0.1 \text{ m} = 10 \text{ cm}$$ (1 mark) **Step 2 — Compute the progressive phase $\beta$.** $$\beta = -\frac{2\pi d}{\lambda} \cos\theta_0 = -\frac{2\pi (\lambda/2)}{\lambda} \cos(60°) = -\pi \times 0.5 = -\frac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad} = -90°$$ (2 marks) **Step 3 — Compute the 3-dB beamwidth (broadside HPBW, $\theta_0 = 90°$ case for reference).** $$\theta_{HP} \approx \frac{0.886 \lambda}{N d} = \frac{0.886 \lambda}{10 \times (\lambda/2)} = \frac{0.886}{5} = 0.1772 \text{ rad} \approx 10.15°$$ (2 marks) **Step 4 — Grating lobe check.** The condition to avoid grating lobes is $d < \lambda / (1 + \vert \cos\theta_0 \vert)$. $$d_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{1 + \cos 60°} = \frac{\lambda}{1.5} = 0.667 \lambda$$ Since the actual $d = 0.5\lambda < 0.667\lambda$, **no grating lobes are present**. (2 marks) **Final Answer:** $\beta = -90°$, HPBW $\approx 10.15°$, no grating lobes. --- > **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **adaptive beamforming** system. Discuss the **Least Mean Squares (LMS) algorithm** for weight updation. (7 marks) **(b)** An **8-element uniform linear array** with $d = 0.6\lambda$ is required to form a beam that points **broadside** ($\theta_0 = 90°$). Determine: (7 marks) 1. The value of the **progressive phase shift** $\beta$. 2. The **angular positions of the first two nulls** of the array factor on either side of the main beam. 3. The **directivity** of this broadside array in dB. --- **Model Solution (14 marks):** **Part (a) — Adaptive Beamforming Block Diagram (7 marks):** Adaptive beamforming is a closed-loop signal processing technique where the complex weights $w_n(t)$ applied to each antenna element are continuously updated in real time to optimize a chosen performance criterion — typically to maximize the **Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio (SINR)**. **Block Diagram (textual description for KTU answer script, 3 marks):** The system consists of $N$ antenna elements, each followed by an LNA, ADC, and a **complex weight multiplier** $w_n$. The weighted signals are summed in a combining network to produce the array output $y(t) = \sum w_n^* x_n(t)$. This output, along with a **reference signal** $d(t)$ (or a Direction-of-Arrival estimate), is fed to an **adaptive algorithm** (e.g., LMS, RLS, MVDR), which iteratively updates the weights using the error signal $e(t) = d(t) - y(t)$. The updated weights are then re-injected to the multipliers, closing the feedback loop. **LMS Algorithm (4 marks):** The LMS algorithm updates weights using the method of steepest descent on the Mean Squared Error (MSE) cost function $\xi = E[|e(t)|^2]$. The weight update equation is: $$w_n(t+1) = w_n(t) + \mu \cdot x_n(t) \cdot e^*(t)$$ where $\mu$ is the **step size** (small positive constant that controls convergence speed and stability). The convergence condition is $0 < \mu < 2 / \lambda_{max}$, where $\lambda_{max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of the input correlation matrix. The LMS algorithm is simple, robust, and computationally efficient, making it the most widely used adaptive beamforming algorithm in practice. --- **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Given:** $N = 8$, $d = 0.6\lambda$, $\theta_0 = 90°$ (broadside). **Step 1 — Progressive phase shift $\beta$ for broadside.** For broadside, the beam must point at $\theta_0 = 90°$. Using $\beta = -(2\pi d/\lambda)\cos\theta_0$: $$\beta = -\frac{2\pi (0.6\lambda)}{\lambda} \cos(90°) = -1.2\pi \times 0 = 0 \text{ rad}$$ (1 mark) **Step 2 — Angular positions of the first two nulls.** The array factor nulls occur when $\sin(N\Phi/2) = 0$, where $\Phi = \beta + (2\pi d/\lambda)\cos\theta = (2\pi d/\lambda)\cos\theta$ (since $\beta = 0$). This requires $N\Phi/2 = \pm m\pi$, with $m = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ (excluding $m = N$ for the main beam). $$\Phi_m = \pm \frac{2m\pi}{N} = \pm \frac{m\pi}{4}$$ Setting $\Phi_m = (2\pi d/\lambda)\cos\theta_m$: $$\cos\theta_m = \frac{m \lambda}{4 d} = \frac{m \lambda}{4 \times 0.6\lambda} = \frac{m}{2.4}$$ **For $m = 1$ (first null):** $$\cos\theta_1 = \frac{1}{2.4} = 0.4167 \implies \theta_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.4167) = 65.4°$$ (2 marks) **For $m = 2$ (second null):** $$\cos\theta_2 = \frac{2}{2.4} = 0.8333 \implies \theta_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.8333) = 33.6°$$ (2 marks) **Note:** The array factor is symmetric about $\theta = 90°$ (broadside), so the nulls also appear at mirror angles: $90° + (90° - 65.4°) = 114.6°$ and $90° + (90° - 33.6°) = 146.4°$. **Step 3 — Directivity in dB.** For a uniform linear array, $D_0 \approx 2Nd/\lambda$: $$D_0 = 2 \times 8 \times 0.6 = 9.6 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$ $$D_0 (\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(9.6) = 9.82 \text{ dB}$$ (2 marks) **Final Answer:** $\beta = 0$, first nulls at $\theta = 65.4°$ and $114.6°$, second nulls at $\theta = 33.6°$ and $146.4°$, directivity = 9.82 dB. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:** > > 1. **Forgetting to state the convention of $\theta$** — Whether $\theta$ is measured from the broadside ($\sin\theta$ in formula) or from the array axis ($\cos\theta$ in formula). KTU examiners deduct **1–2 marks** if you do not explicitly write: *"The angle $\theta$ is measured from the array axis."* > > 2. **Confusing the array factor formula with the normalized form** — Always divide by $N$ to get the normalized AF; otherwise the main lobe magnitude will be $N$, not $1$, and your plotted pattern will be wrong. > > 3. **Skipping the grating lobe check** — Whenever $d > \lambda/2$, the examiner expects you to verify whether grating lobes exist. Forgetting this loses 2 marks. > > 4. **Mixing up $\psi$ and $\Phi$** — In some derivations, $\psi = (2\pi/\lambda)d\cos\theta$ is the *intrinsic* phase difference, while $\Phi = \psi + \beta$ is the *total* phase difference including progressive shift. KTU examiners check this carefully. > > 5. **Forgetting the factor of $2\pi$ in the steering equation** — A common error: writing $\beta = -(d/\lambda)\cos\theta_0$ instead of $\beta = -(2\pi d/\lambda)\cos\theta_0$. This is a **3-mark deduction**. > > 6. **Not mentioning the unit (radians vs. degrees)** — Always state: *"$\beta$ is in radians"* or convert to degrees at the end. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master These 8 Points Before Walking Into the KTU Exam Hall:** - [ ] **Array Factor (AF)** is the *normalized* far-field pattern of an N-element array assuming isotropic elements. Always divide by $N$. - [ ] The **steering equation** is $\beta = -\frac{2\pi d}{\lambda} \cos\theta_0$. This single equation lets you find *either* the required phase *or* the beam direction. - [ ] **Broadside** means $\beta = 0$ and beam at $\theta_0 = 90°$ (perpendicular to array). - [ ] **End-fire** means $\beta = -\pi$ (ordinary) and beam at $\theta_0 = 0°$ or $180°$ (along array). - [ ] **Hansen-Woodyard enhanced end-fire** condition: $\beta = -\pi + 2.94/N$, which gives nearly double the directivity. - [ ] **Grating lobe condition** to avoid: $d < \lambda / (1 + \vert \cos\theta_0 \vert)$. The safe universal choice is $d = \lambda/2$. - [ ] **HPBW (broadside ULA)** $\approx 0.886\lambda / (Nd)$. The narrower the beam, the higher the directivity. - [ ] **Directivity (broadside ULA)** $\approx 2Nd / \lambda$, which in dB is $D_0(\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(2Nd/\lambda)$. Doubling $N$ or $d$ increases $D_0$ by **3 dB**. - [ ] **Adaptive beamforming (LMS)** uses weight update: $w_n(t+1) = w_n(t) + \mu x_n(t) e^*(t)$. The step size $\mu$ controls the trade-off between convergence speed and steady-state error. - [ ] **MVDR (Minimum Variance Distortionless Response)** is the optimal beamformer that minimizes total output power subject to unity gain in the look direction — used in radar and 5G. > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Tip:** When a KTU question gives you $N$, $d$, and $\theta_0$ and asks "design a beamformer," your answer should explicitly contain: (1) the steering equation, (2) the computed $\beta$, (3) the array factor formula, (4) the null positions, (5) the HPBW, and (6) the grating lobe check. This 6-step template is what examiners look for in a complete 14-mark answer.

topics.title: Mobile Ad hoc Networks (MANETs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mobile Ad hoc Networks (MANETs)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Mobile Ad hoc Networks (MANETs) — Module 3

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Mobile Ad hoc Network (MANET)** is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of mobile devices (nodes) connected by wireless links. Each node is free to move independently in any direction, and will therefore change its links to other devices frequently. The union of all nodes forms an arbitrary topology where each node acts both as a **host** (end-system running applications) and a **router** (forwarding data for other nodes). The network operates without a fixed centralized base station, access point, or pre-existing infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** A *Mobile Ad Hoc Network (MANET)* is a collection of wireless mobile nodes that dynamically self-organize into a temporary network without the aid of any pre-existing network infrastructure or centralized administration. Routing in MANETs is multi-hop, where intermediate nodes relay packets from a source to a destination.

**Key Terminology (as per IEEE 802.11 / RFC 2501 standards):**

| Term | Meaning | KTU Standard Notation |
|------|---------|------------------------|
| **Node** | A mobile wireless device | N |
| **Host** | Source or destination of data | H |
| **Router** | Intermediate forwarding device | R |
| **Hop** | One wireless link traversal | H_c = 1 |
| **Multi-hop** | ≥2 wireless links between source & destination | H_c ≥ 2 |
| **Topology** | Dynamic graph G(V,E) where V = nodes, E = wireless links | G(V,E,t) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stranger's Megaphone"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you're lost in a dense forest with no cell towers. To call for help, you shout (transmit). But your voice doesn't reach the rescue team 2 km away. Fortunately, 10 other lost hikers are scattered between you and the team. Each hiker can hear you and the next hiker, but not the team directly. So, hiker-1 relays your shout to hiker-2, hiker-2 to hiker-3, and so on, until the message reaches the rescue team. **You are the Source, the team is the Destination, and the in-between hikers are the Mobile Routers.** This is exactly how a MANET works — every node is simultaneously a user **and** a relay. The forest has no cell tower, just like a MANET has no base station.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant)

- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Free-space path loss frequency:** Operates typically in the **2.4 GHz** (ISM band) and **5 GHz** bands
- **Standard transmission range:** $R_{tx} \approx 100$–$300$ m (outdoor), $30$–$100$ m (indoor)
- **Node mobility model:** Random Waypoint, Gauss-Markov, Reference Point Group Mobility (RPGM)
- **Routing metric standard:** Hop count, Expected Transmission Count (ETX), Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio (SINR)

### 1.4 Why MANETs Matter in the Context of Antenna & Wave Propagation

Since this course is **PEECT868 – Antenna Theory and Wave Propagation**, the relevance of MANETs lies in three engineering realities:

1. **Antenna radiation pattern** directly determines which neighbors a node can "hear" (coverage angle).
2. **Multipath fading & path loss** in mobile environments causes link breakage — antennas with diversity mitigate this.
3. **Propagation models** (Two-Ray Ground, Rician, Rayleigh) govern the **link budget** that a MANET routing protocol must adapt to.
