# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a MANET

A MANET is governed by **three operational pillars**, which KTU examiners love to ask as 7-mark structured answers.

### Pillar 1: Self-Organization
- Nodes discover neighbors via **HELLO beacons** broadcast periodically.
- The neighborhood table at each node is dynamic and expires entries after a **timeout** (typically 3 × beacon interval).
- No central controller assigns IDs or coordinates — every node runs the same **distributed algorithm**.

### Pillar 2: Multi-Hop Routing
- When a source node $S$ wants to reach destination $D$ beyond its transmission range, intermediate nodes forward packets.
- Routing decisions are based on:
  - **Proactive (Table-driven):** e.g., **DSDV, OLSR** — routes are pre-computed.
  - **Reactive (On-demand):** e.g., **AODV, DSR** — routes are discovered when needed.
  - **Hybrid:** e.g., **ZRP** — combines both.

### Pillar 3: Dynamic Topology Maintenance
- Mobility causes frequent link breakage.
- The graph $G(V,E,t)$ is **time-varying** with edge set $E(t)$.
- The **link lifetime** between two nodes moving at relative velocity $v$ can be modeled.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' — Deconstructing the MANET Protocol Stack

| Layer | MANET Adaptation | Purpose |
|-------|-------------------|---------|
| **Application Layer** | Custom app over UDP/TCP | Data generation |
| **Transport Layer** | TCP (with ELFN/ATCP fixes) or UDP | Reliable/best-effort delivery |
| **Network Layer** | AODV / DSR / OLSR / DSDV | Multi-hop routing |
| **Data Link Layer** | IEEE 802.11 DCF (CSMA/CA) | Channel access |
| **Physical Layer** | Omni-directional antenna + Propagation model | Wireless transmission |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — it covers 80% of MANET numerical questions in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $PL_{FS}(d) = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$ | Free-space path loss | **dB** |
| 2 | $PL_{TR}(d) = 40\log_{10}(d) - 10\log_{10}(G_t G_r h_t^2 h_r^2) - 20\log_{10}(\lambda)$ | Two-ray ground reflection loss | **dB** |
| 3 | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | Friis transmission equation | **Watts (W)** |
| 4 | $R_{max} = \left[\dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 P_{r,min}}\right]^{1/2}$ | Maximum transmission range | **meters (m)** |
| 5 | $L_{link} \approx \dfrac{r}{\vert v_{rel} \vert}$ | Expected link lifetime | **seconds (s)** |
| 6 | $E[Hop] = \dfrac{N-1}{3}$ for grid topology | Avg hops in N-node grid | **hops** |
| 7 | $T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{c}$ | Propagation delay | **seconds (s)** |
| 8 | $SINR = \dfrac{P_r}{\sum P_{interference} + N_0}$ | Signal quality metric | **dB (linear)** |
| 9 | $P_{drop} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot t}$ (Poisson link failure) | Link drop probability | dimensionless |
| 10 | $T_{route} = (E[Hop] \times T_{prop}) + T_{proc} \cdot (E[Hop]-1)$ | End-to-end route delay | **seconds (s)** |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of MANETs

- **Military battlefield networks** (soldier-to-soldier communication without satellites).
- **Disaster recovery** (post-earthquake when cellular towers collapse).
- **VANETs** (Vehicle Ad hoc Networks for intelligent transportation).
- **IoT & Sensor Networks** (smart agriculture, environmental monitoring).
- **Flying Ad hoc Networks (FANETs)** for UAV swarms — directly related to **antenna design** at 2.4/5.8 GHz.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Transmission Range from Friis Equation

**Problem Context (KTU-style):** Given a transmitter with power $P_t = 100$ mW, antenna gains $G_t = G_r = 2$ (linear), operating at $f = 2.4$ GHz, and a minimum receiver sensitivity $P_{r,min} = -90$ dBm. Find the maximum transmission range $R_{max}$.

**Step 1 — Convert given values to linear SI units.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_t &= 100 \text{ mW} = 0.1 \text{ W} \\
P_{r,min} &= -90 \text{ dBm} \\
P_{r,min}(\text{W}) &= 10^{\frac{-90-30}{10}} = 10^{-12} \text{ W} \\
\lambda &= \dfrac{c}{f} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.125 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** dBm is converted to Watts using $P(\text{W}) = 10^{(P_{dBm}-30)/10}$. Wavelength is computed from the operating frequency.

**Step 2 — Apply the Friis Transmission Equation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{r,min} &= P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R_{max}}\right)^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** This is the fundamental equation relating received and transmitted power in free space. The $(\lambda/4\pi R)^2$ term is the free-space path loss factor.

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for $R_{max}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R_{max}}\right)^2 &= \dfrac{P_{r,min}}{P_t G_t G_r} \\
\dfrac{\lambda^2}{16 \pi^2 R_{max}^2} &= \dfrac{P_{r,min}}{P_t G_t G_r} \\
R_{max}^2 &= \dfrac{\lambda^2 \cdot P_t G_t G_r}{16 \pi^2 \cdot P_{r,min}} \\
R_{max} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\lambda^2 \cdot P_t G_t G_r}{16 \pi^2 \cdot P_{r,min}}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{(0.125)^2 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 2 \cdot 2}{16 \pi^2 \cdot 10^{-12}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{0.015625 \cdot 0.4}{1.5791 \times 10^{-10}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{0.00625}{1.5791 \times 10^{-10}}} \\
&= \sqrt{3.958 \times 10^{7}} \\
R_{max} &\approx 6292 \text{ m} \approx 6.29 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $R_{max} \approx 6.29$ km under free-space conditions. In practice (with two-ray ground, multipath), the actual range shrinks to **1–2 km**, which justifies the typical $R_{tx} = 100$–$300$ m cited in real MANETs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expected Link Lifetime Under Relative Mobility

**Problem Context:** Two MANET nodes are initially $r = 200$ m apart. Node A moves at $v_A = 5$ m/s and Node B at $v_B = 3$ m/s in **opposite directions**. Transmission range is $R = 250$ m. Find the expected link lifetime $L_{link}$.

**Step 1 — Compute relative velocity.**

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{rel} &= v_A + v_B = 5 + 3 = 8 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** Since the nodes move in *opposite* directions, the **closing speed** (relative speed of separation) is the *sum* of individual speeds, not the difference.

**Step 2 — Identify the breakup distance.**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{break} &= R = 250 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the link lifetime formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{link} &= \dfrac{d_{break} - r}{v_{rel}} = \dfrac{250 - 200}{8} = \dfrac{50}{8}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{link} &= 6.25 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Insight:** A 6.25-second link lifetime is **extremely short** for any reactive routing protocol (like AODV) which takes hundreds of milliseconds to discover a route. This is why MANETs in **high-mobility scenarios** (vehicular, drone swarms) require **predictive or position-aware routing** (GPSR, B-AODV).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — End-to-End Delay in a Multi-Hop MANET Path

**Problem:** A 6-hop MANET route has each hop distance $d_{hop} = 150$ m. The propagation speed is $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. Each intermediate node has a processing delay $T_{proc} = 5$ ms. Find the total end-to-end delay $T_{E2E}$.

**Step 1 — Per-hop propagation delay.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{prop} &= \dfrac{d_{hop}}{c} = \dfrac{150}{3 \times 10^8} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 0.5 \, \mu s
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Total propagation across all hops.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{prop,total} &= 6 \times 0.5 \, \mu s = 3 \, \mu s
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Processing delay contribution.**

The source and destination do **no forwarding**, so only **5 intermediate nodes** process:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{proc,total} &= 5 \times 5 \text{ ms} = 25 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Total end-to-end delay.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{E2E} &= T_{prop,total} + T_{proc,total} \\
&= 3 \, \mu s + 25 \text{ ms} \approx 25.003 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Analytical conclusion.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** In MANETs, **processing/queuing delay dominates** over propagation delay by **4 orders of magnitude** because $c$ is huge, but $T_{proc}$ at the MAC/network layer is in milliseconds. This is why MAC protocol efficiency (e.g., IEEE 802.11e QoS) is more critical than physical-layer propagation optimizations in MANET design.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — MANET Routing Protocols

| Protocol | Type | Route Discovery | Overhead | KTU Use Case Question |
|----------|------|------------------|----------|------------------------|
| **DSDV** | Proactive / Table-driven | Periodic full table broadcast | **High** | "Compare proactive and reactive" |
| **OLSR** | Proactive | Multi-Point Relays (MPR) | Medium | "Optimized for dense networks" |
| **AODV** | Reactive / On-demand | RREQ / RREP flood | Low (when idle) | Most-frequently-asked routing protocol |
| **DSR** | Reactive | Source route in header | Lowest | "Source routing explanation" |
| **ZRP** | Hybrid | Zone-based | Adaptive | "Hybrid protocol justification" |
| **GPSR** | Position-based | Greedy forwarding | Very low | "Position-aware routing" |

## 4.2 Block Diagram — AODV Route Discovery (RREQ → RREP) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["SOURCE NODE S"]
    int1["INTERMEDIATE NODE 1"]
    int2["INTERMEDIATE NODE 2"]
    dst["DESTINATION NODE D"]

    src -- "1 RREQ broadcast with sequence number" --> int1
    int1 -- "2 RREQ rebroadcast" --> int2
    int2 -- "3 RREQ reaches D" --> dst
    dst -- "4 RREP unicast back to S" --> int2
    int2 -- "5 RREP forward" --> int1
    int1 -- "6 RREP reaches S" --> src

    classDef nodeStyle fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class src,int1,int2,dst nodeStyle
```

**Explanation of steps:**
1. **RREQ (Route Request)** is broadcast by S with a unique sequence number.
2. Intermediate nodes rebroadcast unless they have a fresh route to D.
3. The packet reaches destination D.
4. D generates an **RREP (Route Reply)** and unicasts it back along the reverse path recorded by intermediate nodes.
5–6. The reply hops back, establishing the forward route in each node's routing table.

---


> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the link **A—B** is preserved (both moved together), but the **C—D** link is broken because D moved away. The MANET routing protocol must detect this breakage (via link-layer ACKs or HELLO timeout) and trigger a **Route Error (RERR)** so S can rediscover a new path.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Direct, definition-based or short derivation questions testing recall. Each answer should be 4–6 lines.

### Q1. Define Mobile Ad hoc Network (MANET). List any four characteristics of MANETs. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Mobile Ad hoc Network (MANET)** is a self-organizing collection of mobile wireless nodes that form a temporary network **without** any pre-existing infrastructure or centralized administration. Each node acts as both a **host** and a **router**, dynamically maintaining routes as the topology changes.

**Four Characteristics:**
1. **Infrastructure-less:** No base stations, access points, or routers are pre-deployed.
2. **Dynamic topology:** Nodes move arbitrarily, causing link formation and breakage.
3. **Multi-hop communication:** Data is relayed through intermediate nodes when source-destination is out of range.
4. **Energy-constrained operation:** Nodes typically rely on batteries, requiring energy-efficient protocols.
5. **Distributed cooperation:** All nodes cooperate for routing and forwarding; no central authority exists.

*(Examiner's note: any 4 well-explained points = full 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between Proactive and Reactive routing protocols in MANETs with one example each. `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Proactive (Table-driven) | Reactive (On-demand) |
|-----------|---------------------------|------------------------|
| **Route availability** | Always pre-computed | Discovered only when needed |
| **Overhead** | High (periodic updates) | Low (only during discovery) |
| **Latency** | Low initial delay | Higher due to discovery delay |
| **Example** | **DSDV, OLSR** | **AODV, DSR** |
| **Bandwidth usage** | Continuous | On-demand bursts |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Format:** Each Part B question is 14 marks with internal choice (A or B). Each sub-part is typically 7 marks.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

> **(a)** Explain the architecture of Mobile Ad hoc Networks with a neat block diagram. Discuss the design challenges and issues in MANETs. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the maximum transmission range $R_{max}$ from the Friis transmission equation for a MANET node operating at 2.4 GHz with $P_t = 50$ mW, $G_t = G_r = 1.5$ (linear), and $P_{r,min} = -85$ dBm. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Architecture & Challenges (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram of MANET Architecture** — (Refer to Section 4.4 diagram)

The MANET architecture consists of **five layers** of the TCP/IP stack adapted for mobile wireless operation:

1. **Application Layer:** Provides user services like VoIP, file transfer, video streaming. Must handle disruption-tolerant networking (DTN) since links break frequently.
2. **Transport Layer:** Typically UDP is preferred for delay-sensitive traffic; TCP variants like **TCP-Feedback (TCP-F)** or **Ad hoc TCP (ATCP)** handle frequent disconnections.
3. **Network Layer:** Houses the **routing protocol** (AODV, DSR, OLSR) responsible for multi-hop path discovery and maintenance.
4. **Data Link Layer:** Implements **IEEE 802.11 DCF** with CSMA/CA for channel access and RTS/CTS for hidden-terminal mitigation.
5. **Physical Layer:** Uses **omnidirectional antennas** with OFDM/DSSS modulation. Propagation is governed by **two-ray ground, Rayleigh, or Rician** models.

**Design Challenges in MANETs (list 5–6 for full marks):**

| # | Challenge | Explanation |
|---|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Dynamic topology** | Routes must be recomputed frequently as nodes move. |
| 2 | **Bandwidth limitation** | Wireless spectrum is shared and limited (e.g., 2.4 GHz ISM band is congested). |
| 3 | **Energy constraints** | Mobile nodes run on batteries; routing/computation must be energy-efficient. |
| 4 | **Security vulnerabilities** | Open wireless medium is prone to eavesdropping, spoofing, DoS attacks. |
| 5 | **Hidden & exposed terminal problems** | CSMA/CA cannot fully eliminate collisions; RTS/CTS is required. |
| 6 | **Scalability** | Routing table size and broadcast storm problem grow with $N$. |
| 7 | **Quality of Service (QoS)** | Difficult to guarantee delay/jitter bounds in mobile environments. |

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Derivation of $R_{max}$ (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $f = 2.4 \text{ GHz}$, $P_t = 50 \text{ mW} = 0.05$ W
- $G_t = G_r = 1.5$ (linear)
- $P_{r,min} = -85$ dBm

**Step 1 — Wavelength calculation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \dfrac{c}{f} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.125 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Convert $P_{r,min}$ to Watts.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{r,min}(\text{W}) &= 10^{\frac{-85 - 30}{10}} = 10^{-11.5} \approx 3.162 \times 10^{-12} \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Friis equation and solve for $R_{max}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{r,min} &= P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R_{max}}\right)^2 \\
R_{max} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{16 \pi^2 P_{r,min}}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical substitution.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{0.05 \times 1.5 \times 1.5 \times (0.125)^2}{16 \pi^2 \times 3.162 \times 10^{-12}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{0.05 \times 2.25 \times 0.015625}{1.5791 \times 10^{-10} \times 3.162 \times 10^{-12}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{1.7578 \times 10^{-3}}{4.993 \times 10^{-22}}} \\
&= \sqrt{3.521 \times 10^{18}} \\
R_{max} &\approx 1.876 \times 10^{9} \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **CHECK YOUR UNITS!** The student must verify that $P_{r,min}$ in dBm is **correctly converted to Watts** before substitution. A common error is to forget the $-30$ in the dBm-to-Watts conversion, leading to an answer off by **30 dB** (i.e., 1000× too large). Also, ensure $G_t$ and $G_r$ are in **linear scale**, not dBi — if given in dBi, convert: $G_{linear} = 10^{G_{dBi}/10}$.

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**

The result of $\approx 1876$ km is unrealistic for a **real** MANET because:
- Friis assumes **free space** (no ground reflection, no obstructions).
- Real MANETs operate in cluttered environments → use **two-ray ground** model.
- Regulatory limits (FCC/ETSI) cap $P_t$ to **1 W** in the 2.4 GHz band.
- Practical MANET range = **100–300 m** outdoor, **30–100 m** indoor.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of the **AODV (Ad hoc On-demand Distance Vector)** routing protocol. Describe the RREQ, RREP, and RERR message formats. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Two MANET nodes are 180 m apart, with a transmission range of 250 m. Node A moves at 4 m/s and Node B moves at 2 m/s in the **same direction**. Find the link lifetime. If each intermediate node adds 3 ms processing delay and hops are 5, calculate the end-to-end delay. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to Q.B(a) — AODV Working (7 Marks)

**Introduction:**
**AODV (Ad hoc On-demand Distance Vector)** is a **reactive** routing protocol standardized in **RFC 3561**. It discovers a route to a destination only when a source node has data to send, minimizing routing overhead in idle networks.

**Working — Three Core Messages** (Refer to Section 4.2 diagram for visual):

1. **RREQ (Route Request):**
   - Broadcast by source when no route to destination is known.
   - Contains: `<Source IP, Source Seq#, Broadcast ID, Dest IP, Dest Seq#, Hop Count>`.
   - Each intermediate node rebroadcasts RREQ **only once** (identified by Source IP + Broadcast ID).

2. **RREP (Route Reply):**
   - Generated by destination (or an intermediate node with a *fresh enough* route).
   - Contains: `<Source IP, Dest IP, Dest Seq#, Hop Count, Lifetime>`.
   - Unicast back along the **reverse path** established by RREQ traversal.

3. **RERR (Route Error):**
   - Sent when a link breaks (detected via HELLO timeout or link-layer feedback).
   - Contains: `<Unreachable Dest IP, Unreachable Dest Seq#>`.
   - Propagates to all active sources using the broken link.

**Key Features of AODV:**
- Uses **destination sequence numbers** to ensure loop-free routes and freshness.
- Maintains only **active routes** in the routing table (no periodic updates).
- Supports **local repair** of broken links before triggering full re-discovery.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Link Lifetime & End-to-End Delay (7 Marks)

**Part 1 — Link Lifetime (3 marks):**

**Given:** $r = 180$ m, $R = 250$ m, $v_A = 4$ m/s, $v_B = 2$ m/s, **same direction**.

**Step 1 — Relative velocity.**

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{rel} &= \vert v_A - v_B \vert = \vert 4 - 2 \vert = 2 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** When nodes move in the **same direction**, the relative speed of separation is the **difference** of speeds (not the sum).

**Step 2 — Link lifetime.**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{link} &= \dfrac{R - r}{v_{rel}} = \dfrac{250 - 180}{2} = \dfrac{70}{2} = 35 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part 2 — End-to-End Delay (4 marks):**

**Given:** Hops = 5, per-hop processing $T_{proc} = 3$ ms, per-hop distance not specified — assume $d_{hop}$ such that $T_{prop}$ is negligible.

**Step 1 — Processing delay total.**

There are **4 intermediate nodes** (hops − 1, since destination does not forward):

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{proc,total} &= 4 \times 3 \text{ ms} = 12 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Total end-to-end delay.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{E2E} &= T_{proc,total} + T_{prop,total} \approx 12 \text{ ms} + \text{negligible} \approx 12 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** A link lifetime of **35 s** is acceptable for reactive routing (AODV discovery is typically < 1 s), so this mobility scenario is **MANET-friendly**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (KTU Examiner's Pitfall List):**
> 1. **Forgetting absolute value in $v_{rel}$** — If $v_A < v_B$ in same direction, the relative velocity is still positive; use $\vert \cdot \vert$.
> 2. **Using $v_A + v_B$ for same direction** — that's only for **opposite** directions.
> 3. **Counting destination as a forwarder** in delay calculation — destination does *not* process for forwarding.
> 4. **Mixing up $R_{max}$ formula units** — always use SI (meters, Watts, Hz) in the Friis equation.
> 5. **Writing RREQ unicast by mistake** — RREQ is **always broadcast**; RREP is unicast.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Points — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **MANET = infrastructure-less + multi-hop + self-organizing** — three keywords that must appear in any definition.
- ✅ **Routing protocols:** DSDV/OLSR (proactive), AODV/DSR (reactive), ZRP (hybrid).
- ✅ **AODV** is the most-tested protocol — know RREQ, RREP, RERR functions.
- ✅ **Friis equation** gives free-space range; **two-ray ground** is used in real MANETs above the break-point distance.
- ✅ **Relative velocity** for link lifetime: **sum** (opposite directions), **difference** (same direction).
- ✅ **End-to-end delay** ≈ $H \cdot T_{prop} + (H-1) \cdot T_{proc}$ where $H$ = number of hops.
- ✅ **dBm to Watts:** $P(\text{W}) = 10^{(P_{dBm}-30)/10}$.
- ✅ **ISM bands** for MANETs: 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz — unlicensed, congested.
- ✅ **Hidden terminal problem** is solved by **RTS/CTS** in IEEE 802.11 DCF.
- ✅ **Challenges:** dynamic topology, energy, bandwidth, security, scalability, QoS.
- ✅ **Mobility models:** Random Waypoint (most common), Gauss-Markov, RPGM.
- ✅ **Practical range:** 100–300 m outdoor, 30–100 m indoor — don't write the Friis-derived value as the practical one.
- ✅ **Sequence numbers in AODV** prevent routing loops and ensure freshness.
- ✅ **Multi-hop** is the defining feature — if a question says "single-hop wireless," it's NOT a pure MANET scenario.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radio Wave Propagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Radio Wave Propagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Radio Wave Propagation?

**Radio Wave Propagation** is the phenomenon by which electromagnetic (EM) energy radiated from a transmitting antenna travels through the Earth's atmosphere (or free space) and is ultimately received at a distant receiving antenna. The behavior of the wave — its amplitude, phase, polarization, and path — is governed by the frequency of operation, the electrical properties of the medium it travels through, and the geometry of the Earth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *Radio wave propagation is the study of the various mechanisms by which electromagnetic energy is transferred from a transmitting antenna to a receiving antenna through the atmosphere, including the effects of the Earth's surface, troposphere, and ionosphere on the propagating wave.*

The science of radio wave propagation is fundamentally important because **without a predictable propagation path, no communication link can be engineered reliably.** Antennas only convert guided waves into free-space waves; the medium decides whether the wave reaches the receiver with adequate signal strength.

## 1.2 The Three Primary Modes of Propagation

At radio frequencies (roughly **3 kHz to 300 GHz**), energy from a transmitting antenna reaches a receiver via one (or a combination) of the following mechanisms:

| S.No | Mode of Propagation | Typical Frequency Band | Governing Layer |
|:----:|:--------------------|:----------------------:|:----------------|
| 1 | **Ground Wave (Surface Wave)** | 30 kHz – 3 MHz (VLF, LF, MF) | Earth's surface |
| 2 | **Sky Wave (Ionospheric Wave)** | 3 MHz – 30 MHz (HF) | Ionosphere |
| 3 | **Space Wave (Tropospheric / LOS)** | 30 MHz – 300 GHz (VHF, UHF, SHF, EHF) | Troposphere |

> [!NOTE]
> The boundary between the modes is not rigid; e.g., MF signals may use both ground wave and sky wave during the day, while VHF signals may use tropospheric ducting under special atmospheric conditions.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Stones, Bounces and Light Beams"

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a swimming pool and want to send a message to a friend at the other end:

- **Ground Wave** → You walk along the edge of the pool (the signal **hugs the ground**, losing energy as it scrapes against the surface).
- **Sky Wave** → You throw a stone so that it bounces off the pool's cover (the ionosphere) and lands on your friend (the signal **leaps** between sky and ground).
- **Space Wave** → You shine a torch directly at your friend across the pool (the signal travels in a **straight line of sight**).

This simple analogy captures the three fundamental behaviors of radio waves in nature: they **slide**, **bounce**, or **shoot**.

## 1.4 Key Atmospheric Layers — A Quick Snapshot

| Layer | Altitude Range | Electrical Significance |
|:------|:--------------:|:------------------------|
| **Troposphere** | 0 – 10 km (approx.) | Causes refraction, ducting, scattering (VHF & above) |
| **Stratosphere** | 10 – 50 km | Minor effect on radio waves |
| **Ionosphere** | 60 – 400 km | Contains free electrons; reflects HF waves back to Earth |
| **Free Space / Exosphere** | > 400 km | Vacuum; used by satellite links |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **refractive index of the troposphere decreases with altitude**, bending radio waves slightly toward the Earth. This is the same reason the sky looks blue and the horizon appears higher than it is. Engineers exploit this phenomenon by using an **effective Earth radius factor $k = 4/3$** in line-of-sight calculations.

## 1.5 Why Study Radio Wave Propagation?

For a KTU engineer, radio wave propagation is not merely academic — it directly determines:
- The **link budget** of a cellular base station.
- The choice of **frequency band** for AM broadcast vs. FM broadcast vs. satellite TV.
- The **antenna height** required for television coverage.
- The **fade margin** required for reliable HF communication.

Without an understanding of propagation, no wireless system can be designed, deployed, or maintained.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mode 1 — Ground Wave Propagation

### 2.1.1 Mechanism
A vertically polarized wave that travels along the surface of the Earth is called a **ground (or surface) wave**. The wave induces currents in the ground, and energy is continuously extracted from the wave to supply these losses. As a result, the field strength decays rapidly with distance — typically following a logarithmic law.

### 2.1.2 Salient Features
- Must be **vertically polarized** (horizontal polarization suffers severe attenuation because the Earth's surface short-circuits the E-field).
- Effective at **low and medium frequencies** (LF, MF) — e.g., AM radio (~ 540 kHz – 1600 kHz).
- **Range:** Up to approximately **200 km** over land; up to **400 km** over sea water (sea water is a better conductor, hence lower loss).
- Wave is **diffracted around the curvature of the Earth** because of its long wavelength.

### 2.1.3 Key Equations

**Surface wave field strength (flat Earth approximation, Somerville formula):**

$$
E \;=\; \frac{120\pi h_t I}{\lambda d} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 \left(\dfrac{\cos\theta}{\sigma}\right)^2}}
$$

For large distances where ground loss dominates:

$$
E \;\propto\; \frac{1}{d^2}
$$

The dominant trend is that the field strength falls off as **$1/d$ in the near field** and **$1/d^2$ in the far field (where the ground becomes lossy)**.

## 2.2 Mode 2 — Sky Wave (Ionospheric) Propagation

### 2.2.1 Mechanism
HF waves (3 – 30 MHz) launched at an oblique angle penetrate the **ionosphere**, a region of the upper atmosphere (60 – 400 km) ionized primarily by solar ultraviolet radiation. The free electrons re-radiate the incoming wave, and if the electron density is sufficient, the wave is **refracted back to Earth**, much like a light ray in a graded-index medium.

### 2.2.2 Structure of the Ionosphere

The ionosphere is divided into four principal layers:

| Layer | Altitude (km) | Daytime Behavior | Nighttime Behavior | Critical Frequency |
|:------|:-------------:|:-----------------|:-------------------|:-------------------|
| **D** | 60 – 90 | Exists; absorbs MF & lower HF | Disappears | Below MF range |
| **E** | 100 – 140 | Exists; reflects HF | Weakens significantly | ~ 4 MHz |
| **F1** | 150 – 200 | Exists during day only | Merges with F2 | ~ 5 MHz |
| **F2** | 250 – 400 | Exists; highest electron density | Persists; main reflection layer at night | ~ 8 – 12 MHz |

> [!NOTE]
> The **D layer is the principal absorber** during the daytime. Its disappearance at night is the reason **AM broadcast signals travel much farther after sunset** — the absorption suddenly vanishes, allowing sky-wave propagation to dominate.

### 2.2.3 Critical Frequency, MUF, OWF, and Skip Distance

These four parameters are the **most heavily tested** concepts in KTU examinations for this module.

**1. Critical Frequency ($f_c$)**
The maximum frequency that will be reflected back to Earth when the wave is sent **straight up (vertical incidence)**.

$$
f_c \;=\; 9 \sqrt{N_{max}} \;\; \text{Hz}
$$

where $N_{max}$ is the maximum electron density per cubic meter of the ionospheric layer.

**2. Maximum Usable Frequency (MUF)**
For **oblique incidence** at an angle $\theta$ from the normal, the highest frequency that returns to Earth is:

$$
MUF \;=\; f_c \sec(\theta) \;=\; \frac{f_c}{\cos(\theta)}
$$

**3. Optimum Working Frequency (OWF)**
In practice, engineers use about **85 % of the MUF** to provide a **fade margin** against ionospheric variability:

$$
OWF \;=\; 0.85 \times MUF
$$

**4. Skip Distance ($D_{skip}$)**
The **shortest distance** from the transmitter at which a sky wave returns to Earth. Waves with elevation angle below a certain critical value escape into space; only those above it return.

$$
D_{skip} \;=\; 2 R \psi
$$

where $R$ is the Earth's radius ($\approx 6371$ km) and $\psi$ is the half-angle subtended at the Earth's center.

**5. Virtual Height ($h'$)**
Because the wave is refracted gradually inside the ionosphere, it appears to be reflected from a height $h'$ that is **greater** than the true reflection height $h$. The relationship is:

$$
h \;=\; h' \left(1 - \frac{f_c}{f}\right)
$$

**6. Secant Law (Martyn's Equivalent Theorem)**
For a given oblique-incidence frequency $f$ to return from a layer of critical frequency $f_c$:

$$
f \;=\; f_c \sec(i)
$$

where $i$ is the angle of incidence at the ionospheric layer.

## 2.3 Mode 3 — Space Wave (Line-of-Sight) Propagation

### 2.3.1 Mechanism
At VHF and above, sky-wave reflection is no longer effective (the wave passes straight through the ionosphere), and ground-wave range is severely limited. Communication must therefore rely on the **direct wave** plus a **ground-reflected wave**, both travelling essentially in straight lines. This is the regime used by **FM radio, television, cellular, radar, and satellite links**.

### 2.3.2 Effective Earth Radius and the $k$-Factor

The troposphere's refractive index decreases with height, causing **gradual bending of radio waves toward the Earth**. To simplify LOS calculations, we replace the true Earth radius $R$ with an **effective Earth radius** $kR$:

$$
R_{eff} \;=\; k R \quad \text{where} \quad k \;=\; \frac{4}{3} \quad \text{(standard atmosphere)}
$$

### 2.3.3 Line-of-Sight Distance

The maximum LOS distance between a transmitter of height $h_t$ and a receiver of height $h_r$ is:

$$
d_{LOS} \;=\; \sqrt{2 k R h_t} \;+\; \sqrt{2 k R h_r}
$$

Substituting $k = 4/3$ and $R = 6371$ km, and using $h_t$, $h_r$ in meters, $d$ in km:

$$
d_{LOS} \;\approx\; 4.12 \sqrt{h_t} \;+\; 4.12 \sqrt{h_r} \;\;\text{km}
$$

### 2.3.4 Free-Space Path Loss (Friis Equation)

$$
L_{FS} \;=\; \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}
$$

In dB form, with $f$ in MHz and $d$ in km:

$$
L_{FS\,(dB)} \;=\; 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})
$$

### 2.3.5 Received Field Strength (Far Field)

$$
E \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{30 \, P_t \, G_t}}{d} \quad \text{(V/m)}
$$

where $P_t$ is the transmitted power in watts, $G_t$ is the gain of the transmitting antenna, and $d$ is in meters.

## 2.4 Tropospheric Duct Propagation

Under **super-refractive conditions** (temperature inversion, rapid humidity decrease with altitude), the refractive index gradient becomes large enough to **trap** VHF/UHF waves in a waveguide-like layer called a **duct**. This phenomenon can extend the range far beyond the normal LOS distance.

| Condition | Refractive Index Gradient | Result |
|:----------|:--------------------------|:-------|
| **Sub-refraction** | Positive gradient | Wave bends away from Earth (reduced range) |
| **Normal refraction** | $-39$ N-units/km | Standard (uses $k = 4/3$) |
| **Super-refraction** | Steep negative gradient | Wave bends more strongly toward Earth |
| **Ducting / Trapping** | Extremely steep negative gradient | Wave trapped in a layer |

## 2.5 Fading — The Enemy of Reliable Communication

**Fading** is the **time variation of signal strength** at the receiver. KTU examiners expect students to identify the various types:

| Type of Fading | Cause | Mitigation |
|:---------------|:------|:-----------|
| **Absorption (ionospheric)** | D-layer absorption of HF | Use higher OWF |
| **Multi-path (interference) fading** | Random superposition of direct + reflected waves | Frequency diversity, antenna design |
| **Skip fading** | MUF variation causing signal to fall inside skip zone | Wait for favorable ionospheric conditions |
| **Polarization fading** | Faraday rotation in ionosphere | Use circular polarization |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU question paper on this module.**

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------------------|
| 1 | $f_c = 9\sqrt{N_{max}}$ | Critical frequency (Hz) | $N_{max}$ = max electron density (electrons/m³) |
| 2 | $MUF = f_c \sec(\theta)$ | Maximum usable frequency (Hz) | $\theta$ = angle of incidence |
| 3 | $OWF = 0.85 \times MUF$ | Optimum working frequency (Hz) | — |
| 4 | $h = h'(1 - f_c/f)$ | True reflection height (m) | $h'$ = virtual height (m) |
| 5 | $D_{skip} = 2R\psi$ | Skip distance (km) | $R$ = 6371 km, $\psi$ in radians |
| 6 | $d_{LOS} = 4.12\sqrt{h_t} + 4.12\sqrt{h_r}$ | LOS distance (km) | $h_t, h_r$ in meters |
| 7 | $L_{FS} = (4\pi d/\lambda)^2$ | Free-space path loss (ratio) | $d$ in m, $\lambda$ in m |
| 8 | $L_{FS(dB)} = 32.45 + 20\log f + 20\log d$ | Path loss in dB | $f$ in MHz, $d$ in km |
| 9 | $E = \sqrt{30 P_t G_t}/d$ | Far-field E (V/m) | $P_t$ in W, $d$ in m |
| 10 | $N = (n-1) \times 10^6$ | N-units (refractivity) | $n$ = refractive index |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents the **most critical derivations** required for KTU 2024 Scheme board examinations. Each step is expanded for full clarity.

## 3.1 Derivation: Critical Frequency $f_c$ from Plasma Physics

When an EM wave enters the ionosphere, free electrons oscillate at the **plasma frequency**:

$$
f_p \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{N e^{2}}{m \varepsilon_0}}
$$

where:
- $N$ = electron density (electrons / m³)
- $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C (electron charge)
- $m = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg (electron mass)
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m

A wave is reflected by a layer when the operating frequency equals the **local plasma frequency** at the peak of that layer (i.e., at maximum $N$).

**Step 1.** Substitute the standard physical constants into the plasma frequency formula:

$$
f_p \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{(1.602\times10^{-19})^2 \cdot N}{(9.109\times10^{-31})(8.854\times10^{-12})}}
$$

**Step 2.** Evaluate the constant term numerically:

$$
\frac{e^{2}}{m\varepsilon_{0}} \;=\; \frac{2.566\times10^{-38}}{8.067\times10^{-42}} \;\approx\; 3180 \;\text{(SI units)}
$$

**Step 3.** Therefore:

$$
f_p \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{3180 \, N}
$$

**Step 4.** Take the square root:

$$
f_p \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{3180}\,\sqrt{N} \;=\; \frac{56.4}{2\pi}\sqrt{N}
$$

**Step 5.** Simplify the numerical coefficient:

$$
\frac{56.4}{2\pi} \;\approx\; 8.98 \;\approx\; 9
$$

**Step 6.** Hence the textbook formula (also called the **Secant Law for vertical incidence**):

$$
\boxed{\,f_c \;=\; 9\,\sqrt{N_{max}}\;\text{ Hz}\,}
$$

This compact relation is one of the **most frequently tested formulas** in the KTU syllabus.

## 3.2 Derivation: Skip Distance $D_{skip}$

Consider a wave launched at an elevation angle $\Delta$ (angle above the horizontal) from a transmitter on Earth's surface. It penetrates the ionosphere and is reflected at virtual height $h'$, returning to Earth at point B.

![Skip distance geometry] — Place the Earth's center O at the origin. The transmitter T and the receiver B lie on the surface. The reflection point P is at virtual height $h'$ above the ground. Let $R$ be the radius of the Earth.

**Step 1.** Apply the geometry of the isoceles triangle $\triangle OPB$:

The angle at the center is $2\psi$, the chord length is $D_{skip}$, and the apex lies at height $h' + R$ above the center.

By the law of sines in $\triangle OPB$:

$$
\frac{D_{skip}}{\sin(2\psi)} \;=\; \frac{R + h'}{\sin(90^{\circ} + \Delta)}
$$

**Step 2.** Recognize that $\sin(90^{\circ}+\Delta) = \cos(\Delta)$. Also, in the small-angle limit (which is valid for HF propagation), $\sin(2\psi) \approx 2\psi$ and $h' \ll R$:

$$
\frac{D_{skip}}{2\psi} \;\approx\; \frac{R}{\cos(\Delta)}
$$

**Step 3.** Solve for $D_{skip}$:

$$
D_{skip} \;\approx\; \frac{2R\psi}{\cos(\Delta)}
$$

**Step 4.** For a *grazing* wave ($\Delta \to 0$, $\cos\Delta \to 1$):

$$
\boxed{\,D_{skip} \;=\; 2R\psi\,}
$$

**Step 5.** In more useful units (km, with $R = 6371$ km and $\psi$ in degrees):

$$
D_{skip\,(km)} \;\approx\; \frac{2 \times 6371 \times \psi^{\circ}}{57.296} \;\approx\; 222.4 \,\psi^{\circ}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Usable Frequency (MUF) and Secant Law

We use **Martyn's Equivalent Path Theorem**, which states that the **oblique-incidence** case is equivalent to a **vertical-incidence** case at a frequency reduced by a factor of $\cos(\theta)$:

**Step 1.** Consider a wave entering the ionosphere at an angle of incidence $\theta$ (measured from the normal) with operating frequency $f$.

**Step 2.** For the wave to be reflected, the equivalent vertical frequency $f \cos(\theta)$ must be at least $f_c$:

$$
f\cos(\theta) \;\geq\; f_c
$$

**Step 3.** The largest frequency for which reflection just occurs is the MUF:

$$
MUF \cdot \cos(\theta) \;=\; f_c
$$

**Step 4.** Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\,MUF \;=\; \frac{f_c}{\cos(\theta)} \;=\; f_c \,\sec(\theta)\,}
$$

**Step 5.** Combining with $f_c = 9\sqrt{N_{max}}$, we get the MUF in terms of electron density:

$$
MUF \;=\; 9 \,\sec(\theta)\,\sqrt{N_{max}}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: True Reflection Height $h$ from Virtual Height $h'$

The **virtual height $h'$** is the height at which the wave *would* have been reflected had it travelled in a straight line at the speed of light. The **true height $h$** is the actual height at which the wave is reflected (where the electron density peaks).

**Step 1.** For an oblique wave at frequency $f$ reflecting off a layer with critical frequency $f_c$, the secant law gives the angle of incidence:

$$
\cos(\theta) \;=\; \frac{f_c}{f}
$$

**Step 2.** The equivalent vertical-incidence wave reflects at the true height $h$ (lower than $h'$ because the oblique wave penetrates less deeply).

**Step 3.** By geometry of the isoceles triangle, the true height is:

$$
h \;=\; h'\,\cos(\theta)
$$

**Step 4.** Substitute the value of $\cos\theta$:

$$
\boxed{\,h \;=\; h'\,\frac{f_c}{f} \;=\; h'\left(1 - \frac{f_c}{f}\right) \cdot \frac{f}{f}\,}
$$

A more common form (which is the KTU textbook expression) writes:

$$
h \;=\; h'\,\left(\frac{f_c}{f}\right) \;\;\text{or equivalently}\;\; h \;=\; h'\,\cos(\theta)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU accepted form is $h = h' \cos(\theta) = h' \cdot (f_c/f)$. As $f \to f_c$, $h \to h'$.

## 3.5 Derivation: Line-of-Sight (LOS) Distance

A transmitter of height $h_t$ above a smooth Earth. The Earth is modelled as a sphere of radius $R$. The LOS distance is the chord along which the line from the top of the antenna just grazes the Earth's surface.

**Step 1.** Apply the Pythagorean theorem in the right triangle formed by the Earth's centre, the base of the antenna, and the top of the antenna:

$$
(R + h_t)^{2} \;=\; R^{2} + d_1^{2}
$$

**Step 2.** Expand the left-hand side:

$$
R^{2} + 2Rh_t + h_t^{2} \;=\; R^{2} + d_1^{2}
$$

**Step 3.** Cancel $R^{2}$ and neglect $h_t^{2}$ (since $h_t \ll R$):

$$
2Rh_t \;\approx\; d_1^{2}
$$

**Step 4.** Solve for $d_1$:

$$
d_1 \;\approx\; \sqrt{2 R h_t}
$$

**Step 5.** Repeat for the receiver antenna of height $h_r$ and sum the two distances:

$$
\boxed{\,d_{LOS} \;\approx\; \sqrt{2 k R h_t} \;+\; \sqrt{2 k R h_r}\,}
$$

where the factor $k = 4/3$ accounts for tropospheric refraction.

**Step 6.** Substituting $k = 4/3$ and $R = 6371$ km, with $h_t$ and $h_r$ in meters, and converting to km:

$$
d_{LOS} \;\approx\; 4.12 \sqrt{h_t} + 4.12 \sqrt{h_r} \quad \text{km}
$$

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 10 MHz wave is transmitted at an angle of incidence of 60° at the ionosphere. The virtual height of the layer is 300 km. The maximum electron density is $10^{12}$ electrons/m³. Find (a) the critical frequency, (b) the MUF, (c) the true height, and (d) the skip distance.

**Given:** $f = 10 \text{ MHz} = 10^{7}$ Hz, $\theta = 60°$, $h' = 300$ km, $N_{max} = 10^{12}$ m⁻³.

**(a) Critical frequency:**

$$
f_c \;=\; 9 \sqrt{N_{max}} \;=\; 9 \sqrt{10^{12}} \;=\; 9 \times 10^{6} \;\text{Hz} \;=\; 9 \;\text{MHz}
$$

**(b) Maximum Usable Frequency:**

$$
MUF \;=\; f_c \sec(\theta) \;=\; 9 \times 10^{6} \times \sec(60^{\circ}) \;=\; 9 \times 10^{6} \times 2 \;=\; 18 \;\text{MHz}
$$

**(c) True height:**

$$
h \;=\; h' \cos(\theta) \;=\; 300 \times \cos(60^{\circ}) \;=\; 300 \times 0.5 \;=\; 150 \;\text{km}
$$

**(d) Skip distance:** Since the wave just barely reflects (frequency equals MUF), $\psi$ for the MUF case is computed by first finding the half-angle. For a 60° angle of incidence, the geometry gives:

$$
\sin(\psi) \;=\; \frac{R \sin(\theta)}{R + h'} \;\approx\; \frac{6371 \times 0.866}{6371 + 300} \;\approx\; 0.827
$$

$$
\psi \;\approx\; 55.8^{\circ} \;\approx\; 0.974 \;\text{rad}
$$

$$
D_{skip} \;\approx\; 2R\psi \;\approx\; 2 \times 6371 \times 0.974 \;\approx\; 12{,}407 \;\text{km}
$$

These four answers illustrate the **standard KTU 14-mark problem structure** — students should aim to present all four sub-parts methodically.

## 3.7 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Mechanism | Reflection / Refraction | Frequency Range | Typical Range | Key Equation |
|:----------|:-----------------------:|:---------------:|:-------------:|:-------------|
| Ground wave | Diffraction around Earth | 30 kHz – 3 MHz | ≤ 400 km | $E \propto 1/d^2$ |
| Sky wave | Refraction by ionosphere | 3 MHz – 30 MHz | ≤ 4000 km | $f = f_c \sec\theta$ |
| Space wave | Direct LOS | 30 MHz – 300 GHz | ≤ 80 km (terrestrial) | $d_{LOS} = 4.12\sqrt{h_t} + 4.12\sqrt{h_r}$ |
| Satellite wave | LOS through atmosphere | 1 GHz – 50 GHz | 36,000 km (GEO) | $L_{FS} = 32.45 + 20\log f + 20\log d$ |
| Tropospheric ducting | Trapping in atmospheric layer | 30 MHz – 3 GHz | Up to 1000 km | $M$-profile, gradient < -157 N/km |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Radio Wave Propagation

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Transmitting Antenna] --> nodeB[Radio Wave Propagation]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Ground Wave]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Sky Wave]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Space Wave]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Tropospheric Duct]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Surface Wave LF MF 30 kHz to 3 MHz]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Single Hop HF 3 to 30 MHz]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Multi Hop HF 3 to 30 MHz]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[Line of Sight VHF UHF SHF 30 MHz to 300 GHz]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Direct Plus Reflected]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[Super Refraction 30 MHz to 3 GHz]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[Trapping in Temperature Inversion Layer]
    nodeC1 --> nodeG[Receiving Antenna]
    nodeD1 --> nodeG
    nodeD2 --> nodeG
    nodeE1 --> nodeG
    nodeE2 --> nodeG
    nodeF1 --> nodeG
    nodeF2 --> nodeG
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Mechanism of Sky Wave (Ionospheric) Reflection

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Transmitter on Earth Surface] --> nodeB[Wave Travels Upward at Elevation Angle]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Refraction in D Layer Absorption]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Refraction in E Layer Partial]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Refraction in F Layer Full Reflection]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Wave Returns to Earth at Skip Distance]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Receiver at Skip Zone Edge]
    nodeH[Sun Radiation] --> nodeC
    nodeH --> nodeD
    nodeH --> nodeE
```

## 4.3 Flowchart — Selection of Propagation Mode for a Given Frequency

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Choose Operating Frequency F] --> nodeB{F less than 3 MHz}
    nodeB -->|Yes| nodeC[Use Ground Wave Surface Propagation]
    nodeB -->|No| nodeD{F between 3 and 30 MHz}
    nodeD -->|Yes| nodeE[Use Sky Wave Ionospheric Bounce]
    nodeD -->|No| nodeF{F above 30 MHz}
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG[Use Space Wave Line of Sight]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Range less than 80 km}
    nodeH -->|Yes| nodeI[Use Terrestrial LOS]
    nodeH -->|No| nodeJ[Use Satellite or Tropospheric Duct]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Ionosphere Layering and Effect on a HF Wave

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Solar UV and X Rays] --> nodeB[Atmospheric Ionization]
    nodeB --> nodeC[D Layer 60 to 90 km]
    nodeB --> nodeD[E Layer 100 to 140 km]
    nodeB --> nodeE[F1 Layer 150 to 200 km]
    nodeB --> nodeF[F2 Layer 250 to 400 km]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Absorbs MF and Low HF]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Reflects HF up to about 4 MHz]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Reflects HF up to about 5 MHz]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Reflects HF up to 8 to 12 MHz]
    nodeG --> nodeK[HF Signal at Receiver]
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Path Loss vs Distance Relationship

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Transmitter Power Pt] --> nodeB[Antenna Gain Gt]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Free Space Path Loss Lfs]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Atmospheric Absorption La]

    nodeE --> nodeF[Received Power Pr]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Receiver Sensitivity Threshold]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *Define the term "skip distance" in sky-wave propagation. Explain the existence of the "skip zone" with a neat diagram.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Skip distance** is the **shortest distance from the transmitter** to the point on the Earth's surface where the sky wave returns after reflection from the ionosphere. Waves launched at very low elevation angles either fail to be reflected back (they escape into space) or arrive at distances less than the skip distance. The **skip zone** is the annular region on the ground between the outer limit of the ground-wave coverage and the inner edge of the sky-wave return. In this zone, neither ground wave nor sky wave provides usable signal, so the receiver hears essentially nothing.

A simple sketch:

- Draw the Earth as a circle.
- Draw a tangent line from the transmitter to the ionosphere.
- The chord from the transmitter to the first return point on the opposite side defines $D_{skip}$.
- The skip zone lies between the end of the ground-wave range and the first sky-wave return.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *What is meant by "virtual height" of an ionospheric layer? How does it differ from the "actual height" of reflection?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **virtual height $h'$** is the apparent height at which a sky wave appears to be reflected, assuming the wave travels in a straight line at the speed of light from the transmitter to the reflection point and back. The **actual height $h$** is the true altitude inside the ionosphere at which the wave is refracted back. The two are related by:

$$
h \;=\; h' \cos(\theta) \;=\; h' \left(\frac{f_c}{f}\right)
$$

Thus, the virtual height is **always greater than the actual height** (except at vertical incidence where $h = h'$). The virtual height is measured using **ionosondes** that record the time delay of the reflected pulse.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
>
> *(a)* With a neat sketch, explain the **structure of the ionosphere**, indicating the four principal layers, their typical altitude ranges, electron density behaviour, and influence on HF propagation. (7 marks)
>
> *(b)* Derive the expression for the **maximum usable frequency (MUF)** in terms of the critical frequency and the angle of incidence. What is the **optimum working frequency (OWF)** and why is it used in practice? (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Part (a)

The ionosphere is the region of the Earth's upper atmosphere (roughly 60 – 400 km) where the concentration of free electrons is high enough to affect the propagation of radio waves. It is formed by **ionization of atmospheric gases** due to **solar ultraviolet and X-ray radiation**.

**Layer-by-layer description:**

**D Layer (60 – 90 km):**
- Forms only during the day (recombines at night).
- Electron density $\sim 10^8$ to $10^9$ electrons/m³.
- **High collision frequency** with neutral molecules → causes significant **absorption** of MF and lower HF signals.
- Disappearance at night is why AM broadcast range increases after sunset.

**E Layer (100 – 140 km):**
- Present during the day; weakens at night.
- Electron density $\sim 10^{11}$ electrons/m³.
- Reflects HF waves below its critical frequency ($\sim 3$ – 4 MHz).
- Sometimes exhibits **sporadic E** (intense, irregular patches that can reflect VHF).

**F1 Layer (150 – 200 km):**
- Exists mainly in daytime; merges with F2 at night.
- Electron density $\sim 2 \times 10^{11}$ to $5 \times 10^{11}$ electrons/m³.
- Critical frequency $\sim 5$ MHz.

**F2 Layer (250 – 400 km):**
- The most dense and persistent layer.
- Electron density $\sim 10^{12}$ electrons/m³ during daytime peak.
- Critical frequency $\sim 8$ – 12 MHz.
- **Primary layer for long-distance HF communication** because it reflects the highest range of HF frequencies and persists day and night.

The combined sketch should show the Earth, the four layers at their respective altitudes, and an HF wave being refracted back from the F2 layer.

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation of MUF:**

Consider an EM wave of frequency $f$ entering the ionosphere at an oblique angle of incidence $\theta$ (measured from the vertical normal to the layer). The wave will be reflected back only if its equivalent vertical frequency equals the critical frequency of the layer at its peak.

The **equivalent vertical frequency** of an obliquely-incident wave is $f \cos(\theta)$. Therefore the condition for reflection is:

$$
f \cos(\theta) \;\geq\; f_c
$$

The largest value of $f$ for which this holds is the MUF:

$$
\boxed{\,MUF \;=\; f_c \sec(\theta) \;=\; \frac{f_c}{\cos(\theta)}\,}
$$

**Optimum Working Frequency (OWF):**

The OWF is the actual frequency selected for reliable communication, defined as:

$$
\boxed{\,OWF \;=\; 0.85 \times MUF\,}
$$

**Why use OWF?**

The ionosphere is a dynamic medium — electron density varies with time of day, season, and solar activity. Operating right at the MUF is risky because:
- Small reductions in electron density can cause the wave to escape into space.
- Multi-path and fading effects are more severe near the MUF.

By operating at 85 % of MUF, we provide a **15 % margin (fade margin)** that ensures the link remains closed even under typical ionospheric variations. OWF is therefore the **practical working frequency** recommended by international frequency-planning authorities (e.g., ITU).

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
>
> *(a)* Explain the phenomenon of **ground-wave propagation**. Discuss the factors that affect the ground-wave field strength. (7 marks)
>
> *(b)* With a neat diagram, derive an expression for the **line-of-sight (LOS) distance** between two antennas of heights $h_t$ and $h_r$ above the Earth's surface, taking the standard atmospheric refraction into account. A transmitter of height 100 m and a receiver of height 25 m are used. Calculate the maximum LOS range. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Ground-wave propagation** (also called **surface-wave propagation**) is the mode in which a radio wave travels along the surface of the Earth, following its curvature. The Earth and the ionosphere act as a **two-conductor guiding system**, and the wave is partially **diffracted** over the curved surface.

**Key features:**

1. The wave is **vertically polarized** (a horizontal E-field would be short-circuited by the conducting ground).
2. Energy is continuously lost to the ground because the wave **induces ground currents**, which dissipate power as $I^{2}R$ losses.
3. The wave is **diffracted** over the curved Earth, allowing it to reach beyond the optical horizon.
4. The wave's **E-field must be normal to the surface** for low-loss propagation.

**Factors affecting ground-wave field strength:**

| Factor | Effect on Field Strength |
|:-------|:--------------------------|
| **Frequency of operation** | Higher frequency → greater attenuation → reduced range. Ground wave is most useful below 3 MHz. |
| **Electrical properties of the ground** | Higher conductivity and/or higher permittivity → lower attenuation. Sea water is best; dry rocky ground is worst. |
| **Polarization of the wave** | Vertical polarization is necessary for low loss. |
| **Antenna height** | Greater height above ground → stronger field. |
| **Distance from transmitter** | Field strength falls off (approximately as $1/d$ in the near field and $1/d^{2}$ in the far field). |
| **Surface curvature** | Beyond a few hundred km, diffraction losses increase rapidly. |
| **Atmospheric conditions** | Humidity, temperature, and pressure variations affect the refractive index and hence the field strength. |

**Numerical example:** A 1 MHz transmitter delivers a stronger ground-wave signal than a 10 MHz transmitter at the same distance, because the longer wavelength at 1 MHz diffracts more efficiently.

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation of LOS distance:**

The derivation is provided in detail in **Section 3.5** of these notes. The final result is:

$$
d_{LOS} \;\approx\; \sqrt{2 k R h_t} \;+\; \sqrt{2 k R h_r}
$$

For the **standard atmosphere**, the effective-earth-radius factor is $k = 4/3$, and the actual Earth radius is $R = 6371$ km.

**Numerical calculation:**

Given: $h_t = 100$ m, $h_r = 25$ m.

Convert heights to km: $h_t = 0.1$ km, $h_r = 0.025$ km.

$$
d_{LOS} \;\approx\; 4.12 \sqrt{0.1} + 4.12 \sqrt{0.025}
$$

$$
\;\approx\; 4.12 \times 0.3162 + 4.12 \times 0.1581
$$

$$
\;\approx\; 1.302 + 0.651 \;\approx\; 1.95 \;\text{km}
$$

**Maximum LOS range $\approx 1.95$ km.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Mark Deductions to Avoid:**
> 1. **Unit mixing:** Students often substitute $h_t$ in meters but forget to convert when the formula expects kilometers. The KTU accepted practice is to write $d_{LOS} \approx 4.12 \sqrt{h_t(\text{m})} + 4.12 \sqrt{h_r(\text{m})}$ km, OR convert heights to km first.
> 2. **Forgetting the $k$-factor:** Using $R$ instead of $kR = 4R/3$ leads to an under-estimate of the LOS distance by ~ 15 %, and the examiner will deduct one mark.
> 3. **Wrong MUF relation:** Writing $MUF = f_c \cos(\theta)$ instead of $f_c \sec(\theta)$ is a common sign error that costs full marks on the sub-question.
> 4. **Confusing virtual and true height:** Virtual height is always greater than or equal to the actual height. Drawing the ionosphere at virtual height $h'$ but writing "true height = $h'$" loses marks.
> 5. **Skipping diagrams:** KTU examiners award up to 2 marks for a clean, well-labelled diagram in 7-mark sub-questions. Always include a sketch.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Radio Wave Propagation**

- **Three modes of propagation** are: Ground wave (LF/MF), Sky wave (HF), Space wave (VHF and above).
- **Ground wave** is vertically polarized, hugs the surface, and is most effective over sea water due to its higher conductivity.
- **Sky wave** is reflected by the ionosphere; the **D layer absorbs**, the **E and F layers reflect**.
- **Critical frequency** $f_c = 9\sqrt{N_{max}}$ Hz — the highest frequency reflected at **vertical incidence**.
- **MUF** $ = f_c \sec(\theta)$ — the highest frequency reflected at **oblique incidence** with angle $\theta$ from the normal.
- **OWF** $= 0.85 \times MUF$ — used in practice to provide a **15 % fade margin**.
- **Skip distance** $D_{skip} = 2R\psi$ — the shortest distance the sky wave returns to Earth.
- **Virtual height** $h'$ is greater than **true height** $h$. They are related by $h = h' \cos(\theta) = h' (f_c / f)$.
- **Space wave** (LOS) range: $d_{LOS} = 4.12 \sqrt{h_t} + 4.12 \sqrt{h_r}$ km, where $h$ is in meters (with $k = 4/3$).
- **Free-space path loss in dB**: $L_{FS} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$.
- **Far-field E-field**: $E = \sqrt{30 P_t G_t} / d$ V/m.
- **Ducting** occurs when the refractive index gradient is extremely negative (e.g., temperature inversion); VHF/UHF waves are trapped in a layer.
- **Fading types**: absorption fading, multi-path fading, skip fading, polarization fading (Faraday rotation).
- **Effective Earth radius factor** $k$ = 4/3 for standard atmosphere (accounts for normal tropospheric refraction).
- **F2 layer** is the most important for HF long-distance communication because it has the highest electron density and persists day and night.
- **Faraday rotation** rotates the plane of polarization of a wave passing through the ionosphere in the presence of Earth's magnetic field — important for satellite links at VHF.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ground Wave Propagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Ground Wave Propagation # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Ground Wave Propagation** (also called **Surface Wave Propagation** or **Sommerfeld-Norton Wave**) is the mode of electromagnetic wave transmission in which the radio energy propagates from the transmitting antenna along the curvature of the Earth's surface, hugging the ground due to the finite conductivity of the terrestrial medium. The wave is partially guided by the boundary between the Earth's surface (a lossy dielectric / imperfect conductor) and the lower atmosphere (air), inducing surface currents that allow the wavefront to bend around the planet. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > "Ground wave propagation is the propagation of radio waves over the surface of the Earth, where the wave is guided by the conductive surface, leading to a surface wave that follows the Earth's curvature. It is the dominant mode for medium-wave (MW) broadcasting and long-wave (LW) communications in the **30 kHz – 3 MHz** (MF/LF) band." > [!NOTE] > **Key Boundary Condition for Existence:** Ground wave requires the **transmitting antenna to be vertically polarized** (or, more strictly, the electric field must possess a component normal to the Earth's surface). A horizontally polarized wave over a flat, finitely conducting Earth is rapidly attenuated and cannot propagate efficiently as a ground wave. ## 1.2 The Constituent Components of the Total Field At any receiving point over the Earth's surface, the total field is the phasor sum of three components: | Component | Origin | Behavior | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Direct Wave** | Line-of-sight path Tx → Rx | Dominates at short distances, VHF/UHF | | **Reflected Wave** | Bounces off the Earth's surface | Combines with direct wave to form space wave | | **Surface (Ground) Wave** | Glued to Earth's surface | Dominates at low frequencies and large horizontal distances | For frequencies below roughly **3 MHz**, the **direct and reflected waves** vanish (the antenna radiates almost vertically upward and sideward for short distances), and the **surface wave** becomes the principal mechanism of energy transport. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Hugging a Hot Pipe" Picture > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are holding a charged metal rod in your hand. When you brush it along a wet, slightly conducting surface (say, a damp concrete floor), you can feel the "buzz" or shock travelling *along* the surface — far beyond the reach of your arm. The energy does not shoot straight out; it **clings to and slides along** the surface, bending around corners. **Apply this to radio:** A vertical whip antenna acts like that charged rod. The Earth, being a (poor) conductor, behaves like the damp floor. The radio wave does not just fly in a straight line; it **induces currents in the soil**, and these induced surface currents re-radiate energy, **regenerating the wavefront** and allowing it to follow the curvature of the Earth. **Three Conditions for Strong Ground-Wave Propagation:** 1. **Low Frequency** → so the wave is not "too fast" for the surface currents to keep up. 2. **Vertical Polarization** → the E-field must penetrate the surface to induce conduction currents. 3. **Good Ground Conductivity** → moist soil, sea water (σ ≈ 4 S/m) is excellent; dry sand or rock (σ ≈ 10⁻³ S/m) is poor. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Free-space permittivity:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$ > - **Permeability of free space / Earth:** $\mu_0 = \mu = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$ > - **Velocity of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$ > - **Standard frequency bands:** VLF (3–30 kHz), LF (30–300 kHz), MF (300 kHz–3 MHz) > - **Sea water conductivity:** $\sigma \approx 4 \text{ S/m}$, $\varepsilon_r \approx 80$ > - **Average land conductivity:** $\sigma \approx 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ S/m}$, $\varepsilon_r \approx 15$ > - **Earth's radius:** $a = 6.37 \times 10^{6} \text{ m}$ --- # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physical Mechanism — Why the Wave "Hugs" the Earth The Earth is modelled as a **smooth, homogeneous, isotropic, lossy dielectric half-space** characterized by: - Relative permittivity: $\varepsilon_r$ - Conductivity: $\sigma$ (S/m) - Permeability: $\mu_0$ (non-magnetic Earth) When a vertical electric dipole (VED) is placed near this surface, the resulting field at the surface is decomposed into two physical parts: ### 🔹 (a) Space Wave (Direct + Reflected) Resulting from the **launch angle** in free space, attenuated by the **Fresnel reflection coefficient at the air–ground interface**. This part **decays as 1/d** with horizontal distance *d*. ### 🔹 (b) Surface Wave (Sommerfeld–Norton Wave) Arises from the **tilt of the wavefront** in the lossy medium, producing an axial field component. It is **guided by the interface** and decays **faster than 1/d** (exponentially with distance) but, importantly, it travels **farther than the space wave** at low frequencies because the space wave is nearly vertical at the source. > [!NOTE] > The total ground-wave field is the sum of these two parts. The **space wave** is often negligible for ground-wave communication, leaving the **Sommerfeld component** as the dominant mechanism. ## 2.2 The Sommerfeld–Norton Ground-Wave Field Equation The electric field (vertical component) of the ground wave at a distance $d$ over a **flat, smooth Earth** is given by: $$E(d) = \frac{E_0}{d} \cdot A$$ where: - $E_0/d$ is the **free-space field** at distance $d$ (in V/m) - $A$ is the **Ground-Wave Attenuation Factor** (a complex number with magnitude $\le 1$) $$A = F \cdot e^{-j \phi}$$ - $F = \vert A \vert$ is the **magnitude attenuation factor** - $\phi$ is the **phase shift** introduced by the lossy ground **$A$ is a function of two key dimensionless parameters:** ### 🔸 (i) Numerical Distance — $p$ $$p = \frac{\pi d}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{1}{X}$$ where $$X \approx \frac{\sqrt{(\varepsilon_r - 1)^2 + (60\sigma\lambda)^2}}{\varepsilon_r^2 + (60\sigma\lambda)^2} \cdot \varepsilon_r$$ For a **highly conducting ground** ($60\sigma\lambda \gg \varepsilon_r$), this simplifies to: $$p \approx \frac{\pi d}{60\sigma\lambda^2}$$ ### 🔸 (ii) Phase Constant — $b$ $$b = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon_r}{60\sigma\lambda}\right) \quad \text{(in radians, high-conductivity limit)}$$ For very high conductivity ($b \to 0$); for poor ground, $b$ becomes significant. ## 2.3 Empirical Approximations for the Attenuation Factor $A$ > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Trick:** Most numerical problems give you $p$ and $b$ directly — memorize these piecewise formulas! | Range of $p$ | Approximation for $A \approx F$ | Validity | | :---: | :--- | :--- | | $p \le 0.1$ | $A \approx 1$ | "Free-space" region near the antenna | | $0.1 \le p \le 5$ | $A \approx \dfrac{2 + 0.3p}{2 + p + 0.6p^2} - j\,\dfrac{0.6p}{2 + p + 0.6p^2}$ | Transition region | | $p \ge 5$ | $A \approx \dfrac{1}{2p}$ (asymptotic) | Far-field region | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $E(d) = \dfrac{E_0}{d} \cdot A$ | Total ground-wave field strength | | 2 | $E_0 = \dfrac{\sqrt{30\,G_t P_t}}{d}$ (V/m) | Free-space field at distance $d$ (for isotropic) | | 3 | $p = \dfrac{\pi d}{60\sigma\lambda^2}$ | Numerical distance (good ground) | | 4 | $b = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_r}{60\sigma\lambda}\right)$ | Phase constant (radians) | | 5 | $A \approx \dfrac{2 + 0.3p}{2 + p + 0.6p^2}$ | Mid-range attenuation magnitude | | 6 | $A \approx \dfrac{1}{2p}$ (large $p$) | Far-field attenuation | | 7 | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega\mu_0\sigma}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi f \mu_0 \sigma}}$ | Skin depth in lossy Earth (m) | | 8 | $\alpha_s = \dfrac{1}{2\delta}$ | Surface-wave attenuation coefficient (Np/m) | | 9 | $d_{\max} = \sqrt{2 h_t h_r}$ | Line-of-sight horizon distance (m) | | 10 | $E_{\text{flat}} = E_{\text{curved}} \cdot K$ | Earth-curvature correction factor $K < 1$ | | 11 | $A_{\text{total}} = A_{\text{flat}} \cdot K$ | Combined attenuation (flat Earth × curvature) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Table Notation:** Absolute values and "divides" inside these expressions are written as `\vert \cdot \vert` and `\big/`. In KTU exam sheets, the modulus $\vert A \vert$ is usually written as just the magnitude since $A$ is treated as a real number in the approximate formulas above. ## 2.5 Why Engineers Care — Real-World Utility 1. **AM (Medium-Wave) Radio Broadcasting** (530 kHz – 1710 kHz): Ground wave is the *primary* coverage mechanism, enabling national broadcasters to cover a country using a single network of high-power VED transmitters. 2. **Maritime & Aeronautical NDBs (Non-Directional Beacons)** in the LF/MF band: Ships and aircraft use ground-wave NDBs for hundreds of nautical miles, independent of the ionosphere. 3. **Time-Signal Stations** (e.g., DCF77 at 77.5 kHz): Use ground wave for reliable, ionosphere-free reception. 4. **Submarine Communication** (VLF, 3–30 kHz): Ground wave penetrates seawater (skin depth scales as $1/\sqrt{f\sigma}$). 5. **AMATEUR RADIO 1.8 MHz Band**: Nighttime long-distance contacts on "Top Band" rely on ground wave up to ~100 km before the sky wave takes over. --- # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Ground-Wave Field (Sommerfeld–Norton, Flat Earth) ### 🧮 Setup Consider a **vertical electric dipole (VED)** of moment $I\,dl$ placed at height $h$ above a flat, lossy half-space. The Earth's surface is the $z = 0$ plane. The dipole lies on the $z$-axis at $(0, 0, h)$. **Boundary conditions at $z = 0$:** 1. Tangential $E$ is continuous. 2. Normal $D$ is continuous except for surface charge. 3. The half-space below has complex permittivity: $$\tilde{\varepsilon} = \varepsilon_0\!\left(\varepsilon_r - j\,\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon_0}\right)$$ ### 🧮 Step 1 — Free-Space Field In the absence of Earth, the dipole produces (in the far field) a far-zone $E$-field: $$E_\theta^{(0)}(r) = j\,\frac{\eta_0\,I\,dl}{2\lambda r}\,\sin\theta \, e^{-jkr}$$ For points on the ground ($\theta = 90°$), $\sin\theta = 1$, so: $$E_0 = \frac{60\pi I\,dl}{\lambda r} \quad \text{(in V/m, isotropic equivalent)}$$ > The notation $E_0$ here is the free-space field magnitude — used as our reference. ### 🧮 Step 2 — Introduce the Earth's Influence (Reflection Coefficient) When the Earth is present, the reflected field is governed by the **Fresnel reflection coefficient for vertical polarization**: $$\Gamma_v = \frac{\tilde{\eta}_2 \cos\theta_i - \eta_0 \cos\theta_t}{\tilde{\eta}_2 \cos\theta_i + \eta_0 \cos\theta_t}$$ where the wave impedance of the lossy ground is: $$\tilde{\eta}_2 = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega\mu_0}{\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}}$$ > **Sommerfeld's Key Insight:** As we sum the direct and reflected waves, and the antenna height $h \to 0$ (i.e., the dipole is on the surface), the integration over all launch angles $\theta$ does **not** yield just a 1/d decay — it leaves a residual **surface-wave term** that is not captured by the reflection-coefficient picture. This is the **Zenneck surface wave**. ### 🧮 Step 3 — The Total Ground-Wave Field After lengthy analysis (Sommerfeld 1909, Norton 1937), the result for a VED of height $h$ over a flat, finitely conducting Earth is: $$E_z(d) = j\,\frac{60\pi I\,dl}{\lambda d}\,F(p,b)\,e^{-j\Phi(p,b)}\,e^{-jkd}$$ where $F(p,b)$ is the magnitude attenuation factor and $\Phi(p,b)$ the phase lag — both functions of numerical distance $p$ and phase constant $b$. ### 🧮 Step 4 — Numerical Distance $p$ Derived Starting from the surface-wave integral and applying steepest-descent, $p$ emerges as: $$ \begin{aligned} p &= \frac{\pi d}{\lambda}\,\frac{1}{X} \\[4pt] &= \frac{\pi d}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\cos b'}{1 - \frac{1}{2}\cos 2b'} \cdot \frac{1}{...} \end{aligned} $$ In the limit of large $60\sigma\lambda$ (good ground): $$ \begin{aligned} p &\approx \frac{\pi d}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{1}{60\sigma\lambda} \\[4pt] &= \frac{\pi d}{60\,\sigma\,\lambda^2} \end{aligned} $$ > **Conversion logic:** The factor $60\sigma\lambda$ in the denominator arises because the ratio $\eta_0 / \vert \tilde{\eta}_2 \vert \approx 60\sigma\lambda$ for a good conductor. The squaring of $\lambda$ in the denominator comes from $d/\lambda \times 1/\lambda$. ### 🧮 Step 5 — Approximate Attenuation Factor (Van der Pol) For the practical range $0.1 \le p \le 5$ and small $b$ (a condition met for most land/sea paths below ~3 MHz), Van der Pol gave: $$A \approx \frac{2 + 0.3p}{2 + p + 0.6p^2} \; - j\;\frac{0.6p}{2 + p + 0.6p^2}$$ The **magnitude** is: $$F = \vert A \vert = \frac{1}{2 + p + 0.6p^2}\,\sqrt{(2 + 0.3p)^2 + (0.6p)^2}$$ > **Conversion logic:** Use $\vert u + jv \vert = \sqrt{u^2 + v^2}$ for the complex magnitude. Both numerator and denominator are the result of approximating the integral $\int e^{-p(1-\cos\theta)} \sin\theta\,d\theta$. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > [!TIP] > **Problem:** A 1 MHz ground-wave transmission travels over sea water ($\sigma = 4$ S/m, $\varepsilon_r = 80$) for a distance of 100 km. Find the numerical distance, the phase constant, and the attenuation factor $A$. **Step 1 — Wavelength** $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{1 \times 10^{6}} = 300 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $60\sigma\lambda$** $$60 \sigma \lambda = 60 \times 4 \times 300 = 72{,}000$$ **Step 3 — Numerical distance $p$** $$p \approx \frac{\pi d}{60 \sigma \lambda^{2}} = \frac{\pi \times 100 \times 10^{3}}{60 \times 4 \times (300)^{2}}$$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Numerator} &= \pi \times 10^{5} \approx 3.1416 \times 10^{5} \\[4pt] \text{Denominator} &= 60 \times 4 \times 90{,}000 = 2.16 \times 10^{7} \\[4pt] p &\approx \frac{3.1416 \times 10^{5}}{2.16 \times 10^{7}} \approx 0.0145 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Phase constant $b$** $$b = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon_r}{60\sigma\lambda}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{80}{72{,}000}\right) \approx \tan^{-1}(0.00111) \approx 0.00111 \text{ rad}$$ > **Conversion logic:** $b$ is tiny because $60\sigma\lambda \gg \varepsilon_r$. This means the **phase lag is negligible** — sea water behaves almost like a perfect conductor for MF ground waves. **Step 5 — Attenuation factor $A$** Since $p = 0.0145 < 0.1$, we are in the **near-field region**: $$A \approx 1 - j\,0.2\,p \approx 1 - j\,0.0029$$ Magnitude: $$F = \vert A \vert \approx \sqrt{1 + (0.0029)^{2}} \approx 1.000004 \approx 1$$ > **Conversion logic:** At 100 km over sea water, the ground wave is essentially **undiminished** by surface loss — confirming that sea water is a near-perfect ground for MF broadcasting. ## 3.3 Comparison: Land vs. Sea-Water Path (Counter-Intuitive Result) | Path | $\sigma$ (S/m) | $60\sigma\lambda$ (at 1 MHz) | $p$ at 100 km | $F = \vert A \vert$ | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **Sea water** | 4 | 72,000 | 0.0145 | $\approx 1.00$ | | **Wet soil** | $10^{-2}$ | 180 | 5.8 | $\approx 0.16$ | | **Dry land** | $10^{-3}$ | 18 | 58 | $\approx 0.0086$ | | **Rocky terrain** | $10^{-4}$ | 1.8 | 580 | $\approx 8 \times 10^{-4}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pearl:** The attenuation factor is **exponentially sensitive** to ground conductivity. Going from sea water to dry land at 100 km drops the field strength by a factor of more than **1000**! ## 3.4 Effect of Earth's Curvature (Smooth Spherical Earth) For distances approaching the radio horizon, the Earth's curvature introduces an **additional divergence** of the wavefront beyond that of a flat Earth. The corrected attenuation is: $$A_{\text{total}} = A_{\text{flat}}(p)\,K$$ where $K$ is the **curvature factor** ($< 1$), obtained from the residue-series solution (Wait's method or similar): $$K = \sqrt{\frac{x}{x + y}}\;e^{-y}$$ - $x = d\,(k a / 2)^{1/3}$ (dimensionless) - $y = h_{\text{eff}} \cdot (2 / k a^{2})^{1/3}$ (related to antenna height) > **Conversion logic:** The factor $\sqrt{x/(x+y)}$ accounts for **geometric spreading** on a sphere; the $\exp(-y)$ accounts for the **obstruction loss** as the wave grazes the curved surface. ## 3.5 Summary of Analytical Flow $$ \boxed{ \begin{aligned} &\text{1. Input: } d,\ f,\ \sigma,\ \varepsilon_r \\[2pt] &\text{2. Compute } \lambda = c/f \\[2pt] &\text{3. Compute } p = \pi d / (60\sigma\lambda^{2})\ \text{(good ground)} \\[2pt] &\text{4. Compute } b = \tan^{-1}(\varepsilon_r / 60\sigma\lambda) \\[2pt] &\text{5. Pick the correct piecewise formula for } A(p,b) \\[2pt] &\text{6. } E(d) = (E_0 / d) \cdot A \\[2pt] &\text{7. If } d \gtrsim 50 \text{ km: multiply by curvature } K \end{aligned} } $$ --- # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Ground-Wave Propagation Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Transmitting VED Antenna
Vertical Electric Dipole] --> B[EM Wave Launched
in All Directions] B --> C{Component Separation
at the Surface} C --> D[Direct Wave
Free-space 1/d decay] C --> E[Reflected Wave
Fresnel Reflection] C --> F[Surface Wave
Guided by Interface] D --> G[Sum at Receiver
Phasor Addition] E --> G F --> G G --> H[Receiver Antenna
Receives Total Field E d] F --> I[Induces Surface Currents
in Lossy Earth] I --> J[Re-radiates Energy
Wavefront Regenerated] J --> F F --> K{Distance Range} K --> L[Near Field p less than 0.1
A approx 1] K --> M[Transition 0.1 to 5
Van der Pol formula] K --> N[Far Field p greater than 5
A approx 1 over 2p] L --> O[Final Field Strength
E d equals E0 d times A] M --> O N --> O O --> P{Apply Curvature
Correction} P --> Q[Final Field E d total] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow for Attenuation Factor ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Start: Given d, f, sigma, epsilon_r] --> S2[Compute lambda equals c over f] S2 --> S3[Compute numerical distance p
using formula pi d over 60 sigma lambda squared] S3 --> D1{p less than 0.1} D1 -- Yes --> P1[Use A approx 1
Free-space field] D1 -- No --> D2{p between 0.1 and 5} D2 -- Yes --> P2[Use Van der Pol formula
A equals 2 plus 0.3p over 2 plus p plus 0.6p squared] D2 -- No --> P3[Use asymptotic A approx 1 over 2p] P1 --> E1[E d equals E0 over d times A] P2 --> E1 P3 --> E1 E1 --> C1{d greater than 50 km} C1 -- Yes --> K1[Multiply by curvature factor K] C1 -- No --> F1[Final result E d total] K1 --> F1 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Ground-Wave System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Tx_Side[Transmitter Side] TX[RF Source] --> PA[Power Amplifier] PA --> VED[Vertical Whip Antenna
Quarter-wave Monopole] end subgraph Prop_Channel[Propagation Channel - Air Earth Interface] VED --> SW[Surface Wave Launched] SW --> EARTH[Earth Surface sigma, epsilon_r] EARTH --> SC[Surface Currents Induced] SC --> REG[Wavefront Regeneration] REG --> SW end subgraph Rx_Side[Receiver Side] SW --> RVED[Receiving Vertical Antenna] RVED --> LNA[Low-Noise Amplifier] LNA --> DET[Detector / Demodulator] end EARTH -.Lossy Dielectric Half-Space.-> SW ``` ## 4.4 Annotated Geometry — VED Over Curved Earth ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Cross-section of Earth Radius a
Center O] --> B[Transmitter at height h_t
Above Surface] A --> C[Receiver at distance d
Along surface] B -- Surface Wave Path --> C C -- Direct LOS --> B A -- Curvature bulge --> D[Beyond-line-of-sight region
where only surface wave exists] D -- Ground wave bends around --> C C --> E[Geometric Horizon
d approx sqrt 2 a h_t] ``` --- # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] — *3 Marks* **"Define ground wave propagation. State any two conditions favorable for it."** **Model Answer:** > **Ground wave propagation** is the mode of radio wave transmission in which the wave travels along the surface of the Earth, being guided by the lossy conducting boundary between the Earth and the atmosphere. > > **Two favorable conditions:** > 1. The transmitting antenna must be **vertically polarized** (e.g., vertical whip or vertical electric dipole), so that the electric field has a normal component to the Earth's surface capable of inducing conduction currents. > 2.
