The operating frequency should be **low**, typically below **3 MHz** (MF/LF bands), because at high frequencies the ground wave attenuates rapidly with distance due to losses in the Earth. > > *Additionally (bonus mark):* Good ground conductivity (e.g., sea water, moist soil) enhances ground-wave range. --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — *3 Marks* **"What is meant by numerical distance $p$ in ground wave propagation? Write its expression for good ground."** **Model Answer:** > **Numerical distance $p$** is a dimensionless parameter that characterizes the extent to which the surface wave has been attenuated by the lossy Earth. It is the key independent variable in the Sommerfeld–Norton ground-wave attenuation function $A(p, b)$. > > **Expression for highly conducting ground** ($60\sigma\lambda \gg \varepsilon_r$): > > $$p = \frac{\pi d}{60\,\sigma\,\lambda^{2}}$$ > > where $d$ is the path length, $\sigma$ is the ground conductivity, and $\lambda$ is the wavelength. > > **Physical meaning:** Larger $p$ → greater attenuation. For $p < 0.1$ the ground wave is essentially in the free-space region ($A \approx 1$); for $p > 5$ the field has entered the asymptotic $1/(2p)$ decay regime. --- ## 5.2 📝 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question is **14 marks** with two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. You must attempt **ONE of the two choices**; module-level internal choice is mandatory. --- ### ✅ **Choice A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024] **Q.A.** *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the mechanism of **ground wave propagation**. Discuss why a vertically polarized antenna is essential. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Derive the expression for the **numerical distance $p$** and **phase constant $b$** for a wave propagating over a finitely conducting flat Earth. *(7 marks)* --- #### 📖 **Model Solution for Q.A.(a):** **Definition (2 marks):** Ground wave propagation is the transmission of radio waves along the surface of the Earth, guided by the lossy dielectric boundary. The wavefront is "tilted" in the lossy medium, producing an axial $E$-field component that propagates along the surface. **Mechanism (3 marks):** When a vertical electric dipole (VED) radiates near the Earth, three wave components are generated — direct, reflected, and surface. The **surface wave** is bound to the interface because: - Energy is exchanged back and forth between the air region and the lossy Earth. - The lossy ground absorbs energy, but the field that "leaks" along the surface is sustained by regeneration from surface currents. - At low frequencies, the tilt of the wavefront in the lossy medium matches the grazing angle, allowing the wave to slide along the Earth. **Why vertical polarization (2 marks):** A vertically polarized wave has its $E$-field perpendicular to the Earth's surface (along $z$). This $E_z$ component penetrates the surface and drives **conduction currents** $J_z = \sigma E_z$ in the ground. These currents re-radiate, regenerating the wave. A horizontal wave has no $E_z$ component at grazing incidence; its $E$-field is parallel to the surface and cannot drive surface currents effectively — the surface wave vanishes for $E_{horizontal}$ over a flat Earth. #### 📖 **Model Solution for Q.A.(b):** **Starting point (1 mark):** The complex intrinsic impedance of the lossy Earth is: $$\tilde{\eta}_2 = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega\mu_0}{\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}}$$ **Magnitude (2 marks):** For good ground ($\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r$): $$\vert \tilde{\eta}_2 \vert \approx \sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu_0}{\sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{2\pi f \mu_0}{\sigma}}$$ Expressed in terms of $\lambda$: $$\vert \tilde{\eta}_2 \vert = \frac{\eta_0}{\sqrt{2}\,60\sigma\lambda}\ \text{(for } 60\sigma\lambda \gg \varepsilon_r\text{)}$$ **Numerical distance derivation (2 marks):** The dimensionless distance is defined as: $$p = \frac{\pi d}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\cos b'}{1 - \frac{1}{2}\cos 2b'}$$ which in the **good-ground limit** ($b' \to 0$, $\cos b' \to 1$, $\cos 2b' \to 1$) reduces to: $$ \boxed{p = \frac{\pi d}{60\,\sigma\,\lambda^{2}}} $$ **Phase constant derivation (2 marks):** Similarly, $$b = \frac{1}{2}\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon_r - \cos^{2}\theta}{\sigma / (\omega\varepsilon_0)}\right) \xrightarrow[\text{good ground}]{} \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon_r}{60\sigma\lambda}\right)$$ --- ### ✅ **Choice B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q.B.** *(a)* A **1 MHz ground-wave transmission** travels **150 km** over a region with $\sigma = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ S/m and $\varepsilon_r = 10$. Calculate the **numerical distance**, the **phase constant**, and the **attenuation factor $A$** (using the Van der Pol approximation). *(7 marks)* *(b)* A broadcast station radiates **10 kW** from a **quarter-wave vertical monopole** at 1 MHz. Estimate the **field strength at 150 km** over the same path, comparing it to free-space. Comment on the result. *(7 marks)* --- #### 📖 **Model Solution for Q.B.(a):** **Step 1 — Wavelength (1 mark):** $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{1 \times 10^{6}} = 300 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — $60\sigma\lambda$ (1 mark):** $$60 \sigma \lambda = 60 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 300 = 36$$ **Step 3 — Numerical distance (2 marks):** $$p = \frac{\pi d}{60\sigma\lambda^{2}} = \frac{\pi \times 150 \times 10^{3}}{60 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times (300)^{2}} = \frac{4.7124 \times 10^{5}}{1.08 \times 10^{4}} \approx 43.6$$ **Step 4 — Phase constant (1 mark):** $$b = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon_r}{60\sigma\lambda}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{10}{36}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.278) \approx 0.271 \text{ rad} \approx 15.5°$$ **Step 5 — Attenuation factor (2 marks):** Since $p = 43.6 \gg 5$, use the asymptotic formula: $$A \approx \frac{1}{2p} = \frac{1}{2 \times 43.6} \approx 0.0115$$ Therefore: $$F = \vert A \vert \approx 0.0115$$ #### 📖 **Model Solution for Q.B.(b):** **Step 1 — Free-space field $E_0$ at 150 km (2 marks):** Using the formula for the field from an isotropic radiator in free space: $$E_0 = \frac{\sqrt{30\,G_t P_t}}{d} = \frac{\sqrt{30 \times 1 \times 10{,}000}}{150 \times 10^{3}}$$ $$E_0 = \frac{\sqrt{300{,}000}}{1.5 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{547.7}{1.5 \times 10^{5}} \approx 3.65 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V/m} = 3.65 \text{ mV/m}$$ **Step 2 — Ground-wave field $E(d)$ (2 marks):** $$E(d) = \frac{E_0}{1} \cdot A = 3.65 \text{ mV/m} \times 0.0115 \approx 42\ \mu\text{V/m}$$ Wait — note that $E_0/d$ already contains the $1/d$ factor. Re-computing carefully using the original formulation: $$E(d) = \frac{E_0\,(\text{at reference})}{d} \cdot A$$ If we take $E_0$ as the **field at 1 km** for the reference: $$E_0(\text{at 1 km}) = \frac{\sqrt{30 \times 10000}}{1000} = 5.48 \text{ mV/m}$$ Then: $$E(d) = \frac{5.48 \text{ mV/m}}{150} \times 0.0115 = 36.5\ \mu\text{V/m} \times 0.0115 \approx 0.42\ \mu\text{V/m}$$ **Step 3 — Comparison with free space (2 marks):** Free-space value at 150 km (no $A$): $$E_{\text{FS}} = \frac{5.48 \text{ mV/m}}{150} \approx 36.5\ \mu\text{V/m}$$ **Result:** The ground wave over poor land is **~86× weaker** than free space at 150 km. This is why **AM broadcast range over land is limited to ~100–150 km** even with high power, but the same station over sea water can reach **500+ km**. **Comment (1 mark):** The attenuation $A \propto 1/p \propto 1/d$ over poor ground, doubling the distance quarters the field — a far steeper drop than the $1/d$ free-space law. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Ground-Wave Problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting to square the wavelength.** Many students write $p = \pi d / (60\sigma\lambda)$ instead of $\pi d / (60\sigma\lambda^{2})$. This gives an answer off by a factor of 300, often producing a numerical $p$ that is nonsense. > > 2. **Mixing up the asymptotic regime.** When $p > 5$, the *correct* formula is $A \approx 1/(2p)$, **not** the Van der Pol one. Forcing the Van der Pol formula into the asymptotic region will give a value that is close but not accurate, and examiners often deduct a half-mark for "conceptual mismatch". > > 3. **Ignoring the curvature factor $K$ for $d > 50$ km.** KTU papers frequently include a sub-part asking for the curvature correction, and students omit it. > > 4. **Using horizontal polarization.** If a problem says "horizontal dipole over ground", the answer is **no ground wave** — the field is $2 \times$ direct for grazing incidence, not a guided surface wave. Examiners expect this distinction. > > 5. **Mixing up $E_0/d$ vs $E_0$ definitions.** Always state explicitly: "$E_0$ is the field at the *reference* distance in free space." Ambiguity costs 1 mark. > > 6. **Units in the final answer.** $E$ in V/m or mV/m; $p$ is dimensionless; $b$ in radians. State units! --- ## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision (5 minutes to read before exam):** - ☑ **Definition:** Ground wave = wave guided along the Earth’s surface, dominant below **3 MHz**, requires **vertical polarization** and **finite ground conductivity**. - ☑ **Sommerfeld–Norton Field Equation:** $E(d) = (E_0 / d) \cdot A(p, b)$, with $A$ being the **ground-wave attenuation factor**. - ☑ **Two Key Parameters:** numerical distance $p$ and phase constant $b$. - ☑ **Numerical distance (good ground):** $p = \pi d / (60\sigma\lambda^{2})$. Larger $p$ = more attenuation. - ☑ **Phase constant (good ground):** $b = \tan^{-1}(\varepsilon_r / 60\sigma\lambda)$. Small for sea water, larger for dry land. - ☑ **Three Regimes of $A$:** - $p < 0.1$ → $A \approx 1$ (free space) - $0.1 \le p \le 5$ → Van der Pol: $A \approx (2 + 0.3p)/(2 + p + 0.6p^{2})$ - $p > 5$ → $A \approx 1/(2p)$ (asymptotic) - ☑ **Conductivity Hierarchy (best to worst):** Sea water > wet soil > average land > dry land > rocky terrain. AM stations over sea water are heard from **hundreds of km**; over deserts, only **~50 km**. - ☑ **Curvature Correction:** Multiply $A_{\text{flat}}$ by $K < 1$ for $d > 50$ km; $K$ accounts for Earth bulge. - ☑ **Practical Bands:** VLF/LF (3 kHz – 300 kHz) and MF (300 kHz – 3 MHz) — used for AM broadcast, NDBs, time signals, submarine comms. - ☑ **Attenuation over distance** scales as $1/(d^2)$ in far-field (one $1/d$ from spreading, one $1/p \propto 1/d$ from $A$). - ☑ **Polarization Rule:** VED (vertical) → ground wave ✓ ; HED (horizontal) → no ground wave over flat Earth. - ☑ **Skin depth of Earth:** $\delta = \sqrt{2/(\omega\mu_0\sigma)}$; surface-wave attenuation $\alpha_s = 1/(2\delta)$. - ☑ **Earth Radius for horizon:** $d_{\max} = \sqrt{2 h_t a}$ (geometric horizon); the actual radio horizon is ~15 % farther due to atmospheric refraction (k-factor). - ☑ **Golden Equation to Memorize:** $$\boxed{E(d) = \frac{\sqrt{30 G_t P_t}}{d}\cdot\frac{2+0.3p}{2+p+0.6p^2}\ \ \text{with}\ \ p = \frac{\pi d}{60\sigma\lambda^{2}}}$$ > **End of Topic Notes — Module 4: Ground Wave Propagation** ✔️

topics.title: Plane Earth Reflection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Plane Earth Reflection ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plane Earth Reflection** is the phenomenon where an electromagnetic wave launched by a transmitting antenna travels through the atmosphere and strikes the smooth, flat surface of the Earth, producing both a *direct ray* (line-of-sight) and a *ground-reflected ray* (specularly reflected from the Earth's surface). The vector sum of these two rays at the receiving antenna determines the **received field strength**, the **radiation pattern distortion**, and the **fading characteristics** of the link. In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, this model is also called the **"Two-Ray Ground Reflection Model"** and forms the foundation of practical VHF/UHF terrestrial communication link budget analysis. ### 1.2 Physical Geometry of the Problem Consider a transmitting antenna of height $h_t$ and a receiving antenna of height $h_r$ separated by a ground distance $d$. The reflection is assumed to occur at a point **Q** on a flat, smooth Earth such that: - The angle of incidence $\theta_i$ equals the angle of reflection $\theta_r$ (Snell's law for specular surfaces). - The Earth's radius is effectively infinite compared to $h_t, h_r$ (hence the name *plane* Earth). - The Earth acts as a lossy dielectric with relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ and conductivity $\sigma$. ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy:** Imagine standing beside a calm lake at sunset. You see the sun twice in the water — once directly (the *direct ray*) and once as a mirror image (the *reflected ray*). The brightness of the reflected image depends on the angle you look from (governed by the *Fresnel reflection coefficient*) and the polarization of the light (governed by the *Brewster angle*). Now replace the sun with a transmitting antenna, the water with the Earth, and your eye with a receiving antenna — that is precisely *plane earth reflection*. ### 1.4 Why Smooth Earth Assumption? The Earth is treated as a smooth plane when the surface irregularities (hills, buildings, vegetation) are smaller than the **Rayleigh criterion**: $$h_{\text{crit}} = \frac{\lambda}{8 \sin \theta_i}$$ When $h_{\text{roughness}} \ll h_{\text{crit}}$, the surface behaves like a mirror (specular reflection). Otherwise, energy is scattered diffusely. ### 1.5 Key Physical Constants - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** - Intrinsic impedance of free space: $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377$ **Ω** - Earth parameters (typical): $\varepsilon_r \approx 4$ to **15** (depends on soil moisture), $\sigma \approx 10^{-3}$ to **$5 \times 10^{-2}$** S/m (sea water $\sigma \approx 4$ **S/m**). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Physical Mechanisms at Play When an EM wave strikes the Earth's surface, three things happen simultaneously: 1. **Specular reflection** — a coherent ray obeying Snell's law bounces off with a magnitude and phase given by the **Fresnel reflection coefficient** $\Gamma$. 2. **Refraction / penetration** — a portion enters the ground and is rapidly attenuated (lossy dielectric). 3. **Diffuse scattering** — if the surface is rough, energy is scattered in many directions (ignored in the *plane* model). For the plane earth model, only **(1)** matters; (2) and (3) are neglected. ### 2.2 The Two-Ray Field Construction The total received field $\vec{E}_{\text{total}}$ is the phasor sum: $$\vec{E}_{\text{total}} = \vec{E}_{\text{direct}} + \vec{E}_{\text{reflected}}$$ The reflected ray travels a longer path and undergoes a phase reversal/attenuation upon reflection. This interference produces the classic **multi-lobe pattern** of a real antenna installed above ground. ### 2.3 Polarization Matters: Horizontal vs Vertical The reflection coefficient $\Gamma$ is *different* for the two orthogonal polarizations: - **Horizontal polarization (E-field parallel to Earth):** $\Gamma_h$ is always close to **−1** at grazing incidence. The reflected wave nearly cancels the direct wave for low antenna heights. - **Vertical polarization (E-field in the plane of incidence):** $\Gamma_v$ passes through **zero** at the *Brewster angle*, allowing nearly 100% transmission into the ground for one specific angle. > [!IMPORTANT] > This polarization sensitivity is the reason FM radio (vertical, $\sim 100$ MHz) and TV (horizontal, VHF) historically needed different antenna designs even when using identical tower heights. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|----------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Reflection coefficient (vertical pol.) | $\Gamma_v = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \sin \theta_i - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2 \theta_i}}{\varepsilon_r \sin \theta_i + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2 \theta_i}}$ | Dimensionless, complex in lossy case | | 2 | Reflection coefficient (horizontal pol.) | $\Gamma_h = \dfrac{\sin \theta_i - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2 \theta_i}}{\sin \theta_i + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2 \theta_i}}$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | Brewster angle (vertical pol.) | $\sin \theta_B = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2}}$ | $\Gamma_v = 0$ here | | 4 | Critical angle (total internal reflection) | $\sin \theta_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_1}}$ | Only when $\varepsilon_2 < \varepsilon_1$ | | 5 | Two-ray received field magnitude | $\vert E_R \vert = \dfrac{2 E_0 d_0}{d} \left\vert \sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}\right) \right\vert$ | Far-field, $d \gg h_t, h_r$ | | 6 | Path difference $\Delta R$ | $\Delta R = \dfrac{2 h_t h_r}{d}$ | Valid for $d \gg h_t, h_r$ | | 7 | Phase difference $\Delta \Phi$ | $\Delta \Phi = \beta \cdot \Delta R + \angle \Gamma$ | $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$ | | 8 | Far-field crossover distance | $d_c = \dfrac{4 h_t h_r}{\lambda}$ | Below this, two-ray fails; use Friis | | 9 | Receive power (two-ray) | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \dfrac{h_t^2 h_r^2}{d^4}$ | Free-space loss replaced by $1/d^4$ | | 10 | Grazing-incidence limit | $\Gamma \to -1$ as $\theta_i \to 0$ | Direct + reflected destructively cancel | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering utility:** Formula (5) is the *workhorse* of every KTU numerical on mobile base-station coverage planning, VHF link design, and radar horizon analysis. Formula (9) explains why cellular signals fall off as the **fourth power** of distance (the famous "$40$ dB/decade" rule), a direct consequence of the two-ray interference. ### 2.5 Sign Convention and Phasor Pitfalls - $E_0$ is the free-space field at unit distance. - $\Gamma$ is **negative at grazing incidence** (phase reversal of $\pi$ rad). - Always plot $\vert \Gamma \vert$ vs $\theta_i$ for both polarizations when solving KTU numericals — examiners award marks for the *shape* of the curve, not just the formula. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fresnel Reflection Coefficient for Horizontal Polarization **Setup:** A plane wave in free space ($\varepsilon_1, \mu_0$) impinges on a dielectric half-space ($\varepsilon_2, \mu_0$) at angle $\theta_i$. The electric field is perpendicular to the plane of incidence (TE / horizontal polarization). **Step 1 — Write the boundary condition for tangential E-field continuity.** At the interface ($z=0$), the sum of incident and reflected tangential E must equal the transmitted tangential E: $$E_i \cos \theta_i + E_r \cos \theta_r = E_t \cos \theta_t$$ **Step 2 — Apply Snell's law.** $$\theta_i = \theta_r \quad \text{and} \quad \sin \theta_i = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}\,\sin \theta_t$$ Hence $\cos \theta_t = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta_t} = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta_i / \varepsilon_r} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2 \theta_i}}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$ **Step 3 — Write the boundary condition for tangential H-field continuity.** For TE polarization, $H = E/\eta$. Since $H$ is in the plane of incidence, its tangential component scales as $E/\eta \cdot \cos \theta$: $$\frac{E_i}{\eta_1} - \frac{E_r}{\eta_1} = \frac{E_t}{\eta_2} \cdot \frac{\cos \theta_t}{\cos \theta_i}$$ **Step 4 — Solve the two equations for the ratio $\Gamma_h = E_r / E_i$.** Eliminating $E_t$ between steps 1 and 3 (algebraic manipulation, dropping common factors): $$ \begin{aligned} E_i(1 - \Gamma_h) \cdot \frac{\cos \theta_t}{\cos \theta_i} &= \frac{\eta_1}{\eta_2} \cdot E_i(1 + \Gamma_h) \cos \theta_i \\[6pt] \Rightarrow \quad \Gamma_h &= \frac{\dfrac{\eta_2}{\eta_1} \cos \theta_i - \cos \theta_t}{\dfrac{\eta_2}{\eta_1} \cos \theta_i + \cos \theta_t} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute $\eta_2/\eta_1 = 1/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ and the cosine relation from Step 2.** $$ \begin{aligned} \Gamma_h &= \frac{\dfrac{\cos \theta_i}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} - \dfrac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2 \theta_i}}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}}{\dfrac{\cos \theta_i}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} + \dfrac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2 \theta_i}}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}} \\[10pt] &= \frac{\cos \theta_i - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2 \theta_i}}{\cos \theta_i + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2 \theta_i}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Convert from $\cos\theta$ to $\sin\theta$ (KTU textbook form).** Using $\cos \theta_i = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\theta_i}$ and dividing numerator and denominator by $\cos \theta_i$: $$ \boxed{\;\Gamma_h = \frac{\sin \theta_i - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2 \theta_i}}{\sin \theta_i + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2 \theta_i}}\;} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Brewster Angle for Vertical Polarization **Step 1 — Force $\Gamma_v = 0$.** From the vertical-polarization form: $$\Gamma_v = \frac{\varepsilon_r \sin \theta_i - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2 \theta_i}}{\varepsilon_r \sin \theta_i + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2 \theta_i}} = 0$$ This requires the **numerator to vanish** (denominator is non-zero for $\theta_i < 90°$): $$\varepsilon_r \sin \theta_i = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2 \theta_i}$$ **Step 2 — Square both sides and use $\cos^2\theta_i = 1 - \sin^2\theta_i$.** $$ \begin{aligned} \varepsilon_r^2 \sin^2 \theta_i &= \varepsilon_r - \cos^2 \theta_i \\ \varepsilon_r^2 \sin^2 \theta_i &= \varepsilon_r - (1 - \sin^2 \theta_i) \\ \varepsilon_r^2 \sin^2 \theta_i - \sin^2 \theta_i &= \varepsilon_r - 1 \\ \sin^2 \theta_i \,(\varepsilon_r^2 - 1) &= \varepsilon_r - 1 \\ \sin^2 \theta_i \,(\varepsilon_r + 1)(\varepsilon_r - 1) &= \varepsilon_r - 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Cancel $(\varepsilon_r - 1)$ (valid for any non-trivial dielectric where $\varepsilon_r \neq 1$).** $$ \sin^2 \theta_i = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_r + 1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\;\sin \theta_B = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\varepsilon_r + 1}}\;} $$ > [!NOTE] > If instead the wave originates *inside* the dielectric (medium 1 = Earth, medium 2 = air), the formula inverts to $\sin\theta_B = \sqrt{\varepsilon_2/(\varepsilon_1+\varepsilon_2)}$, which is the form KTU uses when it specifies direction. **Always read the question carefully.** ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Two-Ray Received Field (Classic KTU Numerical) **Geometry:** Transmitter at $(0, 0, h_t)$, receiver at $(d, 0, h_r)$, reflection point at $(d/2, 0, 0)$. **Step 1 — Direct path length.** $$R_1 = \sqrt{d^2 + (h_t - h_r)^2}$$ **Step 2 — Reflected path length.** $$R_2 = \sqrt{d^2 + (h_t + h_r)^2}$$ **Step 3 — Far-field expansion using binomial approximation** for $d \gg h_t, h_r$: $$ \begin{aligned} R_1 &\approx d + \frac{(h_t - h_r)^2}{2d} \\ R_2 &\approx d + \frac{(h_t + h_r)^2}{2d} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the path difference.** $$\Delta R = R_2 - R_1 = \frac{(h_t + h_r)^2 - (h_t - h_r)^2}{2d} = \frac{4 h_t h_r}{2d} = \frac{2 h_t h_r}{d}$$ **Step 5 — Total field as phasor sum** (with $\Gamma \approx -1$ at grazing incidence, phase reversal $\pi$): $$E_R = E_0 \left( \frac{e^{-j\beta R_1}}{R_1} - \frac{e^{-j\beta R_2}}{R_2} \right)$$ **Step 6 — In the far field, $R_1 \approx R_2 \approx d$ in the denominator (amplitude), but retain the phase difference.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_R &= \frac{E_0}{d}\left( e^{-j\beta R_1} - e^{-j\beta R_2} \right) \\ &= \frac{E_0}{d}\,e^{-j\beta R_1}\left( 1 - e^{-j\beta \Delta R} \right) \\ &= \frac{E_0}{d}\,e^{-j\beta R_1}\cdot 2j\, e^{-j\beta \Delta R/2} \sin\!\left(\frac{\beta \Delta R}{2}\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Take the magnitude and substitute $\beta \Delta R / 2 = (2\pi/\lambda)(h_t h_r/d)$.** $$ \boxed{\;\vert E_R \vert = \frac{2 E_0}{d} \left\vert \sin\!\left( \frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d} \right) \right\vert\;} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > When the argument of the sine is small (very large $d$), use $\sin x \approx x$ to recover the famous **inverse-fourth-power** law: $\vert E_R \vert \to \dfrac{4\pi h_t h_r E_0}{\lambda d^2}$, i.e. **$P_r \propto 1/d^4$**. This is the *raison d'être* of cellular frequency reuse — without ground reflection, the cell radius would be vastly larger. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Geometry of the Two-Ray Model ```mermaid flowchart TB Tx["Tx Antenna
Height ht"] Rx["Rx Antenna
Height hr"] Q["Reflection Point Q
on Earth Surface"] DirectRay["Direct Ray R1"] ReflectedRay["Reflected Ray R2"] GroundLine["Plane Earth Surface"] AngleInc["Angle of Incidence = theta_i"] AngleRef["Angle of Reflection = theta_i"] Tx -->|R1| Rx Tx -->|downward ray| Q Q -->|R2| Rx Q --- GroundLine AngleInc -.-> Q AngleRef -.-> Q ``` ### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of the Received Signal ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Transmitter["Transmitter Side"] TxBlock["Tx Power Pt"] TxAnt["Tx Antenna
Gain Gt, Height ht"] end subgraph Channel["Propagation Channel"] DirectPath["Direct Path
Length R1
Free-Space Attenuation 1/R1"] GroundBlock["Ground Reflection
Point Q
Fresnel Coeff Gamma
Path Length R2, 1/R2 Attenuation"] end subgraph Receiver["Receiver Side"] RxAnt["Rx Antenna
Gain Gr, Height hr"] RxBlock["Received Power Pr
+ Phasor Sum"] end TxBlock --> TxAnt TxAnt -->|Ray 1| DirectPath TxAnt -->|Ray 2| GroundBlock DirectPath -->|E_direct| RxBlock GroundBlock -->|E_reflected = Gamma * E_direct| RxBlock RxBlock --> RxAnt ``` ### 4.3 Reflection Coefficient Behavior vs Incidence Angle ```mermaid flowchart TB Start["Incident Wave strikes Earth at angle theta_i"] Polar1{"Polarization?"} BranchH["Horizontal Polarization
Gamma_h is always negative
approaches -1 at grazing
approaches +1 at normal incidence"] BranchV["Vertical Polarization
Gamma_v = 0 at Brewster angle
approaches -1 at grazing
approaches +1 at normal incidence"] Brewster["At theta = theta_B:
ALL power is transmitted
into the ground
NO reflected wave"] Critical["At theta > theta_c:
Total internal reflection
Gamma magnitude = 1
Phase shift only"] Use1["Application: VHF TV broadcast"] Use2["Application: FM radio, mobile comms"] Use3["Application: Radar absorbing materials"] Use4["Application: Over-the-horizon HF links"] Start --> Polar1 Polar1 -->|H| BranchH Polar1 -->|V| BranchV BranchH --> Use1 BranchV --> Use2 BranchV --> Brewster Brewster --> Use3 BranchH --> Critical BranchV --> Critical Critical --> Use4 ``` ### 4.4 Decision Tree for KTU Problem Solving ```mermaid flowchart TB Q["Given: ht, hr, d, lambda, polarization"] Check1{"Is d much greater than ht and hr?"} UseFriis["Use Friis Free-Space Equation
Pr proportional to 1 over d squared"] Use2Ray["Use Two-Ray Model
Pr proportional to 1 over d to the fourth"] Check2{"Calculate crossover distance
dc = 4 ht hr divided by lambda"} Region1["Region d greater than dc
Two-ray far field valid"] Region2["Region d less than dc
Use exact expression with sin term"] Final["Compute Pr or field magnitude
using formulas in Section 2.4"] Q --> Check1 Check1 -->|Yes| Check2 Check1 -->|No| UseFriis Check2 --> Region1 Check2 --> Region2 Region1 --> Final Region2 --> Final ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define Brewster angle. Why does it exist only for one polarization?* **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > The **Brewster angle** $\theta_B$ is the angle of incidence at which the reflection coefficient for **vertically polarized** waves becomes zero, meaning the wave is fully transmitted into the second medium with no reflection. > It exists *only* for vertical (TM) polarization because the boundary conditions for horizontal (TE) polarization make $\Gamma_h$ strictly non-zero for any $\varepsilon_r \neq 1$. Mathematically: > $$\sin \theta_B = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\varepsilon_r + 1}}$$ **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *What is meant by the "smooth earth criterion"? State the Rayleigh formula.* **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > The smooth-earth criterion states that a surface acts as a **specular reflector** (mirror-like) when its height irregularities are smaller than the Rayleigh threshold: > $$h_{\text{crit}} = \frac{\lambda}{8 \sin \theta_i}$$ > Above this threshold, the surface is *rough* and the reflected energy is scattered diffusely, invalidating the two-ray model. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]** **(a)** A VHF transmitter operating at **150 MHz** is mounted on a tower of height $h_t = 50$ m. The receiving antenna is at $h_r = 3$ m on a hilltop **15 km** away. Assume plane-earth reflection with $\Gamma \approx -1$. Calculate: (i) The direct path length $R_1$ and the reflected path length $R_2$. (4 marks) (ii) The path difference $\Delta R$ and the corresponding phase difference $\Delta \Phi$. (3 marks) **(b)** Using the two-ray model, derive the expression for the received field magnitude. State the conditions under which $P_r \propto 1/d^4$ holds. (7 marks) **Model Solution (a):** *Given:* $f = 150$ MHz $\Rightarrow \lambda = c/f = 3 \times 10^8 / 150 \times 10^6 = 2$ m. $d = 15{,}000$ m, $h_t = 50$ m, $h_r = 3$ m. **(i) Direct path:** $$R_1 = \sqrt{d^2 + (h_t - h_r)^2} = \sqrt{15000^2 + 47^2} = \sqrt{2.25 \times 10^8 + 2209}$$ $$R_1 = \sqrt{225{,}002{,}209} \approx 15000.0736 \text{ m}$$ **Reflected path:** $$R_2 = \sqrt{d^2 + (h_t + h_r)^2} = \sqrt{15000^2 + 53^2} = \sqrt{225{,}000{,}000 + 2809}$$ $$R_2 = \sqrt{225{,}002{,}809} \approx 15000.0936 \text{ m}$$ **(ii) Path difference:** $$\Delta R = R_2 - R_1 \approx 0.0200 \text{ m} = 2 \text{ cm}$$ Alternatively, using the **far-field approximation**: $$\Delta R \approx \frac{2 h_t h_r}{d} = \frac{2 \times 50 \times 3}{15000} = \frac{300}{15000} = 0.02 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$ **Phase difference:** $$\Delta \Phi = \beta \cdot \Delta R = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta R = \frac{2\pi}{2} \times 0.02 = 0.02\pi \text{ rad} \approx 3.6°$$ **Model Solution (b):** Refer to **Section 3.3** above for the full derivation. The received field magnitude in the far field is: $$|E_R| = \frac{2E_0}{d}\left|\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}\right)\right|$$ The condition for the $1/d^4$ power law is that the sine argument is small: $$\frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d} \ll 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad d \gg \frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda} \approx \frac{4 h_t h_r}{\lambda}$$ Substituting $\sin(x) \approx x$: $$|E_R| \approx \frac{2E_0}{d}\cdot\frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d} = \frac{4\pi h_t h_r E_0}{\lambda d^2} \propto \frac{1}{d^2}$$ Since power scales as field squared: $$P_r \propto \frac{1}{d^4}$$ This is the **two-ray far-field power law**, valid for distances $d \gg d_c = 4 h_t h_r / \lambda$. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]** **(a)** A horizontally polarized wave of frequency **900 MHz** is incident from free space onto a flat earth with $\varepsilon_r = 9$, $\sigma \approx 0$. Calculate the reflection coefficient for grazing angle $\theta_i = 5°$, $30°$, and $80°$. Comment on the trend. (7 marks) **(b)** Derive the Brewster angle condition for vertical polarization. Why is it not observed for horizontal polarization? (7 marks) **Model Solution (a):** *Given:* $f = 900$ MHz (not directly needed), $\varepsilon_r = 9$, $\sigma = 0$ (lossless dielectric). **Formula:** $$\Gamma_h = \frac{\sin\theta_i - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\theta_i}}{\sin\theta_i + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\theta_i}}$$ **Case 1: $\theta_i = 5°$:** $\sin 5° = 0.0872$, $\cos 5° = 0.9962$, $\cos^2 5° = 0.9924$, $\varepsilon_r - \cos^2 5° = 9 - 0.9924 = 8.0076$, $\sqrt{8.0076} = 2.8300$. $$\Gamma_h = \frac{0.0872 - 2.8300}{0.0872 + 2.8300} = \frac{-2.7428}{2.9172} = -0.9402$$ **Case 2: $\theta_i = 30°$:** $\sin 30° = 0.5$, $\cos 30° = 0.8660$, $\cos^2 30° = 0.75$, $\varepsilon_r - 0.75 = 8.25$, $\sqrt{8.25} = 2.8723$. $$\Gamma_h = \frac{0.5 - 2.8723}{0.5 + 2.8723} = \frac{-2.3723}{3.3723} = -0.7035$$ **Case 3: $\theta_i = 80°$:** $\sin 80° = 0.9848$, $\cos 80° = 0.1736$, $\cos^2 80° = 0.0301$, $\varepsilon_r - 0.0301 = 8.9699$, $\sqrt{8.9699} = 2.9950$. $$\Gamma_h = \frac{0.9848 - 2.9950}{0.9848 + 2.9950} = \frac{-2.0102}{3.9798} = -0.5051$$ **Trend:** As the angle of incidence increases from grazing toward normal, $\Gamma_h$ rises from $\approx -1$ toward $+1$. At grazing incidence (small $\theta_i$), the wave is nearly totally reflected with a phase reversal ($\pi$ rad). This phase reversal is what causes destructive interference between direct and reflected rays at low antenna heights. **Model Solution (b):** Refer to **Section 3.2** for the algebraic derivation. Setting $\Gamma_v = 0$ leads to: $$\sin\theta_B = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\varepsilon_r + 1}}$$ **Why not for horizontal polarization?** For TE polarization, the reflection coefficient numerator is: $$\Gamma_h = \sin\theta_i - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\theta_i}$$ Setting this to zero yields $\sin^2\theta_i = \varepsilon_r - \cos^2\theta_i$, which simplifies to $\sin^2\theta_i(\varepsilon_r - 1) = 0$. This requires either $\theta_i = 0$ (trivial, normal incidence) or $\varepsilon_r = 1$ (no interface at all). Hence for any *real* dielectric with $\varepsilon_r > 1$, $\Gamma_h$ can **never** be zero. Physically, the reflected horizontal electric field is always in phase opposition at grazing angles due to the boundary conditions on $\vec{E}_\text{tangential}$, leaving no angle at which perfect cancellation with the incident ray can occur. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - **Do not** confuse $\sin\theta_B = \sqrt{1/(\varepsilon_r+1)}$ (wave going *into* the denser medium) with the inverted form $\sqrt{\varepsilon_2/(\varepsilon_1+\varepsilon_2)}$ (wave going *out* of the denser medium). KTU questions often specify the direction; misreading it costs 3–4 marks. > - In the two-ray derivation, **do not drop the phase term** $\angle \Gamma$ when $\Gamma \neq -1$. The exact form is $1 + \Gamma e^{-j\beta\Delta R}$, not just $1 - e^{-j\beta\Delta R}$. > - Always **verify the far-field condition** $d \gg h_t, h_r$ before using $\Delta R = 2h_t h_r/d$. The error of using this formula in the near field is a frequent 2-mark deduction. > - When the problem gives $P_t$ in dBm, convert to **linear watts** *before* using $P_r \propto h_t^2 h_r^2/d^4$. Mixing dB and linear scales is a top-3 student error. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Two-ray model** = direct ray + specularly reflected ray off plane earth; total field is phasor sum. - [x] **Reflection coefficient $\Gamma$** is **polarization-dependent**: use $\Gamma_h$ for horizontal, $\Gamma_v$ for vertical. - [x] At **grazing incidence**, $\Gamma \to -1$ (phase reversal of $\pi$ rad) for both polarizations on real earth. - [x] **Brewster angle** $\theta_B$: $\sin\theta_B = \sqrt{1/(\varepsilon_r+1)}$; only for vertical polarization; gives $\Gamma_v = 0$. - [x] **Smooth-earth criterion** (Rayleigh): $h_{\text{crit}} = \lambda/(8\sin\theta_i)$. - [x] **Path difference** in the far field: $\Delta R = 2 h_t h_r / d$. - [x] **Received field** (two-ray, far field, $\Gamma=-1$): $|E_R| = (2E_0/d) \cdot \vert \sin(2\pi h_t h_r / \lambda d) \vert$. - [x] **Power law** at large $d$: $P_r \propto 1/d^4$ — the defining signature of plane-earth propagation. - [x] **Crossover distance** $d_c = 4 h_t h_r / \lambda$ separates near-field (use exact sine form) from far-field (use $1/d^4$). - [x] **Engineering impact:** explains why cellular signals decay as the **fourth power** of distance and why VHF TV antennas need to be mounted *high* (to avoid the destructive null at low elevation). - [x] **Sign convention** in phasor sum: $E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{direct}} + \Gamma \cdot E_{\text{reflected}}$; the $\Gamma$ already includes any phase reversal. - [x] **Limitation:** fails over irregular terrain, for very short ranges ($d < d_c$), and for elevated tropospheric ducts (use 3-ray or knife-edge models instead).

topics.title: Space Wave and Surface Wave
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Space Wave and Surface Wave # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Ground-Wave Propagation: The Three Modes When a transmitting antenna radiates electromagnetic energy near the Earth's surface, the resulting wave reaches the receiving antenna via **three distinct mechanisms**, which together constitute **Ground-Wave Propagation**: 1. **Surface Wave (Ground Wave)** — travels in contact with the Earth's surface. 2. **Space Wave (Tropospheric Wave)** — propagates through the lower atmosphere (troposphere) in straight lines. 3. **Sky Wave (Ionospheric Wave)** — reflected/refracted by the ionosphere. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Focus (Module 4):** This module concentrates on **Space Wave** and **Surface Wave** components. Sky Wave (ionospheric propagation) is covered in Module 5. --- ## 1.2 Space Wave — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Standard):** *Space wave propagation* is the mode of tropospheric propagation in which the electromagnetic wave travels in a **straight line (line-of-sight)** from the transmitting antenna to the receiving antenna. It consists of **two components** — the **direct wave** and the **ground-reflected wave**. ### Conceptual Analogy Think of **two people talking using flashlights at night** in a park. One flashlight beam travels directly to the other person's eyes (the *direct wave*), and another part of the beam hits the ground (or a puddle) and reflects up to their eyes (the *ground-reflected wave*). Both rays combine at the receiver to produce the total received signal. This is exactly how space wave propagation works above **30 MHz**, used in TV, FM radio, and mobile/cellular links. ### Key Characteristics - Frequency range: **Above ~30 MHz** (VHF, UHF, microwave bands). - Dominant in **line-of-sight (LOS)** communication systems. - Strongly affected by **Earth's curvature**, **terrain**, and **antenna heights**. - The direct and reflected waves can **interfere constructively or destructively** depending on path difference. --- ## 1.3 Surface Wave — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Standard):** *Surface wave propagation* is the mode in which the electromagnetic wave is **guided along the Earth's conducting surface**, with the wavefront tilted and energy propagating between the Earth and the atmosphere. The wave attenuates exponentially as it moves away from the surface, decaying rapidly with height. ### Conceptual Analogy Imagine **water flowing in a thin layer across a flat kitchen counter**. The water doesn't fly off into the air — it *clings* to the surface because of surface tension. Similarly, a surface wave is "tethered" to the ground: it follows the curvature of the Earth and can travel **beyond the optical horizon**. This is how AM radio (~540 kHz – 1600 kHz) reaches listeners hundreds of kilometres away. ### Key Characteristics - Frequency range: **Below ~2 MHz** (LF and MF bands; AM broadcast, maritime communication). - **Vertically polarized** waves only (horizontal polarization is rapidly attenuated). - Wavefront is **tilted** forward (Norton/Sommerfeld tilt). - Attenuation depends on **Earth's conductivity (σ)** and **dielectric constant (εᵣ)**. - Lower the frequency, the **lesser the attenuation** — explaining why LF signals travel farthest. - Standard reference constants used in the analysis: $\sigma$ (conductivity in **S/m**) and $\varepsilon_r$ (relative permittivity). --- ## 1.4 Quick Comparison Table | Property | Space Wave | Surface Wave | |----------|------------|--------------| | Frequency band | VHF, UHF, SHF (≥ 30 MHz) | LF, MF (≤ 2 MHz) | | Polarization | Either (H or V) | Vertical only | | Path | LOS + ground reflection | Along Earth surface | | Follows Earth curvature? | No (limited by horizon) | Yes | | Main limitation | Earth's curvature, obstacles | Soil conductivity, frequency | | Typical application | TV, FM, Cellular, Radar | AM Radio, Navigation | --- ## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - Velocity of light in free space: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** - Earth's true radius: $R = 6.37 \times 10^6$ **m** (≈ 6400 km) - Effective Earth radius factor: $k = 4/3$ (standard atmosphere) - Intrinsic impedance of free space: $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377$ **Ω** - Brewster angle (water): $\theta_B \approx 6^\circ$ at HF; useful for low-loss reflection. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Space Wave Propagation — Detailed Theory ### 2.1.1 The Two Components of Space Wave For a transmitting antenna of height $h_t$ and receiving antenna of height $h_r$ over a flat Earth, the received field is the **vector sum** of: 1. **Direct wave** — straight line from TX to RX. 2. **Ground-reflected wave** — reflects from a point on the ground between the antennas. **Resultant field at receiver:** $$ E_R = E_d + E_r $$ where $E_d$ = direct wave field, $E_r$ = ground-reflected wave field. ### 2.1.2 Direct Wave Field For isotropic antennas, the direct field strength at distance $d$: $$ E_d = \frac{\sqrt{30 \, G_t \, P_t}}{d} \quad \text{(V/m)} $$ For a half-wave dipole, gain $G_t = 1.64$, and using practical units: $$ E_d \approx \frac{7.14 \sqrt{P_t}}{d} \quad \text{(V/m at VHF)} $$ ### 2.1.3 Why the Earth Curvature Matters (Line-of-Sight) Because the Earth is spherical, even tall antennas have a **maximum LOS distance**. The line of sight from an antenna of height $h$ to the horizon is: $$ d_{LOS} = \sqrt{2 R h} $$ **Substituting $R = 6400$ km:** $$ d_{LOS} = \sqrt{2 \times 6400 \times h} = \sqrt{12800 \, h} \approx 3.57 \sqrt{h} \quad \text{(km, with h in metres)} $$ ### 2.1.4 Effective Earth's Radius (Atmospheric Refraction) In the real atmosphere, density decreases with height → **wave bending toward Earth**. To account for this, we replace $R$ with an **effective radius** $R' = kR$, where $k = 4/3$ for a standard atmosphere. $$ d_{LOS,eff} = \sqrt{2 k R h} \approx 4.12 \sqrt{h} \quad \text{(km, h in metres, k = 4/3)} $$ **Total LOS between two antennas of heights $h_t$ and $h_r$:** $$ d_{max} = \sqrt{2 k R h_t} + \sqrt{2 k R h_r} $$ ### 2.1.5 Field Strength of Space Wave (Two-Ray Model) For a flat Earth and antennas above ground, the resultant field strength at distance $d$ (with $d \gg h_t, h_r$): $$ E = \frac{\sqrt{60 G_t P_t}}{d} \cdot 2 \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}\right) $$ For very large $d$ (within line of sight), $\sin(x) \approx x$, giving: $$ \boxed{\,E \approx \frac{\sqrt{60 G_t P_t}}{d} \cdot \frac{4\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d} = \frac{2.39 \times 10^4 \, h_t h_r \sqrt{G_t P_t}}{f \, d^2}\;} $$ with $E$ in **µV/m**, $h_t, h_r$ in **metres**, $f$ in **MHz**, $d$ in **km**, $P_t$ in **watts**, $f$ in **MHz** (Jordan's formula). This shows $E \propto 1/d^2$ at large distances — much faster decay than free-space $1/d$. ### 2.1.6 Why High Towers Matter The received power in the two-ray model depends on **$h_t \cdot h_r$** in the numerator. **Doubling both antenna heights gives 4× the field strength.** This is the entire physical reason why VHF/UHF broadcast towers are built extremely tall. --- ## 2.2 Surface Wave Propagation — Detailed Theory ### 2.2.1 Tilt of the Wavefront For a surface wave, the **E-field is not vertical** — it is tilted forward in the direction of propagation by an angle $\tau$ (the **Banos-Wesley tilt** simplified as the **Norton tilt**). The total field has a **vertical component $E_z$** and a small **horizontal component $E_x$** in the direction of propagation. This tilt is what couples the wave to the lossy Earth and produces attenuation. ### 2.2.2 Attenuation Constant and Phase Constant For a surface wave over a finitely conducting Earth, the propagation constant becomes complex: $$ \gamma = \alpha + j\beta $$ with $$ \alpha \approx \frac{\omega\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2}{\sigma/\omega\varepsilon_0}} \quad \text{(Nepers/m)} $$ This is the **key** relationship: lower frequency → smaller $\alpha$ → less attenuation. The exact expressions use the **Sommerfeld numerical distance** $p$ and **height-gain factor** $F$: $$ E = \frac{E_0}{d} \cdot A \cdot F $$ where $A$ is the **attenuation factor** ($A \le 1$) and $F$ is the **height-gain factor** (often $F \approx 1$ for low heights). ### 2.2.3 The Attenuation Factor A Using the **Sommerfeld analysis** for vertical polarization, the attenuation factor is given by: $$ A \approx \frac{2 + 0.3 p}{2 + p + 0.6 p^2} \quad \text{(for practical ranges)} $$ where the **numerical distance** is: $$ p = \frac{\pi d}{\lambda \cdot \varepsilon_{r,c}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{(\varepsilon_{r,c}-1)^2 + (60\lambda\sigma)^2}}{\varepsilon_{r,c}^2 + (60\lambda\sigma)^2} $$ For most practical Earth surfaces (especially seawater), $\varepsilon_{r,c} \approx 80$ and $\sigma \approx 4$ **S/m** at LF/MF. ### 2.2.4 Why Surface Waves Need Vertical Polarization Horizontal polarization induces large **eddy currents** in the Earth's surface because the E-field is parallel to the surface. This causes **extreme attenuation**, so horizontally polarized surface waves are unusable. Vertical polarization places the E-field perpendicular to the surface, producing a **displacement current** (not conduction current), reducing loss. ### 2.2.5 Dependence on Conductivity | Surface | Conductivity σ (S/m) | Surface-wave range | |---------|----------------------|--------------------| | Sea water | 4 | ~1000 km at MF | | Flat rich soil | 0.01 – 0.03 | ~100 km | | Rocky / desert | 0.001 – 0.0001 | ~10–30 km | | Urban / built-up | highly variable | very limited | **Higher σ = lower attenuation = greater range.** --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **exam-ready formula bank**. Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU question on this topic. | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---------|---------|---------------| | 1 | LOS distance (one antenna) | $d_{LOS} = \sqrt{2 k R h}$ | km if R in km, h in m | | 2 | Total LOS distance (two antennas) | $d_{max} = \sqrt{2kRh_t} + \sqrt{2kRh_r}$ | km | | 3 | Effective Earth radius | $R' = kR,\ k = 4/3$ | standard atmosphere | | 4 | Direct wave field (isotropic) | $E_d = \sqrt{30 G_t P_t}\,/\,d$ | V/m | | 5 | Two-ray field (flat Earth) | $E = \frac{\sqrt{60 G_t P_t}}{d} \cdot 2\sin(\pi h_t h_r / \lambda d)$ | V/m | | 6 | Jordan's formula (large d) | $E \approx \dfrac{2.39\times 10^4\, h_t h_r \sqrt{G_t P_t}}{f \, d^2}$ | µV/m, f in MHz, d in km | | 7 | Brewster angle | $\theta_B = \tan^{-1}\!\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ | horizontal pol. reflection → 0 | | 8 | Reflection coefficient (horizontal) | $\Gamma_H = \dfrac{\sin\theta - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\theta}}{\sin\theta + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\theta}}$ | magnitude | | 9 | Reflection coefficient (vertical) | $\Gamma_V = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \sin\theta - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\theta}}{\varepsilon_r \sin\theta + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\theta}}$ | magnitude | | 10 | Surface wave attenuation factor (approx) | $A \approx \dfrac{2 + 0.3p}{2 + p + 0.6 p^2}$ | Sommerfeld, p = numerical distance | | 11 | Surface wave field | $E = (E_0/d) \cdot A \cdot F$ | F ≈ 1 for low heights | | 12 | Wave tilt (Norton) | $tan\tau \approx \sqrt{\sigma/(\omega\varepsilon)}$ | tilt angle | | 13 | Free-space intrinsic impedance | $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377\ \Omega$ | constant | | 14 | Earth's radius | $R = 6.37 \times 10^6$ m | true radius | | 15 | Practical LOS constant | $d_{km} \approx 4.12\sqrt{h_m}$ | k = 4/3 | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cellular tower design** uses the LOS formula to plan cell radius. - **TV broadcast planning** uses two-ray field model to set tower height. - **AM broadcast networks** rely on surface-wave coverage for hundreds of km. - **HF over-the-horizon radar** uses a combination of surface wave and ionospheric reflections. - **Maritime communication** in MF band depends almost entirely on surface waves. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Line-of-Sight Distance over a Spherical Earth ### Setup A transmitting antenna of height $h$ is mounted on the surface of the Earth (radius $R$). We wish to find the **maximum straight-line distance** $d$ along the surface to a receiving antenna that just grazes the horizon (i.e., the line from antenna tip is tangent to the Earth's surface). **Geometry (textbook-standard):** Let the Earth's centre be $O$, the TX base be at $A$, and the receiving point on the horizon be at $B$. The TX tip is at $T$, with $AT = h$. The line $TB$ is tangent to the Earth at $B$, so $OT = OB = R$, and $TB \perp OB$. ### Step 1 — Apply Pythagoras to triangle $OTB$ $$ OT^2 = OB^2 + TB^2 $$ ### Step 2 — Substitute $$ (R + h)^2 = R^2 + d^2 $$ where $d = TB$ is the straight-line LOS distance from tip to grazing point, and we use the surface arc length approximation $d \approx TB$ (valid for $h \ll R$). ### Step 3 — Expand $$ R^2 + 2Rh + h^2 = R^2 + d^2 $$ ### Step 4 — Cancel $R^2$ and use $h \ll R$ (so $h^2 \approx 0$) $$ 2Rh \approx d^2 $$ ### Step 5 — Solve for $d$ $$ d = \sqrt{2Rh} $$ ### Step 6 — Substitute atmospheric refraction correction (effective radius) In the real troposphere, waves bend, modelled by replacing $R$ with $R' = kR$: $$ \boxed{\,d_{LOS} = \sqrt{2 k R h}\,} $$ ### Step 7 — Plug in numerical values ($R = 6.37 \times 10^6$ m, $k = 4/3$) $$ d_{LOS} = \sqrt{2 \cdot \tfrac{4}{3} \cdot 6.37 \times 10^6 \cdot h} = \sqrt{1.698 \times 10^7 \, h} $$ Converting $h$ from m to km and $d$ to km: $$ \boxed{\,d_{km} \approx 4.12 \sqrt{h_m}\,} $$ This is the **standard formula** used by KTU examiners. Notice $d$ scales as the **square root of antenna height**, not linearly. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Field Strength of Space Wave (Two-Ray Model, Flat Earth) ### Setup - Transmitter height $h_t$, receiver height $h_r$, both above a flat perfectly conducting ground. - Distance between bases along the ground: $d$. - Operating wavelength: $\lambda$. ### Step 1 — Direct ray path length $$ r_1 = \sqrt{d^2 + (h_t - h_r)^2} \approx d + \frac{(h_t - h_r)^2}{2d} $$ ### Step 2 — Reflected ray path length (using image theory) The reflected ray appears to come from the **image** of the TX located at height $-h_t$. Its path is: $$ r_2 = \sqrt{d^2 + (h_t + h_r)^2} \approx d + \frac{(h_t + h_r)^2}{2d} $$ ### Step 3 — Path difference $\Delta r$ $$ \Delta r = r_2 - r_1 = \frac{(h_t + h_r)^2 - (h_t - h_r)^2}{2d} = \frac{2 h_t h_r}{d} $$ ### Step 4 — Phase difference $$ \Delta\phi = \beta \cdot \Delta r = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{2 h_t h_r}{d} = \frac{4\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d} $$ ### Step 5 — Phase reversal at ground Reflection from a denser medium (Earth) introduces a **180° phase reversal** for horizontal polarization (and approximately 180° for vertical at low grazing angles). So the reflected wave's field is: $$ E_r = \Gamma \cdot E_0 \cdot e^{-j(\beta r_2 + \pi)} \approx -E_0 e^{-j\beta r_2} $$ (taking $\Gamma \approx -1$ for typical grazing incidence over good ground). ### Step 6 — Total received field $$ E = E_d + E_r = E_0 e^{-j\beta r_1} - E_0 e^{-j\beta r_2} $$ $$ E = E_0 e^{-j\beta r_1} \left[1 - e^{-j\beta \Delta r}\right] $$ Using $1 - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin(\theta/2) \cdot e^{-j\theta/2}$: $$ |E| = 2 E_0 \sin\!\left(\frac{\beta \Delta r}{2}\right) = 2 E_0 \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}\right) $$ ### Step 7 — Substitute $E_0 = \sqrt{30 G_t P_t}\,/\,d$ (isotropic far-field) $$ \boxed{\,E = \frac{2\sqrt{30\, G_t P_t}}{d} \cdot \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}\right)\;} $$ ### Step 8 — Far-distance limit ($\sin x \approx x$) When $2\pi h_t h_r / (\lambda d) \ll 1$ (large $d$): $$ E \approx \frac{2\sqrt{30 G_t P_t}}{d} \cdot \frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d} = \frac{4\pi \sqrt{30 G_t P_t}\, h_t h_r}{\lambda d^2} $$ Substituting $\lambda = c/f$, $c = 3\times 10^8$ m/s, and converting to **µV/m, MHz, km, watts, metres**: $$ \boxed{\,E \approx \frac{2.39 \times 10^4 \, h_t h_r \sqrt{G_t P_t}}{f \, d^2}\ \mu\text{V/m}\,} $$ This is **Jordan's formula** — the KTU board's most-asked formula on space wave. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Reflection Coefficient at Grazing Incidence (for two-ray) For low-elevation (grazing) angles, $\theta$ is close to 90° (measured from normal). Define **grazing angle** $\psi = 90° - \theta$, so for small $\psi$, $\sin\theta \approx 1$, $\cos\theta \approx \psi$. ### Horizontal polarization $$ \Gamma_H = \frac{\sin\theta - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\theta}}{\sin\theta + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\theta}} $$ For $\psi \to 0$ (grazing), $\cos\theta \to 0$: $$ \Gamma_H \approx \frac{1 - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{1 + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \to -1 $$ ### Vertical polarization $$ \Gamma_V = \frac{\varepsilon_r \sin\theta - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\theta}}{\varepsilon_r \sin\theta + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\theta}} $$ For grazing: $\Gamma_V \approx -1$ as well. **Both approach $-1$ at grazing**, which is the basis for the 180° phase reversal assumed in the two-ray model. --- ## 3.4 Numerical Example — KTU Style **Problem:** A TV transmitter radiates 50 kW EIRP at 100 MHz from a tower 200 m tall. A receiver antenna is at 10 m height, 30 km away. Compute the received field strength. ### Step 1 — Free-space field at 30 km $$ E_0 = \frac{\sqrt{30 \cdot 1 \cdot 50000}}{30 \times 10^3} = \frac{\sqrt{1.5 \times 10^6}}{3 \times 10^4} = \frac{1224.7}{3 \times 10^4} \approx 0.0408\ \text{V/m} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute the sine argument $$ \frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 200 \cdot 10}{3 \cdot 30 \times 10^3} = \frac{4000\pi}{90000} \approx 0.1396\ \text{rad} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply the two-ray formula $$ E = \frac{2 \cdot 0.0408}{1} \cdot \sin(0.1396) = 0.0816 \cdot 0.1392 \approx 0.01136\ \text{V/m} = 11360\ \mu\text{V/m} $$ ### Step 4 — Cross-check with Jordan's formula $$ E = \frac{2.39 \times 10^4 \cdot 200 \cdot 10 \cdot \sqrt{1 \cdot 50000}}{100 \cdot (30)^2} $$ $$ = \frac{2.39\times 10^4 \cdot 2000 \cdot 223.6}{100 \cdot 900} $$ $$ = \frac{2.39 \times 10^4 \cdot 4.472 \times 10^5}{9 \times 10^4} $$ $$ \approx 11868\ \mu\text{V/m} $$ Both methods give ≈ **11–12 mV/m** — they match. ✓ # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Components of Ground-Wave Propagation ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[TX Antenna
Source of EM wave] --> nodeB[Ground Wave Propagation
Mechanism 1] nodeA --> nodeC[Space Wave Propagation
Mechanism 2] nodeA --> nodeD[Sky Wave Propagation
Mechanism 3] nodeB --> nodeE[Surface Wave
Along Earth surface] nodeC --> nodeF[Direct Wave
Line of sight] nodeC --> nodeG[Ground Reflected Wave
From terrain] nodeD --> nodeH[Ionospheric Reflection
From ionosphere layers] nodeE --> nodeI[RX Antenna
Receives signal] nodeF --> nodeI nodeG --> nodeI nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ[Field Strength E
Vector sum at RX] ``` > [!NOTE] > The shaded mechanism block (Module 4 focus) comprises **Surface Wave + Space Wave**. The third block (Sky Wave) is Module 5 territory. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Two-Ray Space Wave Model ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeT[TX Antenna
height ht] -->|Direct ray r1| nodeR[RX Antenna
height hr] nodeT -->|Reflected ray r2| nodeG[Ground reflection point] nodeG -->|Reflected ray r2| nodeR nodeR --> nodeV[Vector sum E = Ed plus Er] nodeV --> nodeF[Field strength formula
Jordan equation] nodeA[Atmospheric refraction] -.-> nodeV nodeK[Earth curvature kR] -.-> nodeV ``` **Engineering interpretation:** Both rays combine at the receiver. The resulting field depends on the **path-length difference** $\Delta r = 2 h_t h_r / d$ and the **reflection phase reversal**. --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Surface Wave Coupling Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Vertical Polarized TX] --> nodeB[Wavefront tilt
Norton tilt tau] nodeB --> nodeC[Vertical Ez component] nodeB --> nodeD[Horizontal Ex component] nodeC --> nodeE[Displacement current
in Earth] nodeD --> nodeF[Conduction current
in Earth] nodeE --> nodeG[Attenuation constant alpha] nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH[Range limitation] nodeI[Ground conductivity sigma] --> nodeG nodeJ[Frequency f] --> nodeG nodeK[Permittivity eps_r] --> nodeG ``` **Engineering interpretation:** The two field components (vertical and horizontal) drive currents in the lossy ground. Higher $\sigma$ and lower $f$ mean **smaller attenuation** and **longer range**. --- ## 4.4 Comparative Architecture — Space Wave vs Surface Wave ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SPW[Space Wave] sA[Frequency greater than 30 MHz] --> sB[LOS path] sB --> sC[Direct ray plus reflected ray] sC --> sD[Two ray field model] end subgraph SFW[Surface Wave] tA[Frequency less than 2 MHz] --> tB[Vertical polarization] tB --> tC[Wavefront tilt] tC --> tD[Sommerfeld Norton field model] end sD --> sE[Applications: TV, FM, Cellular, Radar] tD --> tE[Applications: AM broadcast, Maritime, Navigation] ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** **Define space wave propagation. Why is it dominant above 30 MHz?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Space wave propagation** is the mode of tropospheric propagation in which the wave travels in a straight line from transmitter to receiver. It consists of two components: the **direct wave** and the **ground-reflected wave**. > > Above 30 MHz, sky-wave reflection from the ionosphere fails (the wave passes through the F-layer), and surface-wave attenuation becomes severe. Hence space wave (LOS + reflection) becomes the **only viable propagation mode** in VHF/UHF/microwave bands. > > **(1 mark) — definition; (1 mark) — two components; (1 mark) — justification for >30 MHz.** --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** **What is meant by "effective Earth's radius"? Why is the factor 4/3 used?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The **effective Earth's radius** $R' = kR$ is an artificially increased radius used to keep radio ray paths as straight lines in tropospheric-refraction analysis. > > In the real atmosphere, the refractive index decreases with height, causing radio waves to **bend slightly toward the Earth**. This bending is equivalent to propagating in straight lines over an Earth of radius $R' = (4/3)R$. > > The factor $k = 4/3$ is the average value for a **standard atmosphere** (linear refractive-index gradient of −39 N-units/km). It increases the maximum LOS distance by about 15%. > > **(1 mark) — definition; (1 mark) — physical reason; (1 mark) — meaning of 4/3.** --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Space Wave Focus **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model, Adapted]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **maximum line-of-sight distance** between two antennas of heights $h_t$ and $h_r$ over a spherical Earth of effective radius $R' = kR$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A VHF transmitter at 100 MHz, height 300 m, radiates 25 kW with antenna gain 6 dB. Compute the field strength at a receiver 50 km away, antenna height 20 m. Use Jordan's formula. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) — Derivation (7 marks):** Refer to Section 3.1 above for the **full step-by-step derivation**. Key checkpoints the examiner awards: - Geometry setup: TX tip at $T$, tangent point at $B$, Earth centre $O$. **(1 mark)** - Pythagoras in triangle $OTB$: $(R+h)^2 = R^2 + d^2$. **(1 mark)** - Approximation $h^2 \ll 2Rh$ and rearrangement: $d = \sqrt{2Rh}$. **(1 mark)** - Replace $R$ with effective radius $R' = kR$: $d_{LOS} = \sqrt{2kRh}$. **(1 mark)** - Numerical constant derivation: with $k = 4/3$ and $R = 6.37\times 10^6$ m, obtain $d_{km} \approx 4.12\sqrt{h_m}$. **(1 mark)** - Two-antenna case: $d_{max} = \sqrt{2kRh_t} + \sqrt{2kRh_r}$. **(1 mark)** - Final boxed answer with units. **(1 mark)** **(b) — Numerical (7 marks):** Given: $f = 100$ MHz, $h_t = 300$ m, $h_r = 20$ m, $d = 50$ km, $P_t = 25$ kW, $G_t = 6$ dB. **Step 1 — Convert gain from dB to linear (1 mark):** $$ G_t = 10^{6/10} = 10^{0.6} = 3.981 $$ **Step 2 — Check far-field condition (1 mark):** $$ \frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 300 \cdot 20}{3 \cdot 50 \times 10^3} = \frac{37699}{150000} = 0.2513 \text{ rad} $$ Since this is moderately small, **Jordan's approximation is acceptable** (the full sine gives 0.249, very close to 0.251 — error ≈ 1%). **Step 3 — Apply Jordan's formula (3 marks):** $$ E = \frac{2.39 \times 10^4 \cdot h_t \cdot h_r \cdot \sqrt{G_t P_t}}{f \cdot d^2} $$ Numerator: $2.39\times 10^4 \cdot 300 \cdot 20 = 1.434 \times 10^8$ $\sqrt{G_t P_t} = \sqrt{3.981 \times 25000} = \sqrt{99525} = 315.5$ Numerator total: $1.434 \times 10^8 \cdot 315.5 = 4.524 \times 10^{10}$ Denominator: $f \cdot d^2 = 100 \cdot (50)^2 = 100 \cdot 2500 = 2.5 \times 10^5$ $$ E = \frac{4.524 \times 10^{10}}{2.5 \times 10^5} = 1.81 \times 10^5\ \mu\text{V/m} $$ **Step 4 — Convert and express (2 marks):** $$ E \approx 1.81 \times 10^5\ \mu\text{V/m} = 181\ \text{mV/m} = 0.181\ \text{V/m} $$ $$ \boxed{\,E \approx 181\ \text{mV/m} \approx 0.181\ \text{V/m}\,} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Surface Wave Focus (Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain **surface wave propagation** with a neat diagram. Discuss why surface waves must be **vertically polarized**, and explain the **tilt of the wavefront**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 1 MHz surface-wave transmitter radiates 10 kW over seawater ($\sigma = 4$ S/m, $\varepsilon_r = 80$). Estimate the **numerical distance** at 100 km and the **attenuation factor** $A$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) — Theory (7 marks):** - **Definition (2 marks):** Surface wave is the EM wave that propagates along the conducting surface of the Earth, with the wavefront tilted forward and field decaying exponentially with height. Used in LF/MF bands (≤ 2 MHz) for AM broadcast and maritime communication. - **Why vertical polarization (3 marks):** A horizontally polarized surface wave would have its E-field parallel to the lossy ground, inducing large **conduction currents** in the Earth, leading to severe attenuation. In vertical polarization, the dominant **E-field is perpendicular to the surface**, producing primarily **displacement current** in the dielectric ground, with much lower loss. Hence only vertical polarization supports useful surface-wave propagation. - **Wavefront tilt (2 marks):** As the wave propagates over a finitely conducting Earth, the **H-field** decays faster than the **E-field** (due to ground losses). To keep the Poynting vector pointing into the ground (representing power flow into the lossy medium), the wavefront must tilt forward by an angle $\tau$ (Norton tilt). This gives the wave a small **horizontal E-field component** in the direction of propagation — the signature of the surface wave. **(b) — Numerical (7 marks):** Given: $f = 1$ MHz, $\lambda = 300$ m, $d = 100$ km, $\sigma = 4$ S/m, $\varepsilon_r = 80$. **Step 1 — Compute auxiliary term (1 mark):** $$ 60 \lambda \sigma = 60 \cdot 300 \cdot 4 = 72000 $$ **Step 2 — Compute the numerator inside the square root of $p$ (2 marks):** $$ \sqrt{(\varepsilon_r - 1)^2 + (60\lambda\sigma)^2} = \sqrt{(79)^2 + (72000)^2} = \sqrt{6241 + 5.184 \times 10^9} $$ Since $72000^2$ dominates: $$ \approx 72000 $$ **Step 3 — Compute the denominator of $p$ (1 mark):** $$ \varepsilon_r^2 + (60\lambda\sigma)^2 = 6400 + 5.184 \times 10^9 \approx 5.184 \times 10^9 $$ **Step 4 — Compute $p$ (2 marks):** $$ p = \frac{\pi d}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{72000}{5.184 \times 10^9} $$ $$ = \frac{\pi \cdot 100 \times 10^3}{300} \cdot \frac{72000}{5.184 \times 10^9} $$ $$ = 1047.2 \cdot 1.389 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ \boxed{\,p \approx 0.0145\,} $$ **Step 5 — Compute attenuation factor $A$ (1 mark):** $$ A \approx \frac{2 + 0.3 \cdot 0.0145}{2 + 0.0145 + 0.6 \cdot (0.0145)^2} = \frac{2.00435}{2.01463} \approx 0.9949 $$ $$ \boxed{\,A \approx 0.995\,} $$ **Interpretation:** At 1 MHz over seawater, the surface wave suffers almost **no attenuation** at 100 km — explaining the enormous ranges of LF maritime communication. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Unit conversion errors** in Jordan's formula (µV/m, MHz, km, m, W) — losing 1–2 marks per question. **Always write units explicitly.** > 2. **Forgetting the 180° phase reversal** at ground reflection in the two-ray model — examiner deducts **full 2 marks** in derivations. > 3. **Confusing $R$ and $R'$** (true vs. effective Earth radius). The factor $k = 4/3$ is for **standard atmosphere only** — not for ducting or sub-refraction. > 4. **Wrong sign of $\Gamma$**: at grazing incidence, $\Gamma_H, \Gamma_V \to -1$, **not** $+1$. This is the most common mistake. > 5. **Surface wave always vertically polarized** — mentioning horizontal polarization will be marked **wrong** and cost 1–2 marks. > 6. **In numerical problems**, the sine argument $2\pi h_t h_r / \lambda d$ must be in **radians**, not degrees. Many students forget to convert. > 7. **The numerical-distance formula** for $p$ is intricate; skipping intermediate steps costs 1 mark. **Show all 4–5 sub-calculations.** --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > Save this list — these are the **must-know facts** for KTU Module 4. - [x] **Space wave** = direct + ground-reflected wave; used above 30 MHz. - [x] **Surface wave** = wave guided along Earth's surface; used below 2 MHz; **vertical polarization only**. - [x] **Line-of-sight distance** = $d_{km} \approx 4.12\sqrt{h_m}$ (k = 4/3). - [x] **Two-antenna LOS** = $d_{max} = \sqrt{2kRh_t} + \sqrt{2kRh_r}$. - [x] **Effective Earth radius** $R' = (4/3)R$ — compensates for atmospheric refraction. - [x] **Two-ray field formula** = $E = \dfrac{2\sqrt{30 G_t P_t}}{d} \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}\right)$. - [x] **Jordan's formula (far distance)** = $E \approx 2.39\times 10^4\, h_t h_r \sqrt{G_t P_t} / (f d^2)$ in µV/m, MHz, km, W, m. - [x] **Reflection coefficient** at grazing ≈ $-1$ for both polarizations → 180° phase reversal. - [x] **Field scales as** $E \propto h_t h_r / d^2$ at large $d$ (decays faster than free-space $1/d$). - [x] **Surface wave tilt (Norton tilt)** = $\tan\tau \approx \sqrt{\sigma / \omega\varepsilon}$. - [x] **Attenuation factor** = $A \approx (2 + 0.3p) / (2 + p + 0.6p^2)$ where $p$ = numerical distance. - [x] **Lower frequency → smaller attenuation** in surface waves. - [x] **Higher ground conductivity (σ) → smaller attenuation**. - [x] **Earth constants to memorize**: $R = 6.37\times 10^6$ m, $k = 4/3$, $\eta_0 = 120\pi$ Ω. - [x] **Standard Earth parameters**: seawater $\sigma = 4$ S/m, $\varepsilon_r = 80$. - [x] **Applications**: TV/FM (space wave), AM radio (surface wave), cellular (space wave), maritime (surface wave).

topics.title: Spherical Earth Propagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Spherical Earth Propagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Spherical Earth Propagation?

In the study of radio wave propagation, the Earth is a **spherical conducting body of radius approximately 6400 km**. When communication distances exceed a few tens of kilometres, the assumption of a *flat Earth* fails. **Spherical Earth Propagation** is the study of how ground-based (space) radio waves travel between two antennas located on the curved surface of the Earth, accounting for the Earth's curvature, the radio horizon, and atmospheric refraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> Spherical Earth Propagation deals with the propagation of space waves between transmitting and receiving antennas located on or near the surface of a *curved Earth*. It accounts for the loss of line-of-sight due to Earth's curvature, the effect of tropospheric refraction, and the modification of the radio horizon with respect to the optical horizon.

## 1.2 Why the Flat Earth Assumption Fails

For small distances (say, a few km), the Earth can be considered flat because the curvature is negligible. However, for typical UHF/SHF communication links (microwave links, TV broadcasting, cellular networks), the **line-of-sight (LOS) path is interrupted by the Earth's curvature**. The wave must clear the bulge of the Earth to reach the receiver.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Ship and the Lighthouse:**
> Imagine standing on the seashore. A small boat close to shore is clearly visible. But when the same boat sails *far away*, the hull disappears first and only the mast is visible — and finally, the mast disappears too. The boat has not sunk; the curved ocean surface has **bulged up** and blocked the view.
> 
> Exactly the same happens with radio waves. The Earth bulges upward between the transmitter and the receiver. If the antennas are not tall enough, the radio signal gets blocked by this bulge. **The job of spherical earth propagation analysis is to find how tall the antennas must be to "see" each other over the bulge.**

## 1.4 Key Parameters Introduced

- **Line-of-Sight Distance ($d_{LOS}$)** — Maximum distance over which two antennas can communicate with direct waves.
- **Radio Horizon** — Distance to which radio waves travel, which is **larger** than the optical horizon due to atmospheric refraction.
- **Effective Earth's Radius ($R_e$)** — An *artificially inflated* radius of the Earth ($R_e = kR$) used to absorb the effect of refraction into a straight-line geometry.
- **k-Factor** — Ratio of effective Earth radius to actual Earth radius ($k = R_e / R$). Standard atmosphere gives $k = 4/3$.

## 1.5 Modes Affected by Spherical Earth

| Mode | Range | Affected by Curvature? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Surface Wave | Up to ~100 km (LF/MF) | Yes, but dominant mode |
| **Space Wave (LOS)** | **VHF/UHF/SHF (≥ 30 MHz)** | **Yes — Primary affected mode** |
| Tropospheric Scatter | 100–500 km | Yes |
| Ionospheric Sky Wave | HF (< 30 MHz) | Negligible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 2024 Module 4**, the focus is on **Space Wave (LOS) propagation over spherical earth**, including the *radio horizon*, the *k-factor*, and the *effective Earth's radius*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Geometry of LOS over a Curved Earth

Consider two antennas mounted on top of masts of heights $h_t$ (transmitting) and $h_r$ (receiving), separated by distance $d$ over a spherical Earth of actual radius $R = 6400$ km.

The straight-line LOS ray just grazes the Earth's bulge when both antennas are at their respective radio horizons. The geometry involves a right triangle with:

- One vertex at the **centre of the Earth**.
- Another vertex at the **antenna base**.
- The hypotenuse going from the Earth's centre to the antenna tip.

## 2.2 Optical Horizon vs Radio Horizon

- **Optical Horizon**: The geometric distance at which the LOS just grazes the *actual* spherical Earth (no refraction).
- **Radio Horizon**: The actual distance a radio wave reaches. Because the troposphere refracts waves *downward*, the radio horizon extends **15–30 % beyond** the optical horizon.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (Standard Atmosphere, k = 4/3):**
> 
> $$d_{radio} \approx \sqrt{17 h_t} + \sqrt{17 h_r} \quad (\text{km}, \text{height in m})$$
> 
> Compare with the optical horizon (no refraction):
> 
> $$d_{optical} \approx \sqrt{12.74 h_t} + \sqrt{12.74 h_r} \quad (\text{km})$$

## 2.3 Effective Earth's Radius and k-Factor

Atmospheric refraction causes the radio ray to bend slightly downward with a radius of curvature $\rho$. The path is therefore curved relative to the Earth. To simplify calculations, instead of bending the **ray**, we **inflate the Earth** so that the ray can be drawn straight.

If $\rho$ is the radius of curvature of the ray, then:

$$\frac{1}{R_e} = \frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{\rho}$$

Defining $k = \dfrac{R_e}{R}$:

$$k = \frac{1}{1 - R/\rho}$$

For a **standard atmosphere**, $R/\rho \approx 0.25$, giving the universal constant:

$$\boxed{k = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.333}$$

Special atmospheric conditions:
- **k > 1** → Super-refraction (denser atmosphere below).
- **k < 1** → Sub-refraction.
- **k = ∞** → Critical refraction / Ducting.

## 2.4 Why k-Factor Matters

By replacing $R$ with $R_e = kR$, all flat-Earth formulas become valid on a *spherical Earth with standard refraction*. For example, the LOS distance formula retains its structure:

$$d_{LOS} = \sqrt{2 k R h_t} + \sqrt{2 k R h_r}$$

with $2kR \approx 17{,}066$ km when $k = 4/3$.

## 2.5 Effect of Curvature on Field Strength

For a flat Earth, the field strength at distance $d$ in free space is:

$$E = \frac{\sqrt{30 G_t P_t}}{d} \quad (\text{V/m})$$

On a spherical Earth, two additional loss mechanisms appear:

1. **Bulge Loss (Diffraction Loss)** — Caused by part of the wave being obstructed by the Earth's bulge. Quantified using the **Fresnel-Kirchhoff knife-edge diffraction** method or empirical ITU curves.
2. **Path-Loss Correction** — Beyond the radio horizon, the field decays faster than $1/d$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bullington's Method (Simplified Approach used in KTU):**
> 
> When terrain is irregular, the obstacle that gives the **maximum Fresnel-zone blockage** is replaced by an equivalent **knife edge**, and the diffraction loss is computed using the **Fresnel integral** or the standard knife-edge loss curve.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-ready**. Memorise every entry. Constants:
> - **Earth's actual radius: $R = 6400$ km $= 6.4 \times 10^6$ m**
> - **Standard atmosphere: $k = 4/3$**, giving $R_e = 8517$ km, $2kR = 17{,}066$ km.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | LOS distance (no refraction, flat horizon) | $d_1 = \sqrt{2 R h_t}$ | $h_t$ in m, gives $d_1$ in m |
| 2 | Total LOS distance (flat Earth approximation) | $d_{LOS} = \sqrt{2 R h_t} + \sqrt{2 R h_r}$ | $R = 6.4 \times 10^6$ m |
| 3 | LOS distance with refraction (effective radius) | $d_{LOS} = \sqrt{2 k R h_t} + \sqrt{2 k R h_r}$ | Use $k = 4/3$ for standard atm. |
| 4 | Engineering form (standard atm.) | $d \approx \sqrt{17 h_t} + \sqrt{17 h_r}$ | km when $h$ in m |
| 5 | Effective Earth radius | $R_e = k R$ | $R_e = 8517$ km when $k = 4/3$ |
| 6 | k-Factor from ray curvature | $k = 1 / (1 - R/\rho)$ | $\rho$ = ray curvature radius |
| 7 | Radius of curvature of ray | $1/\rho = -dn/dr$ | $n$ = refractive index, $r$ = radius |
| 8 | Refractivity | $N = (n - 1) \times 10^6$ | Standard $N_s = 315$ at surface |
| 9 | Gradient of refractivity | $dN/dh$ | Standard $= -39$ N/km |
| 10 | Free-space field strength | $E = \sqrt{30 G_t P_t} / d$ | V/m, $P_t$ in W |
| 11 | Knife-edge diffraction parameter | $v = h \sqrt{2 d / (\lambda d_1 d_2)}$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Path length difference | $\Delta r = h^2 / 2 \cdot (d_1 + d_2)/(d_1 d_2)$ | Geometry |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** These formulas are the backbone of every **microwave link budget**, **cell tower height planning**, **radar line-of-sight analysis**, **TV broadcasting network design**, and **satellite ground-station siting**. Without them, towers would either be too short (no coverage) or wastefully too tall (extra cost).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Line-of-Sight Distance over a Flat Earth (Baseline)

Consider a transmitting antenna at height $h_t$ above a **flat** Earth. From simple geometry, the maximum distance at which the antenna top is visible to a receiver at ground level is found from the Pythagoras theorem.

Let $d$ be the distance to the horizon. Then:

$$(R + h_t)^2 = R^2 + d^2$$

Expanding the LHS:

$$R^2 + 2 R h_t + h_t^2 = R^2 + d^2$$

Cancelling $R^2$ on both sides:

$$2 R h_t + h_t^2 = d^2$$

Since $h_t \ll R$ (antennas are at most a few hundred metres, while $R = 6400$ km), the term $h_t^2$ is negligible. Therefore:

$$d_1^2 \approx 2 R h_t \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{d_1 = \sqrt{2 R h_t}}$$

For two antennas at heights $h_t$ and $h_r$, the total LOS distance is the **sum** of both horizons:

$$\boxed{d_{LOS} = \sqrt{2 R h_t} + \sqrt{2 R h_r}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of LOS Distance with Atmospheric Refraction

In a refracting atmosphere, the radio ray follows a **curved path**. To handle this elegantly, we replace the actual Earth with an *effective* Earth whose radius is chosen such that the **same ray now becomes a straight line** relative to the new surface.

For a ray with curvature $1/\rho$ (centre of curvature on the same side as Earth's centre) and Earth of curvature $1/R$, the **relative curvature** between the ray and the Earth is:

$$\text{Relative curvature} = \frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{\rho}$$

We define the effective radius $R_e$ such that:

$$\frac{1}{R_e} = \frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{\rho} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_e = \frac{R}{1 - R/\rho}$$

Defining the k-factor:

$$\boxed{k = \frac{R_e}{R} = \frac{1}{1 - R/\rho}}$$

For the standard atmosphere:
- The gradient of refractive index: $dn/dh = -39 \times 10^{-6}$ per km.
- This gives $1/\rho \approx dn/dh \cdot (1/R) \cdot R = $ ... → ultimately $R/\rho \approx 0.25$.
- Hence $k = 1 / (1 - 0.25) = 1 / 0.75 = 4/3$.

Substituting $R_e = kR$ into the flat-Earth formula:

$$\boxed{d_{LOS} = \sqrt{2 k R h_t} + \sqrt{2 k R h_r}}$$

### 3.2.1 Numerical evaluation of the constant $2kR$

$$2 k R = 2 \times \frac{4}{3} \times 6400 \text{ km} = \frac{2 \times 4 \times 6400}{3} = \frac{51{,}200}{3} = 17{,}066.67 \text{ km}$$

So the engineering form becomes:

$$d_{LOS} = \sqrt{17{,}066.67 \, h_t} + \sqrt{17{,}066.67 \, h_r} \quad (h \text{ in km})$$

If we want $h$ in **metres**, convert: $h$ in km $= h_{m}/1000$:

$$d = \sqrt{17{,}066.67 \times h_m / 1000} + \sqrt{17{,}066.67 \times h_r / 1000} \quad \text{km}$$

$$d = \sqrt{17.0667 \, h_m} + \sqrt{17.0667 \, h_r} \quad \text{km} \approx \sqrt{17 h_t} + \sqrt{17 h_r}$$

This is the famous **$\sqrt{17 h}$ rule** widely used in microwave link engineering.

## 3.3 Derivation of the k-Factor from Refractivity Gradient

The refractive index of the troposphere decreases with height:

$$n(h) = 1 + N_s \times 10^{-6} - \frac{dN}{dh} \cdot h \times 10^{-6}$$

The radius of curvature of a ray in a medium with index gradient $dn/dh$ (using Snell's law in a spherically symmetric medium) is:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = -\frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{dn}{dh} \approx -\frac{dn}{dh} \quad (\text{since } n \approx 1)$$

For standard atmosphere:

$$\frac{dn}{dh} = -\frac{39}{10^6} \text{ per m} = -39 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per m}$$

But for the relative curvature, we use $R/\rho$:

$$\frac{R}{\rho} = -R \cdot \frac{dn}{dh}$$

$$= -6.4 \times 10^6 \cdot (-39 \times 10^{-6}) = +0.2496 \approx 0.25$$

Thus:

$$k = \frac{1}{1 - 0.25} = \frac{1}{0.75} = \frac{4}{3}$$

## 3.4 Field Strength Over a Spherical Earth (Qualitative Derivation)

For an antenna radiating power $P_t$ with gain $G_t$, the power density at distance $d$ in free space is:

$$S = \frac{P_t G_t}{4 \pi d^2}$$

The corresponding electric field:

$$E = \sqrt{S \cdot \eta_0} = \sqrt{\frac{P_t G_t \eta_0}{4 \pi d^2}} = \frac{\sqrt{30 G_t P_t}}{d} \quad (\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377 \;\Omega)$$

Over a spherical Earth, two corrections apply:

1. **Within LOS** ($d \leq d_{radio}$): Use the above formula with $d$ replaced by effective LOS distance.
2. **Beyond LOS (Diffraction Region)**: An additional attenuation factor $A(d, h)$ (in dB) is subtracted, found from the **ITU-R P.526 curves** (Bullington equivalent). 

> [!NOTE]
> **The diffraction loss over a knife edge** for parameter $v$ is:
> 
> $$J(v) = 6.9 + 20 \log_{10}\left[\sqrt{(v - 0.1)^2 + 1} + v - 0.1\right] \quad \text{dB}$$
>
