> where $v$ is the Fresnel-Kirchhoff diffraction parameter defined earlier.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Two microwave antennas operate at 6 GHz. The transmit antenna is 100 m above ground, and the receive antenna is 80 m above ground. Compute the radio horizon distance using (a) optical horizon formula (no refraction), and (b) standard atmosphere ($k = 4/3$).

### Solution

**Given:**
- $h_t = 100$ m $= 0.1$ km
- $h_r = 80$ m $= 0.08$ km
- $R = 6400$ km

**(a) Optical horizon (flat-Earth, no refraction):**

$$d_{optical} = \sqrt{2 R h_t} + \sqrt{2 R h_r}$$

Computing each term:

$$2 R h_t = 2 \times 6400 \times 0.1 = 1280 \text{ km}^2$$

$$\sqrt{2 R h_t} = \sqrt{1280} = 35.78 \text{ km}$$

$$2 R h_r = 2 \times 6400 \times 0.08 = 1024 \text{ km}^2$$

$$\sqrt{2 R h_r} = \sqrt{1024} = 32.00 \text{ km}$$

Total:

$$d_{optical} = 35.78 + 32.00 = 67.78 \text{ km}$$

**(b) With standard refraction ($k = 4/3$):**

$$2 k R = 17{,}066.67 \text{ km}$$

$$d_{radio} = \sqrt{17{,}066.67 \times 0.1} + \sqrt{17{,}066.67 \times 0.08}$$

$$= \sqrt{1706.67} + \sqrt{1365.33}$$

$$= 41.31 + 36.95 = 78.26 \text{ km}$$

**Improvement due to refraction:** $78.26 - 67.78 = 10.48$ km (≈ 15.5 % more).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Even with relatively modest masts (100 m and 80 m), the radio horizon extends to nearly 80 km — sufficient for typical point-to-point microwave links and cellular backhaul.

## 3.6 Bullington's Equivalent Knife-Edge Method (Outline)

For irregular terrain, the **Bullington construction** works as follows:

1. Draw profiles from Tx and Rx to each of the terrain obstacles.
2. Compute the obstruction angle of each profile with respect to the LOS line.
3. The obstacle with the **largest obstruction angle** becomes the equivalent knife edge.
4. Compute $v$ for that knife edge using:
$$v = h \sqrt{\frac{2 d}{\lambda d_1 d_2}}$$
5. Look up the knife-edge diffraction loss from standard curves or the formula in §2.6, row 11.

This is the practical way spherical-Earth propagation is handled in real link budgets.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Geometry of LOS over Spherical Earth

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Centre of Earth O"] --> B["Transmitter Tower (height ht)"]
    A --> C["Receiver Tower (height hr)"]
    A --> D["Earth Bulge Point P (tangent point)"]
    B --> D
    C --> D
    B -. "LOS ray grazing the bulge" .-> D
    D -. "Distance d1 = sqrt of 2 R ht" .-> B
    D -. "Distance d2 = sqrt of 2 R hr" .-> C
    style A fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#bbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#bfb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#fbb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The transmitter tower of height $h_t$ sees the horizon at distance $d_1 = \sqrt{2 R h_t}$.
- The receiver tower of height $h_r$ sees the horizon at distance $d_2 = \sqrt{2 R h_r}$.
- The total LOS distance is $d_{LOS} = d_1 + d_2$.

## 4.2 Refraction Effect: Ray Bending vs Earth Inflation

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Actual Geometry"] --> B["Bent Ray over Real Earth radius R"]
    C["Effective Geometry"] --> D["Straight Ray over Inflated Earth radius kR where k=4 by 3"]
    B --> E["Complex Calculations"]
    D --> F["Same flat-Earth formulas apply"]
    style A fill:#ffe,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#efe,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#fcc,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#cfc,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- *Left branch:* In reality, the radio ray curves in the troposphere. The geometry is messy.
- *Right branch:* We *replace* this with an effective geometry where the Earth is **inflated** ($R_e = kR$), making the ray appear straight. All formulas then retain their familiar flat-Earth form.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Spherical Earth Propagation System

```mermaid
graph TD
    TX["Transmitter Antenna height ht"] --> PATH1["LOS Path over Spherical Earth"]
    PATH1 --> BULGE["Earth Bulge Obstruction Region"]
    BULGE --> DEC1{"Beyond Radio Horizon?"}
    DEC1 -- NO --> RX1["Receiver Antenna height hr Direct LOS"]
    DEC1 -- YES --> DIFFR["Diffraction over Bulge Loss in dB"]
    DIFFR --> RX2["Receiver Antenna height hr Diffracted Field"]
    RX1 --> BUDGET["Link Budget Computation"]
    RX2 --> BUDGET
    PATH1 -. "Refraction modifies geometry k-factor" .-> PATH1
    style TX fill:#bdf,stroke:#333
    style BULGE fill:#fbb,stroke:#333
    style DIFFR fill:#fbb,stroke:#333
    style BUDGET fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The wave leaves the Tx antenna and travels as a space wave.
- If the receiver is within the radio horizon, **direct LOS** is received.
- If the receiver is **beyond** the horizon, the wave must diffract over the Earth's bulge, incurring significant loss.
- The k-factor (refraction) **modifies the path** and effectively *increases* the radio horizon.

## 4.4 Effect of k-Factor on Ray Path (Conceptual Plot)

```mermaid
graph TD
    K1["k LESS THAN 1 Sub-refraction"] --> K1E["Ray bends less Earth appears flatter Horizon shrinks"]
    K2["k EQUAL TO 1 No refraction"] --> K2E["Straight ray normal horizon"]
    K3["k EQUAL TO 4 BY 3 Standard atmosphere"] --> K3E["Ray bends moderately Horizon extends 15 percent"]
    K4["k GREATER THAN 4 BY 3 Super-refraction"] --> K4E["Ray bends more Horizon extends further"]
    K5["k EQUAL TO INFINITY Ducting"] --> K5E["Ray follows curvature Trapping possible"]
    style K3 fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
    style K5 fill:#f99,stroke:#333
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]** 
*Define Radio Horizon. How is it different from Optical Horizon? Why does the Radio Horizon extend further than the Optical Horizon?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Radio Horizon:** The maximum distance over which a radio wave can travel between two antennas before being blocked by the Earth's bulge, considering atmospheric refraction.

**Optical Horizon:** The maximum geometric LOS distance assuming a straight ray over the actual Earth's surface (no refraction).

**Why Radio Horizon > Optical Horizon:** The troposphere has a refractive index that **decreases with altitude**, causing the radio ray to bend **toward the Earth**. This bending effectively *extends* the horizon distance. In the equivalent effective-Earth-radius concept, this is captured by the **k-factor** ($k = 4/3$ for standard atmosphere), giving:

$$d_{radio} = \sqrt{2 k R h_t} + \sqrt{2 k R h_r} > d_{optical} = \sqrt{2 R h_t} + \sqrt{2 R h_r}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only that "the radio horizon is larger" without explaining the *cause* (atmospheric refraction → effective Earth radius inflation). **Award full marks only with the cause-effect link.**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*What is the k-factor? State its standard value and explain what happens when k → ∞.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **k-factor** is the ratio of the **effective Earth's radius** to the **actual Earth's radius**:

$$k = \frac{R_e}{R} = \frac{1}{1 - R/\rho}$$

where $\rho$ is the radius of curvature of the radio ray.

**Standard value:** $k = 4/3$ for a **standard atmosphere** (refractivity gradient $dN/dh = -39$ N/km).

**When k → ∞:** The denominator $(1 - R/\rho) \rightarrow 0$, which means $R/\rho \rightarrow 1$. The ray's curvature matches the Earth's curvature, and the ray follows the Earth's surface indefinitely. This phenomenon is called **ducting** (or **critical refraction**), common in tropical coastal regions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget that **k → ∞ means ducting, not normal propagation**. Also, *never write* $k > 1$ is always refraction — clarify it is *super-refraction*, not just normal refraction.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Spherical Earth LOS with k-factor*

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the maximum **line-of-sight distance** between two antennas of heights $h_t$ and $h_r$ mounted on a spherical Earth of radius $R$, **assuming no atmospheric refraction**.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A microwave link uses antennas at heights $h_t = 120$ m and $h_r = 60$ m, operating at 6 GHz in a standard atmosphere ($k = 4/3$, $R = 6400$ km).
- (i) Compute the radio horizon distance.
- (ii) If the actual link distance is 90 km, what fraction of the first Fresnel zone is obstructed, given that the path is symmetric?
- (iii) Comment on whether the link will work reliably.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (Question A)**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Consider a transmitter antenna of height $h_t$ above the Earth's surface. The receiver is at ground level. Let $d_1$ be the distance from the transmitter base to the *horizon point* P.

In the right triangle OAP (with O at Earth's centre, A at the antenna tip, P at the horizon tangent point):

$$OA^2 = OP^2 + AP^2$$

$$(R + h_t)^2 = R^2 + d_1^2$$

Expanding:

$$R^2 + 2 R h_t + h_t^2 = R^2 + d_1^2$$

$$d_1^2 = 2 R h_t + h_t^2$$

Since $h_t \ll R$, $h_t^2$ is negligible:

$$\boxed{d_1 = \sqrt{2 R h_t}}$$

Similarly, the receiver at height $h_r$ sees a horizon at distance:

$$\boxed{d_2 = \sqrt{2 R h_r}}$$

Total LOS distance:

$$\boxed{d_{LOS} = \sqrt{2 R h_t} + \sqrt{2 R h_r}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $h_t = 0.12$ km, $h_r = 0.06$ km, $k = 4/3$, $R = 6400$ km, $f = 6$ GHz.

**(i) Radio horizon distance:**

$$2 k R = 2 \times \frac{4}{3} \times 6400 = 17{,}066.67 \text{ km}$$

$$d_1 = \sqrt{17{,}066.67 \times 0.12} = \sqrt{2048} = 45.25 \text{ km}$$

$$d_2 = \sqrt{17{,}066.67 \times 0.06} = \sqrt{1024} = 32.00 \text{ km}$$

$$\boxed{d_{radio} = 45.25 + 32.00 = 77.25 \text{ km}}$$

**(ii) Fresnel-zone obstruction:**

Wavelength: $\lambda = c/f = 3 \times 10^8 / 6 \times 10^9 = 0.05$ m.

Total link distance $d = 90$ km, $d_1 = 45$ km, $d_2 = 45$ km (symmetric).

The first Fresnel-zone radius at the midpoint is:

$$r_1 = \sqrt{\frac{\lambda d_1 d_2}{d}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.05 \times 45{,}000 \times 45{,}000}{90{,}000}}$$

$$r_1 = \sqrt{\frac{0.05 \times 2.025 \times 10^9}{9 \times 10^4}} = \sqrt{1125} = 33.54 \text{ m}$$

For a clear LOS, **60 % of the first Fresnel zone must be clear** (i.e., obstruction < 40 %). Since the radio horizon (77.25 km) is **less** than the actual link distance (90 km), the link is **beyond LOS**, and the Earth's bulge obstructs more than 100 % of the first Fresnel zone — **the link will not work reliably** in pure LOS mode.

**(iii) Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\text{Link will NOT work reliably without relay, taller antennas, or diffraction boost.}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q-A):**
> 1. **Don't forget the $h_t^2$ term justification** — explicitly say "$h_t^2 \ll 2Rh_t$" before dropping it (1 mark).
> 2. **Convert units carefully** — $h$ in metres vs km changes the constant from $\sqrt{2R}$ to $\sqrt{17{,}066.67/1000}$.
> 3. **Fresnel-zone condition:** students often state "100 % clearance needed" — actually **60 % clearance** of the first Fresnel zone is the design rule (40 % obstruction is acceptable).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Effective Earth's Radius and k-Factor*

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 4, Model Question]**

**(a)** What is the **effective Earth's radius** concept? Derive the relation between $k$, $R$ and the ray's radius of curvature $\rho$. Show that for standard atmosphere, $k = 4/3$.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tropospheric radio link is designed for $k = 4/3$. If the refractivity gradient changes to $-100$ N/km (super-refraction), compute the new k-factor and determine the new radio horizon for $h_t = 200$ m, $h_r = 100$ m. Take $R = 6400$ km.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (Question B)**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

In the real atmosphere, a radio ray travels along a curved path because the refractive index decreases with height. Instead of tracking the curved ray, we **enlarge the Earth's radius** to $R_e$ such that the ray can be drawn as a **straight line** over the new surface. This is the **effective Earth radius** concept.

For a ray of curvature $1/\rho$ and Earth of curvature $1/R$, the *relative curvature* between the ray and the Earth is:

$$\frac{1}{R_e} = \frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{\rho}$$

Solving for $R_e$:

$$R_e = \frac{R \rho}{\rho - R} = \frac{R}{1 - R/\rho}$$

Defining $k$:

$$\boxed{k = \frac{R_e}{R} = \frac{1}{1 - R/\rho}}$$

For standard atmosphere, the radius of curvature of the ray is:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = -\frac{dn}{dh}$$

where $n$ is the refractive index.

The refractivity $N = (n-1)\times 10^6$, so $n = 1 + N \times 10^{-6}$, and:

$$\frac{dn}{dh} = 10^{-6} \cdot \frac{dN}{dh}$$

Standard atmosphere: $\dfrac{dN}{dh} = -39$ N/km $= -39 \times 10^{-6}$ per m.

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = 39 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per m}$$

$$\frac{R}{\rho} = 6.4 \times 10^6 \times 39 \times 10^{-6} = 0.2496 \approx 0.25$$

$$k = \frac{1}{1 - 0.25} = \frac{1}{0.75} = \frac{4}{3}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\dfrac{dN}{dh} = -100$ N/km $= -100 \times 10^{-6}$ per m.

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = -\frac{dn}{dh} = -(-100 \times 10^{-6}) = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per m}$$

$$\frac{R}{\rho} = 6.4 \times 10^6 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 0.64$$

$$k = \frac{1}{1 - 0.64} = \frac{1}{0.36} = 2.778$$

**New radio horizon:**

$$2 k R = 2 \times 2.778 \times 6400 = 35{,}558 \text{ km}$$

$h_t = 0.2$ km, $h_r = 0.1$ km:

$$d_1 = \sqrt{35{,}558 \times 0.2} = \sqrt{7111.6} = 84.33 \text{ km}$$

$$d_2 = \sqrt{35{,}558 \times 0.1} = \sqrt{3555.8} = 59.63 \text{ km}$$

$$\boxed{d_{radio} = 84.33 + 59.63 = 143.96 \text{ km}}$$

**Comparison:** With super-refraction, the horizon extends from ~78 km (standard) to ~144 km — almost **double** the distance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q-B):**
> 1. **Sign convention:** The refractivity gradient $dN/dh$ is **negative** (decreasing with height). Many students miss the minus sign and get $k$ negative. **Always verify:** negative $dN/dh$ → positive $k$.
> 2. **Unit conversion:** $dN/dh$ in N/km must be converted to N/m (or vice-versa) when computing $R/\rho$ — $R$ is in metres.
> 3. **Critical refraction limit:** $k \to \infty$ occurs when $R/\rho = 1$, i.e., $dN/dh = -157$ N/km. Don't write $k$ can be arbitrarily large without this physical limit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Spherical Earth Propagation):**

- [ ] **Earth's actual radius:** $R = 6400$ km $= 6.4 \times 10^6$ m.
- [ ] **Optical horizon formula:** $d_{optical} = \sqrt{2 R h_t} + \sqrt{2 R h_r}$.
- [ ] **Radio horizon formula (with refraction):** $d_{radio} = \sqrt{2 k R h_t} + \sqrt{2 k R h_r}$.
- [ ] **Standard atmosphere k-factor:** $k = 4/3$.
- [ ] **Effective Earth's radius:** $R_e = k R = 8517$ km (when $k = 4/3$).
- [ ] **Engineering rule:** $d \approx \sqrt{17 h_t} + \sqrt{17 h_r}$ km (h in metres).
- [ ] **k-factor definition:** $k = 1 / (1 - R/\rho)$.
- [ ] **Refractivity gradient:** $dN/dh = -39$ N/km (standard atmosphere).
- [ ] **k → ∞ ⇒ Ducting** (critical refraction).
- [ ] **Beyond LOS:** Diffraction loss follows knife-edge / Bullington method.
- [ ] **Fresnel zone rule:** 60 % of first Fresnel zone must be clear for reliable link.
- [ ] **Real-world applications:** Microwave links, TV broadcasting, cellular network planning, radar siting.
- [ ] **Common exam question:** Derive LOS distance formula OR compute radio horizon for given antenna heights.
- [ ] **Always show the $h_t^2 \ll 2Rh_t$ justification** before simplifying the Pythagorean step.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line memory hook:** *"Earth bulges, refract bends, k-factor pretends, link budget rewards."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tropospheric Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT868 - ANTENNA THEORY AND WAVE PROPAGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tropospheric Waves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Tropospheric Waves** are electromagnetic radio waves that propagate through the **troposphere**, the lowest region of the Earth's atmosphere extending from the surface of the Earth up to an altitude of approximately **10 km**. In this region, the neutral atmosphere is composed of dry air, water vapor, and various gases whose densities and refractive properties change with altitude, temperature, pressure, and humidity.

Tropospheric propagation mechanisms enable VHF, UHF, and SHF radio wave communication between points that may be beyond the optical line of sight. The principal mechanisms governing tropospheric wave propagation are:

- **Atmospheric refraction** (bending of radio waves due to a gradient in the refractive index)
- **Duct propagation** (trapping of energy between the Earth and an elevated refractive layer)
- **Tropospheric scatter** (forward scattering from refractive index irregularities)
- **Diffraction and reflection** (from terrain features and atmospheric layers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Tropospheric propagation is a high-yield Module 4 topic. Students must master: refractive index profile, Earth radius factor ($k$), effective Earth's radius, super/sub-refraction, duct formation, and tropospheric scatter theory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Wavy Highway of the Sky"

Imagine a thin layer of warm air sitting above a layer of cool, dense air near the ground — like a *layered chocolate cake*. When a light beam (or radio wave) crosses from the dense lower layer into the rarer upper layer, it does not travel in a straight line; instead, it bends slightly, just as a pencil appears bent when half-submerged in a glass of water. This bending, when amplified by atmospheric conditions, can cause radio waves to "hug" the Earth's curvature and travel far beyond the optical horizon.

**Real-world implication:** This is why you can sometimes receive a faint, but usable, television or FM signal from a transmitter hundreds of kilometers away — well beyond line of sight.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following standard values are universally used in KTU numerical problems:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Actual Earth's radius | $a$ | **6370 km** |
| Standard refractive index (sea level) | $n_0$ | **1.000315** |
| Standard refractivity (sea level) | $N_0$ | **315 N-units** |
| Standard lapse rate of refractivity | $\frac{dN}{dh}$ | **−39 N-units/km** |
| Standard Earth radius factor | $k$ | **4/3 ≈ 1.333** |
| Effective Earth's radius (standard) | $a_e$ | **8493 km** |

## 1.4 Refractive Index of the Troposphere

The radio refractive index $n$ of the troposphere is a dimensionless quantity, slightly greater than unity, expressed by the relation:

$$n = 1 + \frac{77.6}{T}\left(p + \frac{4810\,e}{T}\right) \times 10^{-6}$$

where:
- $T$ = absolute temperature in **Kelvin (K)**
- $p$ = total atmospheric pressure in **millibars (mb)**
- $e$ = partial pressure of water vapor in **millibars (mb)**

For engineering convenience, the **refractivity** $N$ is defined as:

$$N = (n - 1) \times 10^{6}$$

so that $N$ is a more manageable number (order of hundreds). Therefore:

$$N = \frac{77.6}{T}\left(p + \frac{4810\,e}{T}\right)$$

## 1.5 Variation of Refractive Index with Height

Under standard atmospheric conditions, both temperature and water vapor content decrease with altitude, causing the refractive index $n$ to decrease smoothly with height $h$. The empirical model used is:

$$N(h) = N_s \cdot e^{-\beta h}$$

For practical radio engineering purposes, this is approximated by the linear relation:

$$N(h) = N_s - \alpha h$$

where:
- $N_s$ = surface refractivity ≈ **315 N-units**
- $\alpha$ = lapse rate ≈ **39 N-units/km**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under **standard atmospheric conditions**, the refractivity decreases at approximately **39 N-units per km** of altitude increase. This value is foundational for the $k = 4/3$ Earth-radius factor used in all KTU numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bending of Radio Waves in the Troposphere

When the refractive index decreases with height, a horizontally launched radio wave front travels slightly faster at higher altitudes than at lower altitudes. As a result, the wavefront gradually tilts downward, effectively curving the wave path toward the Earth. This is called **atmospheric refraction**.

The amount of bending depends on the **vertical gradient of refractive index**:

$$\frac{dN}{dh} < 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Wave bends toward Earth (normal refraction)}$$

## 2.2 Earth Radius Factor ($k$-factor)

To simplify ray-tracing diagrams, the curved ray path over an actual spherical Earth is replaced with a **straight-line ray** over an **equivalent Earth** whose radius is artificially enlarged.

**Definition:**

$$k = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{a}{n}\frac{dn}{dh}}$$

For the troposphere, since $n \approx 1$:

$$k \approx \frac{1}{1 + a\frac{dn}{dh}}$$

Expressed in terms of refractivity $N$:

$$k = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{a}{10^{6}}\frac{dN}{dh}}$$

Substituting the standard values ($a = 6370$ km, $dN/dh = -39$ N-units/km):

$$k = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{6370 \times (-39)}{10^{6}}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.2484} = \frac{1}{1.2484} \approx \frac{4}{3}$$

## 2.3 Effective Earth's Radius

$$a_{e} = k \cdot a$$

With $k = 4/3$:

$$a_{e} = \frac{4}{3} \times 6370 \approx 8493 \text{ km}$$

The straight-line ray is drawn over a sphere of radius **8493 km**, while the actual atmosphere bends the ray to follow the same trajectory.

## 2.4 Classification of Refraction Conditions

| Condition | Gradient $dN/dh$ (N-units/km) | $k$-factor | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sub-refraction** | $> -39$ (e.g., 0 or positive) | $k < 1$ | Ray bends away from Earth; horizon shrinks |
| **Standard refraction** | $-39$ | $k = 4/3 \approx 1.333$ | Normal atmospheric bending |
| **Super-refraction** | Between $-39$ and $-157$ | $k > 4/3$ | Ray bends sharply toward Earth |
| **Critical refraction** | $-157$ | $k = \infty$ | Ray path matches Earth's curvature |
| **Ducting / Trapping** | $< -157$ | $k < 0$ (mathematically) | Ray is trapped; energy guided |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** *"Sub = less than normal, Super = more than normal, Duct = trapped"*.
> The boundary between super-refraction and ducting is precisely when $dN/dh = -157$ N-units/km.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU question on tropospheric waves uses one or more of these equations.**

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = (n-1) \times 10^{6}$ | Refractivity definition |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{77.6}{T}\left(p + \dfrac{4810 e}{T}\right)$ | Refractivity from atmospheric parameters |
| 3 | $N(h) = N_s - \alpha h$ | Linear refractivity profile |
| 4 | $k = \dfrac{1}{1 + a\dfrac{dn}{dh}}$ | Earth radius factor |
| 5 | $k = \dfrac{1}{1 - \dfrac{a}{10^{6}}\dfrac{dN}{dh}}$ | $k$-factor in terms of $N$ gradient |
| 6 | $a_e = k \cdot a$ | Effective Earth's radius |
| 7 | $d_{\max} = \sqrt{2 k a h_t} + \sqrt{2 k a h_r}$ | **Maximum line-of-sight distance** (radio horizon) |
| 8 | $M = N + \dfrac{h}{a} \times 10^{6}$ | **Modified refractivity** (duct analysis) |
| 9 | $\dfrac{dM}{dh} = \dfrac{dN}{dh} + \dfrac{10^{6}}{a}$ | Modified refractivity gradient |
| 10 | Duct if $\dfrac{dM}{dh} \le 0$ | Duct formation condition |
| 11 | $L = 25 + 30 \log f + 30 \log d$ | Tropospheric scatter path loss (dB), $f$ in MHz, $d$ in km |
| 12 | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi)^{3} R^{4} L_s}$ | Tropospheric scatter received power |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In Formula 7, the quantities $h_t$ and $h_r$ are the **transmitting** and **receiving** antenna heights in **meters**, while $a$ is in **km**, and $d_{\max}$ comes out in **km** when $k = 4/3$ is used with the simplified form.

## 2.6 Modified Refractivity and Duct Formation

Standard plots of $N$ vs $h$ can be misleading because of Earth's curvature. To linearize the analysis, the **modified refractivity** $M$ is introduced:

$$M = N + \frac{h}{a} \times 10^{6}$$

The gradient becomes:

$$\frac{dM}{dh} = \frac{dN}{dh} + \frac{10^{6}}{a}$$

Substituting $a = 6370$ km:

$$\frac{dM}{dh} = \frac{dN}{dh} + 157$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Duct formation criterion:** A duct forms whenever $\dfrac{dM}{dh} \le 0$, i.e., $\dfrac{dN}{dh} \le -157$ N-units/km.

### Types of Ducts

1. **Surface Duct** — Trapping layer touches the Earth's surface. Caused by temperature inversions or sharp humidity drops near the ground.
2. **Elevated Duct** — Trapping layer is suspended above the ground, sandwiched between two refractive layers.
3. **Ground-Based Duct** — A combination where the duct extends from the surface up to a certain height.

## 2.7 Tropospheric Scatter Propagation

Beyond the radio horizon, weak but usable signals are received via **forward scattering** from turbulent irregularities (blobs) in the troposphere. The scattering volumes are formed where refractive index varies randomly due to turbulent mixing.

**Key features:**
- Operating frequency: **40 MHz to 4 GHz**
- Range: **100 km to 1000 km**
- Path loss follows: $L_s = 25 + 30 \log f + 30 \log d$ (dB, $f$ in MHz, $d$ in km)
- Slow fading due to atmospheric changes
- Used in military and remote-area communications

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Mechanism Used |
|---|---|
| FM radio reception beyond horizon | Super-refraction / ducting |
| Long-range radar over oceans | Surface duct propagation |
| Intercontinental TV/FM | Sporadic E and tropospheric ducting |
| Beyond-horizon UHF links | Tropospheric scatter |
| GPS error correction | Tropospheric delay modeling |
| Weather radar | Tropospheric scattering by hydrometeors |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Earth Radius Factor ($k$)

**Given:** The refractive index $n$ varies linearly with height $h$ in the troposphere.

**To Prove:** $k = \dfrac{1}{1 + a \dfrac{dn}{dh}}$

**Setup:** Consider a horizontally traveling ray at altitude $h$. After traversing a horizontal distance $\delta s$, the ray descends by $\delta h$. The geometry of the curved Earth and the refracted ray path is shown below.

Let the wave front be $PQ$ initially. After refraction through a layer of thickness $\delta h$, the lower edge of the wave front in the denser medium travels at velocity $v_1 = c/n_1$ while the upper edge travels at $v_2 = c/n_2$, where $n_2 < n_1$ (index decreases with height).

The **angular tilt** of the wave front per unit horizontal distance is:

$$\frac{d\theta}{ds} = -\frac{1}{n}\frac{dn}{dh}$$

This is the rate at which the ray curves downward (toward the Earth) per unit path length.

The curvature of the Earth as seen from the ray is $\dfrac{1}{a}$. For the ray to appear straight with respect to the equivalent Earth, the curvature of the equivalent Earth $\dfrac{1}{a_e}$ must equal the difference:

$$\frac{1}{a_e} = \frac{1}{a} - \frac{1}{n}\frac{dn}{dh}$$

Since $n \approx 1$ in the troposphere:

$$\frac{1}{a_e} = \frac{1}{a} - \frac{dn}{dh}$$

$$\frac{1}{a_e} = \frac{1}{a}\left(1 + a\frac{dn}{dh}\right)$$

Therefore, the **Earth radius factor** $k$ is:

$$\boxed{k = \frac{a_e}{a} = \frac{1}{1 + a\frac{dn}{dh}}}$$

Converting to refractivity $N$ (since $n = 1 + N \times 10^{-6}$, so $\dfrac{dn}{dh} = \dfrac{1}{10^{6}}\dfrac{dN}{dh}$):

$$k = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{a}{10^{6}}\dfrac{dN}{dh}}$$

## 3.2 Standard Atmosphere Verification of $k = 4/3$

**Standard values:**
- $a = 6370$ km
- $\dfrac{dN}{dh} = -39$ N-units/km

**Step 1:** Compute the term $\dfrac{a}{10^{6}}\dfrac{dN}{dh}$:

$$\frac{a}{10^{6}}\frac{dN}{dh} = \frac{6370 \times (-39)}{10^{6}} = \frac{-248430}{10^{6}} = -0.24843$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the $k$ formula:

$$k = \frac{1}{1 + (-0.24843)} = \frac{1}{0.75157}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerical value:

$$k = 1.3305 \approx \frac{4}{3}$$

Therefore, for **standard atmospheric conditions**, $k = 4/3$.

## 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Line-of-Sight Distance

For a transmitter at height $h_t$ and a receiver at height $h_r$ above a spherical Earth of effective radius $a_e = k a$, the radio horizon distance $d_{\max}$ is given by:

$$d_{\max} = d_t + d_r$$

where $d_t$ is the distance from transmitter to its horizon and $d_r$ is from receiver to its horizon.

Using the geometry of a right triangle (tangent from elevated point to sphere):

$$d_t^{2} + a_e^{2} = (a_e + h_t)^{2}$$

$$d_t^{2} = a_e^{2} + 2 a_e h_t + h_t^{2} - a_e^{2} = 2 a_e h_t + h_t^{2}$$

Since $h_t \ll a_e$, the term $h_t^{2}$ is negligible:

$$d_t \approx \sqrt{2 a_e h_t}$$

Similarly:

$$d_r \approx \sqrt{2 a_e h_r}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{d_{\max} \approx \sqrt{2 k a h_t} + \sqrt{2 k a h_r}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A transmitter antenna is mounted at a height of **225 m** and a receiver at **49 m**. Assuming standard atmospheric refraction ($k = 4/3$) and $a = 6370$ km, calculate the maximum line-of-sight distance.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the effective Earth's radius:

$$a_e = k \cdot a = \frac{4}{3} \times 6370 = 8493.33 \text{ km}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the transmitter horizon distance:

$$d_t = \sqrt{2 \times 8493.33 \times \frac{225}{1000}} \text{ km}$$

(heights converted to km)

$$d_t = \sqrt{2 \times 8493.33 \times 0.225}$$

$$d_t = \sqrt{3822.0}$$

$$d_t \approx 61.82 \text{ km}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the receiver horizon distance:

$$d_r = \sqrt{2 \times 8493.33 \times \frac{49}{1000}}$$

$$d_r = \sqrt{2 \times 8493.33 \times 0.049}$$

$$d_r = \sqrt{832.35}$$

$$d_r \approx 28.85 \text{ km}$$

**Step 4:** Compute total maximum distance:

$$d_{\max} = d_t + d_r = 61.82 + 28.85 = 90.67 \text{ km}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** $d_{\max} \approx 90.67$ km. Notice that if we had ignored atmospheric refraction (i.e., used $k = 1$), the answer would be $\approx 83$ km — atmospheric bending effectively extends the radio horizon by approximately **10%**.

## 3.5 Derivation of Duct Formation Condition

The modified refractivity $M$ is defined to "flatten out" the Earth's curvature:

$$M = N + \frac{h}{a} \times 10^{6}$$

For a ray to be trapped, it must continually bend downward more strongly than the Earth curves downward. The rate of bending is governed by the gradient $\dfrac{dn}{dh}$, while Earth's curvature is $\dfrac{1}{a}$. The duct condition is:

$$\left|-\frac{dn}{dh}\right| > \frac{1}{a} \quad \Rightarrow \quad -\frac{dn}{dh} > \frac{1}{a}$$

Converting to refractivity:

$$-\frac{1}{10^{6}}\frac{dN}{dh} > \frac{1}{a}$$

$$-\frac{dN}{dh} > \frac{10^{6}}{a}$$

$$-\frac{dN}{dh} > \frac{10^{6}}{6370} \approx 157 \text{ N-units/km}$$

**Critical gradient:**

$$\boxed{\frac{dN}{dh}\bigg|_{\text{critical}} = -157 \text{ N-units/km}}$$

A duct forms whenever $\dfrac{dN}{dh} < -157$ N-units/km.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Mechanism | Frequency Range | Typical Range | Path Loss Behavior | Fading |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Line-of-sight** | All | Up to radio horizon | Free-space $1/d^{2}$ | Minimal |
| **Tropospheric refraction** | VHF–SHF | Slightly beyond horizon | Slightly higher than free-space | Slow |
| **Duct propagation** | VHF–SHF | 100–1000 km (over water) | Low (trapped energy) | Very slow |
| **Tropospheric scatter** | 40 MHz – 4 GHz | 100–1000 km | $1/d^{3}$ to $1/d^{4}$ (rapid) | Slow + fast |
| **Diffraction (over ridges)** | All | Slightly beyond horizon | High | Slow |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

    A --> E[Diffraction and Reflection]

    B --> B1[Standard k equals 4/3]
    B --> B2[Super refraction k greater than 4/3]
    B --> B3[Sub refraction k less than 4/3]

    C --> C2[Elevated Duct]
